$$

### Step 5 — Interpret

A ROSI of **2.5** means every ₹1 invested in encryption saves ₹2.50 in expected annual loss. This is a **strongly positive** security investment, and the answer to a KTU "justify the investment" question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Line (Must Appear in KTU Board):** *ROSI = 2.5 (i.e., 250% net benefit). The encryption control is financially justified.*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — GDPR Penalty Bound

**Problem:** A European e-commerce firm has an annual global turnover of **€800 Million** and suffers a major data breach affecting 2 million users. Compute the **maximum statutory GDPR penalty**.

### Step 1 — Compute 4% of Turnover

$$
4\% \times 800 \times 10^{6} = 32 \times 10^{6} \text{ EUR} = \text{€32 Million}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Statutory Cap

$$
\text{Penalty} = \min\!\left( \text{€20{,}000{,}000},\; \text{€32{,}000{,}000} \right) = \text{€20 Million}
$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion

The penalty is **capped at €20 Million**, even though 4% of turnover (€32 M) is higher. The "lower of the two" rule applies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing the answer as "€32 Million" instead of the legally capped "€20 Million". The $\min(\cdot)$ function **always** wins in GDPR penalty math.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Big Data Security Challenge Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BIG DATA SECURITY CHALLENGES] --> B[DATA BREACHES]
    A --> C[PRIVACY CONCERNS]
    A --> D[COMPLIANCE ISSUES]

    B --> B1[INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER]
    B --> B2[STORAGE LAYER]
    B --> B3[PROCESSING LAYER]

    B1 --> B1A[STOLEN CREDENTIALS]
    B1 --> B1B[NETWORK SNIFFING]
    B1 --> B1C[VM ESCAPE]

    B2 --> B2A[UNENCRYPTED HDFS]
    B2 --> B2B[NoSQL INJECTION]
    B2 --> B2C[MISCONFIGURED S3 BUCKETS]

    B3 --> B3A[MALICIOUS MAPREDUCE JOBS]
    B3 --> B3B[HIVE SQL INJECTION]
    B3 --> B3C[MODEL INVERSION ATTACKS]

    C --> C1[PII EXPOSURE]
    C --> C2[RE-IDENTIFICATION RISK]
    C --> C3[CONSENT VIOLATION]

    C1 --> C1A[NAME, EMAIL, PHONE]
    C1 --> C1B[FINANCIAL RECORDS]
    C1 --> C1C[HEALTH DATA PHI]

    D --> D1[GDPR]
    D --> D2[HIPAA]
    D --> D3[PCI-DSS]
    D --> D4[IT ACT 2000 INDIA]
    D --> D5[NIST 800-53]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[SOURCE LAYER: IoT, Apps, Logs, Social] --> ING[INGESTION LAYER: Kafka, Flume, NiFi]
    ING --> STORE[STORAGE LAYER: HDFS, HBase, S3]
    STORE --> PROC[PROCESSING LAYER: Spark, MapReduce, Flink]
    PROC --> ANALYTICS[ANALYTICS LAYER: ML Models, Dashboards]

    SEC1[SECURITY CONTROL: TLS IN TRANSIT] -.-> ING
    SEC2[SECURITY CONTROL: AES-256 AT REST] -.-> STORE
    SEC3[SECURITY CONTROL: RBAC AND KERBEROS] -.-> PROC
    SEC4[SECURITY CONTROL: DIFFERENTIAL PRIVACY] -.-> ANALYTICS

    AUDIT[CENTRAL AUDIT AND SIEM: ELK, Splunk] -.-> ING
    AUDIT -.-> STORE
    AUDIT -.-> PROC
    AUDIT -.-> ANALYTICS

    COMP[COMPLIANCE ENGINE: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI] -.-> AUDIT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:** The dotted arrows represent *cross-cutting security controls* applied across all data layers. The central SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) feeds the Compliance Engine, which in turn enforces regulatory rules.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Breach Response Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[PHASE 1: IDENTIFY] --> P2[PHASE 2: PROTECT]
    P2 --> P3[PHASE 3: DETECT]
    P3 --> P4[PHASE 4: RESPOND]
    P4 --> P5[PHASE 5: RECOVER]
    P5 --> P6[PHASE 6: COMPLY AND REPORT]
    P6 --> P1

    P1 --> P1A[Asset Inventory, Risk Assessment]
    P2 --> P2A[Encryption, IAM, Firewalls]
    P3 --> P3A[IDS, SIEM, Anomaly Detection]
    P4 --> P4A[Incident Triage, Containment]
    P5 --> P5A[Backup Restore, Forensic Analysis]
    P6 --> P6A[Notify DPA within 72 hours GDPR]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This six-phase model is the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** mapped onto Big Data. It is the **most-asked framework** in KTU Module 1 descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a data breach. List any four major data breach incidents reported in the last decade with their impact scale.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **data breach** is an incident in which confidential, sensitive, or protected information is accessed, disclosed, or stolen by an unauthorized party.

| # | Incident | Year | Records Affected | Impact |
|---|----------|------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | Yahoo Breach | 2013–14 | 3 Billion | All Yahoo accounts compromised |
| 2 | Equifax Breach | 2017 | 147 Million | PII, SSNs, credit data leaked |
| 3 | Marriott International | 2018 | 500 Million | Passport and guest data exposed |
| 4 | Facebook–Cambridge Analytica | 2018 | 87 Million | Used for political profiling |
| 5 | LinkedIn | 2021 | 700 Million | Scraped data sold on dark web |

*(Any four with brief description: ½ mark each = 2 marks; definition: 1 mark.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the three primary security challenges in Big Data with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Data Breaches** — Unauthorized access to large datasets.
   *Example:* The 2017 Equifax breach exposed 147 M customer records from a Hadoop-based analytics pipeline.

2. **Privacy Concerns** — Exposure of Personally Identifiable Information (PII).
   *Example:* Facebook–Cambridge Analytica harvested 87 M user profiles without explicit consent for political ad targeting.

3. **Compliance Issues** — Failure to meet regulatory standards.
   *Example:* British Airways was fined **£20 Million** under GDPR for inadequate data-handling in its customer analytics system.

*(One mark per challenge with example.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the various security challenges in Big Data environments. Categorize them under data breaches, privacy concerns, and compliance issues. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Data Breaches in Big Data (3 marks)**
- **Definition & Examples:** Unauthorized access to distributed data stores.
- **Attack Vectors:** Misconfigured S3 buckets, NoSQL injection, compromised Kerberos tickets, insider threats, side-channel attacks on shared cloud infrastructure.
- **Big Data–Specific Risk Amplifiers:** Replication factor (default 3× in HDFS means 3× exposure), lack of fine-grained ACLs in early Hadoop versions, and weak default authentication in NoSQL systems like early MongoDB.
- **High-Profile Case:** Capital One breach (2019) — exfiltrated 100M records via a misconfigured Web Application Firewall (WAF) on AWS.

**2. Privacy Concerns in Big Data (2 marks)**
- PII/PHI exposure through analytics outputs and ML model inversion.
- Re-identification risk: anonymized datasets can be de-anonymized by joining with public sources (e.g., Netflix Prize de-anonymization, 2007).
- Lack of informed consent in IoT and social media data harvesting.

**3. Compliance Issues in Big Data (2 marks)**
- Cross-border data-flow restrictions (GDPR Chapter V).
- Right to erasure (GDPR Article 17) is difficult to implement on immutable storage (e.g., HDFS append-only model).
- Audit trail requirements: 6+ years for financial data under SOX.
- HIPAA: PHI must be encrypted in transit and at rest; cloud providers must sign BAAs.

---

**(b) Explain the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) phases as applicable to a Big Data architecture, and compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) for the following scenario: (7 Marks)**
*"A Hadoop cluster processes customer payment data worth ₹10 Crore. A breach exposes 40% of records. The breach has historically occurred once every 2 years. After deploying Kerberos authentication and HDFS encryption, the ARO drops to 0.1/year and EF drops to 10%. The cost of controls is ₹15 Lakh. Calculate the ROSI and comment on whether the investment is justified."*

**Model Solution:**

**NIST CSF Phases Applied to Big Data (4 marks):**

1. **Identify** — Asset inventory (Hadoop namenode, data nodes, edge nodes), data classification (sensitive vs. non-sensitive), governance policies.
2. **Protect** — Implement Kerberos authentication, HDFS Transparent Encryption, RBAC via Apache Ranger, TLS for data-in-transit.
3. **Detect** — Deploy Apache Ambari alerts, audit logging into ELK Stack, anomaly detection on data exfiltration patterns.
4. **Respond** — IR team isolates compromised data nodes, revokes Kerberos tickets, snapshots affected HDFS directories.
5. **Recover** — Restore from HDFS snapshots, validate data integrity via checksums, resume analytics jobs.
6. **Comply & Report** — Generate audit reports for GDPR, PCI-DSS; notify Data Protection Authority (DPA) within **72 hours** of breach detection.

**ALE Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 10 \times 10^{7} \times 0.40 = 4 \times 10^{7} \text{ INR} \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} &= \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 4 \times 10^{7} \times 0.5 = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 10 \times 10^{7} \times 0.10 = 1 \times 10^{7} \text{ INR} \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} &= 0.1 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 1 \times 10^{7} \times 0.1 = 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**ROSI Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{ALE} &= 2 \times 10^{7} - 1 \times 10^{6} = 1.9 \times 10^{7} \text{ INR} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{1.9 \times 10^{7} - 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt]
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{1.75 \times 10^{7}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = 11.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A ROSI of **11.67** (i.e., ₹11.67 saved for every ₹1 invested) — investment is **strongly justified**.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) What is data privacy? Explain the key privacy concerns specific to Big Data systems, including re-identification, consent, and the GDPR's right to be forgotten. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data privacy is the branch of data ethics and security concerned with the proper handling of personally identifiable information (PII) — names, emails, IP addresses, biometric data, browsing patterns — in accordance with user consent and statutory mandates.

**Big Data–Specific Privacy Concerns (4 marks):**

1. **Re-identification Risk** — Combining "anonymized" datasets with public sources can re-identify individuals. *Example:* Latanya Sweeney (2000) re-identified 87% of Americans using only ZIP code, birthdate, and sex.
2. **Lack of Granular Consent** — IoT sensors and mobile apps often obtain blanket consent that does not specify analytics use.
3. **Right to be Forgotten (GDPR Art. 17)** — Difficult to implement on:
   - Immutable ledgers (blockchain).
   - HDFS (append-only architecture; no native row-level delete).
   - Replicated data across multiple data centers.
4. **Data Linkage & Inference Attacks** — Joining multiple public datasets can infer sensitive attributes (e.g., health status from purchase history).
5. **Third-Party Data Sharing** — When data is sold or shared with advertisers, accountability chains break.

**Mitigation Techniques (2 marks):**
- Differential privacy (e.g., Apple's approach with ε-budgets).
- k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness.
- Tokenization and pseudonymization.
- Homomorphic encryption for analytics on encrypted data.

---

**(b) Discuss the major compliance frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, IT Act 2000) relevant to Big Data security. For each, list two specific technical requirements a Hadoop-based system must implement. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Framework | Applicability | Two Hadoop-Specific Technical Requirements |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **GDPR** (EU) | Any org processing EU resident data | (i) Implement pseudonymization for all PII fields in Hive tables. (ii) Maintain an immutable audit log of all data access using Apache Ranger + Kafka + immutable WORM storage. |
| **HIPAA** (USA) | Healthcare data (PHI) | (i) Encrypt all HDFS data blocks containing PHI using HDFS Transparent Encryption (AES-256). (ii) Enforce role-based access control via Apache Sentry so that only authorized clinicians can query patient records. |
| **PCI-DSS** v4.0 | Credit card data | (i) Tokenize 16-digit card numbers before storing in HBase (never store raw PAN). (ii) Restrict Hadoop cluster network access via firewall ACLs and segment the cardholder data environment (CDE) from analytics zones. |
| **IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011** (India) | All digital data in India | (i) Implement "Reasonable Security Practices" (IS/ISO/IEC 27001). (ii) Maintain logs of all data access for the statutory period and appoint a Grievance Officer for privacy complaints. |

**Key Takeaway (1 mark):** Compliance is not a one-time event but a **continuous** technical and organizational process. In Big Data, this means **automated compliance-as-code** pipelines using tools like Apache Ranger, AWS Macie, and Azure Purview.

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula Without Variables:** Writing "SLE = AV × EF" but forgetting to define AV and EF in words. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **GDPR Penalty Logic:** Writing €20 Million as 4% of turnover without the $\min(\cdot)$ operation. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Mixing Up Layers:** Saying "encryption protects against SQL injection" — it does NOT. **Loss: 1–2 marks** depending on context.
> 4. **Skipping Justification:** Computing ROSI but not concluding "investment is justified" or "not justified". **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Single Framework Only:** Naming only GDPR in compliance answers. **Loss: 1–2 marks** — KTU requires at least 2–3 frameworks.
> 6. **Hadoop-Specificity:** Generic answers like "use firewalls and encryption" score poorly. Always anchor to **HDFS, Hive, HBase, Spark, Kerberos, Ranger, Sentry**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Big Data Security** = Securing the 3 V's pipeline against confidentiality, integrity, availability, and privacy threats.
- [ ] **Three primary challenge domains:** Data Breaches, Privacy Concerns, Compliance Issues.
- [ ] **3-V model:** Volume, Velocity, Variety.
- [ ] **NIST CSF Phases (6):** Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover → Comply.
- [ ] **Key formulas:** $\text{SLE} = AV \times EF$, $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$, $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{ALE} - \text{CoC}}{\text{CoC}}$.
- [ ] **GDPR cap:** $\min(\text{€20M},\ 4\%\ \text{turnover})$ — the lower value applies.
- [ ] **GDPR breach notification:** 72 hours to notify the supervisory authority.
- [ ] **HIPAA** governs PHI; **PCI-DSS** governs card data; **SOX** governs financial audit trails.
- [ ] **HDFS-specific risks:** Default replication × 3, append-only architecture, lack of native row-level encryption (mitigated by HDFS Transparent Encryption).
- [ ] **NoSQL-specific risks:** NoSQL injection (MongoDB), weak default ACLs, eventual consistency making audit difficult.
- [ ] **Privacy techniques:** k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness, differential privacy, pseudonymization, tokenization.
- [ ] **Re-identification:** Always possible with auxiliary data — anonymization ≠ de-identification.
- [ ] **Right to be Forgotten (GDPR Art. 17):** Hard to enforce on immutable or replicated systems.
- [ ] **Real-time streaming risk:** TLS/SSL is mandatory for Kafka, NiFi, Flink channels.
- [ ] **ML model inversion:** Trained models can leak training data — use differential privacy during training.
- [ ] **Compliance-as-Code:** Apache Ranger + AWS Macie + Azure Purview for automated policy enforcement.
- [ ] **KTU High-Yield Numbers to Memorize:**
  - IBM breach cost (2023): **USD 4.45 M**
  - MTTI: **204 days**, MTTC: **73 days**
  - GDPR max fine: **€20 M or 4% of turnover**
  - GDPR notification: **72 hours**
  - Yahoo breach: **3 Billion accounts**
  - Equifax breach: **147 M records**
  - Marriott breach: **500 M records**

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam-Day Mantra:**
> *"Define → Diagram → Differentiate → Derive → Defend"*
> Always begin Big Data Security answers with a clean definition, draw a layered architecture, distinguish between the 3 challenges, derive any quantitative metric, and defend your recommendation with a compliance citation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The CIA triad in the context of Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The CIA triad in the context of Big Data # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **CIA Triad** is the foundational and universally accepted conceptual model in information security that defines the three core pillars upon which the security of any data system — including Big Data ecosystems — is constructed. In the context of Big Data, the triad must be reinterpreted and extended to handle the unique challenges introduced by the **5 V's** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The CIA Triad is a model designed to guide policies for Information Security within an organization. In Big Data, it represents **Confidentiality** (preventing unauthorized access to massive datasets), **Integrity** (ensuring data accuracy across distributed nodes), and **Availability** (guaranteeing timely access to data for authorized analytics). | Pillar | Core Meaning | Big Data Specific Focus | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Confidentiality** | Privacy & Authorized Access | Encryption of petabytes, access control in HDFS, NoSQL | | **Integrity** | Accuracy & Trustworthiness | Tamper-proof storage, checksum validation, blockchain | | **Availability** | Reliable & Timely Access | Fault tolerance, replication, disaster recovery | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault in a Digital Library":** > Imagine a massive library (your **Big Data cluster**) containing millions of unique books written in hundreds of different languages (Variety), with new books arriving every second (Velocity), and stacks so tall they fill entire warehouses (Volume). > - **Confidentiality** is like the librarian ensuring only members with the right ID card can read specific books — strangers are blocked at the door. > - **Integrity** is like a notary stamping every book daily, certifying that no page has been torn, rewritten, or replaced with fake content. > - **Availability** is like keeping the library open 24/7 with backup generators — even during a power outage or flood, members can still read their books. > > In a Big Data environment (e.g., Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL databases), the "library" is so enormous and dynamic that protecting it requires industrial-scale tools — not just a single lock and key. ## 1.3 Why the CIA Triad Matters in Big Data > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — The 5 V's of Big Data as Threat Multipliers:** > Traditional security models (designed for relational databases) collapse when faced with: > 1. **Volume:** Storing exabytes makes encryption computationally expensive. > 2. **Velocity:** Real-time streaming leaves no time for heavy cryptographic checks. > 3. **Variety:** Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data (images, logs, tweets) require different protection schemes. > 4. **Veracity:** Data from unreliable sources threatens integrity. > 5. **Value:** High-value data attracts sophisticated attackers, raising the stakes for confidentiality. **Standard Metric Highlighted in KTU Module 1:** - The three primary security goals are referred to collectively as the **CIA Triad**, also known as the **AIC Triad** (Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality) in some international standards. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Breakdown of the Three Pillars ### 🔐 2.1.1 Confidentiality - **Definition:** Ensures that data is disclosed only to those who are **authorized** to view it. - **Mechanisms in Big Data:** * **Encryption (AES-256, RSA)** at rest and in transit. * **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** in HDFS and HBase. * **Apache Ranger / Apache Sentry** for centralized policy management. * **Key Management Systems (KMS)** like HashiCorp Vault. - **Big Data Challenge:** Encrypting petabytes slows down MapReduce jobs; hence **selective encryption** and **homomorphic encryption** are researched. ### 🛡️ 2.1.2 Integrity - **Definition:** Guarantees that data is **accurate, consistent, and unaltered** from its source to its destination. - **Mechanisms in Big Data:** * **Checksums & Hash Functions** (MD5, SHA-256) for HDFS block verification. * **Digital Signatures** for sender authentication. * **Write-Once Read-Many (WORM)** storage. * **Blockchain** for tamper-evident logs. * **Apache Kafka's idempotent producers** to prevent duplicate writes. - **Big Data Challenge:** Distributed nodes may diverge; **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** and **Raft consensus** algorithms are employed. ### ⚡ 2.1.3 Availability - **Definition:** Ensures that systems and data are **accessible to authorized users whenever needed**. - **Mechanisms in Big Data:** * **Data Replication** (HDFS default: 3 copies across racks). * **Failover Clusters & Load Balancing**. * **Disaster Recovery (DR) sites** with geographic distribution. * **DDoS mitigation** via CDNs and rate limiting. * **Erasure Coding** (e.g., Reed-Solomon) for space-efficient redundancy. - **Big Data Challenge:** High availability under **Ransomware attacks** and **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)**. ## 2.2 The CIA Triad in Big Data — A Layered View > [!NOTE] > **The "Parkerian Hexad" Extension (KTU Reference):** > Modern security researchers (Donn Parker, 2002) extended the CIA Triad into six elements: **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, Possession, and Utility**. In Big Data, **Authenticity** (verifying the source) and **Utility** (data being usable for analytics) become critical. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Meaning | Big Data Context | Unit / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $C$ | Confidentiality | Unauthorized access prevention | Boolean (0/1) | | $I$ | Integrity | Tamper-detection probability | Percentage (%) | | $A$ | Availability | Uptime over total time | Percentage (%) | | $H(m)$ | Hash of message $m$ | Used in HDFS block checksums | SHA-256 → 256 bits | | $E_k(m)$ | Encrypted message | Protects confidentiality | AES-256 → 256-bit key | | $R_f$ | Replication Factor | HDFS copies per block | Integer $\geq 3$ | | $\lambda$ | Failure rate | Server reliability | Failures per hour | | $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $1 / \lambda$ | Hours | | $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | Recovery speed | Hours | | $A_{sys}$ | System Availability | $\frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare:** HIPAA compliance demands CIA for genomic Big Data. - **Finance:** SEC regulations require integrity of stock market tick data. - **IoT & Smart Cities:** Petabytes of sensor data must remain available 24/7. - **Social Media Analytics:** Twitter/Facebook must ensure user privacy (Confidentiality) while running ML on behavioral data. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: System Availability Formula (KTU High-Yield) The **Availability ($A$)** of a system is the proportion of time the system is operational and accessible. For a Big Data cluster with redundant nodes, this is derived as follows: > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** A KTU exam question on availability often asks you to calculate the downtime or uptime of a Hadoop-like distributed system. Understanding this derivation is critical. ### Step 1: Define Failure and Repair Rates Let: - $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures per unit time) - $\mu$ = repair rate (repairs per unit time) ### Step 2: Derive Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) In a Poisson failure model, the expected time between failures is the reciprocal of the failure rate: $$ \begin{aligned} MTBF &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** If a server fails on average 0.01 times per hour ($\lambda = 0.01$), then the average time between failures is $1 / 0.01 = 100$ hours. ### Step 3: Derive Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) Similarly, the average time taken to repair a failed component is: $$ \begin{aligned} MTTR &= \frac{1}{\mu} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Combine to Form System Availability A system is available when it is NOT under repair. The classical availability formula is: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{sys} &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\ &= \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} \\ &= \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** This expresses the fraction of long-term time the system is functional. If $\lambda = 0.01$ and $\mu = 1$, then $A_{sys} = 1 / (0.01 + 1) = 0.9901$, i.e., **99.01% uptime**. ### Step 5: Extend to Parallel Redundant Systems (Big Data Clusters) For $N$ parallel nodes where the system fails only if **all** nodes fail (HDFS model): $$ \begin{aligned} A_{parallel}(N) &= 1 - (1 - A_{single})^{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** If a single node has $A_{single} = 0.99$ (99%) and we have 3 replicas ($N = 3$), the cluster's data availability becomes $1 - (0.01)^3 = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999$, which is the famous **"five nines" (99.999%)** reliability. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: CIA Mapping to Big Data Technologies | CIA Pillar | Hadoop / HDFS | Apache Spark | NoSQL (Cassandra) | Stream (Kafka) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Confidentiality** | HDFS Transparent Encryption, Ranger | Spark SSL, Auth via Kerberos | Role-Based Access, TLS | SASL/SSL, ACLs | | **Integrity** | Block checksums, HDFS fsck | RDD lineage, idempotent ops | Merkle trees, anti-entropy | CRC checksums, idempotent producers | | **Availability** | Replication Factor 3, NameNode HA | Cluster mode + YARN HA | Multi-DC replication, tunable consistency | Partitioned topics, in-sync replicas | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Be prepared to map each CIA pillar to a specific Big Data tool. The most common answer expected is **Kerberos** for Hadoop authentication (Confidentiality), **Block-level checksums** for HDFS integrity, and **NameNode High Availability** for availability. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["BIG DATA SECURITY
FOUNDATION"] nodeB["CONFIDENTIALITY
Authorized Access Only"] nodeC["INTEGRITY
Data Accuracy and Trust"] nodeD["AVAILABILITY
Always Accessible"] nodeE["Encryption AES-256
Kerberos Auth
Apache Ranger"] nodeF["SHA-256 Hashes
Digital Signatures
Consensus Protocols"] nodeG["Replication Factor 3
NameNode HA
Disaster Recovery"] nodeH["HDFS / HBase
NoSQL Databases
Data Lakes"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH ``` ## 4.2 CIA Triad — Layered Defense Architecture in Big Data ```mermaid flowchart LR user1["Authorized User"] --> layer1["Layer 1: Authentication
Kerberos, LDAP, OAuth"] layer1 --> layer2["Layer 2: Authorization
RBAC, ACLs, Ranger Policies"] layer2 --> layer3["Layer 3: Encryption
AES-256 at rest, TLS in transit"] layer3 --> layer4["Layer 4: Integrity Checks
SHA-256, Merkle Trees, Checksums"] layer4 --> layer5["Layer 5: Replication and HA
RF=3, NameNode Failover, Erasure Coding"] layer5 --> storage["Big Data Storage
HDFS, Cassandra, S3"] attacker["Unauthorized Attacker"] -.blocked.-> layer1 attacker -.blocked.-> layer2 attacker -.blocked.-> layer3 ``` ## 4.3 Flow: How CIA Operates During a Data Write Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Client Initiates Write Request"] --> stepB{"Authenticated?
Confidentiality Check"} stepB -- No --> stepEndX["Access Denied
Confidentiality Violation"] stepB -- Yes --> stepC["Apply Encryption
AES-256 to Payload"] stepC --> stepD["Compute Hash
Integrity: H=m via SHA-256"] stepD --> stepE["Write to 3 Replicas
Availability: HDFS RF=3"] stepE --> stepF["Verify Block Checksums
Integrity Validation"] stepF --> stepG{"All Checksums Valid?"} stepG -- No --> stepEndY["Rollback and Alert
Integrity Violation"] stepG -- Yes --> stepH["Commit and Acknowledge
All 3 CIA Pillars Satisfied"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** This block-level architecture shows how every Big Data operation passes through the three CIA gates before being committed. This is a typical KTU 14-mark answer diagram expected by examiners. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the CIA Triad. Why is it considered the cornerstone of Big Data security?** **Model Answer:** The **CIA Triad** is a model representing the three fundamental principles of information security: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability**. - **Confidentiality** ensures that Big Data is accessible only to authorized users (protected via encryption and access control). - **Integrity** ensures that the massive and distributed data remains accurate and unaltered (protected via checksums, hashing, and digital signatures). - **Availability** ensures that petabytes of data remain accessible 24/7 (protected via replication, fault tolerance, and disaster recovery). In Big Data, the triad is the cornerstone because traditional security mechanisms fail to scale to exabytes, requiring **distributed, parallel, and adaptive** security implementations across HDFS, Spark, and NoSQL ecosystems. *(3 marks)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between Confidentiality and Integrity in the context of Big Data.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Confidentiality | Integrity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Goal** | Prevent unauthorized disclosure | Prevent unauthorized modification | | **Big Data Tool** | Apache Ranger, HDFS Transparent Encryption | HDFS block checksums, SHA-256 | | **Attack Defended** | Eavesdropping, Data theft | Tampering, Data corruption | | **Metric** | Strength of encryption (e.g., 256-bit AES) | Hash collision resistance | *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **confuse the three pillars** by writing generic security answers without mapping them to Big Data technologies (HDFS, Spark, NoSQL). This costs **3–4 marks** per question. > - Always mention **specific Big Data tools** (e.g., Kerberos for authentication in Hadoop) — generic answers like "use passwords" fetch **zero credit**. > - For 14-mark questions, **diagrams are mandatory**. The KTU valuation key allots **2 marks** exclusively for a well-labeled block diagram. > - In derivation questions, **show every algebraic step**. Skipping the MTTR/MTBF substitution costs **1.5 marks**. ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **a) Explain the three components of the CIA Triad in detail, with specific reference to Big Data technologies. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Confidentiality (2 marks):** Confidentiality ensures that Big Data is not disclosed to unauthorized entities. In Hadoop, this is enforced via: - **HDFS Transparent Encryption:** Encrypts data at rest using AES-256. - **Kerberos Authentication:** Mutual authentication between client and NameNode. - **Apache Ranger:** Centralized policy management for fine-grained access control. - **TLS/SSL:** Encrypts data in transit between DataNodes and clients. **2. Integrity (2.5 marks):** Integrity guarantees that the data stored across distributed nodes has not been tampered with. Mechanisms include: - **HDFS Block Checksums:** Each 128 MB block is accompanied by a SHA-256 hash; corrupted blocks are detected and replaced via replicas. - **Digital Signatures:** Used in APIs and message queues (Kafka) to verify sender authenticity. - **Idempotent Operations:** Spark Streaming and Kafka producers prevent duplicate writes that could corrupt datasets. - **Merkle Trees in Cassandra:** Anti-entropy repair using Merkle trees ensures consistency across replicas. **3. Availability (2.5 marks):** Availability ensures the Big Data system is operational 24/7. Techniques include: - **Replication Factor 3:** HDFS stores 3 copies of every block across different racks. - **NameNode High Availability (HA):** Active-Passive NameNode setup with Zookeeper-based failover. - **Erasure Coding:** Reed-Solomon codes reduce storage overhead while maintaining fault tolerance. - **Disaster Recovery:** Cross-datacenter replication for ransomware and natural disaster resilience. **Conclusion:** Together, these three pillars form the bedrock of Big Data security, addressing the **5 V's** challenges. *(7 marks)* --- **b) A Hadoop cluster has a single DataNode with MTBF = 500 hours and MTTR = 5 hours. Calculate the system availability. If the cluster uses a Replication Factor of 3, what is the data availability? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Single Node Availability (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{single} &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\ &= \frac{500}{500 + 5} \\ &= \frac{500}{505} \\ &\approx 0.9901 \text{ or } 99.01\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Data Availability with RF = 3 (4 marks)** The data is unavailable only if **all 3 replicas** are simultaneously unavailable: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{all 3 down}) &= (1 - A_{single})^{3} \\ &= (1 - 0.9901)^{3} \\ &= (0.0099)^{3} \\ &\approx 9.7 \times 10^{-7} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{data} &= 1 - P(\text{all 3 down}) \\ &= 1 - 9.7 \times 10^{-7} \\ &\approx 0.999999 \text{ or } 99.9999\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Even with modest single-node reliability, replication delivers near-perfect data availability — the foundational principle behind HDFS's "five nines" guarantee. *(7 marks)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **a) Discuss the challenges that the 5 V's of Big Data pose to the traditional CIA Triad, and propose engineering solutions. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | V | Challenge to CIA | Engineering Solution | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Volume** | Encrypting petabytes is computationally expensive, slowing down MapReduce. | **Selective encryption** of sensitive fields; use of **hardware accelerators** (Intel AES-NI). | | **Velocity** | Real-time streams (Kafka, Flink) cannot afford heavy cryptographic overhead. | **Lightweight ciphers** (ChaCha20), **homomorphic encryption** for in-place analytics. | | **Variety** | Heterogeneous data (images, logs, JSON) requires diverse security policies. | **Schema-aware policies** in Apache Ranger; **metadata-driven encryption**. | | **Veracity** | Untrusted data sources compromise integrity. | **Provenance tracking** (Apache Atlas), **blockchain-based audit trails**. | | **Value** | High-value data attracts advanced persistent threats (APTs). | **Zero-Trust Architecture**, **AI-based anomaly detection** (Apache Metron). | **Conclusion:** The CIA Triad must be implemented as a **dynamic, distributed, and policy-driven** system, not a static perimeter defense. *(7 marks)* --- **b) Explain with a diagram how Apache Hadoop enforces the CIA Triad during a typical read operation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Confidentiality (2 marks)** The client presents a **Kerberos ticket** to the NameNode. Without valid authentication, the read is rejected. TLS encrypts the data channel between client and DataNode. **Step 2: Integrity (2.5 marks)** When the client receives a block from a DataNode, it computes a fresh **SHA-256 hash** of the data and compares it with the stored checksum. If they mismatch, the block is discarded, and a replica is requested from another DataNode. **Step 3: Availability (2.5 marks)** The NameNode identifies the **closest live replica** (e.g., on the same rack) and serves the read. If the local DataNode fails, the NameNode transparently redirects to a secondary replica, ensuring uninterrupted access. **Diagram (expected in answer):** ```mermaid flowchart LR client["Client"] --> kerb["Kerberos Auth
Confidentiality"] kerb --> nn["NameNode
Block Location"] nn --> dn1["DataNode 1
Primary Replica"] nn --> dn2["DataNode 2
Secondary Replica"] dn1 --> chk["Checksum Verify
Integrity SHA-256"] dn2 --> chk chk --> client dn1 -.failover.-> dn2 ``` **Conclusion:** Hadoop's distributed design inherently satisfies all three CIA pillars through **Kerberos, checksums, and replication**. *(7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — CIA Triad in Big Data:** - ✅ **CIA** stands for **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** — the bedrock of Big Data security. - ✅ The **5 V's** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) act as *threat multipliers* against traditional CIA implementations. - ✅ **Confidentiality in Big Data** = HDFS Transparent Encryption, Kerberos, Apache Ranger, TLS/SSL. - ✅ **Integrity in Big Data** = SHA-256 block checksums, Merkle trees, digital signatures, idempotent operations. - ✅ **Availability in Big Data** = Replication Factor 3, NameNode HA, Erasure Coding, Disaster Recovery. - ✅ The availability formula is $A_{sys} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$. - ✅ For $N$ parallel replicas, $A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_{single})^{N}$ — this is the basis of HDFS "five nines" reliability. - ✅ **Parkerian Hexad** extends CIA to include **Authenticity, Possession, Utility** — relevant for KTU theory questions. - ✅ **HDFS default block size** = 128 MB; default **replication factor** = 3. - ✅ **Default encryption** in HDFS Transparent Encryption is **AES-256**. - ✅ Always map every CIA pillar to a **specific Big Data tool** in your exam answer to score full marks. - ✅ **Kerberos** is Hadoop's default authentication mechanism, not passwords or simple ACLs. - ✅ **NoSQL databases** (Cassandra, MongoDB) use **eventual consistency** for availability, trading strict integrity for performance. - ✅ **Ransomware attacks** primarily target the **Availability** pillar (denying access via encryption). - ✅ **Insider threats** primarily target the **Confidentiality** pillar (unauthorized data exfiltration).

topics.title: Big Data Architecture: Hadoop ecosystem: HDFS, Map Reduce, YARN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Big Data Architecture: Hadoop ecosystem: HDFS, Map Reduce, YARN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Big Data Architecture

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Big Data Architecture refers to the logical and physical layout of computational, storage, and networking resources designed to ingest, process, store, and analyze massive datasets that exceed the capabilities of traditional RDBMS — characterized by the **5 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Big Data is NOT a single technology; it is an **ecosystem** of distributed storage, distributed processing, and resource management frameworks working in coordination.

## 1.2 The Hadoop Ecosystem

**Definition:**
Hadoop is an open-source, Java-based **distributed computing framework** maintained by the Apache Software Foundation. It enables the storage and processing of petabytes of data across clusters of commodity hardware using simple programming models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Instead of moving *data* to computation (as in traditional systems), Hadoop moves *computation* to the data — exploiting data locality.

### Components of the Hadoop Ecosystem

| Layer | Component | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| Storage | **HDFS** | Distributed, fault-tolerant file system |
| Processing | **MapReduce** | Distributed batch processing model |
| Resource Mgmt | **YARN** | Cluster resource scheduling & job management |
| Higher-level | Hive, Pig, Spark | SQL-like / in-memory processing |
| Coordination | **ZooKeeper** | Distributed coordination service |
| Serialization | Avro, Parquet | Efficient data formats |
| Ingestion | Sqoop, Flume | Bulk data import |

---

## 1.3 HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System

**Definition:**
HDFS is a **distributed, scalable, portable, and fault-tolerant file system** written in Java, designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a Hadoop cluster by splitting them into **blocks** (default **128 MB** or **256 MB**).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Library System"

Imagine a **massive library** containing millions of books (your big data files). Instead of stuffing them into one giant building:
- Books are **split into chapters (blocks)** of fixed size.
- Each chapter is **photocopied multiple times (replication, default 3x)** and placed on different library branches.
- A **central librarian (NameNode)** maintains the index — *which chapter is in which branch*.
- **Branch workers (DataNodes)** physically hold the chapters.

If one branch burns down, copies still exist in other branches. The librarian is the only failure-prone point — hence **secondary support (Secondary NameNode)** keeps backup metadata.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Block Size:** 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+) and 256 MB (Hadoop 3.x+). Default **Replication Factor = 3**.

### HDFS Key Features
- **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** model
- Streaming data access (high throughput, not low latency)
- Handles hardware failure gracefully
- Built on commodity hardware (cheap, scalable)

---

## 1.4 MapReduce

**Definition:**
MapReduce is a **distributed parallel programming model** used by Hadoop to process massive datasets in two primary phases — **Map** (filter, transform, sort) and **Reduce** (aggregate, summarize) — separated by an implicit **Shuffle & Sort** phase.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Census Counting System"

Suppose the government wants to count citizens in every district of India:
- **Mappers (Phase 1):** Each regional officer counts people in their district and produces **(state, count)** pairs.
- **Shuffle & Sort (Automatic):** All pairs are grouped by key — all "Kerala" pairs together, all "Tamil Nadu" pairs together.
- **Reducers (Phase 2):** A state-wise officer **sums up** the counts from every district belonging to that state, producing a final tally.

The actual MapReduce code simply provides the `map()` and `reduce()` logic — Hadoop handles distribution, fault tolerance, and the shuffle phase automatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** MapReduce uses a **key-value pair (K, V)** data model throughout every stage of execution.

---

## 1.5 YARN — Yet Another Resource Negotiator

**Definition:**
YARN (introduced in **Hadoop 2.0**) is the **cluster resource management layer** of Hadoop, decoupling resource management from data processing logic. It allows multiple data processing engines (MapReduce, Spark, Tez) to share a single cluster.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel General Manager"

Before YARN, Hadoop 1.0's JobTracker was like a single overworked hotel manager handling **room allocation AND guest services**. If one big group booked, no other guests got rooms.

With YARN:
- **ResourceManager = Hotel General Manager:** Allocates rooms (containers) to guests (applications) based on availability.
- **NodeManager = Floor Supervisor:** Manages rooms on a specific floor.
- **ApplicationMaster = Group Tour Leader:** Manages one specific group's needs.
- **Container = Actual Hotel Room:** Holds the resources (CPU + RAM) where work runs.

> [!NOTE]
> **YARN enabled Hadoop to evolve from a "batch-only" system into a multi-purpose data platform.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS — Architecture Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Master-Slave Architecture

HDFS follows a **Master/Slave (NameNode/DataNode) topology**:

**1. NameNode (Master)**
- Stores **metadata** (file name, permissions, block locations, replication factor) in RAM.
- File contents are *NOT* stored here.
- Single point of failure in HDFS 1.x (mitigated in 2.x by High Availability).
- Maintains two files: **FsImage** (checkpoint) and **EditLog** (transaction log).

**2. DataNode (Slave)**
- Stores actual **data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** (default every 3 seconds) to NameNode.
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication on instruction from NameNode.

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)**
- **NOT a backup** of NameNode despite the name.
- Periodically merges **FsImage + EditLog** to prevent EditLog from growing indefinitely.
- Failure of NameNode still requires manual recovery.

### 2.1.2 Block-Splitting & Replication Strategy

When a file is uploaded:
- File is split into blocks of fixed size (default 128 MB / 256 MB).
- Each block is replicated (default 3 times) across different DataNodes.
- **Rack Awareness Algorithm:** Replicas are placed such that:
  - 1 replica on local rack
  - 1 replica on different rack (same node as 1st replica's neighbor)
  - 1 replica on a completely different rack

This guarantees that even a **full rack failure** does NOT cause data loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability vs Storage Trade-off:** Higher replication = higher fault tolerance but more storage cost. The replication factor is configurable per-file via `setReplication()`.

### 2.1.3 HDFS Read/Write Flow

**Write Operation Pipeline:**
1. Client contacts NameNode → receives list of DataNodes for block placement.
2. Client sends block to first DataNode.
3. First DataNode forwards to second; second forwards to third (pipeline).
4. Acknowledgments flow back; first DataNode sends ack to client.
5. On completion, metadata is updated at NameNode.

**Read Operation:**
1. Client requests file from NameNode.
2. NameNode returns block locations (sorted by proximity).
3. Client reads directly from the nearest DataNode.

---

## 2.2 MapReduce — Programmatic Model

### 2.2.1 Execution Phases

| Phase | Responsibility |
|-------|----------------|
| **Input Split** | Data divided into logical splits; each assigned to a Mapper |
| **Map** | User-defined `map(key_in, value_in)` emits intermediate (k, v) pairs |
| **Combine** *(Optional)* | Local aggregation per Mapper to reduce shuffle traffic |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Framework groups all values by key, transfers to Reducers |
| **Reduce** | User-defined `reduce(key, value_list)` emits final (k, v) pairs |
| **Output** | Final results written back to HDFS |

### 2.2.2 Word Count Example (Conceptual)

**Input:** `"big data is big and data is huge"`

**Mapper Output (intermediate):**
$$(\text{big}, 1), (\text{data}, 1), (\text{is}, 1), (\text{big}, 1), (\text{and}, 1), (\text{data}, 1), (\text{is}, 1), (\text{huge}, 1)$$

**After Shuffle & Sort (grouped by key):**
$$(\text{big}, [1,1]), (\text{data}, [1,1]), (\text{is}, [1,1]), (\text{and}, [1]), (\text{huge}, [1])$$

**Reducer Output (final):**
$$(\text{big}, 2), (\text{data}, 2), (\text{is}, 2), (\text{and}, 1), (\text{huge}, 1)$$

### 2.2.3 Data Locality Optimization

Hadoop assigns tasks based on where data resides:
- **Node-local** (preferred): Task runs on the DataNode holding the block.
- **Rack-local**: Task runs on a node in the same rack.
- **Off-rack** (worst): Task runs on a node in a different rack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Locality Principle:** Moving computation to data is 1000× cheaper than moving data to computation over a network.

---

## 2.3 YARN — Deep Architecture

### 2.3.1 Core Components

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global authority; has Scheduler + ApplicationsManager |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent; manages containers, monitors resource usage, sends heartbeats |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs to execute tasks |
| **Container** | Abstract resource unit (CPU cores + RAM in MB) where actual tasks run |

### 2.3.2 YARN Job Submission Flow

1. Client submits application to **ResourceManager**.
2. RM allocates a **container** and launches the **ApplicationMaster**.
3. AM registers itself with RM.
4. AM requests containers from RM (via resource requests).
5. RM grants containers; AM instructs **NodeManagers** to launch tasks.
6. Tasks report progress to AM.
7. On completion, AM deregisters and resources are released.

### 2.3.3 Schedulers in YARN

- **FIFO Scheduler:** First-In-First-Out (simple, but not fair).
- **Capacity Scheduler:** Guarantees capacity for queues; used in production (default in Apache Hadoop).
- **Fair Scheduler:** Allocates resources fairly across all jobs.

### 2.3.4 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units / Notes |
|------------------|----------|----------------|
| **HDFS Block Count** | $N_b = \lceil \frac{F_s}{B_s} \rceil$ | $F_s$ = file size, $B_s$ = block size |
| **Total Replicas** | $R_t = N_b \times R_f$ | $R_f$ = replication factor |
| **Storage Overhead** | $O = (R_f - 1) \times 100\%$ | Extra % of logical storage used |
| **Map Tasks** | $M = \frac{D}{S_{split}}$ | $D$ = data size, $S_{split}$ = input split size |
| **Max Reducers (heuristic)** | $R = 0.95 \times N \times C$ | $N$ = nodes, $C$ = containers per node |
| **Container RAM** | $M_{container} \approx \frac{Cluster_{RAM}}{C_{total}}$ | MB allocated per container |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The storage overhead formula is critical in KTU numerical problems. With $R_f = 3$, every file consumes $3\times$ its logical size, so $200\%$ overhead — a key cost factor in cloud Big Data deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 HDFS Block Count — Derivation

**Problem:** A file of size **$F_s = 1450$ MB** is uploaded to HDFS with a default block size $B_s = 128$ MB and replication factor $R_f = 3$. Calculate:
1. Number of blocks
2. Total physical storage used
3. Storage overhead percentage

### Step 1 — Number of Blocks

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{F_s}{B_s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1450}{128} \right\rceil$$

**Conversion Logic:** The ceiling function ensures that even a partial final block counts as a complete block — partial bytes cannot be stored in a half-block in HDFS.

$$\frac{1450}{128} = 11.328$$

**Logic:** Since 11 blocks would only cover $11 \times 128 = 1408$ MB (insufficient for the last 42 MB), we need a 12th block.

$$N_b = 12 \text{ blocks}$$

### Step 2 — Total Physical Storage

Each block is replicated $R_f = 3$ times:

$$S_{total} = N_b \times B_s \times R_f$$

$$S_{total} = 12 \times 128 \times 3 = 4608 \text{ MB}$$

### Step 3 — Storage Overhead

$$O = \frac{S_{total} - F_s}{F_s} \times 100\%$$

$$O = \frac{4608 - 1450}{1450} \times 100\% = \frac{3158}{1450} \times 100\%$$

$$O \approx 217.79\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every 1 MB of logical data, the system consumes ~3.18 MB of physical storage. This is the **storage cost of fault tolerance**.

---

## 3.2 MapReduce Task Calculation

**Problem:** A cluster has $N = 10$ nodes. Each node runs $C = 5$ containers. Each container executes 1 Reducer. The cluster has $D = 2$ TB of input data, and the HDFS block size is $B_s = 128$ MB.

### Step 1 — Map Tasks
The number of Map tasks equals the number of input splits (typically aligned to HDFS blocks):

$$M = \frac{D}{B_s} = \frac{2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}}$$

$$M = \frac{2097152}{128} = 16384 \text{ map tasks}$$

### Step 2 — Max Reducers
Using the YARN heuristic:

$$R = 0.95 \times N \times C = 0.95 \times 10 \times 5 = 47.5$$

$$R_{max} = 47 \text{ reducers (floor to integer)}$$

### Step 3 — Per-Reducer Data Volume

$$V_{per\,reducer} = \frac{D}{R} = \frac{2 \text{ TB}}{47} \approx 43.5 \text{ GB/reducer}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the per-reducer data volume exceeds 1–2 GB, performance degrades sharply due to memory pressure. Solution: increase $R$ (add nodes) or reduce input data via filtering.

---

## 3.3 YARN Resource Allocation Derivation

**Problem:** A cluster has 10 nodes, each with 64 GB RAM and 16 cores. YARN reserves 8 GB RAM and 2 cores per node for system services.

### Step 1 — Available Resources per Node

$$RAM_{avail} = 64 - 8 = 56 \text{ GB per node}$$

$$Cores_{avail} = 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ cores per node}$$

### Step 2 — Container Size (assuming 4 GB per container)

$$C_{total\,per\,node} = \frac{RAM_{avail}}{Container_{RAM}} = \frac{56}{4} = 14 \text{ containers}$$

This matches available cores, so 4 GB containers are feasible.

### Step 3 — Total Cluster Capacity

$$C_{cluster} = N \times C_{per\,node} = 10 \times 14 = 140 \text{ containers}$$

$$RAM_{cluster} = 10 \times 56 = 560 \text{ GB usable RAM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world tuning:** In production, `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` and `yarn.nodemanager.resource.cpu-vcores` are set in `yarn-site.xml` to lock in these values.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Hadoop 1.0 | Hadoop 2.x with YARN |
|---------|------------|----------------------|
| Resource Mgmt | JobTracker | ResourceManager + NodeManager |
| Per-Job Master | TaskTracker (limited) | ApplicationMaster (per app) |
| Processing Models | MapReduce ONLY | MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm |
| Scalability | ~4,000 nodes | 10,000+ nodes |
| Cluster Utilization | Poor (static slots) | Dynamic containers |
| Multi-tenancy | No | Yes |
| Single Point of Failure | Yes (JobTracker) | Reduced (RM HA optional) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Hadoop Ecosystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application]
    subgraph "Hadoop Cluster"
        direction TB
        subgraph "YARN Layer"
            rm[ResourceManager]
            nm1[NodeManager 1]
            nm2[NodeManager 2]
            am1[ApplicationMaster - MapReduce Job]
            am2[ApplicationMaster - Spark App]
        end
        subgraph "HDFS Layer"
            nn[NameNode]
            snn[Secondary NameNode]
            dn1[DataNode 1]
            dn2[DataNode 2]
            dn3[DataNode 3]
        end
        subgraph "Processing Frameworks"
            mr[MapReduce Engine]
            sp[Spark Engine]
            hve[Hive Engine]
        end
    end
    client --> rm
    client --> nn
    rm --> am1
    rm --> am2
    am1 --> nm1
    am1 --> nm2
    am2 --> nm2
    nn --> dn1
    nn --> dn2
    nn --> dn3
    nn -. checkpoint .-> snn
    nm1 --> dn1
    nm2 --> dn2
    am1 --> mr
    am2 --> sp
    nn --> hve
```

## 4.2 HDFS Block Replication — Rack-Aware Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client[Client Uploading File]
    subgraph "Rack 1"
        dn1[DataNode A - 1st Replica]
        dn2[DataNode B - 2nd Replica]
    end
    subgraph "Rack 2"
        dn3[DataNode C - 3rd Replica]
        dn4[DataNode D]
    end
    nn[NameNode - Rack Aware Scheduler]
    client -- write request --> nn
    nn -- block 1 location list --> client
    client -- pipeline write --> dn1
    dn1 -- forward --> dn2
    dn1 -- forward --> dn3
    dn1 -- ack --> client
    dn2 -- ack --> dn1
    dn3 -- ack --> dn2
```

## 4.3 MapReduce Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input Splits in HDFS]
    subgraph "Map Phase"
        m1[Mapper 1]
        m2[Mapper 2]
        m3[Mapper 3]
    end
    subgraph "Shuffle and Sort"
        part[Partitioner + Grouping by Key]
    end
    subgraph "Reduce Phase"
        r1[Reducer 1]
        r2[Reducer 2]
    end
    output[Output to HDFS]
    input --> m1
    input --> m2
    input --> m3
    m1 -- k,v pairs --> part
    m2 -- k,v pairs --> part
    m3 -- k,v pairs --> part
    part -- grouped k,v list --> r1
    part -- grouped k,v list --> r2
    r1 --> output
    r2 --> output
```

## 4.4 YARN Resource Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant RM as ResourceManager
    participant NM as NodeManager
    participant AM as ApplicationMaster
    C->>RM: 1. submitApplication
    RM->>NM: 2. allocateContainer for AM
    NM->>AM: 3. launch ApplicationMaster
    AM->>RM: 4. registerApplicationMaster
    loop Resource Negotiation
        AM->>RM: 5. request containers
        RM-->>AM: 6. allocate containers
        AM->>NM: 7. launch task in container
        NM-->>AM: 8. task status / progress
    end
    AM->>RM: 9. finishApplicationMaster
    RM->>C: 10. application completed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The YARN sequence diagram is one of the most frequently asked KTU 2-mark and 7-mark diagrams. Memorize the **10-step flow** in sequence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is HDFS? List any two advantages of HDFS over traditional file systems.**

**Model Answer:**
HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed, fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across clusters of commodity hardware by splitting them into fixed-size blocks (default 128 MB / 256 MB) with default replication factor 3.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Fault Tolerance:** Data is automatically replicated across multiple DataNodes and racks, ensuring availability even under hardware failure.
2. **Scalability:** Easily scales horizontally by adding more nodes — petabytes of data can be stored without architectural changes.
3. **High Throughput:** Optimized for batch/streaming reads (not low-latency), giving excellent bandwidth for large file processing.

*(Any two well-explained advantages = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Map and Reduce functions in MapReduce.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Map Function | Reduce Function |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Purpose** | Filter, transform, parse input | Aggregate, summarize grouped data |
| **Input** | (key1, value1) | (key2, list of values2) |
| **Output** | (key2, value2) intermediate | (key3, value3) final |
| **Execution** | Runs in parallel on Mappers | Runs after Shuffle & Sort |
| **Number** | One per input split | Few (configurable) |

> Map performs **per-record processing**; Reduce performs **per-key aggregation**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A file of size **2.4 GB** is uploaded to HDFS with block size **128 MB** and replication factor **3**.
(i) Calculate the number of blocks.
(ii) Calculate the total physical storage consumed.
(iii) Calculate the storage overhead percentage.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — HDFS Architecture (7 marks)

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture** consisting of three core daemons.

**1. NameNode (Master):**
- Stores all **metadata** in RAM: filename, directory structure, block-to-DataNode mapping, permissions, replication factor.
- Files are *not* stored here — only metadata.
- Receives **heartbeat** signals (every 3 sec) from every DataNode.
- Maintains **FsImage** (snapshot) and **EditLog** (incremental changes).
- Acts as the single point of contact for all client requests.

**2. DataNode (Slave):**
- Stores **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends heartbeats to NameNode.
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication on NameNode's instruction.
- A cluster typically has hundreds/thousands of DataNodes.

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):**
- NOT a hot backup despite its name.
- Periodically downloads FsImage and EditLog from NameNode.
- Merges them into a new FsImage and sends back to NameNode.
- Prevents the EditLog from becoming too large and memory-bound.
- Reduces NameNode startup time after a crash.

> **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 HDFS Layer (3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation).

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Number of blocks:**

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{2400 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 18.75 \right\rceil = 19 \text{ blocks}$$

**(ii) Total physical storage:**

$$S_{total} = 19 \times 128 \times 3 = 7296 \text{ MB} \approx 7.13 \text{ GB}$$

**(iii) Storage overhead:**

$$O = \frac{7296 - 2400}{2400} \times 100\% = \frac{4896}{2400} \times 100\% = 204\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget the ceiling function** and answer 18 blocks. This loses 2 marks. Always check if a partial final block exists!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **YARN architecture**. List the responsibilities of ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **phases of MapReduce execution** with a Word Count example. Show intermediate (K, V) pairs for the input: *"hadoop is big data and big data is hadoop"*.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — YARN Architecture (7 marks)

YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator), introduced in **Hadoop 2.0**, decouples resource management from data processing. It enables multi-tenant, multi-framework clusters.

**Core Components:**

**1. ResourceManager (RM):** *(2 marks)*
- Global master daemon running on the master node.
- Two sub-components:
  - **Scheduler:** Allocates resources (containers) to applications based on policy (FIFO / Capacity / Fair).
  - **ApplicationsManager (AsM):** Accepts job submissions and launches ApplicationMasters.

**2. NodeManager (NM):** *(2 marks)*
- Per-slave-node agent.
- Manages **containers** (abstract CPU+RAM bundles).
- Monitors resource usage (CPU, memory, disk, network).
- Sends heartbeats to RM (default 1 sec).

**3. ApplicationMaster (AM):** *(2 marks)*
- **One per application** — not a global daemon.
- Negotiates resources with RM.
- Works with NMs to execute and monitor tasks.
- Reports job status back to RM.

**4. Container:**
- Resource allocation unit: bundle of **CPU cores + RAM (in MB)**.
- Runs actual task processes (Map/Reduce/Container).

> **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 YARN sequence diagram (1 mark for diagram).

#### Part (b) — MapReduce Phases + Word Count (7 marks)

**Phases of MapReduce Execution** *(3 marks)*:

1. **InputSplit** — Input file divided into logical splits.
2. **Map** — User-defined `map()` produces intermediate (K, V) pairs.
3. **Combine** (optional) — Local pre-aggregation per Mapper.
4. **Shuffle & Sort** — Framework groups values by key, transfers to Reducers.
5. **Reduce** — User-defined `reduce()` aggregates values per key.
6. **Output** — Final results written back to HDFS.

**Word Count Example** *(4 marks)* — Input: *"hadoop is big data and big data is hadoop"*

**Step 1 — Mapper Output (intermediate K,V):**
$$(\text{hadoop}, 1), (\text{is}, 1), (\text{big}, 1), (\text{data}, 1), (\text{and}, 1), (\text{big}, 1), (\text{data}, 1), (\text{is}, 1), (\text{hadoop}, 1)$$

**Step 2 — After Shuffle & Sort (grouped by key):**
$$(\text{hadoop}, [1, 1]), (\text{is}, [1, 1]), (\text{big}, [1, 1]), (\text{data}, [1, 1]), (\text{and}, [1])$$

**Step 3 — Reducer Output (final):**
$$(\text{hadoop}, 2), (\text{is}, 2), (\text{big}, 2), (\text{data}, 2), (\text{and}, 1)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are deducted if students (1) **miss showing the Shuffle & Sort intermediate step**, (2) **forget parentheses around (K, V) pairs**, or (3) **confuse the order of phases**. Always show all three steps explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ HDFS splits files into **blocks of 128 MB / 256 MB** with default **replication factor = 3**.
- ✅ **NameNode** stores metadata only; **DataNodes** store actual block data.
- ✅ **Secondary NameNode** is NOT a backup — it performs checkpointing of FsImage + EditLog.
- ✅ HDFS follows a **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** model.
- ✅ **Rack Awareness** algorithm places replicas across at least 2 different racks.
- ✅ **MapReduce** uses a **(Key, Value)** data model throughout all phases.
- ✅ MapReduce has **5 execution phases**: InputSplit → Map → (Combine) → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output.
- ✅ **YARN** decouples resource management from data processing — enabling multi-framework clusters.
- ✅ YARN's core daemons: **ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container**.
- ✅ The **ApplicationMaster is per-application**, not a global daemon — a common viva question.
- ✅ **Hadoop 1.0** had JobTracker/TaskTracker; **Hadoop 2.0+** uses YARN.
- ✅ **Data Locality** = moving computation to data, not data to computation.
- ✅ Default **heartbeat interval** of DataNode = 3 sec; NodeManager = 1 sec.
- ✅ **Storage Overhead Formula:** $O = (R_f - 1) \times 100\%$ for full-sized files.
- ✅ **Block Count Formula:** $N_b = \lceil F_s / B_s \rceil$ — always use ceiling, never floor.
- ✅ YARN enabled Hadoop to support **Spark, Tez, Storm, Flink** beyond MapReduce.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects **diagrams** in every 7-mark architecture question — practice drawing HDFS, YARN, and MapReduce flowcharts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apache Spark: Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs) and Data Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Apache Spark: Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs) and Data Frames # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Apache Spark — A Foundational Recap **Apache Spark** is an open-source, distributed, in-memory data processing engine designed for speed, ease of use, and sophisticated analytics. It provides high-level APIs in **Java, Scala, Python (PySpark), and R**, and an optimized engine that supports **general execution graphs (DAGs)**. Spark was developed at the **AMPLab, University of California, Berkeley**, and is now maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation**. > [!NOTE] > Spark is **not a data store** — it is a **computation engine** that can read from HDFS, S3, Cassandra, HBase, Kafka, or any other storage. ## 1.2 Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs) — The Core Abstraction **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** is the **fundamental data structure of Apache Spark** — an immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel across a cluster. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):** > An RDD is a **read-only, partitioned collection of records** that supports **two types of operations**: **transformations** (lazy, return a new RDD) and **actions** (trigger execution, return a value to the driver or write to storage). ### Breaking Down the Acronym "RDD" | Letter | Property | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **R** | Resilient | Fault-tolerant via **lineage graph** (can recompute lost partitions) | | **D** | Distributed | Data is partitioned across nodes in a cluster | | **D** | Dataset | Initial dataset can be created from a file, database, or collection | ## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — RDD as a "Distributed Spreadsheet" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a giant Excel spreadsheet that is **too large for one computer's RAM**. Spark splits the spreadsheet into **chunks (partitions)** and stores each chunk on a different machine. The whole thing is called an **RDD**. If one machine crashes and loses its chunk, Spark can **rebuild it from the original recipe (lineage)** — that's the *Resilient* part. **Key Properties of RDDs (The "5 Pillars"):** 1. **Partitions** — Dataset divided into logical chunks. 2. **Dependencies** — Lineage tracking the parent RDD. 3. **Compute Function** — The transformation logic. 4. **Partitioner (Optional)** — How the data is partitioned (e.g., `HashPartitioner`). 5. **Preferred Locations (Optional)** — HDFS block locations for data locality. ## 1.4 DataFrames — The Higher-Level Abstraction **DataFrame** is a **distributed collection of data organized into named columns** (conceptually equivalent to a **relational table** or a **Pandas DataFrame** with distribution). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):** > A DataFrame is a **Dataset organized into named columns**, with a **schema** known to the Spark SQL engine, which enables **Catalyst optimizer**-based query optimization and **Tungsten**-based binary execution. ### RDD vs. DataFrame at a Glance | Feature | RDD | DataFrame | |---|---|---| | Data Structure | Untyped, distributed collection | Typed, named columns + schema | | Optimization | No automatic optimization | **Catalyst Optimizer** + **Tungsten** | | API Style | Functional (map, reduce, filter) | SQL + DSL (`df.select()`) | | Performance | Slower (no optimization) | **10x–100x faster** (per Spark docs) | | Compile-time Safety | Yes (Scala/Java) | Limited | | Best For | Unstructured, low-level | Structured, analytical | ## 1.5 Why RDDs and DataFrames Matter in Big Data Security In the context of **PECCT743 – Big Data Security**, understanding RDDs and DataFrames is essential because: - **Security analytics** (anomaly detection, log mining) runs on Spark. - **Cryptographic operations** are often distributed via RDD transformations. - **Access control, audit logging**, and **data masking** rely on Spark's lineage and partition model. - **Encryption-at-rest** policies (e.g., HDFS Transparent Encryption) interact with how Spark partitions read encrypted blocks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an RDD ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ RDD Object │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ partitions : List[Partition] │ │ dependencies : List[Dependency[_]] │ │ compute : (Partition => Iterator[T]) │ │ partitioner : Option[Partitioner] │ │ preferredLocs : Map[Node, List[String]] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.1.1 The Five Internal Components (Paper-Level Detail) 1. **Partitions** — Atomic chunks of dataset. Each partition is processed by one **task**. 2. **Dependencies** — Defines the **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**. Two types: - **Narrow dependency** — Each parent partition contributes to at most ONE child partition (e.g., `map`, `filter`). **No shuffle.** - **Wide dependency** — Each parent partition contributes to MULTIPLE child partitions (e.g., `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`). **Triggers shuffle.** 3. **Compute Function** — Iterator-based function to compute a partition given its parent. 4. **Partitioner** — Custom partitioning logic (e.g., `RangePartitioner`, `HashPartitioner`). 5. **Preferred Locations** — HDFS block locations → **data locality optimization**. ## 2.2 RDD Operations — Transformations vs. Actions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lazy Evaluation:** Spark does NOT execute transformations when called. It builds a **DAG** and only materializes when an **action** is triggered. ### Transformation Catalogue (Lazy — Returns a new RDD) | Transformation | Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `map(func)` | Narrow | Apply `func` to each element | | `filter(func)` | Narrow | Keep elements where `func` returns true | | `flatMap(func)` | Narrow | Map then flatten | | `mapPartitions(func)` | Narrow | Apply to whole partition | | `union(rdd)` | Narrow | Combine two RDDs | | `distinct()` | Wide | Remove duplicates | | `groupByKey()` | Wide | Group values by key | | `reduceByKey(func)` | Wide (with combiner) | Aggregate by key | | `join(rdd)` | Wide | Inner join on keys | | `repartition(n)` | Wide | Change number of partitions | ### Action Catalogue (Eager — Triggers execution) | Action | Result | |---|---| | `collect()` | Returns all elements as array | | `count()` | Number of elements | | `first()` | First element | | `take(n)` | First `n` elements | | `reduce(func)` | Aggregate all elements | | `saveAsTextFile(path)` | Write to storage | | `foreach(func)` | Apply function (no return) | ## 2.3 Lazy Evaluation & DAG Construction — Step Logic **Step 1:** User calls `lines = sc.textFile("hdfs://...")` → RDD created (no execution). **Step 2:** `errors = lines.filter(lambda x: "error" in x)` → Another RDD (DAG node added). **Step 3:** `counts = errors.map(lambda x: (x, 1)).reduceByKey(lambda a,b: a+b)` → RDD chain. **Step 4:** `counts.collect()` → **Action triggered**. Spark now: - Builds the **logical plan (DAG)**. - Splits it into **stages** (boundaries at wide dependencies / shuffles). - Submits **tasks** to executors. > [!TIP] > **Why Lazy?** It allows Spark to **optimize the entire pipeline** (e.g., predicate pushdown, pipelining) before execution — a key reason Spark is fast. ## 2.4 DataFrames — Deep Architecture ### 2.4.1 Catalyst Optimizer Pipeline ``` User Query (SQL / DSL) ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ 1. Unresolved │ ← Parses query, builds AST │ Logical Plan │ └──────────────────────┘ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ 2. Analyzer │ ← Resolves tables/columns via Catalog └──────────────────────┘ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ 3. Optimizer │ ← Rule-based + Cost-based optimization │ (Catalyst) │ (predicate pushdown, constant folding) └──────────────────────┘ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ 4. Physical Plan │ ← Selects execution strategy └──────────────────────┘ ↓ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ 5. Code Generation │ ← Tungsten: Whole-stage codegen │ (Tungsten) │ (Java bytecode, off-heap memory) └──────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.4.2 Tungsten Execution Engine **Tungsten** is Spark's execution backend that focuses on: - **Memory management** — Off-heap memory, eliminating JVM GC overhead. - **Cache locality** — Explicit cache layout for CPU efficiency. - **Code generation** — Compiles query plans to **Java bytecode** at runtime. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet > [!NOTE] > Spark does not have many "formulas" like Physics/Math, but there are key **metrics and rules** every KTU student must memorize. | Metric / Rule | Formula / Definition | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Stage Count Rule** | $\text{Stages} = \text{Number of wide dependencies} + 1$ | stages | | **Task Count Rule** | $\text{Tasks} = \sum_{i=1}^{S} P_i$ where $P_i$ = partitions in stage $i$ | tasks | | **RDD Size Estimate** | $\text{Memory} \approx \text{Partitions} \times \text{Avg. partition size} \times 2 \text{ (deserialization)}$ | bytes | | **Shuffle Block Threshold** | Default $\text{spark.sql.shuffle.partitions} = 200$ | partitions | | **Data Locality Levels** | $PROCESS\_LOCAL \to NODE\_LOCAL \to RACK\_LOCAL \to ANY$ | priority order | | **Lineage Cost** | $\text{Recompute cost} = \sum \text{parent RDD compute time}$ | ms | | **Wide Dependency Indicator** | Triggers shuffle when output partition reads from $>1$ input partition | boolean | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` in tables (this sheet obeys the KTU engine rule). The table above uses textual descriptors to avoid LaTeX-pipe collisions. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of RDDs and DataFrames | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Cybersecurity Log Analysis** | Aggregate millions of log lines via `reduceByKey` | | **Anomaly Detection** | MLlib on DataFrames for intrusion detection | | **Cryptographic Workloads** | Distributed AES/RSA key generation via RDD `map` | | **Compliance & Auditing** | SQL queries on DataFrames for GDPR/HIPAA reporting | | **Forensics** | Lineage graph reconstructs data flow for chain-of-custody | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stage and Task Count from an RDD DAG **Problem Statement:** Given an RDD lineage of operations, derive the number of stages and tasks Spark will launch. ### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough **Given lineage:** $$\text{input} \xrightarrow{\text{map}} R_1 \xrightarrow{\text{filter}} R_2 \xrightarrow{\text{groupByKey}} R_3 \xrightarrow{\text{map}} R_4 \xrightarrow{\text{collect()}} \text{Result}$$ **Step 1 — Identify dependency type per edge.** - `input → R_1` (map): **Narrow** ✓ - `R_1 → R_2` (filter): **Narrow** ✓ - `R_2 → R_3` (groupByKey): **Wide** ✗ (shuffle boundary) - `R_3 → R_4` (map): **Narrow** ✓ - `R_4 → collect()` (action): **Trigger** **Step 2 — Count wide dependencies.** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \text{Number of wide dependencies} \\ &= 1 \quad \text{(groupByKey)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute stage count.** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= W + 1 \\ &= 1 + 1 \\ &= 2 \text{ stages} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** Stage 1 = `map → filter`. Stage 2 = `groupByKey → map`. **Step 4 — Compute task count per stage.** Let input partitions $P = 4$, no coalescing/repartitioning. $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= P_1 = 4 \text{ tasks (Stage 1)} \\ T_2 &= P_2 = \text{Output partitions of groupByKey} \\ &= \text{spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default } 200 \text{)} \\ &= 200 \text{ tasks (Stage 2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Total task count.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= T_1 + T_2 \\ &= 4 + 200 \\ &= 204 \text{ tasks} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Each task runs on a single executor slot. If cluster has 10 cores, Spark runs **204 tasks in 10 parallel waves of ~20.4** — actual scheduling is dynamic. ## 3.2 Derivation: DataFrame Catalyst Optimization — Predicate Pushdown **Problem Statement:** Demonstrate how Catalyst pushes a filter BEFORE a costly join. ### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough **Original (Unoptimized) Logical Plan:** ```sql SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id WHERE u.country = 'IN'; ``` **Step 1 — Build Unresolved AST** (Abstract Syntax Tree). Catalyst parses the SQL into a tree of logical operators: - `Project(name, total)` - `Filter(country = 'IN')` - `Join(users, orders, id = user_id)` - `Scan(users)`, `Scan(orders)` **Step 2 — Analyzer resolves symbols.** Column references like `u.country` get bound to actual schema fields. Type checking happens here. **Step 3 — Optimizer applies rule: Predicate Pushdown.** Original tree (top-down): $$ \begin{aligned} \pi_{name, total}\big( \sigma_{country='IN'}(users \bowtie_{id} orders) \big) \end{aligned} $$ Optimized tree (filter moved DOWN, below join): $$ \begin{aligned} \pi_{name, total}\big( \sigma_{country='IN'}(users) \bowtie_{id} orders \big) \end{aligned} $$ **Why this matters:** Suppose `users` has **10 million rows** but only **100,000 are from 'IN'**. Pushing the filter before the join reduces the join's input size by **99%**, drastically reducing shuffle and join cost. **Step 4 — Cost-Based Optimization (CBO).** Catalyst estimates cardinalities: - `|users| = 10^7`, `|orders| = 10^8` - After filter: `|σ(users)| ≈ 10^5` - Chooses **BroadcastHashJoin** (broadcast `users` to all executors) over **SortMergeJoin** if `users` is small. **Step 5 — Code Generation (Tungsten).** The optimized plan is compiled to **Java bytecode** via Janino.
