The generated code: - Uses **primitive arrays** instead of objects. - Avoids virtual function calls. - Operates on **off-heap memory**. > [!TIP] > **Performance gain:** Catalyst + Tungsten together provide **5x–10x speedup** over the Volcano-style row-at-a-time iterator model (e.g., traditional DBMS). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RDD vs. DataFrame vs. Dataset (Spark 2.0+) | Property | RDD | DataFrame | Dataset | |---|---|---|---| | **Type Safety** | Compile-time (Scala/Java) | Runtime (schema-on-read) | Compile-time | | **Structured** | No | Yes (schema) | Yes (schema) | | **Unstructured** | Yes | No (forces schema) | Limited | | **Optimization** | None | Catalyst + Tungsten | Catalyst + Tungsten | | **API Style** | Functional | SQL + DSL | Type-safe DSL | | **Serialization** | Java/Kryo | Tungsten binary | Tungsten binary (Encoder) | | **Best For** | Low-level, raw data | SQL analytics | Type-safe + structured | ## 3.4 KTU Examiner's Validation Steps — The "Show Your Work" Pattern When asked to explain an RDD operation, KTU examiners expect this **4-step structure**: 1. **State the operation** (e.g., `reduceByKey`). 2. **Classify** — Narrow or Wide dependency. 3. **Justify** — Why it is wide/narrow (input/output partition mapping). 4. **Consequence** — Shuffle cost, network I/O, stage boundary. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 RDD Lineage DAG — Narrow vs. Wide Dependencies ```mermaid graph TD A[Input HDFS File
Partition P1 P2 P3] -->|map Narrow| B[RDD1 P1 P2 P3] A -->|map Narrow| B B -->|filter Narrow| C[RDD2 P1 P2 P3] C -->|groupByKey Wide SHUFFLE| D[RDD3 New partitions from shuffle] D -->|map Narrow| E[RDD4 Final output] E -->|collect Action| F[Driver Result] ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice the **single shuffle boundary** after `groupByKey`. This creates **2 stages** in Spark's execution. The dashed/convergent arrows at `groupByKey` represent the **shuffle** — data is repartitioned across the network. ## 4.2 Spark Application Architecture (Driver–Executor Model) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph DRIVER[Driver Program JVM] SP[SparkContext] DAG[DAGScheduler] TS[TaskScheduler] BL[BlockManager] end subgraph CLUSTER[Cluster Manager - YARN Standalone Mesos] CM[Resource Negotiator] end subgraph EXEC1[Executor 1] T1A[Task 1A] T1B[Task 1B] C1[Cache Block 1] end subgraph EXEC2[Executor 2] T2A[Task 2A] T2B[Task 2B] C2[Cache Block 2] end subgraph EXEC3[Executor 3] T3A[Task 3A] T3B[Task 3B] C3[Cache Block 3] end SP --> CM CM --> EXEC1 CM --> EXEC2 CM --> EXEC3 DAG --> TS TS --> T1A TS --> T2A TS --> T3A TS --> T1B TS --> T2B TS --> T3B EXEC1 <-->|shuffle read write| EXEC2 EXEC2 <-->|shuffle read write| EXEC3 ``` ## 4.3 DataFrame Catalyst Optimization Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR A[User SQL Query OR DSL] --> B[Unresolved Logical Plan] B --> C[Analyzer Catalog Lookup] C --> D[Optimized Logical Plan] D --> E[Physical Plan Selection] E --> F[Tungsten Code Generation] F --> G[Java Bytecode Execution] D -.->|Rule Predicate Pushdown| D D -.->|Rule Constant Folding| D E -.->|Cost Based Join Selection| E ``` ## 4.4 RDD Fault Tolerance — Lineage Recovery Flow ```mermaid graph TB A[Original RDD1] -->|map| B[RDD2] B -->|filter| C[RDD3] C -->|groupByKey| D[RDD4 Partition X Lost on Executor 2] D -->|Task Failed| E[TaskScheduler Re-request] E --> F[Lineage: Replay RDD1 to RDD3] F --> G[Recompute Partition X] G --> H[Resume Pipeline] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **lineage** (recipe of transformations) is what makes RDDs "Resilient". No replication of intermediate data is needed — Spark can always **recompute** from the source. ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RDD Internals ```mermaid graph TB subgraph RDDOBJ[RDD Object] P[Partitions List] DEP[Dependencies List] COMP[Compute Function] PARTOPT[Partitioner Optional] PREFOPT[Preferred Locations Optional] end subgraph EXECFLOW[Execution Flow] SC[SparkContext.textFile] LAZY[Lazy DAG Construction] ACT[Action Trigger] STAGE[Stage Split at Wide Dep] TASK[Task Per Partition] end SC --> RDDOBJ RDDOBJ --> LAZY LAZY --> ACT ACT --> STAGE STAGE --> TASK ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define RDD. List and explain any THREE properties of RDD. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > An **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** is an immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel across a cluster. It is the fundamental data structure of Apache Spark. **Three Properties:** 1. **Partitions** — The dataset is divided into logical partitions, each processed by a single task. The number of partitions determines parallelism. 2. **Dependencies (Lineage)** — Each RDD tracks its parent RDDs and the operation that created it. This forms a **DAG** that enables fault recovery by recomputation. 3. **Lazy Evaluation** — Transformations are not executed immediately. Spark builds a logical plan and only triggers execution when an **action** is called, enabling pipeline optimization. *(Acceptable additional properties: Immutability, Coarse-grained operations, Data locality.)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between transformations and actions in Spark with TWO examples each. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Transformations | Actions | |---|---|---| | **Execution** | Lazy — return a new RDD | Eager — return a value or write to storage | | **Trigger DAG** | No (only adds to DAG) | Yes (triggers job execution) | | **Return Type** | RDD | Scala value, array, or unit | **Examples of Transformations:** `map()`, `filter()`, `flatMap()`, `groupByKey()`, `reduceByKey()`, `join()`. **Examples of Actions:** `collect()`, `count()`, `first()`, `take(n)`, `reduce(func)`, `saveAsTextFile(path)`. > *Example walkthrough: `lines.filter(...)` is a transformation — it does not read the file. `lines.count()` is an action — it triggers reading and counting.* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Apache Spark. With a neat diagram, describe the role of Driver, Cluster Manager, and Executors. **(b) [7 Marks]** What is RDD Lineage? Explain narrow and wide dependencies with an example. Why is lineage important for fault tolerance? --- ### Question A — Model Solutions #### (a) Spark Architecture — Model Solution [7 Marks] **Introduction (1 mark):** Apache Spark follows a **master–slave (driver–executor) architecture**. The user's `main()` method launches the Driver process. **Components (5 marks):** 1. **Driver Program** — The process running the `main()` function. It contains: - **`SparkContext`** — The entry point; coordinates with the cluster manager. - **`DAGScheduler`** — Converts RDD lineage into a DAG of stages. - **`TaskScheduler`** — Sends tasks to executors. - **`BlockManager`** — Manages data storage across the cluster. 2. **Cluster Manager** — Allocates resources. Examples: **Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes**. It launches executors on worker nodes. 3. **Executors** — Worker processes that: - Run **tasks** and return results. - Store **cached RDD partitions** in memory or disk. - Provide **in-memory storage** for iterative algorithms. 4. **SparkSession** (Spark 2.0+) — Unified entry point combining `SparkContext`, `SQLContext`, `HiveContext`, `StreamingContext`. **Workflow (1 mark):** User submits app → Driver creates `SparkContext` → requests resources from CM → CM launches Executors → Driver sends Tasks → Executors return results. *(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.2 for illustration.)* --- #### (b) RDD Lineage & Dependencies — Model Solution [7 Marks] **Lineage Definition (2 marks):** > **Lineage** is the **sequence of transformations** that were applied to create an RDD. It is the **recipe** of how an RDD was derived. Spark stores the lineage graph (DAG) for each RDD, enabling **fault recovery via recomputation** rather than data replication. **Narrow Dependency (2 marks):** - Each **parent partition contributes to AT MOST ONE** child partition. - **No shuffle** is required — pipelined execution. - **Examples:** `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union`, `mapPartitions`. **Wide Dependency (2 marks):** - Each **parent partition contributes to MULTIPLE** child partitions. - **Triggers a shuffle** — data moves across the network between executors. - **Examples:** `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey` (without combiner), `join`, `repartition`, `distinct`. **Fault Tolerance Importance (1 mark):** > When an executor fails and loses a partition, Spark consults the **lineage** of the lost RDD and **recomputes only that partition** from the source. This avoids the cost of replicating intermediate data — a major advantage over Hadoop MapReduce's replication strategy. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the DataFrame abstraction in Spark. With a block diagram, describe the Catalyst Optimizer and Tungsten Execution Engine. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of lazy evaluation in Spark. How does it contribute to performance optimization? Demonstrate with a stage and task count derivation example. --- ### Question B — Model Solutions #### (a) DataFrame, Catalyst & Tungsten — Model Solution [7 Marks] **DataFrame (2 marks):** > A **DataFrame** is a distributed collection of data organized into **named columns**, with a **schema** known to the Spark SQL engine. It is conceptually equivalent to a table in a relational database or a Pandas DataFrame with distribution. **Catalyst Optimizer (3 marks):** - **Layer 1 — Parsing:** Converts SQL/DSL query into an **Unresolved Logical Plan** (AST). - **Layer 2 — Analysis:** Resolves column/table references using the Catalog. Produces **Resolved Logical Plan**. - **Layer 3 — Logical Optimization:** Applies rules such as **predicate pushdown**, **constant folding**, **projection pruning**, **null propagation**. Produces **Optimized Logical Plan**. - **Layer 4 — Physical Planning:** Selects physical operators (e.g., `BroadcastHashJoin` vs. `SortMergeJoin`) using **Cost-Based Optimization (CBO)**. - **Layer 5 — Code Generation (Tungsten):** Janino compiles the physical plan to **Java bytecode** for compact, CPU-efficient execution. **Tungsten Execution Engine (2 marks):** - **Off-heap memory management** — Reduces GC overhead, enables explicit memory layout. - **Cache-friendly data structures** — Improves CPU cache hit rate. - **Whole-stage code generation** — Fuses multiple operators into a single function, eliminating virtual dispatch. *(Refer to the Catalyst pipeline diagram in Section 4.3 for illustration.)* --- #### (b) Lazy Evaluation & Stage/Task Derivation — Model Solution [7 Marks] **Lazy Evaluation Concept (3 marks):** > Spark uses **lazy evaluation** for transformations. When a transformation is called (e.g., `map`, `filter`, `join`), Spark does **not** compute it immediately. Instead, it records the operation in a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** maintained by the `DAGScheduler`. Execution is triggered **only when an action** (e.g., `collect`, `count`, `save`) is invoked. **Performance Benefits (2 marks):** - **Pipelining** — Multiple narrow transformations are fused into a single stage. - **Optimization** — Catalyst can rewrite the query plan (e.g., reorder joins, push filters). - **Reduced I/O** — Only the final result is materialized; intermediate RDDs may never be computed if short-circuited. **Derivation Example (2 marks):** > For lineage `textFile → map → filter → groupByKey → map → collect` with **4 input partitions**: > > - **Wide dependencies:** 1 (groupByKey) → **Stages = 2** > - **Tasks in Stage 1:** 4 (one per input partition) > - **Tasks in Stage 2:** 200 (default `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`) > - **Total tasks:** $4 + 200 = 204$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > > 1. **Confusing transformations with actions** — Many students write `collect()` and lose 1 mark by not classifying it correctly. > 2. **Forgetting to identify wide vs. narrow dependency** — A 7-mark question that asks "explain `groupByKey`" MUST mention the **shuffle boundary** and **stage count** to score full marks. > 3. **Not drawing the DAG** — Examiners explicitly award marks for "neat diagram" in architecture questions. > 4. **Mixing up DataFrame vs. Dataset** — DataFrame = untyped (Scala `Row`); Dataset = typed (Scala `case class`). > 5. **Ignoring security context** — In PECCT743 (Big Data Security), students often forget to relate RDD/DataFrame concepts to **secure data processing** (e.g., encryption zones, access control on Spark SQL tables). Always tie back to security when possible. > 6. **Forgetting to state the difference between `groupByKey` and `reduceByKey`** — `reduceByKey` is preferred because it uses a **combiner**, reducing shuffle volume. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ☐ **RDD = Resilient + Distributed + Dataset** — Immutable, partitioned, lazy. - ☐ **5 Internal Components** — Partitions, Dependencies, Compute, Partitioner, Preferred Locations. - ☐ **Narrow vs. Wide** — Narrow = no shuffle; Wide = shuffle (stage boundary). - ☐ **Lazy Evaluation** — Transformations are lazy; Actions trigger jobs. - ☐ **DAGScheduler** — Splits lineage into **stages** at wide dependencies. - ☐ **TaskScheduler** — Sends **tasks** (one per partition per stage) to executors. - ☐ **Stages** $= W + 1$ where $W$ = number of wide dependencies. - ☐ **Total Tasks** $= \sum_{i=1}^{S} P_i$ (sum of partitions across all stages). - ☐ **DataFrame = Dataset of named columns** with a **schema**. - ☐ **Catalyst** = Logical Optimizer (rule + cost-based). - ☐ **Tungsten** = Physical Execution Engine (off-heap + code generation). - ☐ **`reduceByKey` > `groupByKey`** — uses combiner, less shuffle. - ☐ **Lineage = Fault Tolerance** — Recompute lost partition from the source. - ☐ **Data Locality Order** — `PROCESS_LOCAL` → `NODE_LOCAL` → `RACK_LOCAL` → `ANY`. - ☐ **Default shuffle partitions** = 200 (`spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`). - ☐ **DataFrame is untyped; Dataset is typed** (Scala `Row` vs. `case class`). - ☐ **Security tie-back** — Spark SQL's Catalog supports **fine-grained access control**; lineage is useful for **forensic audit trails** in big data environments. - ☐ **Key Spark APIs** — `SparkContext` (RDD), `SparkSession` (DataFrame/SQL, unified). - ☐ **Storage options** — `MEMORY_ONLY`, `MEMORY_AND_DISK`, `DISK_ONLY`, `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` (serialized). - ☐ **Checkpointing** — Used to break long lineages and store RDD to HDFS for true fault tolerance (vs. recomputation). --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

topics.title: NoSQL databases: Cassandra, MongoDB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** NoSQL databases: Cassandra, MongoDB # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is NoSQL? **NoSQL (Not Only SQL)** is a class of database management systems (DBMS) engineered specifically to handle **unstructured, semi-structured, and poly-structured data** that traditional **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)** struggle to scale horizontally. They abandon the rigid, tabular, ACID-strict relational model in favor of **flexible schemas, distributed architectures, and BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent)** properties. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *NoSQL databases are distributed, non-relational data stores designed for horizontal scalability, high availability, and schema flexibility to support Big Data, real-time web, and cloud-native applications.* ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of NoSQL Data Models | **Model Type** | **Storage Paradigm** | **Best For** | **Canonical Example** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Key-Value Stores** | Hash table (Dictionary) | Caching, session management, leaderboards | **Redis**, DynamoDB, Riak | | **Document Stores** | JSON / BSON / XML documents | Content management, catalogs, mobile apps | **MongoDB**, CouchDB | | **Column-Family Stores** | Sparse, multi-dimensional columnar maps | Time-series, IoT, write-heavy analytics | **Cassandra**, HBase | | **Graph Databases** | Nodes + Edges + Properties | Social networks, fraud detection, recommendations | **Neo4j**, JanusGraph | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The "Library vs. Warehouse" Analogy** > Imagine an RDBMS as a **traditional library**: every book (row) must fit a pre-printed catalog card (schema). Adding a new attribute means reprinting every card. Now imagine a **NoSQL warehouse**: books can be of any shape, size, or description. You can throw in a box of photographs next to a diary and label each box with a unique barcode (key). Cassandra is a **giant warehouse with strict aisle rules (column families)**, while MongoDB is a **flexible archive room where each box is a self-contained file folder (document)**. ## 1.4 CAP Theorem — The Governing Principle The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, states that in any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two out of three** of the following properties: - **C** — **Consistency**: Every read receives the most recent write or an error. - **A** — **Availability**: Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes are down. - **P** — **Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network partitions (message loss between nodes). $$ \text{CAP} \Rightarrow \text{You can pick at most 2 from } \{C, A, P\} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > - **MongoDB** traditionally chooses **CP** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) with replica sets. Recent versions offer tunable CA-like behavior. > - **Cassandra** is the textbook **AP** (Availability + Partition Tolerance) system, with eventual consistency as a tunable parameter. ## 1.5 ACID vs. BASE | **Property** | **ACID (RDBMS)** | **BASE (NoSQL)** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Acronym Meaning | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent | | Consistency Model | Strong / Immediate | Eventual / Tunable | | Transaction Scope | Multi-row, multi-table | Single row / document (typically) | | Use Case | Banking, ERP | Social media, IoT, analytics | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Apache Cassandra — Deep Architecture ### 2.1.1 Genesis & Design Philosophy Cassandra was originally built at **Facebook (2008)** by **Avinash Lakshman (one of the authors of Amazon Dynamo)** and **Prashant Malik**. It merged **Dynamo's** peer-to-peer gossip-based replication with **Google Bigtable's** column-family data model — hence the tagline: *"Bigtable + Dynamo."* ### 2.1.2 Core Architecture Concepts - **Node**: A single machine in the cluster. All nodes are equal — there is **no master/slave**, no single point of failure. - **Data Center (DC)**: A logical grouping of nodes (often a physical DC). - **Rack**: A physical grouping within a DC for fault isolation. - **Cluster**: The complete set of data centers in a deployment. - **Commit Log**: Append-only crash-recovery log on disk. - **Mem-table**: In-memory write buffer (sorted). - **SSTable (Sorted String Table)**: Immutable, on-disk data file. - **Bloom Filter**: Probabilistic structure to check key existence before disk I/O. - **Compaction**: Process of merging SSTables to reclaim space and optimize reads. ### 2.1.3 Write Path (High-Yield) 1. Client sends write to a **coordinator node** (any node can coordinate). 2. Write is appended to **Commit Log** (durability). 3. Write is placed in **Mem-table** (in-memory). 4. Mem-table is flushed to **SSTable** when full. 5. SSTables are compacted periodically. ### 2.1.4 Read Path (High-Yield) 1. Coordinator checks **Bloom Filter** to see if key may exist in any SSTable. 2. Checks **Partition Key Cache** → **Key Cache** → **Row Cache** (in-memory). 3. If miss, performs disk lookup across multiple SSTables and merges results. ### 2.1.5 Data Distribution: Consistent Hashing Cassandra uses **consistent hashing** to distribute data across nodes. Each node is assigned a **token** (a 64-bit integer or `Murmur3` hash of a partition key). Data is mapped onto a **ring** of tokens from $0$ to $2^{127}-1$ (MD5) or $0$ to $2^{63}-1$ (Murmur3). $$ \text{Token}(k) = \text{Hash}(\text{PartitionKey}) \mod 2^{N} $$ Data is stored on the next **N** nodes clockwise on the ring, where **N = Replication Factor (RF)**. ### 2.1.6 Replication Strategies - **SimpleStrategy**: Used for single data center deployments; replicates to the next N-1 nodes clockwise. - **NetworkTopologyStrategy**: Used for multi-DC deployments; specifies RF per DC independently. Preferred for production. ### 2.1.7 Tunable Consistency The **Replication Factor (RF)** and **Consistency Level (CL)** are decoupled. The user controls CL per query. **Consistency Level Formulas (High-Yield for KTU):** $$ \text{Strong Read/Write} \iff \text{Block until } CL \text{ replicas acknowledge} $$ $$ \text{Read Repair Condition} \iff R + W > RF $$ where: - $R$ = number of replicas contacted for a read - $W$ = number of replicas that must acknowledge a write - $RF$ = total replicas > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strong Consistency Rule:** $R + W > RF$ guarantees the read will see the latest write. Example: $RF=3, W=2, R=2 \Rightarrow 2+2=4 > 3$ ✓ (Quorum). | **Consistency Level** | **Replicas Required (RF=3)** | **Use Case** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `ONE` | 1 | Fastest, weakest — analytics, logs | | `QUORUM` | $\lceil RF/2 \rceil = 2$ | Balanced strong consistency | | `LOCAL_QUORUM` | 2 in local DC | Multi-DC low-latency strong reads | | `ALL` | 3 | Strongest, slowest — billing | | `EACH_QUORUM` | Quorum in every DC | Multi-DC strong consistency | ## 2.2 MongoDB — Deep Architecture ### 2.2.1 Core Document Model A **MongoDB document** is a BSON (Binary JSON) object — an ordered set of key-value pairs. ```json { "_id": ObjectId("507f1f77bcf86cd799439011"), "username": "arjun_r", "email": "arjun@ktu.ac.in", "skills": ["BigData", "Cassandra", "Security"], "address": { "city": "Kochi", "state": "Kerala" }, "last_login": ISODate("2024-11-15T10:30:00Z") } ``` ### 2.2.2 Architecture Components - **mongod**: The primary database daemon process (one per server). - **mongos**: Query router in sharded clusters. - **Config Servers**: Store cluster metadata and chunk mapping. - **Replica Set**: Group of `mongod` instances maintaining the same data set. - **Primary**: The single node accepting all writes (elected via Raft consensus). - **Secondaries**: Replicate oplog from primary; can serve reads (with read preference). - **Arbiter**: A node that votes in elections but holds no data. ### 2.2.3 Write Concern & Read Concern (Tunable Consistency) - **Write Concern `w`**: Number of nodes that must acknowledge a write. - `w: 0` — Fire and forget. - `w: 1` — Acknowledged by primary. - `w: "majority"` — Acknowledged by majority of replica set. - **j (Journal)**: `true` to ensure write to on-disk journal. - **Read Concern**: `local`, `available`, `majority`, `linearizable`, `snapshot`. **The strong-consistency condition mirrors Cassandra:** $$ w + r > \text{replicaSetSize} $$ > [!NOTE] > For a 3-member replica set: setting `w="majority"` and `r="majority"` satisfies $w+r=3+3=6 > 3$ — guaranteed strong read. ### 2.2.4 Sharding in MongoDB - **Shard Key**: The field used to partition data. - **Chunk**: A contiguous range of shard-key values (default 64 MB). - **Balancer**: Background process that migrates chunks between shards for even distribution. - **Hashed Sharding**: Uses `Murmur3` hash for even distribution. Best for write-heavy workloads. - **Ranged Sharding**: Stores contiguous ranges. Best for range queries (e.g., dates, alphabetical). $$ \text{Chunk Range} = [\text{minKey}, \text{maxKey}) \quad \text{size} \leq 64\,\text{MB} $$ ### 2.2.5 Storage Engine - **WiredTiger** (default since 3.2): Document-level concurrency, compression (snappy/zstd), B-tree based. - **MMAPv1** (legacy): Memory-mapped files, collection-level locking. ### 2.2.6 Aggregation Pipeline A multi-stage data processing framework — `$match → $group → $sort → $project`. Inspired by UNIX pipes. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Engineering Utility** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | CAP Theorem | Pick 2 of 3: C, A, P | Architectural decision-making | | Cassandra Strong Read | $R + W > RF$ | Choosing CL per query | | Quorum Count | $Q = \lceil RF/2 \rceil$ | Default balanced consistency | | MongoDB Strong Read | $w + r > N$ (N = replica set size) | Replica set tuning | | Hashed Shard Spread | $H(k) = \text{Murmur3}(k) \mod 2^{32}$ | Even data distribution | | Token Range | $\text{Tokens} = 2^{64} \div N$ (Murmur3) | Initial cluster capacity planning | | BSON Doc Limit | $16\,\text{MB}$ per document | Schema design constraint | | Cassandra Column | Timestamp $\geq$ existing $\Rightarrow$ accept | Last-Write-Wins conflict resolution | | Read Repair | Anti-entropy via Merkle tree | Decentralized consistency repair | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Cassandra** powers **Netflix's** global streaming metadata (millions of writes/sec), **Apple's** iMessage infrastructure, and **Instagram's** direct messages. > - **MongoDB** is the backbone of **eBay's** metadata catalog, **Cisco's** IoT telemetry, and **Barclays Bank's** customer 360 views. > - In **Big Data Security**, both are used to store security event logs, user-behavior analytics, and threat-intelligence feeds that demand horizontal scalability beyond RDBMS limits. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Cassandra Token Assignment (Murmur3) **Problem:** You are deploying a 4-node Cassandra cluster using `Murmur3Partitioner`. The total token space is $0$ to $2^{63}-1$. Calculate the token for each node assuming equal partition. ### Step 1: Total Token Space $$ T_{total} = 2^{63} - 1 - 0 + 1 = 2^{63} = 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}808 $$ ### Step 2: Tokens Per Node $$ T_{per\_node} = \frac{T_{total}}{N} = \frac{2^{63}}{4} = 2^{61} $$ $$ 2^{61} = 2{,}305{,}843{,}009{,}213{,}693{,}952 $$ ### Step 3: Assign Tokens $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Node}_1 &= 0 \\ \text{Node}_2 &= 0 + 2^{61} = 2^{61} \\ \text{Node}_3 &= 2 \cdot 2^{61} = 2^{62} \\ \text{Node}_4 &= 3 \cdot 2^{61} = 2^{62} + 2^{61} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: General Token Assignment Formula $$ \text{Token}_i = (i-1) \cdot \frac{2^{63}}{N}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N $$ > [!NOTE] > Modern Cassandra uses **vnode (virtual nodes)** — each physical node is assigned **256 tokens** randomly for better load balancing. The above derivation applies to legacy "single-token-per-node" setups (often appearing in KTU numericals). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Quorum Calculation for Multi-DC **Problem:** You have a Cassandra cluster with **RF = 3 per DC**, and **2 data centers (DC1, DC2)**. What is the minimum **LOCAL_QUORUM** for reads/writes to satisfy strong consistency within a single DC? ### Step 1: Replicas Per DC $$ RF_{DC1} = 3, \quad RF_{DC2} = 3, \quad RF_{total} = 6 $$ ### Step 2: Quorum Formula $$ Q_{DC} = \left\lceil \frac{RF_{DC}}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3}{2} \right\rceil = 2 $$ ### Step 3: Apply Strong Read Condition $$ R + W > RF_{total} = 6 $$ If we use `LOCAL_QUORUM` for both read and write in DC1: $$ R_{local} + W_{local} = 2 + 2 = 4 $$ But $4$ is **not** $> 6$. So cross-DC strong consistency requires `EACH_QUORUM`: $$ R_{each} + W_{each} = 2 + 2 = 4 \quad \text{per DC, so total} = 8 > 6 \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: MongoDB Chunk Split Threshold **Problem:** A sharded MongoDB collection has 1 million documents averaging 4 KB each. The default chunk size is **64 MB**. How many chunks are formed before triggering a split? ### Step 1: Total Data Size $$ S_{total} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 4\,\text{KB} = 4{,}000{,}000\,\text{KB} = 3{,}906.25\,\text{MB} $$ ### Step 2: Chunks Formed (Floor Division) $$ C = \left\lfloor \frac{S_{total}}{S_{chunk}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3906.25}{64} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 61.04 \rfloor = 61 $$ ### Step 3: Remaining Data (Triggers 62nd Split) $$ S_{remaining} = 3906.25 - (61 \times 64) = 3906.25 - 3904 = 2.25\,\text{MB} $$ The balancer will create a **62nd chunk** if any insertion causes the active chunk to exceed 64 MB. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing CL for a Security Logging System **Scenario:** A Big Data Security platform ingests 200,000 logs/sec across 6 Cassandra nodes. RF=3, network=A. | **Operation** | **Chosen CL** | **R+W** | **Strong?** | **Rationale** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Ingest log | `ONE` | $W=1$ | No | High velocity, replay-able | | Threat alert | `QUORUM` | $2+2=4>3$ | Yes | Critical; must not lose alerts | | Dashboard query | `ONE` | $R=1$ | No | Eventual is acceptable for analytics | | Compliance audit | `ALL` | $3+3=6>3$ | Yes | Regulatory non-negotiable | > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** pattern: *“Justify consistency level choices for a given application.”* --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Hashed Shard Key Distribution **Problem:** MongoDB uses `Murmur3` to hash a 32-bit integer shard key. With 4 shards, compute the target shard for `k = 12345`. ### Step 1: Hash Assuming simplified hash output $H(12345) = 987654321$. ### Step 2: Modulo Shard Count $$ \text{Shard}_{target} = H(k) \mod N = 987654321 \mod 4 $$ ### Step 3: Compute $$ 987654321 \div 4 = 246913580.25 \Rightarrow \text{remainder} = 1 $$ ### Result $$ \boxed{\text{Document with key } 12345 \text{ routes to Shard}_1} $$ > [!NOTE] > Real `Murmur3` produces 32-bit unsigned output → modulo operation in MongoDB's `config servers` routes the write. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cassandra vs. MongoDB (Master Comparison) | **Criterion** | **Apache Cassandra** | **MongoDB** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Data Model** | Wide-row / column family | Document (BSON/JSON) | | **Query Language** | CQL (SQL-like) | MQL (MongoDB Query Language) | | **CAP Trade-off** | AP (tunable C) | CP (tunable A) | | **Write Master** | Masterless (any node) | Single Primary per replica set | | **Secondary Indexes** | Local + SASI (limited) | First-class (multikey, text, geo, hashed) | | **Aggregation** | Limited (User-Defined Functions) | Rich Aggregation Pipeline | | **Joins** | None (denormalize) | `$lookup` (left outer) | | **Transactions** | LWT (lightweight) — since 2.x | Multi-doc ACID — since 4.0 | | **Best Fit** | Time-series, IoT, write-heavy OLTP | Catalogs, CMS, evolving schemas | | **Consensus Protocol** | Paxos / Gossip | Raft (since 3.4 for replica sets) | | **License** | Apache 2.0 | SSPL (Server Side Public License) | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Cassandra Cluster Architecture (Peer-to-Peer Ring) ```mermaid graph LR client1[Client App] client2[Client App] coord1[Coordinator Node A] coord2[Coordinator Node B] n1[Node 1
Token 0] n2[Node 2
Token 2 to 61] n3[Node 3
Token 2 to 62] n4[Node 4
Token 2 to 62 plus 2 to 61] dc1[Data Center 1] dc2[Data Center 2] client1 --> coord1 client2 --> coord2 coord1 --> n1 coord1 --> n2 coord1 --> n3 coord2 --> n3 coord2 --> n4 n1 --- dc1 n2 --- dc1 n3 --- dc2 n4 --- dc2 n1 <-->|Gossip| n2 n2 <-->|Gossip| n3 n3 <-->|Gossip| n4 n4 <-->|Gossip| n1 ``` ## 4.2 Cassandra Write Path — Detailed Flow ```mermaid graph TD start[Client Write Request] --> coord{Coordinator
Node} coord --> log[1. Append to
Commit Log on Disk] log --> mem[2. Write to
Mem-table In Memory] mem --> ack[Acknowledge to Client] mem --> full{Mem-table Full?} full -->|No| wait[Wait for More Writes] full -->|Yes| flush[3. Flush to SSTable on Disk] flush --> compact[4. Compaction
Merges SSTables] compact --> sstable[Optimized SSTable] ``` ## 4.3 MongoDB Replica Set + Sharding Architecture ```mermaid graph TB app[MongoDB Client Application] router1[mongos Router 1] router2[mongos Router 2] cs1[Config Server 1] cs2[Config Server 2] cs3[Config Server 3] subgraph Shard1[Shard 1 Replica Set] p1[Primary] s1a[Secondary 1] s1b[Secondary 2] p1 --- s1a p1 --- s1b end subgraph Shard2[Shard 2 Replica Set] p2[Primary] s2a[Secondary 1] s2b[Secondary 2] p2 --- s2a p2 --- s2b end subgraph Shard3[Shard 3 Replica Set] p3[Primary] s3a[Secondary 1] s3b[Secondary 2] p3 --- s3a p3 --- s3b end app --> router1 app --> router2 router1 --> cs1 router1 --> cs2 router1 --> cs3 router2 --> cs1 router2 --> cs2 router2 --> cs3 router1 --> p1 router1 --> p2 router1 --> p3 ``` ## 4.4 NoSQL Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Database ```mermaid graph TD start[Need a Database?] start --> q1{Is Data
Highly Relational?} q1 -->|Yes| rdbms[Use RDBMS
PostgreSQL or MySQL] q1 -->|No| q2{Data Shape?} q2 -->|Key Value| kv[Redis or DynamoDB] q2 -->|Document| q3{Flexible Schema
and Aggregation?} q3 -->|Yes| mongo[MongoDB] q3 -->|No| q4{Extreme Write
Throughput and AP?} q4 -->|Yes| cassandra[Apache Cassandra] q4 -->|No| q5{Graph Relationships?} q5 -->|Yes| graph[Neo4j] q5 -->|No| review[Re-evaluate Requirements] ``` ## 4.5 CAP Theorem Visualization (Cassandra vs. MongoDB) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CAP[CAP Theorem Triangle] c[C Consistency] a[A Availability] p[P Partition Tolerance] end cassChoice[Cassandra selects
AP side] mongoChoice[MongoDB selects
CP side] CAP --> cassChoice CAP --> mongoChoice ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always include a labelled Mermaid diagram in 14-mark answers — it is a guaranteed **2-mark boost** for visual presentation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** *Define NoSQL databases. List and briefly explain any four types of NoSQL data models.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** NoSQL ("Not Only SQL") refers to a class of database systems designed to store and retrieve data that is not strictly tabular. They prioritize **horizontal scalability, schema flexibility, and BASE properties** over the strict ACID guarantees of RDBMS. **Four Types (2 marks):** 1. **Key-Value Stores** — Data is stored as a hash map. Fastest lookups. Examples: Redis, DynamoDB. 2. **Document Stores** — Data stored as JSON/BSON documents. Examples: MongoDB, CouchDB. 3. **Column-Family Stores** — Data stored in sparse columns grouped into families. Examples: Cassandra, HBase. 4. **Graph Databases** — Data stored as nodes and edges. Examples: Neo4j, JanusGraph. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** *Explain the CAP theorem. How does Cassandra and MongoDB position themselves with respect to CAP?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **CAP Theorem (1.5 marks):** Formulated by Eric Brewer, it states that a distributed data store can simultaneously deliver **only two of three** guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**. **Positioning (1.5 marks):** - **Cassandra** is an **AP** system. It prefers availability and partition tolerance, with consistency being **tunable** per query via Consistency Level (CL). - **MongoDB** is a **CP** system. It prefers consistency and partition tolerance, using a single primary per replica set to ensure strong consistency. Availability is reduced during primary election. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** #### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** — Cassandra Architecture Deep Dive *With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Apache Cassandra. Discuss the role of Gossip protocol, Commit Log, Mem-table, and SSTable in the write path.* **Model Solution:** **Architecture Overview (2 marks):** Apache Cassandra follows a **masterless, peer-to-peer distributed architecture** where every node is identical and can serve any request. Nodes are arranged in a logical ring based on token assignment (consistent hashing). There is no single point of failure. Multiple data centers can be deployed for geographic redundancy. **Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 Mermaid diagrams)* **Write Path Components (3 marks):** 1. **Gossip Protocol** — A peer-to-peer communication mechanism used by nodes to share cluster topology and state information (e.g., which nodes are up/down, load). Each node gossips with up to 3 other nodes every second. This avoids centralized coordination. 2. **Commit Log** — An append-only file on disk that records every mutation for **crash recovery**. Even if a node crashes, the Commit Log ensures no acknowledged write is lost. After successful mem-table write, the log entry is eligible for truncation. 3. **Mem-table** — A **sorted, in-memory write buffer** keyed by partition. Writes are extremely fast as they only touch memory. When the mem-table is full (or a threshold is reached), it is flushed to disk as an immutable **SSTable**. 4. **SSTable (Sorted String Table)** — The on-disk, immutable data file. Reads may hit multiple SSTables, and Cassandra uses **Bloom filters** to reduce disk I/O. A background **compaction** process merges SSTables to reclaim space and optimize read performance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mentioning the 4 components with one-line functional role = full 3 marks.** --- #### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** — Tunable Consistency & Replication *Explain the concept of tunable consistency in Cassandra. A cluster has RF=5. Determine the minimum number of nodes that must respond for: (i) ONE (ii) QUORUM (iii) LOCAL_QUORUM (iv) ALL.* **Model Solution:** **Tunable Consistency Concept (3 marks):** In Cassandra, the **Consistency Level (CL)** is specified by the client per query, allowing trade-offs between **latency** and **read/write correctness**. The Replication Factor (RF) is the number of copies of data stored across the cluster. The CL for a write ($W$) and read ($R$) determine whether strong consistency is achieved. **Strong Consistency Condition:** $R + W > RF$ **Calculations for RF=5 (4 marks):** | **Consistency Level** | **Replicas Required** | **Formula** | **Answer** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `ONE` | First replica to respond | $1$ | $\mathbf{1}$ | | `QUORUM` | $\lceil RF/2 \rceil$ | $\lceil 5/2 \rceil = 3$ | $\mathbf{3}$ | | `LOCAL_QUORUM` | Quorum in local DC | Depends on RF per DC | $\mathbf{\lceil RF_{DC}/2 \rceil}$ | | `ALL` | All replicas | $RF$ | $\mathbf{5}$ | **For LOCAL_QUORUM** with $RF=5$ split across two DCs ($RF_{DC1}=3, RF_{DC2}=2$): $$ \text{Local Quorum in DC1} = \lceil 3/2 \rceil = 2 $$ $$ \text{Local Quorum in DC2} = \lceil 2/2 \rceil = 1 $$ **Strong consistency check (QUORUM, R=3, W=3):** $$ R + W = 3 + 3 = 6 > 5 \quad \checkmark \text{ Strong consistency achieved} $$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** — MongoDB Architecture & Document Model *Explain the document data model of MongoDB with a suitable example. Discuss the architecture of a sharded MongoDB cluster with a diagram.* **Model Solution:** **Document Model (3 marks):** MongoDB stores data as **BSON (Binary JSON) documents** — self-describing, hierarchical structures. Documents are grouped into **collections** (analogous to tables). Each document can have a different schema, enabling **polymorphic** storage. **Example (1 mark):** ```json { "_id": ObjectId("64a7f0..."), "course": "Big Data Security", "university": "KTU", "credits": 4, "topics": ["NoSQL", "Cassandra", "MongoDB"], "assessment": { "internal": 40, "external": 60 } } ``` **Sharded Cluster Architecture (3 marks):** A sharded MongoDB cluster consists of three components: 1. **Shards** — Each shard is a replica set holding a subset of data. 2. **Mongos Query Routers** — Stateless routers that accept client requests and forward them to the correct shard(s) based on the shard key. 3. **Config Servers** — A replica set that stores cluster metadata, including the mapping of chunks to shards. **Sharding Process:** Data is partitioned by a **shard key**. MongoDB splits the data into **chunks** of 64 MB. The **balancer** process migrates chunks between shards to ensure even distribution. **Diagram (must include — refer to Section 4.3):** Mongos → Config Servers → Multiple Shard Replica Sets. --- #### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** — Replica Set & Tunable Consistency *Explain MongoDB's replica set mechanism. How does MongoDB handle failover? Compare `w:1`, `w:"majority"`, and `w:0` write concerns.* **Model Solution:** **Replica Set Mechanism (3 marks):** A MongoDB **replica set** is a group of `mongod` instances maintaining identical data sets. It consists of: - **One Primary** — receives all writes. - **Multiple Secondaries** — replicate the primary's **oplog** (operations log) asynchronously. - **Optional Arbiters** — participate in elections but hold no data. **Failover Handling (2 marks):** 1. Secondaries continuously ping the primary (default every 2 sec). 2. If no response for **10 seconds** (default election timeout), secondaries call for an **election**. 3. Election uses the **Raft consensus protocol**. The candidate with the highest priority, most recent oplog, and majority votes becomes the new primary. 4. Automatic reconnection: clients redirect writes to the new primary (typically within 10-30 seconds). **Write Concern Comparison (2 marks):** | **Write Concern** | **Behavior** | **Durability** | **Latency** | **Use Case** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `w: 0` | Fire-and-forget; no acknowledgment | None | Lowest | Telemetry, metrics | | `w: 1` | Acknowledged by primary only | Single node | Low | General OLTP | | `w: "majority"` | Acknowledged by majority of replica set | Strong | Higher | Critical financial data | **Strong consistency check for 3-node RS with `w="majority"` and `r="majority"`:** $$ w + r = 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 \quad \checkmark $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing CAP and ACID** — These are *not* the same. CAP is about distributed systems; ACID is about transactions. > 2. **Wrongly stating MongoDB is AP** — It is **CP** by default (single primary ensures consistency). > 3. **Forgetting the formula** $R + W > RF$ in tunable consistency questions — this is the most-marks-losing mistake. > 4. **Omitting diagrams** in 14-mark questions — Mermaid or hand-drawn, both fetch 1-2 visual marks. > 5. **Not specifying the partitioner** when calculating Cassandra tokens — Murmur3 vs. RandomPartitioner changes the math. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - **NoSQL = Not Only SQL** — non-relational, schema-flexible, horizontally scalable, BASE properties. - **Four Models**: Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph. - **CAP Theorem**: Pick 2 of {C, A, P}. Cassandra = **AP**, MongoDB = **CP** (tunable). - **Cassandra = Masterless, peer-to-peer, Bigtable + Dynamo hybrid**, uses **consistent hashing** with token-based ring. - **Cassandra Write Path**: Commit Log → Mem-table → SSTable → Compaction. - **Cassandra Read Path**: Bloom Filter → Partition Key Cache → Key Cache → Row Cache → SSTable Merge. - **Strong Consistency Rule**: $R + W > RF$. - **Quorum** = $\lceil RF/2 \rceil$. - **MongoDB = Document-oriented, BSON, single primary replica set, Raft-based election**. - **MongoDB Sharding** = Shard Key → Chunks (64 MB) → Balancer migrates. - **MongoDB Shard Strategies**: Hashed (even distribution), Ranged (range queries). - **Tunable CL in Cassandra**: ONE, QUORUM, LOCAL_QUORUM, EACH_QUORUM, ALL. - **MongoDB Write Concern**: `w: 0`, `w: 1`, `w: "majority"`, plus `j: true` for journal durability. - **Document size limit in MongoDB = 16 MB**. - **Cassandra License = Apache 2.0**, MongoDB License = **SSPL** (since 2018). - **Gossip protocol** in Cassandra = decentralized, no central registry. - **Use Cassandra for write-heavy, time-series, IoT, AP-critical systems**. - **Use MongoDB for flexible schemas, document-shaped data, rich queries, and aggregations**. - **In Big Data Security**: both store logs, security events, threat intel, user-behavior analytics at petabyte scale. - **Always include diagrams in 14-mark answers** — visual aids fetch easy marks. - **Strong consistency** in MongoDB requires `w="majority"` + `r="majority"` on a replica set of size N. - **Strong consistency** in Cassandra requires `CL=QUORUM` (or stronger) for both reads and writes. - **Token Calculation**: $\text{Token}_i = (i-1) \cdot 2^{63}/N$ for Murmur3Partitioner (legacy mode).

topics.title: Case Studies: Real-world examples of Big Data security breaches and their implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Real-world examples of Big Data security breaches and their implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Security Breach (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *Big Data security breach* is a confirmed incident of unauthorized access, exfiltration, modification, disclosure, or destruction of large-scale, high-velocity, or high-variety data assets (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — the 5V model) held within distributed computing ecosystems such as Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL clusters, cloud data lakes, or streaming platforms. It compromises at least one pillar of the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** and triggers obligations under data protection regulations such as **GDPR, IT Act 2000 (India), HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**.

A *real-world case study* in this context is a forensic, post-incident analysis that captures: **(i)** the threat actor and attack vector, **(ii)** the vulnerable Big Data component (e.g., HDFS, Kafka, S3 bucket, Hive metastore, REST API), **(iii)** the scale of compromise, **(iv)** the regulatory and financial implications, and **(v)** the corrective control framework deployed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Bank Vault" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a Big Data cluster as a **massive digital bank vault** containing billions of customer lockers. A *traditional breach* targets one locker at a time. A *Big Data breach* is like a master thief who, by exploiting a single faulty ventilation duct (a misconfigured API or unpatched Hadoop node), walks into the central control room, photocopies the master key registry, and walks out with **photocopies of every single locker** — in one night, without tripping the perimeter alarm.
>
> The "photocopies" represent *exfiltrated petabytes* of PII (Personally Identifiable Information), PHI (Protected Health Information), and PCI (Payment Card Industry) data. The single faulty duct is the **Initial Access Vector**, and the alarm that did not trigger is the **Detection & Response Failure**.

## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Big Data Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 — PECCT743):** Module 1 establishes the foundational threat landscape. Case studies translate abstract cryptographic and access-control theory into *concrete, monetizable harm*. They also train the student to perform **Threat Modelling** and **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** — both of which are high-weightage assessment outcomes under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.

### 1.3.1 The 5V Characterization of a "Big" Breach

| Dimension | Threshold That Makes a Breach "Big Data" | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | > 1 Million records exposed | Equifax 2017: 147 M records |
| **Velocity** | Real-time streaming pipeline compromised | Capital One 2019: SSRF on AWS |
| **Variety** | Structured + Unstructured + Semi-structured | Yahoo 2013: emails, contacts, security Q&A |
| **Veracity** | Tampered training data in ML pipeline | Microsoft Tay Bot 2016 |
| **Value** | High-sensitivity PII / PHI / PCI | Anthem 2015: medical records |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Big Data Breach — The Kill Chain

A Big Data security breach is rarely a single event. It follows a **Cyber Kill Chain** (Lockheed Martin / MITRE ATT&CK adapted for distributed systems):

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker scans Hadoop NameNode ports, S3 buckets, or Elasticsearch REST endpoints.
2. **Initial Access** — Exploits a misconfiguration (e.g., open Kibana dashboard, public S3 bucket, leaked API key in GitHub).
3. **Privilege Escalation** — Leverages a Kerberos ticket vulnerability, IAM misconfiguration, or container escape.
4. **Lateral Movement** — Pivots across HDFS, Hive, Spark, Kafka, and YARN nodes.
5. **Collection & Aggregation** — Uses distributed Spark jobs to *co-locate* PII fragments across the cluster.
6. **Exfiltration** — Encrypts and transmits petabytes to attacker-controlled object storage via HTTPS, DNS tunneling, or Tor.
7. **Impact** — Data is sold on dark web, used for identity fraud, or ransom is demanded.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Metrics & Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** KTU examiners expect students to memorize for case-study analysis questions. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit | Regulatory Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Cost of Breach (TCB)** | $\text{TCB} = C_{\text{detection}} + C_{\text{containment}} + C_{\text{notification}} + C_{\text{remediation}} + C_{\text{regulatory fines}}$ | USD | — |
| **Cost per Record (CPR)** | $\text{CPR} = \dfrac{\text{TCB}}{N_{\text{records breached}}}$ | USD/record | — |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{breach},i})$ | Days | < 100 days (industry avg) |
| **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{contained},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})$ | Days | < 30 days (target) |
| **Detection Efficiency ($\eta$)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{detected}}}{N_{\text{total events}}} \times 100\%$ | % | > 95% (NIST CSF) |
| **Records Exposed ($N_{\text{exp}}$)** | $N_{\text{exp}} = \vert \text{PII rows} \vert \times \vert \text{attributes per row} \vert$ | Records | — |
| **Penalty Under GDPR Art. 83** | $P_{\text{GDPR}} \le \min\!\big(4\% \text{ of global turnover},\; \texteuro 20\text{M}\big)$ | € | Mandatory within 72 hrs |
| **Penalty Under DPDPA 2023 (India)** | $P_{\text{DPDPA}} \le \texteuro 250\text{Cr}$ | ₹ | Mandatory breach notification |
| **Encryption Coverage ($\varepsilon$)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\vert \text{Encrypted at-rest records} \vert}{\vert \text{Total records} \vert} \times 100\%$ | % | 100% (target) |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

A Big Data security engineer uses these metrics to:

- **Quantify Risk:** Convert abstract threats into dollar values for board-level risk registers.
- **Benchmark Controls:** Compare an organization's MTTD against the industry median (currently ~204 days per IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report).
- **Design SLAs:** Set contractual obligations for cloud providers (e.g., AWS Shared Responsibility Model).
- **Pass Audits:** Satisfy ISO 27001, SOC 2, and NIST CSF 2.0 evidence requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Major Case Studies

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is qualitative-forensic rather than purely mathematical, the "derivation" here is a **structured 6-field forensic matrix** for each canonical breach. This matrix is the **same analytical scaffold** KTU examiners use to grade Part B 14-mark case-study questions.

## 3.1 Case Study A — Yahoo (2013–2014) | The Largest Breach in History

**Step 1 — Identify the Threat Actor:**
A state-sponsored APT group (attributed to APT28 / "Peace" criminal syndicate) orchestrated the attack, possibly with backing from a nation-state intelligence service.

**Step 2 — Identify the Initial Access Vector:**
Yahoo's custom-built cookie-forging mechanism was compromised. The attackers generated forged session cookies from a list of authenticated user credentials, bypassing password authentication entirely — a classic **session-hijack** on a massive Hadoop-backed user-profile store.

**Step 3 — Identify the Big Data Component Compromised:**
- **User profile store** (likely a proprietary distributed key-value store similar to HBase).
- **Backup tape archive** containing security questions and answers in **plaintext** (no encryption-at-rest, ε ≈ 0%).

**Step 4 — Quantify the Breach:**
$$N_{\text{exp}} = 3{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ accounts} \times \approx 12 \text{ attributes per profile}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Detection & Response Failure:**
- MTTD = **~2 years** (breach 2013, disclosed 2016).
- Notification to users was delayed by **~1,083 days**.

**Step 6 — Compute Implications:**
- **Financial:** Reduced Verizon's acquisition offer by **$350 million** (final price $4.48B instead of $4.83B).
- **Regulatory:** $35 million SEC fine (2018) for failing to disclose material breach within 4 business days.
- **Reputational:** Loss of user trust; brand damage estimated in billions.
- **Legal:** Class-action lawsuits; 41 class actions consolidated.
- **Compliance:** Pre-GDPR, but became a textbook case for GDPR Art. 33 mandatory notification.

**Lesson:** Always encrypt sensitive attributes at rest; implement **cryptographic key rotation**; enforce **real-time session anomaly detection** on Hadoop-backed user stores.

---

## 3.2 Case Study B — Equifax (2017) | The Apache Struts 0-Day

**Step 1 — Threat Actor:**
Chinese military-linked APT group (suspected, never formally charged) targeting bulk credit-bureau data.

**Step 2 — Initial Access Vector:**
**CVE-2017-5638** — A remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Apache Struts 2, used by Equifax's online dispute portal. Patch had been available for **2 months** but was not applied to the affected node (a classic patch-management failure).

**Step 3 — Big Data Component Compromised:**
- **Hadoop + NoSQL cluster** holding credit histories of 147 M Americans, 15.2 M Brits, ~19 K Canadians.
- Encryption keys were stored in plaintext within the same HDFS namespace — a fatal key-management flaw.

**Step 4 — Quantify the Breach:**
$$N_{\text{exp}} = 147{,}000{,}000 \text{ records} \times \approx 23 \text{ PII fields}$$
Data exfiltrated: SSN, DOB, addresses, **driver's license numbers** (209,000), **credit card numbers** (209,000).

**Step 5 — Detection & Response Failure:**
- MTTD = **76 days** (May 13 – July 29, 2017).
- SSL inspection certificate had expired **19 months prior**, blinding internal monitoring.

**Step 6 — Implications:**
- **Financial:** $700 million settlement (2019) — at the time the largest ever.
- **Regulatory:** FTC consent order; CFPB fine; UK ICO fine £500,000 (the maximum pre-GDPR).
- **Reputational:** CEO Richard Smith resigned; stock fell ~13%.
- **Compliance:** Triggered NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR 500).
- **Technical Legacy:** Equifax open-sourced **"SafetyDance"** — a tool to auto-inventory vulnerable open-source components.

**Lesson:** Patch within 24–72 hours; never store encryption keys alongside encrypted data; maintain **asset inventory** of all distributed nodes.

---

## 3.3 Case Study C — Capital One (2019) | The Cloud Misconfiguration

**Step 1 — Threat Actor:**
**Paige A. Thompson** (former AWS employee) — an insider threat leveraging external knowledge of cloud internals.

**Step 2 — Initial Access Vector:**
**Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** on a **Modly WAF-protected** AWS EC2 instance. The WAF's IAM role had **excessively permissive S3 read policies** (`s3:GetObject` on `*`).

**Step 3 — Big Data Component Compromised:**
- **AWS S3 buckets** (the "data lake") holding ~106 million credit card applications.
- **AWS Glue metadata**, **EC2 compute**, and **CloudTrail logs** in the same account.

**Step 4 — Quantify the Breach:**
$$N_{\text{exp}} = 100{,}000{,}000 \text{ individuals} \times \approx 14 \text{ fields}$$
1 million Canadian social insurance numbers, 80,000 bank account numbers, 140,000 SSNs.

**Step 5 — Detection Failure:**
- Capital One learned of the breach from an **anonymous tip** via Responsible Disclosure email — not from internal monitoring.
- MTTD ≈ **~3 months** (SSRF in March, breach detected July 2019).

**Step 6 — Implications:**
- **Financial:** $190 million settlement (2022); $80 million in remediation.
- **Regulatory:** OCC fine $80M; CFPB fine $45M.
- **Cloud-Security Legacy:** Became the **canonical case** for **Least-Privilege IAM**, **SSM Session Manager** vs. bastion hosts, and **IMDSv2** (Instance Metadata Service v2) to mitigate SSRF.

**Lesson:** Enforce least-privilege IAM roles; mandate IMDSv2; never grant wildcard S3 permissions; deploy **CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management)** tooling.

---

## 3.4 Case Study D — Marriott / Starwood (2018) | The Acquisition-Borne Breach

**Step 1 — Threat Actor:** Chinese state-sponsored APT (attributed by US Department of Justice 2024 indictment).

**Step 2 — Vector:** **Malware in the Starwood reservation system** that predated Marriott's 2016 acquisition. The acquisition team failed to perform adequate **cybersecurity due diligence**.

**Step 3 — Big Data Component:** Starwood's **guest reservation database** (Hadoop/SAP-backed) containing 500 million guest records.

**Step 4 — Quantify:** 339 million guest profiles exposed; 5.25 million passport numbers (encrypted, but with **stolen encryption keys**).

**Step 5 — Detection Failure:** MTTD ≈ **~2 years** post-acquisition; malware present since 2014.

**Step 6 — Implications:** $123 million GDPR fine (UK ICO, reduced from £99M to £18.4M); reputational damage; class-action suits; **M&A cyber-due-diligence** became a board-level priority.

---

## 3.5 Case Study E — Facebook / Cambridge Analytica (2018) | The API Data Harvest

**Step 1 — Vector:** A personality-quiz app used Facebook's **Open Graph API (v1.0 → v2.0 migration loophole)** to harvest data on **~87 million users** who never consented, plus their entire friend network (consent contagion).

**Step 2 — Big Data Component:** Facebook's **TAO (The Ads Optimization) data warehouse** — a Hive/Spark ecosystem storing trillions of social-graph edges.

**Step 3 — Implications:** $5 billion FTC fine (2019) — the largest ever against a tech company; $100 million SEC fine; GDPR probe; **"consent contagion"** became a textbook GDPR violation.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Forensic Matrix

| Attribute | Yahoo 2013–14 | Equifax 2017 | Capital One 2019 | Marriott 2018 | Facebook 2018 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vector** | Cookie forging | Struts 0-day RCE | SSRF + IAM misconfig | Pre-acquisition RAT | Open Graph API abuse |
| **Component** | Custom K-V store | Hadoop + NoSQL | AWS S3 data lake | Reservation DB | Hive/Spark warehouse |
| **Records ($N_{\text{exp}}$)** | 3 B | 147 M | 100 M | 500 M | 87 M |
| **MTTD** | ~730 days | 76 days | ~90 days | ~730 days | ~2 years |
| **Encryption at Rest ($\varepsilon$)** | 0% | 0% (keys exposed) | Partial | 100% (keys stolen) | Partial |
| **Big Fine (≈)** | $35 M (SEC) | $700 M | $190 M | £18.4 M | $5 B |
| **CIA Triad Violated** | C, I | C, I, A | C | C, I | C, I |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Big Data Breach Kill Chain (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Reconnaissance: Scan Hadoop NameNode and S3 endpoints] --> A1[Initial Access: Exploit unpatched CVE or misconfig]
    A1 --> A2[Privilege Escalation: Kerberoast or IAM abuse]
    A2 --> A3[Lateral Movement: Pivot across YARN and HDFS nodes]
    A3 --> A4[Collection: Spark job aggregates PII fragments]
    A4 --> A5[Exfiltration: Encrypted upload to attacker S3 via DNS tunnel]
    A5 --> A6[Impact: Dark web sale, identity fraud, ransom]

    classDef bad fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#aa6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A0,A1,A2 bad
    class A3,A4 mid
    class A5,A6 bad
```

## 4.2 Shared Responsibility Model — Where Each Major Breach Failed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CloudProvider["CLOUD PROVIDER Responsibility"]
        C1[Physical Security of Data Center]
        C2[Hypervisor and Hardware]
        C3[Base Storage Service Uptime]
    end
    subgraph Customer["CUSTOMER Responsibility"]
        U1[OS and Patch Management]
        U2[IAM and Access Policies]
        U3[Data Encryption at Rest and in Transit]
        U4[Network Firewall and WAF Rules]
        U5[Logging, Monitoring, IR Plan]
    end

    C1 -.->|Always provider| X[SECURE SYSTEM]
    C2 -.->|Always provider| X
    C3 -.->|Always provider| X
    U1 -.->|Equifax failed here| Y[BREACH SURFACE]
    U2 -.->|Capital One failed here| Y
    U3 -.->|Yahoo failed here| Y
    U4 -.->|Struts CVE exploited| Y
    U5 -.->|Yahoo Marriott slow detect| Y

    classDef prov fill:#dceaff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cust fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fail fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    class C1,C2,C3 prov
    class U1,U2,U3,U4,U5 cust

    class Y fail
```

## 4.3 The CIA Triad Damage Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CIA["CIA TRIAD DAMAGE FROM CASE STUDIES"]
        D1[Confidentiality: Yahoo 3B, Equifax 147M, Marriott 500M, Capital One 100M, Facebook 87M]
        D2[Integrity: Equifax tampered credit files, Facebook tampered ad models, Yahoo forged cookies]
        D3[Availability: Target POS outage, Anthem systems offline 6 weeks]
    end
```

## 4.4 Big Data Security Breach Defense Stack (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Layer 1: PERIMETER"]
        L1a[WAF and API Gateway]
        L1b[DDoS Shield]
        L1c[TLS 1.3 Termination]
    end

        L2a[Kerberos and LDAP]
        L2b[RBAC and ABAC Policies]

    end

        L3a[Encryption at Rest AES-256]
        L3b[Encryption in Transit TLS 1.3]

    end

        L4a[SIEM and UEBA]

        L4c[Hadoop Audit Log Analysis]
    end
```
