# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Define Big Data security breach. List any four real-world examples with one-line impact statements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Big Data security breach* is the unauthorized access, exfiltration, or destruction of large-scale distributed data assets that compromises the **CIA Triad**.

Four real-world examples (1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for examples = 3 marks):

1. **Yahoo (2013–14):** 3 billion accounts compromised; reduced Verizon acquisition by $350 M.
2. **Equifax (2017):** 147 M records; $700 M settlement.
3. **Capital One (2019):** 100 M credit applications; SSRF + IAM misconfig; $190 M fine.
4. **Marriott (2018):** 500 M guests; pre-acquisition RAT; £18.4 M GDPR fine.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Explain the significance of MTTD and MTTR in breach management with reference to one case study.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** Average time between breach occurrence and detection. Industry average ≈ 204 days (1 mark).
- **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond):** Average time between detection and containment. Target < 30 days (1 mark).
- **Case Study Reference (1 mark):** Equifax had MTTD = 76 days due to expired SSL inspection certificate; the gap allowed 147 M records to be exfiltrated. Reducing MTTD via UEBA and SIEM could have limited the breach by an estimated 60–80%.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question offers internal choice. **Attempt ANY ONE** of the two sub-questions A or B. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, PECCT743 Module 1]**

> *"Big Data breaches are not caused by a single vulnerability — they are the consequence of cascading failures across the technology stack."* **In light of this statement, answer the following:**

#### (a) With reference to the **Equifax 2017 breach**, analyze the attack vector, the Big Data components compromised, and the root-cause failure chain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Attack Vector (2 marks):**
- **CVE-2017-5638** — a remote code execution vulnerability in **Apache Struts 2** used by Equifax's online dispute portal.
- The patch had been released in **March 2017**, but the affected node was not patched — a **patch management failure**.

**Big Data Components Compromised (2 marks):**
- The credit-bureau data was stored in a **Hadoop + NoSQL** distributed cluster.
- **Encryption keys were stored in plaintext** in the same HDFS namespace, allowing the attacker to decrypt 147 M records.
- Personally Identifiable Information (PII) exposed: SSN, DOB, addresses, **driver's license numbers**, and partial credit card data.

**Root-Cause Failure Chain (3 marks):**
1. **Asset inventory gap** → vulnerable Struts node was untracked.
2. **Patch management failure** → 2-month patch window missed.
3. **SSL inspection certificate expired 19 months prior** → blind monitoring.
4. **Key management failure** → encryption keys co-located with data.
5. **Detection and response gap** → 76 days MTTD.

#### (b) Discuss the **regulatory, financial, and reputational implications** of the breach, and propose a **defense-in-depth control framework** to prevent recurrence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Implications (3.5 marks):**
- **Regulatory:** $700 M settlement (2019) including FTC, CFPB, 50 states; UK ICO fine £500 K; NYDFS enforcement.
- **Financial:** Stock fell 13% post-disclosure; $1.4 B cumulative cost.
- **Reputational:** CEO Richard Smith resigned; consumer trust at historic lows; 41 class-action suits.

**Defense-in-Depth Framework (3.5 marks):**
1. **Patch Management SLA** — critical CVE patches within **72 hours**; automate via Ansible/Chef.
2. **Asset Inventory** — continuous CMDB updates; tag every Hadoop node.
3. **Encryption Key Separation** — store keys in **HSM** or **KMS** (e.g., AWS KMS), never in the data cluster.
4. **TLS Inspection Hygiene** — alert on expiring certificates 90 days ahead.
5. **UEBA + SIEM** — deploy user-and-entity behavior analytics on Hadoop audit logs.
6. **Zero-Trust IAM** — Kerberos + RBAC + MFA; least-privilege service accounts.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, PECCT743 Module 1]**

> *Compare and contrast two of the following Big Data security breaches, emphasizing their attack vector, detection failure, and regulatory implications: (i) Yahoo 2013–14, (ii) Capital One 2019, (iii) Marriott 2018.*

#### (a) Compare **Yahoo 2013–14 and Capital One 2019** with respect to attack vector, Big Data component compromised, and MTTD. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Yahoo 2013–14 | Capital One 2019 |
|---|---|---|
| **Vector** | Cookie forging on proprietary authentication | SSRF on WAF-protected EC2 + IAM misconfig |
| **Big Data Component** | Custom K-V user-profile store | AWS S3 data lake + Glue metadata |
| **Records** | 3 B accounts | 100 M credit applications |
| **MTTD** | ~2 years | ~3 months (tip-off) |
| **Encryption** | Plaintext security Q&A | Partial; S3 was encrypted but readable via wildcard IAM |
| **Threat Actor** | State-sponsored APT | Insider (former AWS employee) |
| **Detection Trigger** | Internal investigation 2016 | Anonymous tip email |

**Key Contrast (2 marks):** Yahoo represents a **traditional perimeter + auth bypass** in a private data center; Capital One is a **cloud-native misconfiguration** exploit. The shift from perimeter-centric to identity-centric failure modes marks the evolution of Big Data threats.

#### (b) Discuss the **regulatory implications** of these two breaches and derive the **Total Cost of Breach (TCB)** using a representative formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Regulatory Implications (3.5 marks):**
- **Yahoo:** $35 M SEC fine (2018) for delayed disclosure; pre-GDPR, so no EU penalty, but became the empirical basis for GDPR Art. 33–34 mandatory notification clauses.
- **Capital One:** $190 M settlement (2022); OCC $80 M; CFPB $45 M; demonstrated that the **AWS Shared Responsibility Model** places the burden of IAM and WAF hardening squarely on the customer.

**TCB Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$\text{TCB} = C_{\text{detection}} + C_{\text{containment}} + C_{\text{notification}} + C_{\text{remediation}} + C_{\text{regulatory fines}}$$

For **Yahoo**, a reasonable estimate:
- $C_{\text{detection}} \approx \$30\text{M}$, $C_{\text{containment}} \approx \$50\text{M}$, $C_{\text{notification}} \approx \$20\text{M}$, $C_{\text{remediation}} \approx \$100\text{M}$, $C_{\text{fines}} \approx \$35\text{M}$.
- Total ≈ **$235 M** (excluding the $350 M Verizon discount).

For **Capital One**:
- $C_{\text{detection}} \approx \$15\text{M}$, $C_{\text{containment}} \approx \$30\text{M}$, $C_{\text{notification}} \approx \$25\text{M}$, $C_{\text{remediation}} \approx \$80\text{M}$, $C_{\text{fines}} \approx \$190\text{M}$.
- Total ≈ **$340 M**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in PECCT743 Case Study Questions:**
>
> 1. **Naming the breach without citing the Big Data component** (e.g., saying "Equifax was hacked" without mentioning Hadoop / HDFS / NoSQL). Always tie the breach to a **distributed data-layer technology**.
> 2. **Ignoring the CIA Triad linkage** — every case-study question expects a one-line mapping of which CIA pillar was violated.
> 3. **Confusing GDPR fines with settlement amounts** — $700 M Equifax settlement is *primarily US class-action + state AG*, **not** GDPR. UK ICO fine was £500 K.
> 4. **Forgetting the patch management timeline** — Struts CVE was disclosed in March 2017; Equifax was breached in May 2017. This 2-month gap is **the single most-graded fact** in the question.
> 5. **No mention of MTTD / MTTR metrics** — examiners award 2 marks for quantitative reasoning using the formulas.
> 6. **Skipping the cloud Shared Responsibility Model** in cloud-based cases (Capital One, Marriott) — this is mandatory under CO2.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** A Big Data security breach compromises CIA on a Volume / Velocity / Variety / Veracity / Value dataset.
- **Top 5 Case Studies (with key numbers):**
   - Yahoo — **3 B accounts** — cookie forging — $35 M SEC.
   - Equifax — **147 M records** — Struts CVE-2017-5638 — **$700 M** settlement.
   - Capital One — **100 M applications** — SSRF + IAM — **$190 M**.
   - Marriott — **500 M guests** — pre-acquisition RAT — **£18.4 M** GDPR.
   - Facebook/Cambridge Analytica — **87 M users** — Open Graph API — **$5 B** FTC.
- **Top 5 Attack Vectors:** Unpatched CVEs (Equifax), SSRF + IAM (Capital One), Cookie forging (Yahoo), API over-permission (Facebook), Pre-acquisition malware (Marriott).
- **Must-Know Formulas:** TCB, CPR, MTTD, MTTR, Encryption Coverage ($\varepsilon$).
- **Must-Know Frameworks:** AWS Shared Responsibility Model, MITRE ATT&CK, CIA Triad, GDPR Art. 33 (72-hour rule), DPDPA 2023 (India).
- **The 2-Month Patch Gap** is the most-tested fact for Equifax.
- **Cloud-Specific Failure Modes** — Capital One SSRF → IMDSv2 mandate.
- **Pre-GDPR vs Post-GDPR** — Yahoo was pre-GDPR; Equifax, Marriott, Capital One are post-GDPR.
- **Key-Management Rule** — encryption keys must NEVER be co-located with the encrypted data (Equifax, Marriott).
- **MTTD Industry Benchmark** ≈ 204 days (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authentication and Authorization: Kerberos, LDAP
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Authentication and Authorization: Kerberos, LDAP

# Authentication and Authorization: Kerberos, LDAP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Authentication vs. Authorization — The Foundational Distinction

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the twin pillars of **Access Control** are formally defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication** is the cryptographic process of *verifying the identity* of a principal (user, service, or device) attempting to access a system.
> **Authorization** is the policy-driven process of *determining what an already-authenticated principal is permitted to do* (read, write, execute, delete).

The relationship is **sequential and strict**: Authorization is *meaningless* unless Authentication has succeeded first.

| Concept | Question It Answers | Typical Mechanism | When It Happens |
|---|---|---|---|
| Authentication | "**Who are you?**" | Passwords, Kerberos tickets, Biometrics, PKI certificates | First step of every session |
| Authorization | "**What are you allowed to do?**" | ACLs, RBAC, LDAP directory entries, XACML | After successful authentication |
| Accounting (AAA) | "**What did you do?**" | Audit logs, Syslog | Continuous during session |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course requires mastery of two industry-grade protocols that implement these concepts at scale: **Kerberos** (the de-facto authentication protocol for Windows Active Directory and many Hadoop clusters) and **LDAP** (the de-facto directory and authorization protocol).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Model

Imagine you are boarding a flight (a metaphor Kerberos inventors at MIT explicitly used):

- **Authentication** = Showing your **passport and boarding pass** at the security checkpoint. The officer verifies (a) you are who you claim (passport photo) and (b) your ticket is valid (not expired, correct flight).
- **Authorization** = Your **boarding pass itself** — it tells the gate agent *which seat* you're allowed to sit in, *whether* you can access the lounge, and *which zones* of the plane you may enter.

You **cannot** show your boarding pass (authorization) before clearing security (authentication). The boarding pass is short-lived (like a Kerberos ticket), while your passport identity lasts longer.

### 1.3 Kerberos — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerberos** is a **symmetric-key**, **ticket-based**, **third-party trusted** authentication protocol developed at MIT (Project Athena, 1980s), named after the three-headed dog *Cerberus* that guards the underworld in Greek mythology. The three "heads" represent the **Client**, the **Server**, and the **Trusted Key Distribution Center (KDC)**.

Current production version: **Kerberos v5** (RFC 4120). It is the default authentication protocol of **Microsoft Active Directory** and is widely used in **Hadoop/YARN**, **HDFS**, and **Kerberized big data ecosystems** — making it essential knowledge for the PECCT743 course.

### 1.4 LDAP — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)** is an **open, vendor-neutral, TCP-based application-layer protocol** (RFC 4510 series) used to access and maintain **distributed directory information services** over an IP network. It is the *lightweight* (simplified, TCP/IP-friendly) successor to the heavyweight **X.500 DAP** protocol.

LDAP stores hierarchical, **read-mostly** identity data (users, groups, devices, permissions) optimized for **fast read** and **bulk search** — exactly what authorization decisions need at scale.

### 1.5 The AAA Triad and Where Kerberos + LDAP Fit

> [!NOTE]
> **AAA** stands for **Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting**. In modern enterprise and Big Data systems, **Kerberos handles Authentication** (ticket-based, cryptographically strong), and **LDAP handles Authorization** (group memberships, role attributes, access policies). Together they form the backbone of identity infrastructure in platforms like Active Directory, FreeIPA, and Kerberized Hadoop.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kerberos — Architectural Components

Kerberos' elegance lies in its **three-headed architecture**, each component performing one well-defined role:

### 2.1.1 The Key Distribution Center (KDC)

The KDC is a **single, trusted logical service** running (typically) on a domain controller. It is the *only* entity that holds the **master secret keys** of all principals. It is logically divided into three sub-services:

1. **Authentication Server (AS)** — Verifies user identity and issues a *Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT)*.
2. **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)** — Issues *service tickets* on presentation of a valid TGT.
3. **Kerberos Database (KDB)** — Stores principal identities and their long-term secret keys (typically encrypted with a *master key* derived from the KDC's *krbtgt* account password).

### 2.1.2 Realms and Principals

- A **Realm** is an administrative domain (e.g., `BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`). Conventionally uppercase.
- A **Principal** is a uniquely named entity. Examples:
  - User: `alice@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`
  - Service: `hdfs/hadoop-node1.bigdata.ktu.edu@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`
  - Host: `host/hadoop-node1.bigdata.ktu.edu@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`

### 2.1.3 The Two Types of Tickets

| Ticket Type | Abbreviation | Issued By | Purpose | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ticket-Granting Ticket** | TGT | Authentication Server (AS) | Proves identity to obtain service tickets | Typically **10–24 hours** (renewable) |
| **Service Ticket** | ST | Ticket-Granting Server (TGS) | Proves identity to access a specific service | Typically **a few minutes to 1 hour** |

### 2.1.4 The Session Key

A **session key** is a fresh **symmetric key** generated by the KDC for every Client–Server pair. It is:
- Generated by the KDC
- Encrypted with the Client's long-term key (sent to client)
- Embedded inside the service ticket (sent to client)
- Used by both Client and Server for that session's symmetric encryption (integrity + optional confidentiality)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Security Property:** The Client's long-term secret key is **never transmitted** over the network. It is used only to decrypt the AS-REP envelope, which contains the TGT and a session key.

## 2.2 Kerberos — The Six-Message Authentication Flow

Below is the **complete, end-to-end** Kerberos v5 exchange between a Client `C`, the AS, the TGS, and a target Service `S`. Memorize this — it is the **single most important diagram** for KTU's Big Data Security exam.

### Message 1: `AS-REQ` (Client → AS, **plaintext**)

The user logs in (e.g., enters password). The client derives $K_C$ (hash of password) and sends:

$$
\text{AS-REQ} = \{ ID_C, ID_{TGS}, \text{Nonce}_1, \text{Realm}_C, \text{AS-options} \}
$$

> **Why plaintext?** Because the user has not yet proven identity. The AS will issue a TGT *blindly*; only the real user can decrypt it because only the real user knows $K_C$.

### Message 2: `AS-REP` (AS → Client, **partially encrypted**)

The AS generates:
- A fresh **Client–TGS session key** $K_{C,TGS}$
- A **TGT** containing $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted with the TGS's secret key $K_{TGS}$

It responds with two parts:

$$
\text{AS-REP} = \{ \underbrace{K_{C,TGS}}_{\text{encrypted with }K_C}, \ \underbrace{TGT = \{ K_{C,TGS}, ID_C, \text{AD}_C, \text{Nonce}_1, \text{Lifetime}, \text{Realm}_C \}}_{K_{TGS}} \}
$$

> The client uses $K_C$ to extract $K_{C,TGS}$. A wrong password → garbled data → authentication fails locally *without* ever contacting the KDC again (avoids a known plaintext attack on the password).

### Message 3: `TGS-REQ` (Client → TGS, **partially encrypted**)

When the client wants to access service `S`, it sends:

$$
\text{TGS-REQ} = \{ ID_S, \text{Nonce}_2, \text{Lifetime}_2, TGT, \underbrace{Authenticator_1}_{K_{C,TGS}} \}
$$

where

$$
\text{Authenticator}_1 = \{ ID_C, \text{Realm}_C, TS_1 \}_{K_{C,TGS}}
$$

### Message 4: `TGS-REP` (TGS → Client, **partially encrypted**)

The TGS decrypts the TGT (using $K_{TGS}$), recovers $K_{C,TGS}$, uses it to verify the Authenticator, then issues a **Service Ticket** and a **Client–Server session key** $K_{C,S}$:

$$
\text{TGS-REP} = \{ \underbrace{K_{C,S}}_{K_{C,TGS}}, \underbrace{ST = \{ K_{C,S}, ID_C, \text{AD}_C, \text{Nonce}_2, \text{Lifetime}_3, \text{Realm}_C, ID_S \}}_{K_S} \}
$$

### Message 5: `AP-REQ` (Client → Service, **partially encrypted**)

$$
\text{AP-REQ} = \{ ST, \underbrace{Authenticator_2}_{K_{C,S}} \}, \quad \text{Authenticator}_2 = \{ ID_C, \text{Realm}_C, TS_2 \}_{K_{C,S}}
$$

### Message 6: `AP-REP` (Service → Client, **optional, encrypted**) — Mutual Authentication

$$
\text{AP-REP} = \{ TS_2 + 1 \}_{K_{C,S}}
$$

The service proves it knows $K_{C,S}$ by incrementing the client's timestamp. The client is now **mutually assured** of the service's identity — preventing **fake server attacks**.

## 2.3 Kerberos — Cryptographic Notations and Their Meanings

| Notation | Meaning | Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| $K_X$ | Long-term secret key of principal $X$ | Symmetric, derived from password |
| $K_{X,Y}$ | Session key shared between $X$ and $Y$ | Symmetric, fresh per session |
| $\{M\}_K$ | Message $M$ encrypted with key $K$ | Symmetric encryption (typically AES-256-CTS) |
| $TS_i$ | Timestamp $i$ (to prevent replay) | Within a clock-skew window (default $\pm$ **5 minutes**) |
| $\text{Nonce}_i$ | A random number $i$ | Defeats replay attacks |
| $\text{AD}_X$ | Authorization Data for $X$ | Group memberships, flags |

## 2.4 LDAP — Structural and Operational Theory

### 2.4.1 The Directory Information Tree (DIT)

LDAP organizes data as a **hierarchical tree** called the **Directory Information Tree (DIT)**. Each node is an **entry**.

A typical enterprise DIT for `bigdata.ktu.edu`:

```
dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu  (root)
├── ou=People
│    ├── uid=alice,...
│    ├── uid=bob,...
│    └── uid=carol,...
├── ou=Groups
│    ├── cn=hadoop-admins
│    ├── cn=hadoop-users
│    └── cn=data-scientists
└── ou=Services
     └── cn=hdfs,ou=Services,...
```

### 2.4.2 Distinguished Name (DN) and Relative Distinguished Name (RDN)

The **DN** uniquely identifies an entry as the *path from the leaf to the root*, written right-to-left:

$$
\text{DN} = \text{RDN}_1, \text{RDN}_2, \dots, \text{RDN}_n
$$

Example for Alice:

$$
\text{DN(Alice)} = \texttt{uid=alice,ou=People,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu}
$$

Each comma-separated piece is an **RDN** (`uid=alice`, `ou=People`, `dc=ktu`, etc.).

### 2.4.3 Entries, Attributes, and ObjectClasses

Each **entry** is a collection of **attribute–value pairs**. Attributes have:
- A **name** (e.g., `cn`, `mail`, `uid`)
- A **syntax** (e.g., `IA5String`, `Integer`, `Binary`)
- A set of permitted values

**ObjectClasses** are schema templates that determine *which attributes an entry must (MUST) and may (MAY) have*.

| ObjectClass | Type | Required Attributes | Common Used Attributes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `inetOrgPerson` | Structural | `cn`, `sn` | `uid`, `mail`, `telephoneNumber`, `userPassword` |
| `posixAccount` | Structural | `cn`, `uid`, `uidNumber`, `gidNumber`, `homeDirectory` | `loginShell`, `gecos` |
| `groupOfNames` | Structural | `cn`, `member` | `description`, `seeAlso` |
| `organizationalUnit` | Structural | `ou` | `description`, `businessCategory` |
| `domain` | Structural | `dc` | — |

### 2.4.4 LDAP Operations (the Protocol's Verb Set)

| Operation | RFC | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Bind** | 4513 | Authenticate (simple bind = DN+password; SASL = Kerberos!) |
| **Search** | 4511 | Query the DIT with filters and scopes |
| **Compare** | 4511 | Test whether an attribute has a specific value |
| **Add** | 4511 | Insert a new entry |
| **Delete** | 4511 | Remove an entry |
| **Modify** | 4511 | Change attribute values |
| **ModifyDN** | 4511 | Rename/move an entry |
| **Unbind** | 4511 | Gracefully end the session |
| **Abandon** | 4511 | Cancel an in-progress operation |

### 2.4.5 LDAP Search Filters — The Heart of Authorization Queries

A **search filter** is an LDAP-specific boolean expression. The **Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) Filter Grammar (RFC 4515)** defines:

- **Presence**: `(objectClass=*)` — any entry
- **Equality**: `(uid=alice)`
- **Substring**: `(cn=Al*ce)` — wildcards
- **Greater/Lesser**: `(age>=18)`
- **AND**: `(&(filter1)(filter2)...)`
- **OR**: `(|(filter1)(filter2)...)`
- **NOT**: `(!(filter))`

**Example**: Find all members of the `hadoop-admins` group whose email ends with `@bigdata.ktu.edu`:

$$
\texttt{(&(memberOf=cn=hadoop-admins,ou=Groups,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu)(mail=*@bigdata.ktu.edu))}
$$

### 2.4.6 LDAP Authentication Mechanisms

LDAP supports several **bind** mechanisms, and the choice is critical for Big Data security:

| Mechanism | Security Level | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Anonymous Bind** | None (NULL) | Default; never use in production |
| **Unauthenticated Bind** | None | Returns `success`; deprecated |
| **Simple Bind** (plaintext password) | Low | Rejected by default in modern servers (LDAPS/TLS) |
| **Simple Bind over LDAPS** (TLS) | Medium | Encrypted in transit; vulnerable to replay |
| **SASL Bind (GSSAPI/Kerberos)** | High | **Industry standard**; integrates with Active Directory / MIT Kerberos |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The combination of **Kerberos + LDAP (via SASL/GSSAPI)** is *exactly* what Microsoft Active Directory, FreeIPA, and Red Hat Identity Management use. This is the **production-grade identity stack** for Kerberized Hadoop clusters (HDFS, YARN, Spark, Hive, HBase).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $K_X$ | Long-term symmetric secret of principal $X$ | Bits (e.g., **AES-256 = 256 bits**) |
| $K_{X,Y}$ | Fresh symmetric session key between $X$ and $Y$ | Bits |
| $\{M\}_K$ | Symmetric encryption of message $M$ with key $K$ | Ciphertext |
| $\Delta t_{\text{skew}}$ | Maximum allowed clock skew | Default **300 s = 5 min** |
| $L_{TGT}$ | TGT lifetime | Typically **10 h** (renewable for 7 days) |
| $L_{ST}$ | Service ticket lifetime | Typically **1 h** or less |
| $\text{DN} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{RDN}_i$ | Distinguished Name = ordered RDN sequence | UTF-8 string |
| $d_{\text{entry}} = \log_2 N_e$ | Information content of a directory of $N_e$ entries | Bits |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hadoop YARN/HDFS** uses Kerberos for `hdfs/hadoop-node1@REALM` service principal authentication and LDAP for user/group lookups during authorization (`hadoop.security.group.mapping.ldap.*`).
- **Active Directory** = Microsoft's commercial implementation of **Kerberos v5 + LDAP v3** + Group Policy.
- **FreeIPA** = open-source cross-domain identity manager for Linux, combining MIT Kerberos + 389 Directory Server (LDAP) + Dogtag CA + SSSD.
- **Apache Kafka**, **Spark on Kubernetes**, and **Presto** all support Kerberos-based authentication with LDAP-backed authorization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world implication for Big Data:** Without Kerberos + LDAP, Hadoop is famously known as *Hadoop's "no security" default* — every user could impersonate every other user. Kerberizing Hadoop (introduced in CDH4/Hadoop 2.x) is what made multi-tenant enterprise deployments viable.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Why Kerberos Uses Symmetric Keys — The Cryptographic Reasoning

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Minimum Key Length from Brute-Force Work Factor

Suppose an attacker attempts an **exhaustive key search** against a Kerberos session key $K_{C,S}$ of length $n$ bits. The probability of guessing $K_{C,S}$ correctly in a single attempt is:

$$
P_{\text{guess}} = \frac{1}{2^n}
$$

If the attacker can perform $N$ guesses per second, the **expected time-to-break** is:

$$
T_{\text{break}} = \frac{2^n}{2 \cdot N} \quad \text{seconds (mean of geometric distribution)}
$$

The factor of $2$ in the denominator reflects the average number of trials needed to find a match.

**Step-by-step application** for AES-128 ($n = 128$, $N = 10^{12}$ guesses/s on a GPU cluster):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{break}} &= \frac{2^{128}}{2 \cdot 10^{12}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.4028 \times 10^{38}}{2 \times 10^{12}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.7014 \times 10^{26} \text{ seconds} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1.7014 \times 10^{26}}{3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \text{ years} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.4 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering conclusion:** Kerberos' use of AES-128/256 makes brute force computationally infeasible. The protocol's *real* vulnerabilities are protocol-level (replay, KDC compromise) — not key-length.

## 3.2 The Replay-Attack Defense — Mathematical Justification

### 3.2.1 Timestamp Window

Kerberos rejects an authenticator whose timestamp $TS$ differs from the KDC's local clock by more than $\Delta t_{\text{skew}}$. The probability that a replayed message is still accepted is:

$$
P_{\text{replay}} = \frac{\Delta t_{\text{skew}}}{T_{\text{validity}}}
$$

With $\Delta t_{\text{skew}} = 300$ s and $T_{\text{validity}} = 36000$ s (10 h TGT):

$$
P_{\text{replay}} = \frac{300}{36000} = \frac{1}{120} \approx 0.83\%
$$

**This is why Kerberos requires synchronized clocks** (via NTP) across all hosts.

### 3.2.2 Authenticator Uniqueness

The TGS keeps a **replay cache** of recently seen (Client ID, Client address, timestamp) tuples for the cache lifetime. The cache hit rate $R$ as a function of cache size $C$ and request rate $\lambda$ is given by the standard caching result:

$$
R(C) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t_{cache}/C}
$$

This is a **first-order derivation** showing why the replay cache prevents repeat attacks within the validity window.

## 3.3 LDAP Search Cost Model — How Directory Size Affects Authorization Latency

### 3.3.1 Linear-Scan (No Index) Cost

For a directory of $N_e$ entries with no index, a search with a filter matching $M$ entries costs:

$$
T_{\text{no-index}} = O(N_e)
$$

### 3.3.2 Indexed Cost

With a B+ tree index on the filter attribute, the cost becomes:

$$
T_{\text{indexed}} = O(\log_B N_e) + O(M \cdot t_{\text{disk}})
$$

where $B$ is the B+ tree branching factor and $t_{\text{disk}}$ is a single disk access time.

**Numerical example**: $N_e = 10^6$ entries, $B = 100$, $M = 50$, $t_{\text{disk}} = 5$ ms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{indexed}} &= \log_{100}(10^6) \cdot t_{\text{seek}} + 50 \times 5 \text{ ms} \\
&= 3 \times 0.01 \text{ s} + 0.25 \text{ s} \\
&\approx 0.28 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is why **indexing LDAP attributes** (`uid`, `mail`, `memberOf`) is a critical tuning step in production Big Data deployments.

## 3.4 The Mutual-Authentication Handshake — Algebraic Proof of Correctness

To prove Message 6 (`AP-REP`) successfully authenticates the Service `S` to the Client `C`:

**Step 1** — The Client `C` constructs $\text{Authenticator}_2 = \{ ID_C, \text{Realm}_C, TS_2 \}_{K_{C,S}}$ and sends it with the Service Ticket $ST$ to `S`.

**Step 2** — Service `S` decrypts $ST$ using its long-term key $K_S$, recovers $K_{C,S}$, decrypts $\text{Authenticator}_2$, and verifies $ID_C$ matches the one inside $ST$. If they match, `S` knows $K_{C,S}$ is genuine.

**Step 3** — `S` computes $TS_2 + 1$, encrypts it with $K_{C,S}$, and sends it as `AP-REP`.

**Step 4** — `C` decrypts the `AP-REP` using $K_{C,S}$ (which `C` itself received in the TGS-REP envelope encrypted with $K_{C,TGS}$). If decryption yields $TS_2 + 1$, then `C` is convinced that **only `S`** could have performed the decryption-and-increment — because only `S` knows $K_S$ to recover $K_{C,S}$ from $ST$.

**Therefore**, the AP-REP exchange completes **mutual authentication**: $C \leftrightarrow S$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Kerberos vs. LDAP

| Criterion | Kerberos | LDAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Authentication (verify identity) | Authorization / Directory lookup (find permissions, groups) |
| **Cryptography** | Symmetric key (AES, 3DES, RC4 in legacy) | Mostly plaintext, but SASL/GSSAPI enables Kerberos binding |
| **Trust Model** | Centralized (KDC is single point of trust) | Decentralized (multi-master replication possible) |
| **Statefulness** | Stateful (tickets, replay cache) | Stateless for queries, stateful for connection |
| **Default Port** | 88 (UDP/TCP) | 389 (LDAP), 636 (LDAPS) |
| **Query Model** | Fixed message exchange (AS-REQ → TGS-REQ → AP-REQ) | Arbitrary filters and scopes (subtree, one-level, base) |
| **Data Layout** | Tickets, principals, realms | DIT — hierarchical tree of entries |
| **Primary RFC** | RFC 4120 (v5) | RFC 4510–4519 (v3) |
| **Strengths** | Strong crypto, mutual auth, no password over wire | Fast read, distributed replication, fine-grained search |
| **Weaknesses** | Single KDC failure = system down (mitigated by replication), clock dependency | Plaintext by default, complex schema management |
| **Big Data Usage** | Hadoop YARN/HDFS, Spark, Kafka, Hive | User/group lookups, POSIX UID/GID resolution, group mapping |

## 3.6 LDAP Bind Operation — Derivation of Security Properties

A **Simple Bind** sends the password in cleartext. TLS upgrades the channel. The security levels can be **ordered** as:

$$
S_{\text{Anon}} < S_{\text{Unauth}} < S_{\text{Simple}} < S_{\text{LDAPS}} < S_{\text{StartTLS}} < S_{\text{SASL-GSSAPI}}
$$

**Derivation rationale**:

- $S_{\text{Anon}}$: No identity asserted; zero security.
- $S_{\text{Unauth}}$: RFC 4513 §5.1.2 forbids it on production servers.
- $S_{\text{Simple}}$: Password is base64-encoded (not encrypted); trivially recoverable.
- $S_{\text{LDAPS}}$: TLS v1.2+ provides confidentiality and integrity; protects against passive eavesdropping only.
- $S_{\text{StartTLS}}$: Upgrades a plaintext LDAP connection to TLS using the StartTLS extended operation (RFC 4511 §4.14).
- $S_{\text{SASL-GSSAPI}}$: Uses Kerberos' mutual-authenticated, ticket-based cryptography — the strongest option.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** SASL = *Simple Authentication and Security Layer* (RFC 4422). GSSAPI = *Generic Security Service Application Program Interface* (RFC 2744). Kerberos V5 = the canonical GSSAPI mechanism.

## 3.7 Real-World Example — Kerberos + LDAP in a Hadoop Cluster

Suppose user `alice` wants to run a Spark job on `hadoop-node1.bigdata.ktu.edu`.

**Step 1** — Alice runs `kinit alice@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`, entering her password. Her client obtains a TGT from the KDC (port 88).

**Step 2** — Alice runs `spark-submit --master yarn ...`. Her Spark client sends an `AP-REQ` to YARN's ResourceManager (`rm/hadoop-node1.bigdata.ktu.edu@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU`), presenting a service ticket.

**Step 3** — YARN's ResourceManager authenticates Alice. To determine her **group memberships** (needed for YARN queue authorization), YARN issues an LDAP search:

$$
\texttt{base DN = ou=People,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu, \ filter = (\&(uid=alice)(objectClass=inetOrgPerson))}
$$

**Step 4** — YARN reads the `memberOf` attribute to learn Alice is in `cn=hadoop-users,ou=Groups,...` and `cn=data-scientists,ou=Groups,...`.

**Step 5** — YARN checks its queue ACLs and admits Alice's job to the `data_science` queue.

> This 5-step sequence is the canonical **Kerberos-then-LDAP** identity flow that governs **every Big Data authorization decision** in enterprise Hadoop.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kerberos v5 — Full Six-Message Authentication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client (Alice)
    participant AS as Authentication Server
    participant TGS as Ticket-Granting Server
    participant S as Target Service (HDFS)

    C->>AS: 1. AS-REQ: ID_C, ID_TGS, Nonce_1, Realm, Options
    Note over AS: AS verifies ID_C exists in KDB
    AS-->>C: 2. AS-REP: {K_C_TGS}K_C, TGT
    Note over C: Decrypts with K_C (password-derived)
    C->>TGS: 3. TGS-REQ: ID_S, Nonce_2, TGT, {Auth_1}K_C_TGS
    Note over TGS: Validates TGT, Auth_1, checks replay cache
    TGS-->>C: 4. TGS-REP: {K_C_S}K_C_TGS, {ST}K_S
    Note over C: Stores Service Ticket ST
    C->>S: 5. AP-REQ: ST, {Auth_2}K_C_S
    Note over S: Decrypts ST with K_S, recovers K_C_S
    S-->>C: 6. AP-REP: {TS_2 + 1}K_C_S
    Note over C: Mutual authentication confirmed
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Steps 1–2: Authentication (proving identity to KDC).
- Steps 3–4: Authorization at the KDC level (granting access to a *specific* service).
- Steps 5–6: Mutual authentication between Client and Service.

## 4.2 KDC Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KDC["Key Distribution Center (KDC)"]
        direction TB
        AS["Authentication Server (AS)"]
        TGS["Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)"]
        KDB[("Kerberos Database (KDB)\nStores principals and master keys")]
    end

    KDB -.lookup.-> AS
    KDB -.lookup.-> TGS

    C1[Client Principal alice] -- AS-REQ --> AS
    AS -- TGT (encrypted) --> C1
    C1 -- TGS-REQ --> TGS
    TGS -- Service Ticket --> C1
    C1 -- AP-REQ --> SV[Service: hdfs-node1]
    SV -.->|validates| TGS
```

## 4.3 LDAP Directory Information Tree (DIT) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu (root)"]
    PPL["ou=People"]
    GRP["ou=Groups"]
    SVC["ou=Services"]

    ROOT --> PPL
    ROOT --> GRP
    ROOT --> SVC

    PPL --> ALC["uid=alice\ninetOrgPerson"]
    PPL --> BOB["uid=bob\ninetOrgPerson"]
    PPL --> CAR["uid=carol\ninetOrgPerson"]

    GRP --> ADM["cn=hadoop-admins\ngroupOfNames"]
    GRP --> USR["cn=hadoop-users\ngroupOfNames"]
    GRP --> DSC["cn=data-scientists\ngroupOfNames"]

    SVC --> HDFS["cn=hdfs\napplicationProcess"]

    ALC -.member.-> ADM
    BOB -.member.-> USR
    CAR -.member.-> DSC
    CAR -.member.-> USR
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Solid arrows: hierarchy (parent → child entries).
- Dashed arrows: `member` attribute references (group membership).
- Each leaf is an LDAP **entry** with a **DN** that includes all ancestor RDNs.

## 4.4 Integration View — Kerberos + LDAP in Big Data

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Alice]
    K[KDC / Kerberos Server\nPort 88]
    L[LDAP Directory Server\nPort 389 / 636]
    RM[YARN ResourceManager]
    NM[NodeManager Worker]
    H[HDFS NameNode]

    U -- 1. kinit: AS-REQ --> K
    K -- 2. TGT --> U
    U -- 3. AP-REQ service ticket --> RM
    RM -- 4. LDAP search: memberOf --> L
    L -- 5. group memberships --> RM
    RM -- 6. AP-REQ delegation ticket --> NM
    NM -- 7. AP-REQ service ticket --> H
    H -- 8. AP-REP mutual auth --> NM
```

This is the **production identity flow** for a Kerberized Hadoop cluster.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization. Give one example of each from the Kerberos/LDAP context.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Authentication** is the process of *verifying the claimed identity* of a principal (user, service, or host) by checking a cryptographic credential. In Kerberos, authentication is performed by exchanging tickets with the KDC — for example, when a user runs `kinit`, the AS-REQ/AS-REP exchange authenticates the user to the realm.
>
> **Authorization** is the process of *deciding what an already-authenticated principal is permitted to do*. In LDAP, authorization is enforced by querying the directory for group memberships and access control lists — for example, a YARN ResourceManager searches the LDAP DIT to verify that user `alice` belongs to `cn=data-scientists` before admitting her job to the `data_science` queue.
>
> The two are sequentially dependent: **authentication must precede authorization**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the KDC in Kerberos? Why is it considered both a strength and a weakness?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** is the **trusted third party** that holds the long-term secret keys of all principals in a realm. It consists of the **Authentication Server (AS)**, the **Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)**, and the **Kerberos Database (KDB)**. The KDC issues Ticket-Granting Tickets (TGTs) to authenticated users and Service Tickets (STs) to authorized clients, eliminating the need to share long-term keys between arbitrary principals.
>
> **Strength:** Centralized key management prevents the $O(n^2)$ pairwise-key-distribution problem — only $n$ master keys must be managed, not $n^2$.
>
> **Weakness:** The KDC is a **single point of failure** — if it crashes, no one can authenticate. It is also a high-value target: compromise of the `krbtgt` account key allows an attacker to mint arbitrary TGTs. Mitigations include KDC replication, multi-master setups, and regular `krbtgt` password rotation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the complete Kerberos v5 authentication protocol with a neat sequence diagram showing all six messages exchanged between the Client, Authentication Server (AS), Ticket-Granting Server (TGS), and the target Service. For each message, state which keys are used for encryption and which keys are transmitted.**

**Model Solution:**

The Kerberos v5 protocol proceeds in **three logical phases**, comprising **six messages**. Let Client = `C`, AS = authentication server, TGS = ticket-granting server, Service = `S`, $K_X$ = long-term key of $X$, $K_{X,Y}$ = session key between $X$ and $Y$, and $\{M\}_K$ = message $M$ encrypted with key $K$.

**Phase 1 — Authentication (AS Exchange, Messages 1–2):**

- **Message 1 (`AS-REQ`):** `C → AS` sends `$\{ID_C, ID_{TGS}, \text{Nonce}_1, \text{Realm}_C, \text{Options}\}$` *in plaintext*. No encryption is used (the client has not yet proven identity).
- **Message 2 (`AS-REP`):** AS generates a fresh **Client–TGS session key** $K_{C,TGS}$ and a **TGT** containing $K_{C,TGS}$ encrypted with the TGS's long-term key $K_{TGS}$. Response = `$\{\{K_{C,TGS}\}_{K_C}, \{TGT\}_{K_{TGS}}\}$`. The first part is encrypted with the client's long-term key $K_C$ (derived from her password); the second is the TGT.

**Phase 2 — Ticket Granting (TGS Exchange, Messages 3–4):**

- **Message 3 (`TGS-REQ`):** `C → TGS` sends `$\{ID_S, \text{Nonce}_2, \text{Lifetime}_2, TGT, \{Authenticator_1\}_{K_{C,TGS}}\}$`. The Authenticator $1 = \{ID_C, \text{Realm}_C, TS_1\}_{K_{C,TGS}}$ proves the client knows $K_{C,TGS}$.
- **Message 4 (`TGS-REP`):** TGS decrypts TGT with $K_{TGS}$, recovers $K_{C,TGS}$, verifies the Authenticator, and generates a fresh **Client–Server session key** $K_{C,S}$. Response = `$\{\{K_{C,S}\}_{K_{C,TGS}}, \{ST\}_{K_S}\}$` where $ST = \{K_{C,S}, ID_C, AD_C, \text{Nonce}_2, \text{Lifetime}_3, \text{Realm}_C, ID_S\}_{K_S}$.

**Phase 3 — Service Access (AP Exchange, Messages 5–6):**

- **Message 5 (`AP-REQ`):** `C → S` sends `$\{ST, \{Authenticator_2\}_{K_{C,S}}\}$`. The Service decrypts the TGT envelope with $K_S$, recovers $K_{C,S}$, and validates the Authenticator's timestamp against its replay cache.
- **Message 6 (`AP-REP`):** `S → C` returns `$\{TS_2 + 1\}_{K_{C,S}}$`. The Client verifies that only the genuine Service could have performed this encryption, achieving **mutual authentication**.

**Diagrammatic summary** (see Section 4.1 above for the full Mermaid diagram).

**Key transmission table:**

| Message | Key Used to Encrypt | Key Transmitted Inside |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | None (plaintext) | None |
| 2 | $K_C$, $K_{TGS}$ | $K_{C,TGS}$ |
| 3 | $K_{C,TGS}$ (Authenticator only) | None |
| 4 | $K_{C,TGS}$, $K_S$ | $K_{C,S}$ |
| 5 | $K_{C,S}$ (Authenticator only) | None |
| 6 | $K_{C,S}$ | None |

**Key principle:** *The Client's long-term key $K_C$ is **never** transmitted over the network.*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the Kerberos ticket structure and explain how timestamps and nonces provide replay-attack resistance. Justify mathematically why the default clock-skew tolerance of 300 seconds gives approximately 0.83% replay-acceptance probability for a 10-hour TGT.**

**Model Solution:**

**Ticket Structure:** A Kerberos v5 ticket is an ASN.1-encoded structure with the following key fields: `tkt-vno` (version), `realm`, `sname` (service principal), `flags` (renewable, forwardable, etc.), `key` (session key $K_{C,S}$), `cname` and `crealm` (client identity), `transited` (cross-realm path), `authtime` (initial authentication time), `starttime`, `endtime`, `renew-till`, and `caddr` (client IP addresses). The entire ticket is sealed by encrypting it with the target service's long-term key $K_S$ so that only `S` can read its contents.

**Nonce Defense:** The client includes a random **nonce** $N_1$ in `AS-REQ` and $N_2$ in `TGS-REQ`. The KDC echoes the nonce back inside the encrypted response. This binds the response to a *specific request* — an attacker cannot replay an old `AS-REP` because the nonce won't match. A nonce is **single-use** and provides protection for the entire ticket lifetime, not just the clock-skew window.

**Timestamp Defense:** Every Authenticator contains a current timestamp $TS$, and the verifier requires $|TS_{\text{received}} - TS_{\text{local}}| \le \Delta t_{\text{skew}}$. Replayed messages with stale timestamps are rejected. The verifier also stores a **replay cache** of recently seen (Client ID, Client IP, Timestamp) tuples for the validity window.

**Mathematical Justification of the Replay Probability:**

A replayed authenticator remains *cryptographically valid* for as long as the original ticket is valid, $T_{\text{validity}}$. However, the verifier accepts it only if the attacker's clock-skew deviation falls within $\Delta t_{\text{skew}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{replay}} &= \frac{\Delta t_{\text{skew}}}{T_{\text{validity}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{300 \text{ seconds}}{10 \text{ hours}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{300}{36000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{120} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.00833 \text{ or } 0.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combined defense:** Nonces defeat *protocol-level* replays (a one-shot protection valid for the ticket lifetime), while timestamps defeat *authenticator-level* replays (a narrow-window protection). Together they form a robust defense — which is exactly why **clock synchronization via NTP** is mandatory in any Kerberos deployment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the architecture of LDAP. With a neat diagram, describe the Directory Information Tree (DIT) and the concept of Distinguished Name (DN) and Relative Distinguished Name (RDN). Illustrate with an example entry for user "alice" in the domain `bigdata.ktu.edu`.**

**Model Solution:**

**LDAP Architecture:** LDAP is a client–server protocol for accessing **directory services** — specialized databases optimized for **read-heavy, write-rare, search-intensive** workloads. An LDAP deployment consists of:
- **Directory Server (DSA — Directory System Agent)**: stores entries; can replicate to peers.
- **Directory Client (DUA — Directory User Agent)**: issues Bind, Search, Modify operations.
- **Schema**: defines object classes and attribute syntaxes.
- **Referrals**: when a server holds a subtree pointer but not the actual data, it returns a referral to the client.

**Directory Information Tree (DIT):** Entries are organized as a **rooted tree**, with the root typically named after the DNS domain (using `dc=` components). Each node is an **entry**, and each entry has exactly one **parent** (except the root). Entries are referenced by their **Distinguished Name (DN)**, which is a unique path from the leaf to the root.

**Distinguished Name (DN):** A DN is an ordered sequence of `key=value` pairs called **Relative Distinguished Names (RDNs)**, written right-to-left from the root:

$$
\text{DN} = \text{RDN}_1, \text{RDN}_2, \dots, \text{RDN}_n
$$

**Example:** For user Alice in `bigdata.ktu.edu`:

$$
\text{DN(Alice)} = \texttt{uid=alice,ou=People,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu}
$$

Breaking it down:

| RDN | Component | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `uid=alice` | Leaf RDN | Unique identifier for Alice |
| `ou=People` | Intermediate RDN | Organizational unit containing user accounts |
| `dc=bigdata` | Domain component | Sub-domain of the enterprise |
| `dc=ktu` | Domain component | Parent domain |
| `dc=edu` | Root-level RDN | Top-level domain |

**Attributes of the entry (illustrative):**

| Attribute | Value | Syntax |
|---|---|---|
| `objectClass` | `inetOrgPerson`, `posixAccount` | OID |
| `uid` | `alice` | IA5String |
| `cn` | `Alice Smith` | UTF-8 |
| `sn` | `Smith` | UTF-8 |
| `mail` | `alice@bigdata.ktu.edu` | UTF-8 |
| `uidNumber` | `10001` | Integer |
| `gidNumber` | `20001` | Integer |
| `homeDirectory` | `/home/alice` | UTF-8 |
| `loginShell` | `/bin/bash` | UTF-8 |
| `memberOf` | `cn=hadoop-users,ou=Groups,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu` | DN |

**Diagrammatic view** of the DIT is provided in **Section 4.3** (Mermaid tree diagram).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: List and explain the LDAP protocol operations. How does LDAP integrate with Kerberos in enterprise Big Data environments like Hadoop? Provide the SASL/GSSAPI bind sequence.**

**Model Solution:**

**LDAP Protocol Operations (RFC 4511):**

1. **Bind** — Establishes the client's identity and starts a session. Supports anonymous, simple (DN + password), and SASL (e.g., GSSAPI/Kerberos) mechanisms.
2. **Search** — Queries the DIT with a base DN, scope (`base`, `one`, `sub`), filter, and attribute list.
3. **Compare** — Tests whether a specific attribute has a given value; returns boolean.
4. **Add** — Inserts a new entry; the parent must already exist and the entry's object classes must allow the supplied attributes.
5. **Delete** — Removes an entry; the entry must have no children (unless a subtree delete is performed).
6. **Modify** — Alters attribute values via add/replace/delete operations.
7. **ModifyDN** — Renames or moves an entry within the DIT.
8. **Unbind** — Gracefully terminates a session.
9. **Abandon** — Cancels an outstanding operation.

**Integration with Kerberos in Hadoop — The SASL/GSSAPI Bind Sequence:**

In a **Kerberized Hadoop cluster** with LDAP-backed authorization (e.g., Cloudera/HDP, EMR security configuration), the sequence is:

**Step 1** — The user runs `kinit alice@BIGDATA.KTU.EDU` and enters her password. Her client obtains a TGT from the KDC.

**Step 2** — When the Hadoop service (e.g., YARN ResourceManager) needs to authenticate the user to LDAP, it performs a **SASL Bind with GSSAPI mechanism**:

$$
\texttt{ldapsearch -Y GSSAPI -U alice -b "dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu" "(uid=alice)"}
$$

**Step 3** — The LDAP client and server perform a Kerberos **AP-REQ/AP-REP** exchange (using the TGT and a service ticket for the LDAP server principal `ldap/ldap.bigdata.ktu.edu`). No password is sent over the wire.

**Step 4** — Upon successful SASL Bind, the LDAP client issues a **Search** request, e.g.:

$$
\texttt{base: ou=People,dc=bigdata,dc=ktu,dc=edu}
$$
$$
\texttt{filter: (\&(uid=alice)(objectClass=inetOrgPerson))}
$$
$$
\texttt{requested attributes: memberOf, mail, uid, gidNumber}
$$

**Step 5** — LDAP returns the `memberOf` attribute values (group DNs), which the YARN scheduler uses to determine **queue authorization** (e.g., `cn=data-scientists → data_science queue`).

**Why this integration matters for Big Data:**

- **Single sign-on (SSO):** Authenticate once to Kerberos, then use the same credentials implicitly across LDAP, HDFS, YARN, Hive, Impala, Spark, and Kafka.
- **Centralized identity:** A new employee is added once in LDAP; Hadoop, Linux, and Active Directory all see her.
- **Strong crypto:** Passwords never traverse the network, eliminating credential-sniffing attacks on the data plane.
- **Audit trail:** Kerberos ticket requests are logged centrally for compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake students make is describing LDAP as an "authentication protocol." It is **primarily a directory-access protocol** — authentication is *one of its bind mechanisms*. Furthermore, the default **Simple Bind sends the password in cleartext** (base64-encoded, not encrypted). Always state the distinction between authentication (Kerberos) and authorization (LDAP) clearly. Marks are routinely lost for confusing the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Authentication and Authorization: Kerberos, LDAP"**

### Kerberos Essentials
- ✅ **Kerberos = symmetric-key, ticket-based, third-party-trusted authentication protocol** (MIT, 1980s; current: v5, RFC 4120).
- ✅ **Three heads = Client, Server, KDC** (Authentication Server + Ticket-Granting Server + Kerberos Database).
- ✅ **Six messages in three phases**: AS-REQ/AS-REP → TGS-REQ/TGS-REP → AP-REQ/AP-REP.
- ✅ **TGT** (Ticket-Granting Ticket) is issued by AS; lifetime **~10 hours** (renewable up to 7 days).
- ✅ **Service Ticket (ST)** is issued by TGS; lifetime **~1 hour**.
- ✅ **Client's long-term key $K_C$ is NEVER transmitted** — only used to decrypt AS-REP.
- ✅ **Session keys** $K_{C,TGS}$ and $K_{C,S}$ are fresh, single-purpose symmetric keys.
- ✅ **Mutual authentication** is achieved by AP-REP = $\{TS_2 + 1\}_{K_{C,S}}$.
- ✅ **Replay defense**: timestamps (clock-skew window, default **5 min**) + nonces (one-shot) + replay cache.
- ✅ **KDC = single point of failure** — mitigated by replication and `krbtgt` rotation.
- ✅ **Big Data usage**: HDFS, YARN, Hive, Spark, Kafka, HBase — all support Kerberos.

### LDAP Essentials
- ✅ **LDAP = Lightweight Directory Access Protocol** (RFC 4510 series) — directory access protocol, not authentication protocol.
- ✅ **DIT (Directory Information Tree)**: hierarchical tree of entries.
- ✅ **DN (Distinguished Name)** = ordered RDN sequence, unique per entry; written right-to-left.
- ✅ **ObjectClasses** (`inetOrgPerson`, `posixAccount`, `groupOfNames`, `domain`) define attribute schema.
- ✅ **Operations**: Bind, Search, Compare, Add, Delete, Modify, ModifyDN, Unbind, Abandon.
- ✅ **Search filters** (RFC 4515): `(attr=value)`, `(attr=*)`, `(attr>=v)`, `(&...)`, `(|...)`, `(!(...))`.
- ✅ **Default port 389** (LDAP), **636** (LDAPS), **LDAPv3** is current.
- ✅ **Bind mechanisms** (security-ranked): Anonymous < Unauthenticated < Simple < LDAPS < StartTLS < **SASL/GSSAPI (Kerberos)**.
- ✅ **SASL = Simple Authentication and Security Layer**; **GSSAPI = Generic Security Service API**; **Kerberos v5 is the canonical GSSAPI mechanism**.
- ✅ **Read-optimized** — ideal for high-frequency authorization lookups in Big Data.

### Kerberos + LDAP Integration
- ✅ **Kerberos handles authentication** (cryptographic identity proof).
- ✅ **LDAP handles authorization** (group membership and policy lookup).
- ✅ **SASL/GSSAPI Bind** is the bridge — it lets LDAP use Kerberos for its own authentication.
- ✅ **Microsoft Active Directory = Kerberos v5 + LDAP v3** (the canonical commercial integration).
- ✅ **FreeIPA = MIT Kerberos + 389-DS LDAP** (the canonical open-source integration).
- ✅ **Hadoop's identity stack** (HDFS/YARN): `kerberos.auth → hadoop.security.authentication` + `hadoop.security.group.mapping.ldap.*` for group lookup.

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- 🚫 Do NOT call LDAP an "authentication protocol" — it is a directory *access* protocol.
- 🚫 Do NOT confuse the TGT (auth ticket) with a Service Ticket (authz ticket).
- 🚫 Do NOT forget that Kerberos uses **symmetric** keys, not PKI/SSL/TLS (TLS is used for some KDC implementations but the *authentication protocol itself* is symmetric).
- 🚫 Do NOT omit the timestamp replay defense — it is mandatory for full marks.
- 🚫 Do NOT draw the DIT upside down (root at the top, leaves at the bottom) — RDN order is right-to-left from the leaf.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Authentication and Authorization: Kerberos, LDAP**
> *Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) — PECCT743 Big Data Security*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control Frameworks: Apache Ranger, Apache Sentry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Access Control Frameworks: Apache Ranger, Apache Sentry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Apache Ranger — Formal Definition

**Apache Ranger** is an open-source, centralized security framework that provides comprehensive **authentication, authorization, audit, and data encryption** capabilities across the entire Apache Hadoop ecosystem. It is the de-facto standard for **fine-grained access control** in modern data lake architectures, supporting components such as HDFS, HBase, Hive, Kafka, Solr, Storm, and YARN.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Apache Ranger is described in KTU 2024 PECCT743 (Module 2) as a **Hadoop-native, policy-based authorization engine** that uses **XACML (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language)** semantics for evaluating access requests against centrally managed policies.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy: The "Smart Office Building"

Imagine a large multinational office tower with 50 companies operating inside. Every company has its own floor, server rooms, and confidential drawers. Apache Ranger acts as the **central building security command center**:

- **Lobby Reception (Admin Portal)** — Where security officers (admins) define which employee can enter which floor, and at what times.
- **Access Card Readers (Plugins)** — Installed on every door (HDFS, Hive, HBase, etc.). When an employee swipes a card, the reader instantly contacts the central command center.
- **Policy Database (Ranger DB)** — Stores all rules: "John from Finance → 7th floor → Mon-Fri 9 AM to 6 PM."
- **Audit Log Server** — Records *every* entry attempt, successful or denied, with timestamp and reason.

Without Ranger, every floor would have its own independent security guard with its own ruleset — chaos, inconsistency, and no central audit trail.

## 1.2 Apache Sentry — Formal Definition

**Apache Sentry** is an open-source, fine-grained **role-based authorization (RBAC)** system originally developed by Cloudera, specifically designed to enforce **data-level security** in Hadoop SQL engines — primarily **Apache Hive** and **Apache Impala**. It was later donated to the Apache Software Foundation in 2016.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Core Differentiator:** While Ranger is **policy-centric** (attribute + action + resource), Sentry is **privilege-centric** (server → database → table → column) with strict **role-based grouping**. Sentry is especially mature in **column-level masking** and **row-level filtering** in the Hadoop SQL stack.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy: The "Library Borrowing System"

Think of a public library with millions of books:

- A **membership card** (Role) classifies you — Student, Faculty, Guest.
- A **librarian database** (Policy Store) records: "Students can borrow only 'General Section' books, max 3 at a time."
- **Reference Section books** are off-limits to students.
- Every borrow is logged in a **registry book** (Audit).

Sentry plays the role of the librarian system: it does *not* issue cards (that's Kerberos/LDAP's job) but it strictly controls **what data** each card-holder may read, modify, or query.

## 1.3 Why Are These Frameworks Critical in Big Data Security?

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In a Hadoop cluster, **data locality**, **multi-tenancy**, and **diverse workloads** (batch, interactive, streaming) make traditional OS-level file permissions (UNIX `chmod`) absolutely **insufficient**. Big data demands *centralized, fine-grained, auditable, and policy-driven* control — precisely what Ranger and Sentry deliver.

| Aspect | OS-Level ACL (chmod) | Ranger / Sentry |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Granularity | File/Directory | Database, Table, Column, Row |
| Central Management | No (per-node) | Yes (single console) |
| Audit Trail | syslog only | Structured audit DB |
| Multi-Tenant | Not supported | Native support |
| Dynamic Policies | No (manual) | Yes (hot-reload) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Ranger — Architectural Components

Ranger follows a **plugin-based, centralized policy-evaluation architecture**. Let us break it down into its six logical components.

### 2.1.1 Ranger Admin Server (The Brain)
- A **Java-based web application** (WAR deployed in Tomcat/Spring Boot) that hosts the **Ranger Admin UI** and the **Policy Manager REST APIs**.
- Stores all policies, users, groups, and audit configurations in a relational database (**MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, or MSSQL**).
- Exposes endpoints for policy CRUD operations and policy download for plugins.

### 2.1.2 Ranger Plugins (The Hands)
Lightweight **Java agents** embedded inside each Hadoop component:
- **HDFS Plugin** — intercepts FileSystem calls.
- **Hive Plugin** — intercepts HiveServer2 driver operations.
- **HBase Plugin** — intercepts RegionServer authorization checks.
- **Kafka Plugin** — intercepts producer/consumer ACL checks.
- **Solr, YARN, Knox, Storm, NiFi, Kylin** plugins also exist.

Each plugin:
1. Pulls policies from Ranger Admin at startup (and periodically).
2. Caches them locally for performance.
3. Evaluates every access request against the cached policies.
4. Logs the decision (allow/deny) to the audit subsystem.

### 2.1.3 Ranger Usersync (The Identity Bridge)
- Synchronizes users and groups from external identity systems — **LDAP / Active Directory / Unix / File-based / Kerberos** — into Ranger's internal store.
- Runs as a periodic sync process (default every 5 minutes).

### 2.1.4 Ranger Audit Framework
- Supports **multiple audit destinations**: HDFS files, Log4j, Kafka topics, Solr indexes, **Amazon CloudWatch**, **Amazon S3**, or relational DB.
- Captures who accessed what resource, with what action, from which IP, at what time, and whether it was allowed.

### 2.1.5 Tag-Based Policies (Ranger TagSync)
- A **dynamic classification** mechanism where data assets are tagged (e.g., `PII`, `Confidential`, `GDPR-Sensitive`) and policies are written *against the tags* rather than against specific resources.
- When a table is tagged `PII`, all its columns automatically inherit the PII policy — saving administrative effort.

### 2.1.6 Ranger Key Management Service (Ranger KMS)
- A **transparent data encryption (TDE)** framework for HDFS encryption zones.
- Stores encryption keys in a secure backend (DB or HSM).
- Provides REST APIs for key creation, rotation, and decryption.

## 2.2 Apache Sentry — Architectural Components

Sentry has a leaner, **three-tier architecture** specifically designed for SQL-on-Hadoop.

### 2.2.1 Sentry Server
- A **Thrift-based service** that stores authorization metadata in a relational DB.
- Hosts the policy engine that evaluates access requests.
- Exposes RPC interfaces for plugin queries.

### 2.2.2 Sentry Plugin (Binding)
- A lightweight **library loaded into HiveServer2 / Impala daemon process** at startup.
- Hooks into the SQL parser to intercept every query and consult the Sentry Server.
- Examples: `sentry-binding-hive`, `sentry-binding-impala`, `sentry-binding-solr`.

### 2.2.3 Policy File / Database
- Policies are defined in **INI-like files** (`sentry-site.xml`) or in a centralized DB.
- Policy structure: **Server → Database → Table → Column**, plus **URIs** (HDFS paths) and **actions** (SELECT, INSERT, ALL).

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 2 answers. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Apache Ranger | Apache Sentry |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Originator | Hortonworks (2014) | Cloudera (2013), Apache 2016 |
| Architecture | Plugin + Admin + Usersync | Server + Binding + Policy DB |
| Policy Model | Resource-based + Tag-based | Role-based (RBAC) privilege model |
| Supported Components | HDFS, Hive, HBase, Kafka, Solr, YARN, Storm, Knox, NiFi, Kylin, Atlas | Hive, Impala, Solr, Sqoop |
| Granularity | DB, Table, Column, Row, Tag | Server, DB, Table, Column, URI |
| Audit Support | Yes (rich, multi-sink) | Yes (basic logging) |
| Data Encryption | Ranger KMS (TDE) | No (delegated to HDFS/KMS) |
| Centralized UI | Yes (web console) | No (CLI / config files) |
| Policy Engine | XACML-style | Custom privilege evaluator |
| Tag/Attribute-Based | Yes (Ranger Tags) | No |
| Industry Adoption | Broad (Hadoop, Spark, EMR) | Strong in Impala/Hive shops |
| License | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Policy Evaluation Logic

Although big data security is not heavily math-driven, the **policy evaluation** in Ranger follows a logical expression model that can be expressed formally. This is often asked in KTU 2-mark and 12-mark questions.

$$
\text{AccessDecision}(\text{user}, \text{action}, \text{resource}) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Policy}_i(\text{user}, \text{action}, \text{resource})
$$

$$
\text{Policy}_i = \left( \text{UserCondition}_i \;\land\; \text{AccessCondition}_i \;\land\; \text{ResourceCondition}_i \;\land\; \neg \text{DenyOverride}_i \right)
$$

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases} \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \exists\, i \text{ such that Policy}_i = \text{true and no Deny rule applies} \\ \text{DENY} & \text{otherwise (Default Deny)} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Banking & Finance | Restrict analysts to read-only views of transaction tables; mask PII columns like card numbers |
| Healthcare (HIPAA) | Tag patient records as `PHI` and enforce encryption + access logging at the column level |
| Retail (e-Commerce) | Region-based access for marketing teams (EU users → EU data only) via row-level filters |
| Telecom | Enforce multi-tenant isolation in shared Hadoop clusters processing CDR data |
| Government / Defense | Audit every query against sensitive national-security datasets with immutable trails |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: How Ranger Evaluates an Access Request

Let us trace a real-world request: **User `alice` runs `SELECT * FROM sales.customer LIMIT 10` in Hive**.

### Step 1 — Authentication (Out of Ranger's Scope)
Alice authenticates via **Kerberos** (or LDAP/SSO). HiveServer2 receives a valid Kerberos ticket and identifies the principal `alice@EXAMPLE.COM`. This step is *not* performed by Ranger.

### Step 2 — HiveServer2 Receives the Query
The HiveServer2 driver parses the SQL and prepares a logical plan. Before executing, it checks authorization.

### Step 3 — Ranger Hive Plugin Intercepts
The embedded **Ranger Hive authorization plugin** is triggered. It collects:
- `user` = `alice`
- `accessTypes` = `[SELECT]`
- `resource` = `database=sales, table=customer`
- `clientIP`, `userGroups`, `sessionEnabled` metadata

### Step 4 — Policy Retrieval (Cached)
The plugin looks up its **local in-memory policy cache** (refreshed every **30 seconds by default**, or via `ranger.plugin.hive.policy.refresh.interval`).

### Step 5 — Policy Matching (Boolean Evaluation)
The plugin iterates through all matching policies. For each policy, the following are evaluated:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Policy}_1 &: \text{User} = \text{alice}, \text{Groups} \in \{\text{analysts}\} \\
\text{Policy}_2 &: \text{Resource} = \text{database=sales, table=customer} \\
\text{Policy}_3 &: \text{Permissions} = \{\text{select}\} \;\text{(no ALL, no WRITE)} \\
\text{Policy}_4 &: \text{AllowConditions (IP, time)} = \text{ALL}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a single policy's boolean AND evaluates to TRUE and the policy has `isAllowed=true` (not a Deny rule), the access is granted.

### Step 6 — Deny Override
Ranger enforces the principle: **Explicit Deny > Explicit Allow**. If any Deny rule matches, the request is denied even if an Allow rule exists.

### Step 7 — Audit Logging
Regardless of Allow/Deny outcome, an audit event is written to all configured audit destinations (HDFS, Solr, DB, Kafka).

### Step 8 — Response to HiveServer2
The plugin returns `TRUE` (allow) or `FALSE` (deny) to HiveServer2, which then either proceeds with execution or returns an `Error 10020: PERMISSION_DENIED`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: How Sentry Evaluates a Privilege

Trace: **User `bob` runs `SELECT name, salary FROM hr.employees` in Impala**.

### Step 1 — Impala Daemon Receives the Query
The `impalad` coordinator receives the query from a JDBC/ODBC client authenticated via Kerberos/LDAP.

### Step 2 — Sentry Binding Hook
The `sentry-binding-impala` library, loaded at `impalad` startup, intercepts the query after parsing and before execution.

### Step 3 — Privilege Construction
The binding constructs a **Sentry Privilege object**:
- `Server` = `server1` (Impala server name)
- `Db` = `hr`
- `Table` = `employees`
- `Column` = `name, salary`
- `Action` = `SELECT`

### Step 4 — Role Lookup
Sentry fetches all roles assigned to user `bob` (via a join on `sentry_role_user_map`). Suppose `bob` belongs to roles `[hr_analyst, all_readers]`.

### Step 5 — Privilege Lookup
For each role, Sentry looks up matching privileges in the `sentry_db_priv`, `sentry_tbl_priv`, `sentry_column_priv` tables.

### Step 6 — Match Evaluation
Suppose:
- Role `hr_analyst` has privilege: `server=server1, db=hr, table=employees, action=SELECT` → **MATCH**
- Role `all_readers` has privilege: `server=server1, db=*, action=SELECT` → **MATCH**

At least one match → **GRANT**.

### Step 7 — Column-Level Masking
Sentry also checks column-level rules. If `salary` is restricted to role `hr_manager` only and `bob` is not in that role, the query is rewritten by Impala to **exclude the `salary` column** (column masking).

### Step 8 — Audit + Response
Sentry returns GRANT, and `impalad` proceeds. An audit event is logged in `sentry_audit_log`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Strengths vs. Weaknesses (Exam Favourite)

| Criterion | Ranger Strength | Ranger Weakness | Sentry Strength | Sentry Weakness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ecosystem Coverage | Broadest in Hadoop | Slight overhead per plugin | Tight Hive/Impala integration | Limited to SQL engines |
| Policy Model | Tag-based, attribute-rich | Steeper learning curve | Simple privilege model | No tag/attribute support |
| Encryption | Native KMS included | Operational complexity | Not applicable | No native encryption |
| UI/UX | Modern web UI | Resource-intensive | CLI-driven | No graphical console |
| Performance | Acceptable with caching | Cached policy staleness | Very fast (in-memory) | Less flexible in scaling |
| Audit Quality | Multi-sink, structured | Misconfiguration complexity | Adequate | No Solr/Kafka audit by default |
| Row-Level Security | Supported via policies | Requires tag setup | Limited (Impala 2.6+) | Less mature |

## 3.4 Detailed Policy Syntax (Model Answer Material)

### 3.4.1 Ranger Policy JSON (Simplified)
A Ranger policy is a JSON document with the following logical structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Policy} = \{\; &\text{policyName}, \;\text{policyType}, \;\text{isEnabled}, \;\text{isDeny}, \\
&\text{resources} : \{\text{database}, \text{table}, \text{column}\}, \\
&\text{policyItems} : \{\text{users}, \text{groups}, \text{roles}, \text{accessTypes}\}, \\
&\text{denyPolicyItems}, \;\text{conditions} : \{\text{IP, time, context\}}\;\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 Sentry Privilege (Conceptual)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Privilege} = \{\; &\text{Server} \supseteq \text{Database} \supseteq \text{Table} \supseteq \text{Column}, \\
&\text{URI (optional)}, \;\text{Action} \in \{\text{SELECT, INSERT, ALL, \dots}\}\;\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Sentry model enforces **hierarchical inheritance**: granting on a database implies rights on all tables within it *only if* configured via `ALL` action; column-level grants are independent of table-level grants (must be specified separately for granularity).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apache Ranger — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    admin[ADMIN UI WEB CONSOLE] --> adminsrv[RANGER ADMIN SERVER]
    adminsrv --> polstore[(RANGER POLICY DATABASE)]
    adminsrv --> auditstore[(RANGER AUDIT STORE)]
    usync[USERSYNC SERVICE] --> adminsrv
    ldap[LDAP ACTIVE DIRECTORY] --> usync
    kerberos[KERBEROS KDC] --> hive[HIVESERVER2]
    hive --> rhive[RANGER HIVE PLUGIN]
    rhive --> adminsrv
    hdfs[HDFS NAMENODE] --> rhdfs[RANGER HDFS PLUGIN]
    rhdfs --> adminsrv
    hbase[HBASE MASTER REGION] --> rhbase[RANGER HBASE PLUGIN]
    rhbase --> adminsrv
    kafka[KAFKA BROKER] --> rkafka[RANGER KAFKA PLUGIN]
    rkafka --> adminsrv
    rhive --> audit1[AUDIT HDFS]
    rhive --> audit2[AUDIT SOLR]
    rhive --> audit3[AUDIT KAFKA]
    rhive --> audit4[AUDIT DB]
```

## 4.2 Apache Sentry — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sentrysrv[SENTRY SERVER THRIFT SERVICE] --> sentrydb[(SENTRY POLICY METADATA DB)]
    sentrysrv --> sentryaudit[(SENTRY AUDIT LOG)]
    hive[HIVE SERVER 2] --> sbh[SENTRY HIVE BINDING]
    impala[IMPALA DAEMON IMPALAD] --> sbi[SENTRY IMPALA BINDING]
    sbh --> sentrysrv
    sbi --> sentrysrv
    sentrysrv --> privileges[PRIVILEGE EVALUATOR]
    privileges --> roles[ROLE HIERARCHY]
    roles --> users[USERS GROUPS]
```

## 4.3 Access Decision Flow — Ranger Policy Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER REQUEST ARRIVES] --> B[AUTHENTICATE KERBEROS LDAP]
    B --> C[PLUGIN INTERCEPTS REQUEST]
    C --> D{IS POLICY CACHE FRESH}
    D -->|NO| E[FETCH POLICIES FROM ADMIN]
    D -->|YES| F[USE CACHED POLICIES]
    E --> F
    F --> G[ITERATE THROUGH ALL POLICIES]
    G --> H{ANY ALLOW POLICY MATCHES}
    H -->|NO| I[CHECK DENY POLICIES]
    H -->|YES| J{ANY EXPLICIT DENY MATCHES}
    J -->|YES| K[DENY ACCESS]
    J -->|NO| L[ALLOW ACCESS]
    I --> K
    L --> M[LOG TO AUDIT SINK]
    K --> M
    M --> N[RETURN DECISION]
```

## 4.4 Sentry Privilege Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[SQL QUERY RECEIVED] --> Q[IMPALA HIVE PARSER]
    Q --> R[SENTRY BINDING HOOK]
    R --> S[CONSTRUCT PRIVILEGE OBJECT DB TABLE COLUMN ACTION]
    S --> T[FETCH USER ROLES FROM SENTRY DB]
    T --> U[FETCH PRIVILEGES FOR EACH ROLE]
    U --> V{HIERARCHICAL MATCH DATABASE THEN TABLE THEN COLUMN}
    V -->|NO MATCH| W[RETURN DENY]
    V -->|MATCH| X[CHECK COLUMN MASKING RULES]
    X -->|COLUMN MASKED| Y[REWRITE QUERY TO EXCLUDE MASKED COLUMNS]
    X -->|NO MASK| Z[ALLOW FULL QUERY]
    Y --> AA[EXECUTE REWRITTEN QUERY]
    Z --> AA
    AA --> AB[LOG AUDIT EVENT]
    W --> AB
```

## 4.5 Ranger vs Sentry — Component Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RANGER
        R1[ADMIN UI] --> R2[POLICY ENGINE]
        R3[USERSYNC] --> R2
        R4[PLUGINS] --> R2
        R2 --> R5[AUDIT MULTI SINK]
        R2 --> R6[TAG BASED ACCESS]
        R2 --> R7[KMS ENCRYPTION]
    end
    subgraph SENTRY
        S1[SENTRY SERVER] --> S2[PRIVILEGE ENGINE]
        S3[HIVE BINDING] --> S1
        S4[IMPALA BINDING] --> S1
        S1 --> S5[AUDIT LOG]
        S1 --> S6[RBAC ROLES]
    end
    USERS[USERS GROUPS] --> R1
    USERS --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Apache Ranger. List any four Hadoop components supported by Ranger.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECCT743]**

**Model Answer:**

Apache Ranger is an open-source, centralized security framework for the Apache Hadoop ecosystem that provides **authentication, authorization, audit, and data-encryption** capabilities through a unified, policy-driven console.

Four Hadoop components supported by Ranger:
1. **HDFS** (file-level access control)
2. **Hive** (database, table, column-level access)
3. **HBase** (column-family, cell-level access)
4. **Kafka** (topic-level publish/subscribe ACLs)

Other supported components include YARN, Solr, Storm, Knox, NiFi, and Kylin.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between policy-based and role-based access control. Give one example framework for each.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECCT743]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Policy-Based Access Control (PBAC) | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Decision Basis | Boolean evaluation of policies containing user, resource, action, and conditions | Membership of users in roles and roles in privileges |
| Flexibility | High (supports context, tags, conditions) | Moderate (group-centric) |
| Example Framework | **Apache Ranger** | **Apache Sentry** |
| Expressiveness | XACML-style (rich attributes) | Privilege model (server → db → table → column) |
| Admin Overhead | Higher (policy authoring) | Lower (role assignment) |

**Example:** Ranger allows policy *"user X can read column Y in table T only between 9 AM–5 PM from IP 10.0.0.0/24"* — this is PBAC. Sentry expresses *"role hr_analyst has SELECT on db hr.table employees"* — this is RBAC.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OPTION 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECCT743 — Module 2]**

#### (a) Explain the architecture of Apache Ranger with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of Ranger Admin, Plugins, and Usersync. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Apache Ranger follows a **three-tier architecture** comprising the Admin layer, the Plugin layer, and the Audit layer.

**1. Ranger Admin Server:**
- Java-based web application that provides a centralized GUI for policy management.
- Hosts the **Ranger Policy Manager** which stores all authorization policies in a relational database.
- Exposes **REST APIs** for policy CRUD operations.
- Acts as the single source of truth for all policy decisions.

**2. Ranger Plugins:**
- Lightweight Java agents embedded inside Hadoop daemons (HDFS NameNode, HiveServer2, HBase Master, Kafka Broker, etc.).
- Pull policies from Ranger Admin at startup and refresh them periodically.
- Intercept access requests, evaluate them against cached policies, and return Allow/Deny.
- Each plugin is specific to one component (e.g., `ranger-hdfs-plugin`, `ranger-hive-plugin`).

**3. Ranger Usersync:**
- A synchronization service that pulls **users and groups** from external identity stores (LDAP, Active Directory, Unix, file-based).
- Populates Ranger's internal user/group store so that policies can reference them.
- Runs on a configurable schedule (default every 5 minutes).

**4. Audit Subsystem:**
- Captures every access decision (Allow/Deny) with details: user, resource, action, IP, timestamp.
- Supports multi-sink audit: HDFS, Solr, Kafka, Amazon S3, CloudWatch, or DB.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1)*

#### (b) Discuss in detail the various access control models supported by Apache Ranger. How is tag-based policy different from resource-based policy? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Apache Ranger supports **three primary access control models**:

**1. Resource-Based Policies:**
- The traditional model where policies directly reference specific resources (e.g., database `sales`, table `customer`, column `ssn`).
- Easy to set up; ideal for static, well-known resources.
- *Limitation:* Must update each policy when new resources are added.

**2. Tag-Based Policies:**
- Resources (tables, columns) are **classified with tags** such as `PII`, `Confidential`, `PHI`, `GDPR-Sensitive`, `Mask`.
- Policies are written *against tags* instead of resources.
- When a column is tagged `PII`, all policies referencing the `PII` tag automatically apply.
- This provides **dynamic, scalable** access control for rapidly evolving data lakes.
- Tag assignment is managed via **Ranger TagSync**, which integrates with **Apache Atlas**.

**3. Attribute-Based / Condition-Based Policies:**
- Allow policies to incorporate contextual conditions: **IP range, time of day, day of week, user-agent, geographical location, or custom attributes**.
- Example: *"Allow group `analysts` to SELECT from `db1.t1` only between 9 AM–6 PM and only from corporate VPN IP range."*

**Difference between Resource-Based and Tag-Based Policy:**

| Aspect | Resource-Based | Tag-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Target | Specific resources | Tags (abstract labels) |
| Scalability | Low (manual) | High (automatic) |
| Best for | Small, static datasets | Large, evolving data lakes |
| Metadata Dependency | None | Requires Apache Atlas or manual tagging |
| Policy Reuse | Limited | Excellent (one policy → many resources) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **OPTION 2** *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECCT743 — Module 2]**

#### (a) Explain the architecture of Apache Sentry. How does it provide fine-grained authorization in Apache Hive and Impala? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Apache Sentry uses a **three-tier, Thrift-based architecture** comprising the Sentry Server, Sentry Bindings, and the Policy Store.

**1. Sentry Server:**
- A standalone **Thrift service** that hosts the policy engine.
- Stores all authorization metadata in a relational database (PostgreSQL/MySQL).
- Exposes RPC APIs for privilege management and authorization checks.
- Performs the **role-privilege join** during query evaluation.

**2. Sentry Bindings (Plugins):**
- `sentry-binding-hive`: Hooks into HiveServer2's authorization layer.
- `sentry-binding-impala`: Hooks into the `impalad` coordinator.
- `sentry-binding-solr`: For Solr collection-level authorization.
- `sentry-binding-sqoop`: For Sqoop import/export.
