H(EC) = -\sum_{v} p(v) \log_2 p(v)
$$

$$
H(EC) = -[0.333 \log_2 0.333 + 0.333 \log_2 0.333 + 0.167 \log_2 0.167 + 0.167 \log_2 0.167]
$$

$$
H(EC) = -[0.333 \times (-1.585) + 0.333 \times (-1.585) + 0.167 \times (-2.585) + 0.167 \times (-2.585)]
$$

$$
H(EC) = -[(-0.528) + (-0.528) + (-0.432) + (-0.432)]
$$

$$
H(EC) = -(-1.920) = 1.920 \text{ bits}
$$

For entropy l-diversity:

$$
H(EC) = 1.920 \geq \log_2 l
$$

$$
2^{1.920} \geq l \implies l \leq 3.79
$$

So the table satisfies entropy l-diversity for $l = 1, 2, 3$ ✓ (but not $l = 4$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for the Board:** The two instantiations of l-diversity give *different* valid $l$ values. Distinct l-diversity is "looser"; entropy l-diversity is "stricter" because it considers the *frequency* of values, not just whether they exist.

## 3.3 Verifying t-Closeness Using Earth Mover's Distance

### Step 1 — The Distributions

Suppose the overall table distribution $P_T$ (representing hospital-wide prevalence) is:

$$
P_T = \left( P_T(\text{Cancer}) = 0.20, \; P_T(\text{Flu}) = 0.30, \; P_T(\text{Diabetes}) = 0.30, \; P_T(\text{Asthma}) = 0.20 \right)
$$

The equivalence class distribution $P_{EC}$ remains:

$$
P_{EC} = \left( 0.333, \; 0.333, \; 0.167, \; 0.167 \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Cumulative Distributions

Order the values: $\{$Cancer, Flu, Diabetes, Asthma$\}$.

**Cumulative $P_T$:**

$$
CDF_T(1) = 0.20, \quad CDF_T(2) = 0.50, \quad CDF_T(3) = 0.80, \quad CDF_T(4) = 1.00
$$

**Cumulative $P_{EC}$:**

$$
CDF_{EC}(1) = 0.333, \quad CDF_{EC}(2) = 0.667, \quad CDF_{EC}(3) = 0.833, \quad CDF_{EC}(4) = 1.000
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Absolute Differences

$$
\Delta_1 = \vert 0.333 - 0.20 \vert = 0.133
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \vert 0.667 - 0.50 \vert = 0.167
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = \vert 0.833 - 0.80 \vert = 0.033
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the EMD Formula

$$
EMD(P_{EC}, P_T) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \Delta_i
$$

$$
EMD = \frac{1}{4-1} \times (0.133 + 0.167 + 0.033)
$$

$$
EMD = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.333
$$

$$
EMD = 0.111
$$

### Step 5 — t-Closeness Verdict

For the table to be t-close, we need $EMD \leq t$. Common thresholds are $t = 0.1, 0.15, 0.2$.

$$
EMD = 0.111 \leq 0.15 \;\;\checkmark
$$

So the table is **0.15-close** but not **0.1-close**.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | k-Anonymity | l-Diversity | t-Closeness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year Proposed** | 1998 | 2007 | 2007 |
| **Main Defense** | Record linkage | Homogeneity attack | Similarity + skewness attack |
| **Key Metric** | Size of equivalence class $\vert EC_i \vert$ | Diversity of SA values | Distribution distance (EMD) |
| **Parameters** | $k$ | $l$ (and $c$ for recursive) | $t$ (threshold) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (polynomial) | Medium | High (NP-hard in general) |
| **Data Utility Loss** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Handles Numeric SA** | N/A | Partially | Yes (via EMD) |
| **Handles Categorical SA** | N/A | Yes (with hierarchy) | Yes (with taxonomy distance) |
| **Vulnerability** | Homogeneity, Background knowledge | Similarity, Skewness | Skewness, semantic distance ambiguity |
| **Successor in the chain** | — | Patches k-anonymity | Patches l-diversity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Privacy Defense Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Microdata Table T] --> B{Remove Explicit Identifiers}
    B --> C[De-identified Table T']
    C --> D{Apply k-Anonymity?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Generalize or Suppress QI]
    E --> F{k ≥ 2 Satisfied?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[k-Anonymous Table]
    G --> H{Apply l-Diversity?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Ensure l distinct SA values per EC]
    I --> J{l ≥ 2 Satisfied?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[l-Diverse Table]
    K --> L{Apply t-Closeness?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Compute EMD per EC]
    M --> N{EMD ≤ t?}
    N -- No --> M
    N -- Yes --> O[t-Close Table]
    O --> P[Publish Anonymized Dataset]
    G --> P
    K --> P
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style P fill:#d4f4dd
    style G fill:#fff4cc
    style K fill:#ffe0b3
    style O fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.2 Attack Models Mapped to Defenses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Record Linkage Attack] -->|Blocked by| D1[k-Anonymity]
    A2[Homogeneity Attack] -->|Blocked by| D2[l-Diversity]
    A3[Background Knowledge Attack] -->|Partially blocked| D3[k-Anonymity and l-Diversity]
    A4[Similarity Attack] -->|Blocked by| D4[t-Closeness]
    A5[Skewness Attack] -->|Blocked by| D5[t-Closeness]
    style A1 fill:#ffcccc
    style A2 fill:#ffcccc
    style A3 fill:#ffcccc
    style A4 fill:#ffcccc
    style A5 fill:#ffcccc
    style D1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style D2 fill:#d4f4dd
    style D3 fill:#fff4cc
    style D4 fill:#d4f4dd
    style D5 fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.3 Anonymization Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        RAW[Raw Dataset with PII]
    end
    subgraph PREPROC[Preprocessing]
        ID1[Detect Explicit Identifiers]
        ID2[Detect Quasi-Identifiers]
        ID3[Detect Sensitive Attributes]
    end
    subgraph ANON[Anonymization Engine]
        GEN[Generalization Module]
        SUP[Suppression Module]
        LDIV[l-Diversity Enforcer]
        TCLOS[t-Closeness Enforcer]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        PUB[Published Anonymized Dataset]
    end
    RAW --> ID1
    RAW --> ID2
    RAW --> ID3
    ID1 --> GEN
    ID2 --> GEN
    GEN --> SUP
    SUP --> LDIV
    LDIV --> TCLOS
    TCLOS --> PUB
    style RAW fill:#ffe0e0
    style PUB fill:#d4f4dd
    style GEN fill:#cce5ff
    style SUP fill:#cce5ff
    style LDIV fill:#cce5ff
    style TCLOS fill:#cce5ff
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Class Formation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Original Table T with QI columns] --> B[Apply Generalization Function G to QI]
    B --> C[Group rows by G projected on QI]
    C --> D{Size of group >= k?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Valid Equivalence Class]
    D -- No --> F[Apply Suppression or Further Generalization]
    F --> G[Re-check group size]
    G --> D
    E --> H[l-Diversity Check]
    H --> I{Distinct SA values >= l?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Valid l-Diverse EC]
    I -- No --> K[Split or Merge with neighbor EC]
    K --> H
    J --> L[t-Closeness Check]
    L --> M{EMD distance <= t?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Final Anonymized EC]
    M -- No --> O[Re-balance EC distribution]
    O --> L
    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style N fill:#d4f4dd
    style E fill:#cce5ff
    style J fill:#cce5ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define *k-anonymity* with respect to a microdata table. How does it protect against record linkage attacks?

**Model Answer:**

k-anonymity is a property of a released microdata table $T$ stating that for every record $t \in T$, there exist at least $k-1$ other records with identical values on the quasi-identifier attributes. Formally, the equivalence class size $\vert EC_i \vert \geq k$ for all $i$.

**Defense Mechanism:** When an attacker attempts to link a target individual to a record using the quasi-identifier (e.g., `Age=29, ZIP=682001`), they will find at least $k$ matching records. Since all $k$ records are indistinguishable on the QI, the probability of correctly re-identifying the target is reduced to at most $1/k$. This thwarts record linkage attacks based on external knowledge of QI values.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the *homogeneity attack*? Why does k-anonymity fail to prevent it, and how does l-diversity address this issue?

**Model Answer:**

A **homogeneity attack** occurs when all records in a k-anonymous equivalence class share the *same* value for a sensitive attribute. Even though the attacker cannot pinpoint which of the $k$ individuals is the target, they can definitively learn the SA value. For example, if all 5 records in an EC have `Disease = Cancer`, the attacker knows with certainty that the target has Cancer.

**Why k-anonymity fails:** k-anonymity only ensures indistinguishability on the QI; it places no constraint on the sensitive attribute distribution within an equivalence class.

**How l-diversity helps:** l-diversity mandates that every equivalence class contain at least $l$ "well-represented" sensitive values. With $l \geq 2$, the attacker cannot be sure of the target's SA because at least two distinct values exist within the group.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** Consider the following microdata table $T$ of a hospital:

| ID | Name | Age | ZIP | Sex | Disease |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 25 | 682001 | M | Cancer |
| 2 | B | 26 | 682001 | F | Flu |
| 3 | C | 27 | 682002 | M | Diabetes |
| 4 | D | 28 | 682002 | F | Asthma |
| 5 | E | 45 | 682010 | M | Cancer |
| 6 | F | 46 | 682010 | F | Flu |
| 7 | G | 47 | 682015 | M | Diabetes |
| 8 | H | 48 | 682015 | F | Cancer |

The quasi-identifier is $QI = \{Age, ZIP\}$ and the sensitive attribute is `Disease`.

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
Apply **generalization** to make the above table **3-anonymous**. Show the generalized table and verify the k-anonymity property.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Generalize Age (ranges of 10) and ZIP (suppress last 3 digits):**

Generalization mapping:
- `Age` $\in [25, 28] \rightarrow$ "20-30"
- `Age` $\in [45, 48] \rightarrow$ "40-50"
- `ZIP` $\in \{682001, 682002, 682010, 682015\} \rightarrow$ "682***"

**Step 2 — Construct the generalized table:**

| ID | Age | ZIP | Disease |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20-30 | 682*** | Cancer |
| 2 | 20-30 | 682*** | Flu |
| 3 | 20-30 | 682*** | Diabetes |
| 4 | 20-30 | 682*** | Asthma |
| 5 | 40-50 | 682*** | Cancer |
| 6 | 40-50 | 682*** | Flu |
| 7 | 40-50 | 682*** | Diabetes |
| 8 | 40-50 | 682*** | Cancer |

**Step 3 — Identify Equivalence Classes:**

- $EC_1 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with QI `(20-30, 682***)` → $\vert EC_1 \vert = 4 \geq 3$ ✓
- $EC_2 = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}$ with QI `(40-50, 682***)` → $\vert EC_2 \vert = 4 \geq 3$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The table is **3-anonymous** because every equivalence class has at least 3 records. **Answer Verified ✓**

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
Verify whether the generalized table satisfies **distinct 3-diversity** and **entropy 2-diversity**. Show all calculations.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Check Distinct 3-Diversity:**

For $EC_1$: Disease values = `{Cancer, Flu, Diabetes, Asthma}` → 4 distinct values $\geq 3$ ✓
For $EC_2$: Disease values = `{Cancer, Flu, Diabetes, Cancer}` → 3 distinct values $\geq 3$ ✓

**The table satisfies distinct 3-diversity.** ✓

**Step 2 — Check Entropy 2-Diversity:**

Compute the entropy of each EC.

**For $EC_1$:** Probability distribution:
$$
P_{EC_1} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25) \text{ for \{Cancer, Flu, Diabetes, Asthma\}}
$$

$$
H(EC_1) = -4 \times 0.25 \log_2 0.25 = -4 \times 0.25 \times (-2) = 2.0 \text{ bits}
$$

Since $H(EC_1) = 2.0 \geq \log_2 2 = 1.0$ → $EC_1$ passes entropy 2-diversity ✓

**For $EC_2$:** Probability distribution:
$$
P_{EC_2} = (0.50, 0.25, 0.25, 0) \text{ for \{Cancer, Flu, Diabetes, Asthma\}}
$$

$$
H(EC_2) = -[0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0]
$$

$$
H(EC_2) = -[0.50 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0]
$$

$$
H(EC_2) = -[-0.50 - 0.50 - 0.50 + 0] = 1.5 \text{ bits}
$$

Since $H(EC_2) = 1.5 \geq \log_2 2 = 1.0$ → $EC_2$ passes entropy 2-diversity ✓

**Conclusion:** The table satisfies **both distinct 3-diversity and entropy 2-diversity**. ✓

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** Explain in detail the **t-closeness** privacy model. How does it overcome the weaknesses of k-anonymity and l-diversity? Use the **Earth Mover's Distance (EMD)** formula in your answer.

### Part (a) [7 Marks]
Discuss the limitations of k-anonymity and l-diversity, and explain why t-closeness was proposed.

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of k-anonymity (Samarati & Sweeney, 1998):**
1. **Homogeneity Attack:** If all records in an equivalence class share the same sensitive attribute, k-anonymity fails to hide the SA value.
2. **Background Knowledge Attack:** External knowledge can be used to infer the SA even when multiple values are present.

**Limitations of l-diversity (Machanavajjhala et al., 2007):**
1. **Similarity Attack:** l-diversity only requires $l$ distinct values but not that they be *semantically diverse*. An EC with `{Lung Cancer, Breast Cancer, Liver Cancer}` is l-diverse but all values are "cancer" — the attacker learns the target has *some form of cancer*.
2. **Skewness Attack:** When the overall distribution of SA is heavily skewed, l-diversity may be impossible to achieve without heavy information loss.
3. **Lack of clear semantic guidance:** l-diversity does not specify how to measure "diversity" for complex or hierarchical SAs.

**Why t-closeness was proposed (Li et al., 2007):**

t-closeness requires that the distribution of the sensitive attribute *within each equivalence class* be "close" (within threshold $t$) to the distribution *in the entire table*. This ensures that any EC is statistically representative of the whole, preventing an attacker from gaining information that differs from background knowledge.

### Part (b) [7 Marks]
Define t-closeness formally and derive the EMD formula for two example distributions. Consider $P = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$ and $Q = (0.3, 0.3, 0.4)$ over ordered values $\{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$. Compute $\text{EMD}(P, Q)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Formal Definition of t-Closeness:**

An equivalence class $EC$ is **t-close** if the distance between the sensitive attribute's distribution in $EC$ (denoted $P_{EC}$) and its distribution in the entire table $T$ (denoted $P_T$) does not exceed threshold $t$:

$$
d(P_{EC}, P_T) \leq t
$$

The distance $d$ is the **Earth Mover's Distance (EMD)** for ordered/numerical SAs, or a hierarchical distance for categorical SAs.

**EMD Formula Derivation:**

For 1-D ordered distributions $P$ and $Q$ over $n$ values, EMD is:

$$
EMD(P, Q) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left\vert \sum_{j=1}^{i} (P_j - Q_j) \right\vert
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $P = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$ and $Q = (0.3, 0.3, 0.4)$ with $n = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute cumulative sums of $(P_j - Q_j)$:**

- $P_1 - Q_1 = 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.2$
- $P_2 - Q_2 = 0.3 - 0.3 = 0.0$
- $P_3 - Q_3 = 0.2 - 0.4 = -0.2$

**Step 2 — Sum cumulatively and take absolute value:**

- At $i=1$: $\vert 0.2 \vert = 0.2$
- At $i=2$: $\vert 0.2 + 0.0 \vert = 0.2$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$
EMD(P, Q) = \frac{1}{3-1} (0.2 + 0.2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.4 = 0.2
$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{EMD}(P, Q) = 0.2$. The two distributions are 0.2-close. If $t \geq 0.2$, the equivalence class satisfies t-closeness. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing QI with SA:** Students frequently interchange the Quasi-Identifier with the Sensitive Attribute in definitions. *Always* state clearly that QI is the *attack vector* and SA is the *target of inference*.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify all ECs:** In k-anonymity, *every* equivalence class must satisfy $\vert EC_i \vert \geq k$. Students often check only one and miss others — **full table verification is mandatory for full marks**.
> 3. **Logarithm base in entropy:** Entropy l-diversity uses $\log_2$ (information bits), not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. Using the wrong base yields a wrong $l$ value. **Always use $\log_2$ unless explicitly specified otherwise.**
> 4. **EMD summation upper bound:** The EMD formula sums from $i=1$ to $n-1$, NOT to $n$. The last term (i = n) is always 0 since the cumulative probabilities both equal 1. **Forgetting to use $n-1$ is a common 1-mark loss.**
> 5. **Neglecting "publishing the SA":** In all three models, the Sensitive Attribute is *published* (it is needed for research); only the QI is generalized. Students sometimes incorrectly say "remove the disease column" — this is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this section the night before the exam!**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [x] **k-anonymity** = "no record in the table is uniquely identifiable using QI"
- [x] **l-diversity** = "every equivalence class has at least l diverse sensitive values"
- [x] **t-closeness** = "the SA distribution in any EC is statistically close (within t) to the global distribution"

### Key Formula Triad
- [x] k-anonymity: $\vert EC_i \vert \geq k$
- [x] l-diversity (distinct): $\vert \text{Unique SA values in } EC_i \vert \geq l$
- [x] t-closeness: $EMD(P_{EC}, P_T) \leq t$

### Attack → Defense Mapping (must know table)
- [x] **Record Linkage** ← k-anonymity
- [x] **Homogeneity** ← l-diversity
- [x] **Background Knowledge** ← l-diversity (partial)
- [x] **Similarity** ← t-closeness
- [x] **Skewness** ← t-closeness

### Mnemonics for the Board Exam
- [x] **"K-I-L-L-T"** = **K**-anon, **L**-diversity, **T**-closeness — the three pillars
- [x] **"G-S"** = **G**eneralization and **S**uppression are the two anonymization operations
- [x] **"EMD = Earth Moving Dirt"** — the formula literally measures how much "probability dirt" you'd have to shovel to make one distribution match the other

### Numerical Quick-Facts
- [x] $k = 1$ means **no anonymity** (every record is unique)
- [x] $k = 5$ is the **de facto industry standard** (HIPAA Safe Harbor recommends this)
- [x] Larger $k$ → **more privacy** but **less utility**
- [x] Larger $l$ or smaller $t$ → **more privacy** but **less utility**
- [x] For entropy l-diversity: $H \geq \log_2 l$ — always use $\log_2$

### Common Exam Traps
- [x] Don't confuse the **threshold $t$** in t-closeness with **l** in l-diversity
- [x] The **Sensitive Attribute (SA) is never removed** in these models — only QI is generalized
- [x] **Information Loss (IL)** always increases as $k$, $l$, or privacy requirements grow
- [x] EMD requires the SA values to be **ordered** (e.g., salary ranges) — for categorical, use a **taxonomy distance** instead

### Quick "If Asked" Reference
- [x] *"Define k-anonymity"* → use Samarati & Sweeney definition with $\vert EC \vert \geq k$
- [x] *"Why l-diversity?"* → "To prevent homogeneity and background knowledge attacks that k-anonymity misses"
- [x] *"Why t-closeness?"* → "To prevent similarity and skewness attacks that l-diversity misses"
- [x] *"Compare the three"* → use the Analytical Matrix from Section 3.4

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Privacy: Concept, mechanisms, and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Privacy: Concept, mechanisms, and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Differential Privacy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Privacy (DP)** is a mathematically rigorous framework that quantifies and bounds the privacy leakage when releasing aggregate statistical information from a dataset. It guarantees that the *output* of an analysis is statistically indistinguishable whether or not any *single individual's* data is included in the input.

Formally, a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy** if for all pairs of neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ (datasets differing in exactly one record), and for every possible output set $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{M}$ is the randomized privacy mechanism (query answering function)
- $D, D'$ are **neighboring datasets** (differ in at most one record)
- $\epsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (a non-negative real number)
- $e^{\epsilon}$ is the multiplicative bound on output probability ratios

## 1.2 The Privacy Budget $\epsilon$ — The Central Knob

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy Budget ($\epsilon$):** A non-negative real number that controls the privacy-utility trade-off. **Smaller $\epsilon$** means **stronger privacy** but **less accurate** answers. **Larger $\epsilon$** means **weaker privacy** but **more accurate** answers.

| Privacy Regime | $\epsilon$ Value | Practical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Strong Privacy** | $\epsilon \leq 0.1$ | High noise, near-perfect privacy |
| **Moderate Privacy** | $0.1 < \epsilon \leq 1$ | Typical production deployments |
| **Weak Privacy** | $\epsilon > 1$ | Apple uses $\epsilon = 2$ to $8$ historically |
| **Non-Private** | $\epsilon = \infty$ | Equivalent to releasing raw data |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Randomized Response"

> [!TIP]
> **The Coin-Flip Survey Analogy:** Imagine a survey asking a sensitive question: *"Have you ever cheated on an exam?"* If you answer truthfully, anyone seeing your response knows a private fact about you. **Differential Privacy solves this through the Randomized Response Technique (introduced by Warner, 1965):**
> 1. Flip a fair coin (in private).
> 2. If **Heads** → answer truthfully.
> 3. If **Tails** → flip again; answer **Yes** or **No** based on the second coin.
> 
> Now, the interviewer **cannot distinguish** whether your answer reflects your truth or the coin. Yet the *aggregate* — the true proportion of "Yes" answers in the population — is still mathematically recoverable from the noisy responses. This is the essence of differential privacy: **plausible deniability for each individual, statistical fidelity for the group.**

## 1.4 Why Differential Privacy? — Motivation

- **Traditional anonymization fails:** Removing names is not enough (linkage attacks, auxiliary information).
- **Mathematical guarantee:** DP provides a *provable*, *quantifiable* privacy bound that does not depend on the adversary's computational power or background knowledge.
- **Composability:** Privacy loss from multiple queries can be tracked and bounded.
- **Post-processing immunity:** Any data-independent transformation of a DP output remains DP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Global vs. Local Differential Privacy

| Property | **Global DP (Central Model)** | **Local DP (Distributed Model)** |
|---|---|---|
| Trusted Curator | Yes — curator holds raw data | No — data is perturbed on user device |
| Privacy Applied | At query/release time | At data collection time |
| Utility | Higher accuracy | Lower accuracy (more noise) |
| Examples | US Census 2020 ($\epsilon \approx 17$ across queries) | Google's RAPPOR, Apple's Emoji collection |

## 2.2 Sensitivity — The "How Much Does One Person Matter?" Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Sensitivity ($\Delta f$)** of a real-valued function $f : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^k$ is the maximum $L_1$ or $L_2$ change in the function's output when *one record* is added or removed from the dataset.

**$L_1$-Sensitivity:**

$$\Delta_1 f = \max_{D, D' \,:\, \Vert D - D' \Vert_1 = 1} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$$

**$L_2$-Sensitivity:**

$$\Delta_2 f = \max_{D, D' \,:\, \Vert D - D' \Vert_1 = 1} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_2$$

## 2.3 Core DP Mechanisms

### 2.3.1 Laplace Mechanism (for $\epsilon$-DP)

Add noise drawn from a zero-mean Laplace distribution with scale $b = \Delta_1 f / \epsilon$:

$$\mathcal{M}_L(D, f, \epsilon) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta_1 f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

The Laplace distribution PDF:

$$p(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Gaussian Mechanism (for $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP)

Add noise from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ where:

$$\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta_2 f \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$(\epsilon, \delta)$-Relaxed Differential Privacy:** Allows a small failure probability $\delta$ in exchange for lower noise. Necessary for Gaussian noise since pure Gaussian cannot satisfy pure $\epsilon$-DP for finite $\epsilon$.

### 2.3.3 Exponential Mechanism (for non-numeric queries)

Selects output $r \in \mathcal{R}$ with probability proportional to a utility score $u(D, r)$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}_E(D) = r] \propto \exp\left(\frac{\epsilon \cdot u(D, r)}{2 \Delta u}\right)$$

Used when the query output is categorical (e.g., "which hospital has highest disease rate?").

## 2.4 Composition Theorems — Tracking Cumulative Privacy Loss

### 2.4.1 Sequential Composition

If $\mathcal{M}_1$ satisfies $\epsilon_1$-DP and $\mathcal{M}_2$ satisfies $\epsilon_2$-DP, then their **adaptive** sequential release satisfies:

$$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2$$

Generalized to $k$ mechanisms: $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i$.

### 2.4.2 Advanced Composition (Dwork-Rothblum)

For $k$ mechanisms each satisfying $\epsilon$-DP:

$$\epsilon_{\text{total}} \leq \sqrt{2k \ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \epsilon + k \epsilon (e^{\epsilon} - 1)$$

with a failure probability $\delta'$.

### 2.4.3 Parallel Composition

If mechanisms operate on **disjoint** subsets of the dataset, privacy loss **does not accumulate** — only the *maximum* $\epsilon$ is consumed.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Domain / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Pure $\epsilon$-DP bound | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | All DP mechanisms |
| $L_1$ Sensitivity | $\Delta_1 f = \max_{\Vert D - D' \Vert_1 = 1} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$ | Counting queries, sums |
| $L_2$ Sensitivity | $\Delta_2 f = \max_{\Vert D - D' \Vert_1 = 1} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_2$ | Vector-valued queries |
| Laplace noise scale | $b = \Delta_1 f / \epsilon$ | Numerical queries |
| Gaussian noise std. dev. | $\sigma \geq \Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$ | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP |
| Sequential composition | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \epsilon_i$ | Adaptive queries |
| Parallel composition | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \epsilon_i$ | Disjoint data partitions |
| Exponential mechanism probability | $\Pr[r] \propto \exp(\epsilon u(D,r) / 2\Delta u)$ | Categorical outputs |
| Randomized response (binary) | $\Pr[\text{Yes} \mid \text{truth=Yes}] = 0.75$ | $\epsilon = \ln 3$ local DP |
| Post-processing | $\mathcal{M}(D) \xrightarrow{f} f(\mathcal{M}(D))$ is DP if $f$ is data-independent | Any transformation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Laplace Noise Achieves $\epsilon$-DP

**Claim:** $\mathcal{M}_L(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta_1 f / \epsilon)$ satisfies $\epsilon$-DP.

**Step 1 — Setup the ratio of output densities.**
For any value $t \in \mathbb{R}$, the density of the output under dataset $D$ is:

$$p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D)}(t) = \frac{\epsilon}{2 \Delta_1 f} \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon \vert t - f(D) \vert}{\Delta_1 f}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Form the likelihood ratio for neighboring $D, D'$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D)}(t)}{p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D')}(t)} &= \frac{\exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon \vert t - f(D) \vert}{\Delta_1 f}\right)}{\exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon \vert t - f(D') \vert}{\Delta_1 f}\right)} \\
&= \exp\left(\frac{\epsilon}{\Delta_1 f}\left(\vert t - f(D) \vert - \vert t - f(D') \vert\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the triangle inequality.**
By the reverse triangle inequality, $\vert \vert t - f(D) \vert - \vert t - f(D') \vert \vert \leq \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D)}(t)}{p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D')}(t)} &\leq \exp\left(\frac{\epsilon \cdot \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert}{\Delta_1 f}\right) \\
&\leq \exp\left(\frac{\epsilon \cdot \Delta_1 f}{\Delta_1 f}\right) \quad \text{(by definition of sensitivity)}\\
&= e^{\epsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
Therefore $p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D)}(t) \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot p_{\mathcal{M}_L(D')}(t)$ for *every* $t$, which integrates to the set-based DP definition. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity of the Counting Query

**Query:** $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (counting how many records have property $x_i = 1$).

**Step 1 — Define neighboring datasets.**
Let $D'$ differ from $D$ by exactly one record. WLOG, assume one record flipped from $0 \to 1$ (adding a 1).

**Step 2 — Compute the change in $f$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert f(D) - f(D') \vert &= \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i' \right\vert \\
&= \vert x_k - x_k' \vert \quad \text{(only the } k\text{-th record differs)} \\
&= \vert 0 - 1 \vert = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.**
$\Delta_1 f = 1$ for the counting query. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Sequential Composition of Privacy Budgets

**Theorem:** If $\mathcal{M}_1$ is $\epsilon_1$-DP and $\mathcal{M}_2$ is $\epsilon_2$-DP, the joint release $(\mathcal{M}_1(D), \mathcal{M}_2(D))$ is $(\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2)$-DP.

**Step 1 — Apply the DP bound twice.** For any output pair $(s_1, s_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)(D) = (s_1, s_2)] &\leq e^{\epsilon_1} \Pr[(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)(D') = (s_1, s_2)] \quad \text{(Step 1: apply } \mathcal{M}_1 \text{ DP)} \\
&= e^{\epsilon_1} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1] \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D) = s_2 \mid \mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bound the conditional probability using $\mathcal{M}_2$ DP.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D) = s_2 \mid \mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1] &\leq e^{\epsilon_2} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D') = s_2 \mid \mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)(D) = (s_1, s_2)] &\leq e^{\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1] \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D') = s_2 \mid \mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1] \\
&= e^{\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2} \Pr[(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)(D') = (s_1, s_2)] \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Differentially Private Mean Release

**Problem:** A hospital holds records of 1000 patients. We want to release the **average age** of patients. Use the Laplace mechanism with $\epsilon = 1$, ages bounded in $[0, 120]$.

**Step 1 — Express the mean as a sum.**
$f(D) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i$, where $a_i \in [0, 120]$.

**Step 2 — Sensitivity of the mean.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_1 f &= \max_{\Vert D - D' \Vert_1 = 1} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \max_{a_i, a_i'} \vert a_i - a_i' \vert \\
&= \frac{120 - 0}{1000} = 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample Laplace noise.**

$$
\text{Noise} \sim \text{Lap}\left(b = \frac{0.12}{1}\right) = \text{Lap}(0.12)
$$

**Step 4 — Release.** Output $f(D) + \text{Lap}(0.12)$. This satisfies $\epsilon = 1$ DP.

**Interpretation:** The released value could differ from the true mean by as much as $\sim 0.36$ (with 95% probability, since $b \ln 20 \approx 0.36$). For 1000 records this is acceptable, but for small $n$ the relative error grows.

## 3.5 Utility Analysis Matrix

| **Mechanism** | **Privacy Guarantee** | **Error Magnitude (Utility)** | **Query Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laplace | Pure $\epsilon$-DP | $O\left(\frac{\Delta_1 f \cdot \log k}{\epsilon}\right)$ with $k$ queries | Numerical |
| Gaussian | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP | $O\left(\frac{\Delta_2 f \cdot \sqrt{\log(1/\delta)}}{\epsilon}\right)$ | High-dimensional |
| Exponential | $\epsilon$-DP | $O\left(\frac{\log \vert \mathcal{R} \vert \cdot \Delta u}{\epsilon}\right)$ | Categorical |
| Randomized Response | $\epsilon = \ln 3$ local DP | $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ per estimate | Binary surveys |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Differential Privacy Framework — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Dataset D with n individuals] --> B[Neighboring Dataset D-prime differ by 1 record]
    A --> C[Privacy Mechanism M randomized algorithm]
    D[Privacy Budget epsilon] --> C
    E[Sensitivity Delta f] --> C
    C --> F[Perturbed Output t]
    F --> G[Statistical Aggregates released to public]
    B --> H[Same Mechanism Applied]
    H --> I[Output t-prime]
    F --> J[Indistinguishability Test]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Probability Ratio bounded by e to the epsilon?}
    K -->|Yes| L[epsilon-DP Satisfied]
    K -->|No| M[Privacy Violation]
```

## 4.2 Mechanism Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Query Type] --> B{Output Domain}
    B -->|Numerical scalar| C[Compute L1 Sensitivity]
    C --> D[Add Laplace Noise scale = Delta/epsilon]
    D --> E[Pure epsilon-DP Output]
    B -->|Numerical vector| F[Compute L2 Sensitivity]
    F --> G[Add Gaussian Noise]
    G --> H[epsilon delta-DP Output with delta failure prob]
    B -->|Categorical non numeric| I[Define Utility Score u]
    I --> J[Exponential Mechanism]
    J --> K[Sample proportional to exp of epsilon times u over 2 Delta u]
    K --> L[Pure epsilon-DP Output]
    B -->|Yes No Binary| M[Randomized Response]
    M --> N[Local epsilon-DP with epsilon = ln 3]
```

## 4.3 Composition of Privacy Budgets

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Query 1 epsilon 1] --> R1[Released Stat 1]
    Q2[Query 2 epsilon 2] --> R2[Released Stat 2]
    Q3[Query k epsilon k] --> Rk[Released Stat k]
    R1 --> S[Sequential Composition]
    R2 --> S
    Rk --> S
    S --> T[Total epsilon = sum of epsilons]
    R1 --> P[Parallel Composition on disjoint partitions]
    R2 --> P
    Rk --> P
    P --> U[Total epsilon = max of epsilons]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: DP in Real Systems (e.g., US Census)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TrustedCurator["TRUSTED CURATOR BOUNDARY"]
        RD[Raw Respondent Data PII Protected] --> ENG[Disclosure Avoidance System]
        ENG --> TM[TopDown Algorithm]
        TM --> NS[Noise Injection per query cell]
    end
    NS --> REL[Public Release Microdata]
    subgraph AttackModel["ADVERSARY MODEL"]
        ATT[Attacker with Auxiliary Data] --> ATT2[Reidentification Attempt]
        REL --> ATT2
    end
    ENG --> EP[epsilon budget allocated per query workload]
    EP --> EP2[Total epsilon about 17 across all 2020 Census releases]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define $\epsilon$-differential privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget $\epsilon$.**

**Model Answer:**

$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy is a property of a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ that ensures for any pair of neighboring datasets $D, D'$ (differing in one record) and any output set $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

The privacy budget **$\epsilon$** quantifies the *maximum allowable information leakage*. Smaller $\epsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy (output is almost the same regardless of any individual's data). Larger $\epsilon$ ⇒ weaker privacy but higher utility. Typical values: $\epsilon = 0.1$ (strong) to $\epsilon = 8$ (weak). **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`
**What is global sensitivity? Compute the $L_1$-sensitivity of the query $f(D) = \text{count of records satisfying condition } C$.**

**Model Answer:**

Global sensitivity $\Delta f$ is the maximum change in the query output when one record is added/removed from the dataset.

For the counting query $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[x_i \in C]$, when a single record is added or removed, the count changes by exactly **1**. Therefore:

$$\Delta_1 f = 1$$

This is independent of the dataset size. **[3 marks]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Laplace mechanism** in detail. Derive the noise scale required to achieve $\epsilon$-DP. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The Laplace mechanism adds noise sampled from a zero-mean Laplace distribution to a numerical query output:

$$\mathcal{M}_L(D, f, \epsilon) = f(D) + Y, \quad Y \sim \text{Lap}(b)$$

**Derivation of scale $b$:**

We need: $\forall t, \; p(t \mid D) \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot p(t \mid D')$.

The density of the output at point $t$ under dataset $D$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t \mid D) &= \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert t - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The likelihood ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(t \mid D)}{p(t \mid D')} &= \exp\left(\frac{\vert t - f(D') \vert - \vert t - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the triangle inequality: $\vert \vert t - f(D) \vert - \vert t - f(D') \vert \vert \leq \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \leq \Delta_1 f$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p(t \mid D)}{p(t \mid D')} &\leq \exp\left(\frac{\Delta_1 f}{b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to be $\leq e^{\epsilon}$, we need $\Delta_1 f / b \leq \epsilon$, i.e.,

$$\boxed{b = \frac{\Delta_1 f}{\epsilon}}$$

This guarantees $\epsilon$-DP. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b)** A dataset of 10,000 patients contains binary indicators for a disease. Use the Laplace mechanism to release the count of diseased patients with $\epsilon = 0.5$. What is the expected magnitude of noise added? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The query is $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{10000} x_i$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 2:** Sensitivity: $\Delta_1 f = 1$ (one patient record changes the count by at most 1).

**Step 3:** Laplace noise scale:

$$
b = \frac{\Delta_1 f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2
$$

**Step 4:** The expected absolute noise is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\vert Y \vert] = b = 2
$$

**Step 5:** Variance:

$$
\text{Var}(Y) = 2b^2 = 8
$$

So the released count is the true count ± approximately **2 patients on average**, with 95% of noise samples within $\pm 2b \ln 20 \approx 6$ patients. The relative error is $2 / 10000 = 0.02\%$, which is highly acceptable. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2022]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Laplace mechanism** and **Gaussian mechanism**. Under what conditions is the Gaussian mechanism preferred? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | **Laplace Mechanism** | **Gaussian Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| Noise Distribution | $\text{Lap}(b)$ | $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ |
| Sensitivity Used | $L_1$ sensitivity $\Delta_1 f$ | $L_2$ sensitivity $\Delta_2 f$ |
| Privacy Guarantee | Pure $\epsilon$-DP | Approximate $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP |
| Noise Scale | $b = \Delta_1 f / \epsilon$ | $\sigma = \Delta_2 f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$ |
| Tail Behavior | Heavy exponential tails | Lighter Gaussian tails |

**When Gaussian is preferred:**

1. **High-dimensional outputs:** For $k$-dimensional vector queries, $L_2$ sensitivity is often $\sqrt{k}$ times smaller than $L_1$ (e.g., for histograms with $k$ bins). Gaussian noise of order $\sqrt{k}$ is much smaller than Laplace noise of order $k$.

2. **Composition efficiency:** Gaussian noise composes additively under advanced composition (sub-linear in $k$ queries), while Laplace noise composes linearly. For many queries, Gaussian achieves the same total $\epsilon$ with less cumulative noise.

3. **Federated learning & deep learning:** DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) uses Gaussian noise for gradient perturbations because gradient vectors are high-dimensional and norms are naturally $L_2$.

4. **Tighter bounds in practice:** For small $\delta$, the Gaussian mechanism achieves $O(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)})$ noise instead of $O(\log(1/\delta))$ for the Laplace mechanism. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b)** Explain the **sequential composition** and **parallel composition** theorems of differential privacy with a suitable example for each. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Sequential Composition:**
If $\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{M}_k$ are each $\epsilon_i$-DP, the joint release satisfies $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i\right)$-DP.

*Example:* A hospital releases the average age ($\epsilon_1 = 0.5$) and the disease count ($\epsilon_2 = 0.5$) on the same dataset. The total privacy loss is $\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 = 1.0$.

**Proof sketch:** By induction, applying the DP bound sequentially yields multiplicative $e^{\epsilon_1} \cdot e^{\epsilon_2} \cdots e^{\epsilon_k} = e^{\sum \epsilon_i}$.

**Parallel Composition:**
If mechanisms operate on **disjoint partitions** of the dataset, the total privacy loss is only $\max_i \epsilon_i$ (not the sum).

*Example:* A company has data partitioned by region (North, South, East, West). It runs a counting query with $\epsilon = 0.5$ on **each region** independently. Since each individual's data appears in only one partition, the total privacy loss is $\max(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5) = 0.5$, **not** $4 \times 0.5 = 2.0$.

This is critical for designing privacy-preserving distributed systems — it means the **same privacy budget can be reused across disjoint data silos**. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_1$ vs $L_2$ sensitivity:** Laplace uses $L_1$, Gaussian uses $L_2$. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that "neighboring" means one-record difference** — not arbitrary changes. Some students write $D$ and $D'$ as completely different datasets, which is wrong.
> 3. **Skipping the proof of the privacy bound** in mechanism derivation questions. Always show the likelihood ratio step explicitly.
> 4. **Stating "DP = anonymization"** — this is conceptually wrong. DP is a *property of the algorithm*, not a data transformation. Removing identifiers ≠ DP.
> 5. **Not mentioning composition** when designing systems — KTU examiners often give a 2-mark follow-up asking "what if multiple queries are made?"

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Differential Privacy** provides a mathematical guarantee that the inclusion/exclusion of any single record barely changes the output distribution.
- The **privacy budget $\epsilon$** controls the trade-off: smaller ⇒ more private, less accurate.
- **Global sensitivity** measures the maximum influence of one record on the query output.
- The **Laplace mechanism** adds $\text{Lap}(\Delta_1 f / \epsilon)$ noise and gives pure $\epsilon$-DP.
- The **Gaussian mechanism** adds $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ noise and gives $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP with $\sigma = \Delta_2 f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \epsilon$.
- The **Exponential mechanism** is used for non-numeric (categorical) outputs.
- **Sequential composition:** privacy budgets **add** when queries are run on the same data.
- **Parallel composition:** privacy budgets **do not add** when queries target disjoint partitions.
- **Post-processing immunity:** any data-independent transformation preserves the DP guarantee.
- **Randomized Response** is a classic local-DP mechanism for binary questions with $\epsilon = \ln 3$.
- **Real-world deployments:** US Census 2020, Google RAPPOR, Apple emoji usage, Microsoft telemetry, Uber internal analytics.
- **Killer exam phrases to use:** "indistinguishability of neighboring datasets," "privacy budget exhaustion," "composition theorem," "sensitivity-based noise calibration," "plausible deniability."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legal and Compliance Frameworks: Overview of GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and their implications for Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Legal and Compliance Frameworks: Overview of GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and their implications for Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Legal and Compliance Frameworks in Big Data

In the context of Big Data Security under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT743)**, **Legal and Compliance Frameworks** refer to the structured set of statutory laws, regulatory mandates, and binding obligations that govern how organizations collect, store, process, share, and dispose of large-scale datasets. These frameworks are essential because Big Data operations frequently cross geographic, industrial, and digital boundaries, exposing organizations to multiple overlapping jurisdictions.

The three foundational frameworks that every KTU Big Data Security student must master are:

**A. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)**

> [!NOTE]
> **GDPR (EU Regulation 2016/679)** is a comprehensive European Union data protection and privacy law that became enforceable on **25 May 2018**. It governs the processing of personal data of individuals residing in the European Economic Area (EEA), regardless of where the processing organization is geographically located.

**B. California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)**

> [!NOTE]
> **CCPA (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1798.100 – 1798.199.100)** is a state-level privacy statute in California, United States, effective from **1 January 2020**. It grants California residents new rights over their personal information held by businesses.

**C. Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)**

> [!NOTE]
> **HIPAA (Public Law 104-191, 1996)** is a United States federal statute that regulates the protection of Protected Health Information (PHI) by covered entities and business associates operating within the U.S. healthcare ecosystem.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Framework | Analogy | Plain-English Intuition |
|-----------|---------|-------------------------|
| **GDPR** | A strict passport control officer in the EU | Before any data crosses the border (or even inside the EU), the officer demands to know **who you are, why you want it, and where you are taking it**. If you cannot justify it, the data does not move. |
| **CCPA** | A transparent real estate agent in California | Every consumer can walk into the data broker's office and ask: "Show me everything you have on me, and let me delete it or stop you from selling it." Transparency is the currency. |
| **HIPAA** | A doctor's strict oath of patient confidentiality | A physician cannot gossip about a patient's illness. Similarly, hospitals, insurers, and any partner handling medical records are bound by oath-like secrecy. |

---

### 1.3 Why These Frameworks Matter for Big Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Big Data systems, data is generated at **velocity, volume, and variety** (the 3Vs). Legal frameworks act as a fourth dimension — **Veracity of lawful origin and lawful processing** — ensuring that the data lake does not become a legal liability lake.

### 1.4 Core Standard Metrics Used in These Frameworks

- **GDPR maximum administrative fine:** **€20 million** or **4% of total worldwide annual turnover** of the preceding financial year, whichever is higher.
- **CCPA penalty per violation:** **$2,500** for unintentional; **$7,500** for intentional or involving minors.
- **HIPAA Tier 1 penalty cap (per violation, 2024 adjusted):** **$137 to $68,928** depending on culpability tier.
- **GDPR breach notification window:** **72 hours** from the moment of becoming aware.
- **HIPAA breach notification window:** **60 days** from discovery for individuals; sooner for media if breach affects **more than 500** individuals.
- **CCPA opt-out response window:** **15 business days** to acknowledge and act upon consumer requests.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Foundational Principles of GDPR (Article 5)

GDPR is built on seven bedrock principles. Every Big Data processing activity involving EU residents must satisfy these principles:

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency** — Data must be processed legally, fairly, and in a transparent manner.
2. **Purpose Limitation** — Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes.
3. **Data Minimization** — Data must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary.
4. **Accuracy** — Data must be accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date.
5. **Storage Limitation** — Data must be kept in a form that permits identification for no longer than is necessary.
6. **Integrity and Confidentiality** — Data must be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security.
7. **Accountability** — The controller is responsible for, and able to demonstrate, compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In a 14-mark question on GDPR, listing all **7 principles with a one-line explanation** for each is worth **3–4 marks** alone. Examiners expect verbatim memorization of the principle names.

---

### 2.2 Six Core Consumer Rights under CCPA

1. **Right to Know** — What personal information is being collected, used, shared, or sold.
2. **Right to Access** — Request a copy of the specific pieces of personal information collected.
3. **Right to Delete** — Request deletion of personal information collected from the consumer.
4. **Right to Opt-Out of Sale** — Direct a business to stop selling their personal information.
5. **Right to Non-Discrimination** — Businesses cannot deny service or charge different prices for exercising rights.
6. **Right to Limit Use of Sensitive Personal Information** (added by CPRA amendments, 2023).

---

### 2.3 The HIPAA Privacy Rule and Security Rule

HIPAA has two principal regulatory components relevant to Big Data:

- **Privacy Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart E):** Establishes national standards to protect individuals' medical records and other personal health information.
- **Security Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart C):** Sets standards for the protection of electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI) through administrative, physical, and technical safeguards.

### 2.4 Covered Entities and Business Associates (HIPAA)

- **Covered Entities:** Health plans, healthcare clearinghouses, and healthcare providers that transmit health information electronically.
- **Business Associates:** Third-party vendors that create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI on behalf of a covered entity (e.g., cloud service providers hosting Big Data healthcare analytics).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Compliance Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> Although legal frameworks are qualitative, KTU examiners love **penalty calculation problems**. Memorize the formulas below.

$$
\text{GDPR Fine} = \min\!\left( 20{,}000{,}000,\; 0.04 \times \text{Annual Global Turnover} \right)
$$

$$
\text{GDPR Lower-Tier Fine} = \min\!\left( 10{,}000{,}000,\; 0.02 \times \text{Annual Global Turnover} \right)
$$

$$
\text{CCPA Fine} = N_v \times P_v \quad \text{where } N_v = \text{ number of violations},\; P_v \in \{2{,}500,\; 7{,}500\}
$$

$$
\text{HIPAA Tier Penalty} = T_i \times N_i \times C_{\text{annual-cap}}
$$

$$
\text{GDPR Breach Window} \le 72 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{HIPAA Breach Window to Individuals} \le 60 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{CCPA Opt-Out Response Window} \le 15 \text{ business days}
$$

$$
\text{HIPAA Media Notification Threshold: } N_{\text{affected}} > 500 \text{ individuals}
$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Cheat Sheet Table (Comparison Matrix)

| Parameter | GDPR | CCPA | HIPAA |
|-----------|------|------|-------|
| Jurisdiction | European Union (27 member states) | California, USA (state-level) | United States (federal) |
| Effective Date | 25 May 2018 | 1 January 2020 | 1996 (Privacy Rule 2003) |
| Scope of Data | Personal data of EU/EEA residents | Personal information of California residents | Protected Health Information (PHI) |
| Data Subject Rights | 8 explicit rights | 6 explicit rights | Limited disclosure rights |
| Maximum Penalty | €20M or 4% turnover | $7,500 per violation | $1.5M annual cap per tier |
| Breach Notification | 72 hours | Reasonable time (no fixed limit) | 60 days |
| Extraterritorial Reach | **Yes** (applies globally if EU data) | Limited (CA businesses) | Limited (US healthcare) |
| Big Data Relevance | Pseudonymization, DPIA mandates | Data broker regulations | De-identification standards |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **CCPA "personal information"** with **GDPR "personal data."** CCPA defines PI more broadly to include household-level information, IP addresses, and inferences drawn from data.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Big Data systems using **Hadoop, Spark, Kafka, or cloud-based data lakes**, these frameworks translate into concrete engineering controls:

- **GDPR Article 25 — Privacy by Design** requires embedding pseudonymization at the data ingestion layer (e.g., hashing user IDs in Kafka topics before they reach the data lake).
- **HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2))** mandates the removal of **18 specific identifiers** (names, dates, biometric identifiers, etc.) before data can be used for analytics.
- **CCPA Right to Delete** requires that Big Data systems implement a **deletion propagation mechanism** that can purge records from HDFS, downstream data warehouses, and backup archives within a defined SLA.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Implication Matrix for Big Data

### 3.1 GDPR Implications for Big Data Architecture — Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data systems process petabyte-scale personal data. GDPR imposes architectural duties at every stage of the data pipeline.**

**Step 1: Data Collection Stage**

GDPR Article 6 requires a lawful basis for processing. For Big Data, the organization must specify **which basis** (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, public task, legitimate interest) applies to each dataset.

**Step 2: Data Storage Stage**

GDPR Article 5(1)(c) — Data Minimization — must be enforced. In Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS), this translates to dropping unused columns at the Hive/Spark level and applying column-level encryption using tools like **HDFS Transparent Encryption** or **Hadoop KMS**.

**Step 3: Processing Stage**

Article 22 prohibits solely automated decision-making, including profiling, that produces legal or similarly significant effects. Spark MLlib pipelines that score credit or employment must include a **human-in-the-loop** review step.

**Step 4: Cross-Border Transfer Stage**

Article 44–50 restrict data transfers outside the EEA. Big Data systems must use **Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs)**, **Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs)**, or rely on an **Adequacy Decision**.

**Step 5: Data Subject Rights Stage**

Articles 15–22 grant rights to access, rectify, erase, restrict processing, data portability, and object. In a Big Data system, this requires a **Data Subject Rights Management (DSRM) module** that can locate and act upon a single individual's data across all distributed nodes.

**Step 6: Breach Notification Stage**

Article 33 mandates notification to the supervisory authority within **72 hours**. Big Data systems must have automated **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** integrations that timestamp incidents from the moment of awareness.

**Step 7: Accountability Stage**

Article 30 requires a **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)**. Article 35 mandates a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** for high-risk processing — directly applicable to large-scale profiling, biometric processing, or public health analytics.

---

### 3.2 CCPA Implications for Big Data — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**Step 1: Determine Applicability**

A business is subject to CCPA if it meets **one or more** of the following thresholds:
- Annual gross revenues **exceeding $25 million**.
- Buys, sells, or shares the personal information of **100,000 or more** California consumers, households, or devices.
- Derives **50% or more** of its annual revenues from selling personal information.

**Step 2: Provide Notice at Collection**

Section 1798.100(b) requires that at or before the point of collection, businesses inform consumers about the categories of PI collected and the purposes.

**Step 3: Honor Consumer Rights**

The system must be able to:
- Verify the consumer's identity (with reasonable security measures).
- Provide a copy of the data within **45 days**.
- Honor opt-out requests by waiting at least **12 months** before re-prompting consent.

**Step 4: Service Provider and Contractor Distinction**

CCPA distinguishes between **service providers** (who process PI on the business's behalf) and **contractors** (who receive PI for a business purpose). Both must be bound by contracts prohibiting secondary use — crucial in Big Data pipelines that pass data to multiple downstream analytics vendors.

---

### 3.3 HIPAA Implications for Big Data — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**Step 1: Identify PHI**

Under the **Safe Harbor method (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2))**, 18 identifiers must be removed:

1. Names
2. Geographic subdivisions smaller than a state
3. Dates (except year) related to an individual
4. Telephone numbers
5. Fax numbers
6. Email addresses
7. Social Security numbers
8. Medical record numbers
9. Health plan beneficiary numbers
10. Account numbers
11. Certificate/license numbers
12. Vehicle identifiers
13. Device identifiers
14. URLs
15. IP addresses
16. Biometric identifiers
17. Full-face photographs
18. Any other unique identifying number, characteristic, or code

**Step 2: Choose De-identification Method**

The two approved HIPAA de-identification methods are:
- **Safe Harbor** (the 18-identifier removal list above).
- **Expert Determination** (a statistician certifies that the risk of re-identification is very small).

**Step 3: Implement Security Rule Safeguards**

For Big Data systems holding ePHI:
- **Administrative safeguards** — workforce training, contingency planning, access management policies.
- **Physical safeguards** — facility access controls, workstation security, device controls.
- **Technical safeguards** — access control (RADIUS, LDAP), audit logging (Apache Ranger, AWS CloudTrail), transmission security (TLS), integrity controls (digital signatures, checksums).

**Step 4: Business Associate Agreement (BAA)**

Every third-party Big Data vendor (e.g., AWS for hosting health data lakes) must sign a **BAA** before accessing PHI.

**Step 5: Breach Risk Assessment**

The four-factor risk assessment (45 CFR 164.402(2)):
- Nature and extent of PHI involved.
- Unauthorized person who used or received the PHI.
- Whether PHI was actually acquired or viewed.
- Extent to which the risk has been mitigated.

---

### 3.4 Mathematical Walkthrough of Penalty Calculations

#### Scenario A: GDPR Maximum Fine for a €500M Turnover Company

$$
\text{GDPR Fine} = \min\!\left( 20{,}000{,}000,\; 0.04 \times 500{,}000{,}000 \right)
$$

$$
\text{GDPR Fine} = \min\!\left( 20{,}000{,}000,\; 20{,}000{,}000 \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{GDPR Fine} = \text{€}20{,}000{,}000}
$$

**Interpretation:** When 4% of turnover equals €20M, both criteria are met. The fine is €20 million.

---

#### Scenario B: GDPR Maximum Fine for a €100M Turnover Company

$$
\text{GDPR Fine} = \min\!\left( 20{,}000{,}000,\; 0.04 \times 100{,}000{,}000 \right)
$$

$$
\text{GDPR Fine} = \min\!\left( 20{,}000{,}000,\; 4{,}000{,}000 \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{GDPR Fine} = \text{€}4{,}000{,}000}
$$

**Interpretation:** For smaller firms, the 4% turnover rule produces a lower fine than the absolute cap.

---

#### Scenario C: CCPA Penalty for 3,000 Unintentional Violations

$$
\text{CCPA Fine} = N_v \times P_v = 3{,}000 \times 2{,}500
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CCPA Fine} = \$7{,}500{,}000}
$$

---

#### Scenario D: HIPAA Annual Cap Calculation

$$
\text{HIPAA Annual Cap} = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ USD per provision per year}
$$

For a single provision violated multiple times:

$$
\text{Total Penalty} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i
$$

where $T_i$ is the penalty per violation based on the culpability tier (Tiers 1–4), and the sum is capped at the annual limit per provision.

---

### 3.5 Cross-Framework Implication Matrix for Big Data

| Big Data Stage | GDPR Touchpoint | CCPA Touchpoint | HIPAA Touchpoint |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Data Ingestion | Article 6 lawful basis | Notice at collection (1798.100) | Minimum necessary standard |
| Data Storage | Article 5(1)(c) minimization | Secure storage obligation | Security Rule §164.306 |
| Processing | Article 22 automated decisions | Opt-out of sale | De-identification (164.514) |
| Cross-Border Transfer | Articles 44–50 | N/A (US-based) | N/A (US-based) |
| Data Subject Request | Articles 15–22 | Sections 1798.105, 1798.110 | Access right (164.524) |
| Breach Response | Article 33 (72 hours) | California Civil Code 1798.82 | 60 days + 60 days extension |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Big Data Processing Pipeline Mapped to Legal Compliance Layers

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams the intersection of Big Data processing stages with the three legal frameworks. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid syntax rules. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[STAGE 1: DATA INGESTION]
    nodeB[STAGE 2: DATA STORAGE - DATA LAKE WAREHOUSE]
    nodeC[STAGE 3: PROCESSING ANALYTICS ML]
    nodeD[STAGE 4: DATA SHARING MONETIZATION]
    nodeE[STAGE 5: BREACH RESPONSE DISPOSAL]

    nodeF[GDPR ARTICLE 6: LAWFUL BASIS]
    nodeG[CCPA 1798.100: NOTICE AT COLLECTION]
    nodeH[HIPAA 164.502: MINIMUM NECESSARY]

    nodeI[GDPR ARTICLE 5 1C: DATA MINIMIZATION]
    nodeJ[CCPA SECURE STORAGE OBLIGATION]
    nodeK[HIPAA SECURITY RULE 164.306]

    nodeL[GDPR ARTICLE 22: AUTOMATED DECISIONS]
    nodeM[CCPA 1798.120: OPT OUT OF SALE]
    nodeN[HIPAA SAFE HARBOR DE IDENTIFICATION]

    nodeO[GDPR ARTICLES 44 50: CROSS BORDER TRANSFER]
    nodeP[CCPA SERVICE PROVIDER CONTRACTS]
    nodeQ[HIPAA BUSINESS ASSOCIATE AGREEMENT]

    nodeR[GDPR ARTICLE 33: 72 HOUR NOTIFICATION]
    nodeS[CCPA 1798.82: BREACH DISCLOSURE]
    nodeT[HIPAA 164.404: 60 DAY INDIVIDUAL NOTICE]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE

    nodeA -.CONTROLS.-> nodeF
    nodeA -.CONTROLS.-> nodeG
    nodeA -.CONTROLS.-> nodeH

    nodeB -.CONTROLS.-> nodeI
    nodeB -.CONTROLS.-> nodeJ
    nodeB -.CONTROLS.-> nodeK

    nodeC -.CONTROLS.-> nodeL

    nodeC -.CONTROLS.-> nodeN

    nodeD -.CONTROLS.-> nodeO

    nodeD -.CONTROLS.-> nodeQ

    nodeE -.CONTROLS.-> nodeR
    nodeE -.CONTROLS.-> nodeS
    nodeE -.CONTROLS.-> nodeT
```

flowchart TD

    nodeB[IS DATA PERSONAL OR PHI?]
    nodeC[APPLY GDPR ARTICLES 44 50]

    nodeA -- NO --> nodeJ
