## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Define GDPR. List any three lawful bases for processing personal data under Article 6 of GDPR.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **GDPR Definition:** The General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679 is a comprehensive European Union regulation on data protection and privacy that became enforceable on 25 May 2018, governing the processing of personal data of individuals within the European Economic Area.

**Three Lawful Bases (Article 6):**

1. **Consent** — The data subject has given freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent.
2. **Contract** — Processing is necessary for the performance of a contract with the data subject.
3. **Legal Obligation** — Processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three lawful bases.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**What is the breach notification timeline under HIPAA, and when must media notification be triggered?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Under **45 CFR 164.404**, a covered entity must notify affected individuals of a breach of unsecured PHI **without unreasonable delay and in no case later than 60 days** after discovery of the breach.

**Media Notification Trigger:**
Under **45 CFR 164.406**, if a breach involves **more than 500 individuals** in a state or jurisdiction, the covered entity must notify prominent media outlets serving the affected area. The media notification should occur within the same 60-day window.

*(3 marks: 2 for the 60-day rule, 1 for the 500-individual media trigger.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The model below provides both **Question A** and **Question B** as full alternative selections.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**(a)** Explain the seven foundational principles of GDPR (Article 5) in detail with examples relevant to Big Data systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the implications of GDPR for Big Data architectures. How does Privacy by Design (Article 25) translate into engineering controls in a Hadoop/Spark-based data lake? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**The Seven Principles of GDPR Article 5 with Big Data Examples:**

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency** — Every Kafka topic ingesting EU user data must be registered with a documented lawful basis (e.g., consent, contract). The data catalog must publish a transparent record.

2. **Purpose Limitation** — Data ingested for fraud detection cannot be repurposed for marketing without fresh consent. Big Data systems must enforce this via **data tagging and access control lists** at the metadata layer (e.g., Apache Atlas).

3. **Data Minimization** — A Spark ingestion job should drop unused PII columns at the schema level using `select` projection before writing to HDFS, not store everything and filter later.

4. **Accuracy** — Master Data Management (MDM) pipelines must periodically reconcile stale records. Example: outdated customer addresses must be refreshed quarterly.

5. **Storage Limitation** — A retention policy must automatically delete raw clickstream data after **6 months** unless a documented lawful basis exists for longer retention.

6. **Integrity and Confidentiality** — Encryption at rest (HDFS Transparent Encryption), encryption in transit (TLS for Kafka), and key management (HashiCorp Vault or Hadoop KMS) are mandatory.

7. **Accountability** — The Data Protection Officer (DPO) must maintain a **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)** under Article 30 and conduct **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)** for high-risk processing.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per principle with appropriate Big Data context.)*

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**GDPR Implications for Big Data Architecture and Privacy by Design:**

**Implication 1: Pseudonymization at Ingestion (Article 25)**
Pseudonymization means replacing direct identifiers (name, email, SSN) with tokens. In a Big Data pipeline, this is implemented at the **Kafka consumer** or **Spark Streaming** layer using deterministic hashing with a salted key, ensuring that downstream systems cannot directly identify the data subject.

**Implication 2: Data Subject Rights Management**
Articles 15–22 require organizations to honor access, rectification, erasure, and portability requests. In a Hadoop ecosystem, this requires a **Data Subject Rights Module** that:
- Searches all HDFS partitions for the subject's pseudonymized ID.
- Triggers a Spark job to export, rectify, or delete the data.
- Propagates deletions to Kafka compacted topics, Hive tables, and HBase.

**Implication 3: DPIA for Big Data Analytics (Article 35)**
A DPIA is mandatory when profiling is performed on a large scale. For a Spark MLlib model predicting creditworthiness, the DPO must conduct a DPIA documenting the data flow, retention, and human review mechanism.

**Implication 4: Cross-Border Transfer Controls (Articles 44–50)**
Big Data systems using multi-region cloud (e.g., AWS Frankfurt to AWS Oregon) must apply **Standard Contractual Clauses** and use region-specific encryption keys managed through **AWS KMS** with per-region key policies.

**Privacy by Design as an Engineering Mandate:**
Privacy by Design (Article 25) translates into **default privacy settings** — meaning the default Spark configuration must be the most privacy-preserving. Examples:
- Field-level encryption enabled by default.
- External table sharing disabled by default in Hive.
- Audit logging of all data subject queries enabled by default in Apache Ranger.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for implications, 3 marks for Privacy by Design, 2 marks for engineering examples.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**(a)** Compare GDPR, CCPA, and HIPAA across any seven significant parameters. Explain how each framework handles the right to deletion/erasure of personal data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital's Big Data analytics platform on AWS holds electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI) of 5 million patients. Discuss the HIPAA Privacy Rule and Security Rule obligations, including the de-identification requirements under the Safe Harbor method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Comparison Across Seven Parameters:**

| Parameter | GDPR | CCPA | HIPAA |
|-----------|------|------|-------|
| Jurisdiction | EU (supranational) | California, USA | USA (federal) |
| Scope | Personal data | Personal information | Protected Health Information |
| Extraterritorial | Yes | No | No |
| Breach Notification | 72 hours | Reasonable time | 60 days |
| Data Subject Rights | 8 rights | 6 rights | Limited |
| Max Penalty | €20M / 4% turnover | $7,500 per violation | $1.5M annual cap |
| Sector Applicability | All sectors | All sectors | Healthcare only |

**Right to Deletion/Erasure:**

- **GDPR Article 17 — Right to Erasure ("Right to Be Forgotten"):** Data subjects can request deletion when data is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn, or processing is unlawful. Exceptions include freedom of expression, legal obligations, and public interest in health.

- **CCPA Section 1798.105 — Right to Delete:** California consumers can request deletion of personal information collected from them. Businesses must delete and direct service providers to delete. Exceptions include completing the transaction, security, lawful compliance, and internal uses aligned with consumer expectations.

- **HIPAA — No General Right to Delete:** HIPAA does not grant patients a right to demand deletion of their PHI. Instead, it grants a **right to amend (45 CFR 164.526)** and **right to access (164.524)**. Covered entities may retain PHI for the duration required by law (typically 6 years under 164.530(j)).

*(7 marks: 4 marks for the comparison table, 3 marks for the deletion discussion.)*

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**HIPAA Privacy Rule Obligations:**

1. **Minimum Necessary Standard (164.502(b)):** The hospital's Big Data platform must ensure that only the minimum necessary PHI is accessed for any given purpose. Spark jobs run by researchers must be tagged with purpose codes enforced by Apache Ranger policies.

2. **Patient Rights:** Patients have rights to access, amend, request an accounting of disclosures, and request restrictions on disclosures. The hospital must implement a portal enabling these requests.

3. **De-identification under Safe Harbor (164.514(b)(2)):**

   The hospital must remove all **18 identifiers** before using PHI for analytics:
   - Names → replaced with study IDs.
   - Geographic subdivisions smaller than state → truncated to first 3 ZIP digits or "000" for low-population ZIPs.
   - Dates (admission, discharge, DOB) → kept only as year.
   - Phone, fax, email, SSN, MRN, account numbers, certificate numbers, vehicle IDs, device IDs, URLs, IP addresses, biometric identifiers, full-face photos, and other unique codes → all removed.

4. **Workforce Training (164.530):** All engineers, data scientists, and contractors accessing ePHI must undergo annual HIPAA training.

**HIPAA Security Rule Obligations for AWS-Hosted ePHI:**

1. **Administrative Safeguards (164.308):** Risk analysis, sanction policy, contingency plan, BAA with AWS.
2. **Physical Safeguards (164.310):** AWS is responsible, but the hospital must verify AWS's SOC 2 reports.
3. **Technical Safeguards (164.312):** Access control via IAM, audit controls via CloudTrail and S3 access logs, integrity controls via SHA-256 checksums, transmission security via TLS 1.2+.

4. **Breach Notification (164.404):** A breach affecting more than 500 individuals requires media notification; the **HHS Secretary** must also be notified within 60 days; individuals must be notified within 60 days; if the breach exceeds 500 individuals, notice to HHS must be filed within **60 days** of discovery.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for Privacy Rule, 4 marks for Security Rule and de-identification.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GDPR fines** — Students write €4 million when the question asks for the lower-tier fine. Always re-read whether the question asks for the **upper** or **lower** tier.
> 2. **Wrong breach timeline** — HIPAA is 60 days, GDPR is 72 hours, CCPA has no fixed number. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 18 identifiers in HIPAA Safe Harbor** — Examiners expect at least 8–10 of the 18 identifiers listed verbatim. A vague "de-identify the data" answer is worth 1 mark; listing identifiers is worth 4.
> 4. **Skipping the Big Data angle** — A question on GDPR's implications for Big Data cannot be answered with generic privacy law definitions. KTU requires engineering-level examples (Kafka, Spark, HDFS, Hadoop, Apache Ranger).
> 5. **Not stating jurisdiction** — GDPR is EU, CCPA is California (state), HIPAA is US (federal). Examiners deduct marks for saying "GDPR is a US law."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GDPR** is a **pan-European** regulation effective from **25 May 2018**, with maximum fines of **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**.
- **GDPR's 7 principles** (Article 5) are: Lawfulness/Fairness/Transparency, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity/Confidentiality, Accountability.
- **GDPR breach notification** must occur within **72 hours** to the supervisory authority.
- **CCPA** is a **California state law** effective **1 January 2020** granting **6 core rights** to consumers.
- **CCPA penalty** is **$2,500** per unintentional violation and **$7,500** per intentional violation or violation involving minors.
- **HIPAA** is a **US federal law** governing Protected Health Information (PHI) through the **Privacy Rule** and **Security Rule**.
- **HIPAA Safe Harbor** mandates removal of **18 specific identifiers** before data can be used for analytics without patient authorization.
- **HIPAA breach notification** to affected individuals must occur within **60 days** of discovery; media notification is triggered if more than **500** individuals are affected.
- **Big Data engineering controls** under these frameworks include: pseudonymization at ingestion, encryption at rest and in transit, access control via Apache Ranger, audit logging via CloudTrail or Auditbeat, and a Data Subject Rights Management module.
- **Privacy by Design (Article 25)** is an engineering mandate — the most privacy-preserving configuration must be the **default** configuration.
- **Cross-border data transfer** under GDPR requires Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules, or an Adequacy Decision; HIPAA's analog is the **Business Associate Agreement (BAA)**.
- **Accountability** is the seventh GDPR principle — it requires demonstrable compliance, not just aspirational compliance.
- **The 4-factor HIPAA breach risk assessment** considers the nature of PHI, the unauthorized recipient, actual acquisition or viewing, and mitigation extent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Landscape: Common threats: Insider threats, APTs, ransomware, and DDoS attacks in Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threat Landscape: Common threats: Insider threats, APTs, ransomware, and DDoS attacks in Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ Threat Landscape in Big Data: Common Threats (Module 3)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Threat Landscape (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat Landscape:** The complete spectrum of potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, attack vectors, and adversarial actors that could potentially compromise the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**) of a Big Data ecosystem. In Big Data contexts, the threat landscape is amplified due to the **5 V's** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value — making data a high-value target.

The **Threat Landscape** in Big Data security refers to the evolving ecosystem of malicious activities targeting distributed storage systems (HDFS, NoSQL), real-time processing frameworks (Apache Kafka, Spark Streaming), and analytics platforms (Hadoop, Hive). Unlike traditional IT infrastructure, Big Data environments face threats that exploit:

1. The **distributed nature** of compute clusters (e.g., hundreds of nodes).
2. The **heterogeneity** of data sources (structured, unstructured, semi-structured).
3. The **real-time processing** pipelines where data never "rests."
4. The **multi-tenancy** in cloud-based Big Data deployments.

### 1.2 The Four Primary Threat Categories

| Threat | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Insider Threats** | Malicious or negligent actions originating from within the organization that have authorized access to Big Data systems. |
| **Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)** | Sophisticated, stealthy, long-term cyber-espionage campaigns typically sponsored by nation-states or organized crime groups. |
| **Ransomware** | Malicious software that encrypts data and demands payment for decryption keys, weaponized against petabyte-scale data lakes. |
| **DDoS Attacks** | Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks that overwhelm Big Data infrastructure with volumetric or protocol-level traffic floods. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Big Data Bank Vault

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Big Data cluster as a massive, 24/7-operating bank vault holding trillions of dollars (data records).**

- **Insider Threats** = A *corrupt bank employee* who already has the master key but uses it to siphon money. They know the alarm codes, the vault layout, and the shift schedules.
- **APTs** = A *highly trained spy team* that infiltrates the bank over **months**, studying blueprints, installing hidden cameras, and waiting for the perfect moment — not for quick robbery, but for *strategic data exfiltration*.
- **Ransomware** = A *criminal who breaks in, sprays acid on all the cash (making it unreadable), and demands $50 million for the antidote formula*.
- **DDoS Attacks** = An *angry mob that floods the bank's entrance*, preventing legitimate customers (data analysts, ETL jobs) from entering. The vault itself isn't touched, but the business grinds to a halt.

### 1.4 Why Big Data is Uniquely Vulnerable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is Big Data more vulnerable than traditional data systems?"* Always mention these 3 points:

1. **Expanded Attack Surface**: A Hadoop cluster may have 1,000+ nodes, each a potential entry point.
2. **Inherent Trust Within Clusters**: Default Hadoop/YARN configurations assume all nodes are trustworthy — a fatal flaw exploited by insiders.
3. **Valuable Data Aggregation**: Combining diverse datasets creates a *single high-value target* — the "**data lake honeypot**" effect.

### 1.5 Key Metrics Used in Threat Quantification

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time to identify a security incident — measured in **hours**.
- **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR):** Average time to contain and remediate — measured in **hours**.
- **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):** Financial impact per year — measured in **USD/year**.
- **Attack Vector Count:** Number of distinct entry points — dimensionless integer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Insider Threats — Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Definition (NIST SP 800-53 Aligned)

An **insider threat** is the risk that an individual with authorized access to an organization's Big Data systems will use that access, either intentionally or unintentionally, to cause harm. This includes **malicious insiders** (disgruntled employees), **negligent insiders** (careless staff), and **compromised insiders** (credentials stolen by external attackers).

### 2.1.2 Taxonomy of Insider Threats in Big Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Major Sub-Categories (Memorize for KTU):**

1. **Malicious Data Exfiltration**: Copying sensitive PII from HDFS to external USB/external cloud.
2. **Privilege Abuse**: Using admin YARN/RBAC credentials to manipulate MapReduce jobs for sabotage.
3. **Data Poisoning**: Injecting malicious records into training datasets used for ML models — corrupting predictions.
4. **Negligent Misconfiguration**: Accidentally exposing Hive metadata service on public internet.

### 2.1.3 Detection Mechanism — User Behavior Analytics (UBA)

Mathematically, insider threat detection relies on **anomaly scoring**:

$$S_{user} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$$

where:
- $S_{user}$ = Anomaly score of the user.
- $x_i$ = Observed behavior metric $i$ (e.g., data downloaded, files accessed, login time).
- $\mu_i$ = Historical mean of behavior metric $i$.
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to metric $i$.
- $n$ = Total number of monitored behaviors.

A user with $S_{user}$ exceeding threshold $T_{alert}$ is flagged as suspicious.

---

## 2.2 Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **APT Definition (KTU 2024):** An **Advanced Persistent Threat** is a prolonged, targeted cyber-attack in which an intruder gains prolonged, unauthorized access to a Big Data network, with the intent to exfiltrate sensitive data rather than cause immediate damage. The attacker uses advanced evasion techniques and operates under the radar for **months to years**.

### 2.2.2 The APT Lifecycle (7 Kill Chain Phases)

| Phase | Name | Big Data Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reconnaissance** | Scanning Hadoop NameNode ports (8020, 9000, 50070). |
| 2 | **Weaponization** | Crafting malicious JAR files disguised as MapReduce jobs. |
| 3 | **Delivery** | Phishing emails to data engineers with "Spark optimization script" attachments. |
| 4 | **Exploitation** | Exploiting unpatched YARN vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2021-25642). |
| 5 | **Installation** | Planting backdoors in edge nodes (often via SSH key theft). |
| 6 | **Command & Control (C2)** | Beaconing out via DNS tunnels to exfiltrate Hive query results. |
| 7 | **Actions on Objectives** | Stealing petabytes of customer analytics data to a foreign server. |

### 2.2.3 APT Detection — Information Entropy Model

APTs cause subtle entropy shifts in network traffic. **Shannon Entropy** quantifies this:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)$$

where:
- $H(X)$ = Entropy in bits per symbol.
- $p(x_i)$ = Probability of observing network packet pattern $x_i$.
- $N$ = Number of distinct packet patterns observed.

> A sudden drop in entropy may indicate C2 beaconing (repetitive patterns), while spikes indicate data exfiltration.

---

## 2.3 Ransomware — Weaponized Data Encryption

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

**Ransomware** is a category of malicious software that encrypts files on infected systems, rendering them inaccessible, and demands a ransom payment (typically in cryptocurrency) in exchange for the decryption key.

### 2.3.2 How Ransomware Targets Big Data Specifically

1. **Data Lake Encryption**: Modern ransomware variants (e.g., **Big Game Hunting** groups like REvil, DarkSide) specifically target Hadoop HDFS and AWS S3 buckets.
2. **Backup Targeting**: Before encryption, they identify and destroy **snapshot backups** to eliminate recovery options.
3. **Double/Triple Extortion**: Beyond encryption, attackers threaten to **leak stolen data publicly** and **DDoS** the company if ransom is unpaid.

### 2.3.3 Risk Quantification — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

where:
- $\text{SLE}$ = Single Loss Expectancy = Asset Value $\times$ Exposure Factor.
- $\text{ARO}$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence.

**Example:** If a Big Data cluster holds data worth $\text{USD }10{,}000{,}000$ and ransomware has a 20% impact factor, with 0.3 attacks/year:

$$\text{SLE} = 10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.20 = 2{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{ALE} = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 0.3 = \text{USD }600{,}000/\text{year}$$

---

## 2.4 Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) Attacks

### 2.4.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **DDoS Definition:** A distributed denial-of-service attack is a malicious attempt to disrupt the normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target or its surrounding infrastructure with a flood of Internet traffic originating from multiple compromised systems (botnets).

### 2.4.2 Three Sub-Categories of DDoS

| Sub-Type | OSI Layer | Attack Vector |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric** | Layer 3-4 | UDP floods, ICMP floods, amplification attacks. |
| **Protocol** | Layer 4 | SYN floods, Ping of Death, Smurf attacks. |
| **Application** | Layer 7 | HTTP GET/POST floods, Slowloris targeting REST APIs used by Spark. |

### 2.4.3 Throughput & Defense Capacity Model

The effective attack success occurs when incoming traffic exceeds server capacity:

$$T_{attack} > C_{server}$$

where:
- $T_{attack}$ = Aggregate attack traffic bandwidth (Gbps).
- $C_{server}$ = Server processing capacity (Gbps).

For Big Data ecosystems, **NameNode metadata service** is a single point of failure that can be DDoS'd with relatively low traffic (e.g., 10 Gbps can cripple a 50-node cluster's metadata operations).

### 2.4.4 DDoS Detection — z-score Anomaly Model

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

where:
- $x$ = Current traffic measurement.
- $\mu$ = Historical mean traffic.
- $\sigma$ = Historical standard deviation.

A traffic spike with $z > 3$ (i.e., 3 standard deviations above normal) typically indicates a DDoS attack in progress.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Use Case | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_{user} = \sum w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$ | Insider threat scoring | Weights, observations, means | Score |
| $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | APT entropy detection | Probability of events | Bits |
| $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Ransomware risk cost | Loss, occurrence rate | USD/year |
| $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | DDoS anomaly detection | Traffic stats | Dimensionless |
| $\text{Exposure Factor} = \text{Loss}/\text{Asset Value}$ | Any threat | Fraction of impact | Ratio |
| $T_{attack} > C_{server}$ | DDoS success condition | Bandwidth | Gbps |
| $\text{MTTD} + \text{MTTR} = \text{MTTR}_{total}$ | Incident response | Detection + recovery | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always substitute numerical values and show unit conversions. Never leave the answer in symbolic form unless explicitly asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: DDoS Impact on a Hadoop NameNode

### Problem Setup

A Hadoop cluster's **NameNode** can handle a maximum of $C_{server} = 8 \text{ Gbps}$ of metadata request traffic. During normal operations, the average traffic is $\mu = 2 \text{ Gbps}$ with a standard deviation of $\sigma = 0.5 \text{ Gbps}$. A volumetric DDoS attack generates sustained traffic of $x = 5.5 \text{ Gbps}$.

### Step 1: Compute the z-score

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting values:

$$z = \frac{5.5 - 2}{0.5}$$

$$z = \frac{3.5}{0.5}$$

$$z = 7.0$$

### Step 2: Interpret the z-score

Since $z = 7.0 > 3$, the traffic exceeds **7 standard deviations** above the mean — an unambiguous statistical anomaly confirming a DDoS attack.

### Step 3: Check Server Saturation

We compare:

$$T_{attack} = 5.5 \text{ Gbps}$$

$$C_{server} = 8 \text{ Gbps}$$

Here, $T_{attack} < C_{server}$, so the NameNode *survives* this specific attack but performance is degraded.

### Step 4: Determine Threshold for Saturation

For the NameNode to be saturated, attack traffic must exceed capacity:

$$x_{critical} > C_{server} = 8 \text{ Gbps}$$

Converting to z-score:

$$z_{critical} = \frac{8 - 2}{0.5} = 12$$

The DDoS attack must generate traffic 12 standard deviations above normal to fully saturate the NameNode.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation: Ransomware Risk for a Big Data Cluster

### Problem Setup

A multinational e-commerce company operates a Hadoop cluster storing customer transaction data valued at $\text{Asset Value} = \text{USD }50{,}000{,}000$. Historical data indicates that a ransomware attack has a $\text{ARO} = 0.25$ (1 attack every 4 years). The exposure factor — the fraction of data rendered inaccessible during an attack — is $0.40$.

### Step 1: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{SLE} = 50{,}000{,}000 \times 0.40$$

$$\text{SLE} = \text{USD }20{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 2: Calculate Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{ALE} = 20{,}000{,}000 \times 0.25$$

$$\text{ALE} = \text{USD }5{,}000{,}000/\text{year}$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

The company faces an expected annual loss of **USD 5 million** due to ransomware. This justifies investing up to (but not exceeding) USD 5 million/year in ransomware defense (immutable backups, EDR tools, security training).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Threat Attributes

| Attribute | Insider Threats | APTs | Ransomware | DDoS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Attack Duration** | Hours to Months | Months to Years | Hours to Days | Minutes to Hours |
| **Skill Level Required** | Low to Medium | Very High | Medium | Low to Medium |
| **Primary Goal** | Data theft / Sabotage | Espionage / Exfiltration | Financial extortion | Service disruption |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Very Hard | Very Hard | Medium | Easy |
| **Primary Mitigation** | UEBA, RBAC, DLP | Threat hunting, Zero Trust | Immutable backups, EDR | Rate limiting, Anycast |
| **Financial Impact** | Medium-High | Very High (espionage) | Very High (extortion) | Medium (downtime) |
| **Big Data Specific Vector** | Hive query abuse | YARN backdoor | HDFS encryption | NameNode flooding |

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Threat Quantification — Layered Risk Score

For an organization, total threat exposure can be computed as:

$$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot R_i$$

where $R_i$ is the risk score for threat $i$ and $w_i$ is its organizational weight.

Using 1-10 scale:

| Threat | $R_i$ | $w_i$ | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Insider Threats | 7 | 0.30 | 2.10 |
| APTs | 6 | 0.20 | 1.20 |
| Ransomware | 9 | 0.25 | 2.25 |
| DDoS | 5 | 0.25 | 1.25 |
| **Total** | — | **1.00** | **6.80** |

This score of **6.8/10** indicates a **HIGH** organizational threat exposure level.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
