When Marriott acquired Starwood in 2016, the acquisition **technical due-diligence** failed to identify that the Starwood reservation system was already compromised by Chinese state actors (since 2014). The legal liability flowed through to Marriott under the principle of **successor liability** for data protection. Solution: include a **cybersecurity compliance audit** as a *condition precedent* in every M&A, covering patch status, encryption posture, IAM hygiene, and prior breach history.

**Step 2 — Facebook–CA Lesson on Consent Management (2 marks):**
Cambridge Analytica's harvesting exploited a **purpose-drift** vulnerability: users consented to *academic research* but the data was sold for *political advertising*. A robust **Consent Management Platform** records the *specific purpose* in machine-readable form (e.g., as a **W3C DATA License / DPV** tag) and the downstream pipeline enforces **purpose-binding**, refusing to use data for any purpose not in the original consent manifest.

**Step 3 — Synthesis (2 marks):**
Both cases teach that compliance is not just a *legal* checkbox but a *technical contract* enforced by the data pipeline itself, with auditable evidence at every handoff.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Mathematical Definition of Differential Privacy (2 marks):**

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for any two datasets $D_1$ and $D_2$ that differ in at most one record, and for any output $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The presence or absence of any *single individual's record* changes the probability of any output by at most a factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$. Smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy.

**Step 3 — Laplace Mechanism (2 marks):**
For a numeric query $f$, add noise drawn from a Laplace distribution:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the *sensitivity* of the query (maximum change when one record is added/removed).

**Step 4 — Anthem Application (2 marks):**
Suppose Anthem wants to publish *"average age of patients diagnosed with condition X"*. A DP-aware pipeline would:
1. Compute $f(D) = \text{mean(age)}$,
2. Bound sensitivity $\Delta f = \frac{\text{max\_age} - \text{min\_age}}{N}$,
3. Inject $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ noise,
4. Release the noisy aggregate, ensuring no individual patient's age can be inferred — even if the attacker has the auxiliary information that *one specific person is in the dataset*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse GDPR fines with HIPAA fines.** GDPR is a *percentage-of-turnover* model (max 4%); HIPAA penalties are *tiered per record* (up to \$1.5 M per year per category). Mixing the two loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Do not write "anonymization" when you mean "pseudonymization".** The GDPR treats them very differently — anonymized data is *out of scope*, pseudonymized data is *still in scope*.
> 3. **Always quantify the case study.** "Marriott was fined" earns 0.5 marks. "Marriott was fined £18.4 M by the UK ICO" earns full marks.
> 4. **For 7-mark sub-parts, draw a diagram.** A neat block diagram adds **1–2 marks** even if the surrounding text is average.
> 5. **Differential privacy must include the *ε* (epsilon) parameter in the answer.** Writing the formula without $\varepsilon$ is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this checklist the night before the exam — it covers ~85% of the marks available for this topic.**

- [ ] **GDPR maximum fine = 4% of global turnover OR €20 M**, whichever is **higher** (upper tier).
- [ ] **GDPR breach notification = 72 hours** from awareness to supervisory authority.
- [ ] **HIPAA Safe Harbor = remove 18 specific identifiers** to de-identify PHI.
- [ ] **PCI-DSS v4.0** mandates AES-128+ for transmission and "rendering unreadable" when stored.
- [ ] **k-anonymity** requires every record to be indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others.
- [ ] **Differential privacy** uses the **Laplace mechanism**: $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
- [ ] **Five case studies to remember:** Equifax, Marriott, Facebook–CA, Anthem, Capital One.
- [ ] **Five technical solutions:** Encryption, Tokenization, ABAC, Immutable Logs, CSPM.
- [ ] **Five governance solutions:** DPO, DPA, Vendor Risk Mgmt, Privacy-by-Design, IRP.
- [ ] **Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)** is *technically hard* due to backups and ML-model entanglement.
- [ ] **M&A** carries **successor liability** for data breaches (Marriott lesson).
- [ ] **Consent Management Platforms** enforce **purpose-binding** to prevent purpose drift.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth = 7 layers** (Governance → Discovery → Encryption → IAM → Network → Audit → IRP).
- [ ] **CSPM** is mandatory for cloud-resident big data (Capital One lesson).
- [ ] **Immutable audit logs** (e.g., AWS QLDB, blockchain anchors) satisfy GDPR Art. 30 + SOX 404.
- [ ] **Average cost of non-compliance = \$4.45 M per breach** (IBM 2023 benchmark).
- [ ] **PAM + MFA** would have prevented the Anthem breach (privileged-credential theft).
- [ ] **Automated patching within 14 days** would have prevented the Equifax breach.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STRIDE, DREAD models for Big Data systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** STRIDE, DREAD models for Big Data systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 STRIDE Model — Formal Definition

**STRIDE** is a **threat classification framework** originally proposed by *Microsoft* (Praerit Garg and Loren Kohnfelder, 1999) that systematically categorizes security threats into **six fundamental classes**. In the context of Big Data systems, STRIDE is used as a structured vocabulary to identify, enumerate, and reason about *what can go wrong* in distributed data processing pipelines (Hadoop, Spark, Kafka, NoSQL clusters, cloud data lakes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STRIDE is a CLASSIFICATION model — it answers: "What kind of threat is this?"**
> It does NOT rank severity. That is the job of DREAD.

The acronym stands for:

| Letter | Threat Class | Core Question Answered |
|--------|--------------|------------------------|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | "Is the identity of a user, node, or service authentic?" |
| **T** | **Tampering** | "Can an attacker modify data in transit or at rest?" |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | "Can a user deny having performed an action (no audit trail)?" |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | "Can confidential data leak to unauthorized parties?" |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | "Can the system be made unavailable or degraded?" |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | "Can a low-privilege actor gain admin/root access?" |

## 1.2 DREAD Model — Formal Definition

**DREAD** is a **quantitative risk-scoring model** (also by Microsoft) used to **rank and prioritize** threats once they have been identified (typically via STRIDE). Each identified threat is scored on five dimensions, and the aggregate score determines its risk priority for remediation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DREAD is a RISK-PRIORITY model — it answers: "How bad is this threat, and what should we fix first?"**

DREAD evaluates:

| Letter | Dimension | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **D** | **Damage Potential** | How much harm if the threat succeeds |
| **R** | **Reproducibility** | How easily the attack can be reproduced |
| **E** | **Exploitability** | How easy it is to launch the attack |
| **A** | **Affected Users** | How many users/systems are impacted |
| **D** | **Discoverability** | How easy it is to find the vulnerability |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Airport Security Audit**
> 
> Imagine KTU's campus security team is auditing the **Kerala International Airport**.
> 
> **STRIDE** is the **checklist of bad things that could happen**:
> - S — Someone uses a *fake passport* (Spoofing)
> - T — Luggage tags are *switched* (Tampering)
> - R — A passenger claims *"I never boarded that flight"* with no CCTV (Repudiation)
> - I — A screen displays *someone's ticket to the wrong gate* (Info Disclosure)
> - D — All check-in counters crash (Denial of Service)
> - E — A cleaning staff uses a *master security key* they shouldn't have (Elevation)
> 
> **DREAD** is the **scoring sheet** the auditors then fill out: "The fake-passport case scores 9/10 on Damage, 3/10 on Reproducibility..." giving a final number that tells the airport *which loophole to plug first*.
> 
> In a **Hadoop cluster**, STRIDE enumerates *what* can go wrong; DREAD tells engineers *which* to fix first.

## 1.4 Why These Models Matter for Big Data

Big Data systems are inherently **distributed, multi-tenant, and data-intensive**, making threat modeling non-trivial. STRIDE/DREAD provide:

- A **common language** for security engineers, architects, and auditors.
- **Repeatable methodology** for threat assessment during design.
- **Compliance mapping** with NIST, ISO 27001, and KTU cybersecurity electives.
- **Prioritization** in environments where thousands of attack surfaces exist (every HDFS block, every YARN container, every Kafka topic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT743 / Module 4):** KTU explicitly tests the student's ability to (i) name all six STRIDE categories with Big Data examples, and (ii) compute DREAD risk scores for sample threats.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 STRIDE in Big Data — Detailed Threat Mapping

Each STRIDE category is now mapped to **real Big Data components** (HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, Spark, Kafka, HBase, Hive, ZooKeeper, Sqoop, Oozie).

### S — Spoofing in Big Data
- **Kerberos ticket impersonation** in unsecured Hadoop clusters.
- **Fake DataNode registration** with the NameNode.
- **Spoofed Kafka brokers** intercepting producer/consumer traffic.
- **Forged IAM credentials** in cloud-hosted EMR/HDInsight/Dataproc clusters.

### T — Tampering in Big Data
- **HDFS block manipulation** during replication (man-in-the-middle on unencrypted RPC).
- **MapReduce job injection** — submitting malicious JARs to YARN.
- **Tampered log files** in Spark's event logs.
- **Hive Metastore corruption** via unsanitized DDL.

### R — Repudiation in Big Data
- **Missing audit logs** in older HBase versions.
- **User actions without traceability** when Kerberos + audit log aggregation is absent.
- **S3 access without CloudTrail** in data lake architectures.
- **Spark job submissions without user-attribution** in shared YARN queues.

### I — Information Disclosure in Big Data
- **HDFS data unencrypted at rest** (no HDFS Transparent Encryption).
- **Sniffed Hadoop RPC traffic** on flat Layer-2 networks.
- **Hive query result leakage** through unsecured JDBC endpoints.
- **Inferring PII** from anonymized clickstreams (re-identification attacks).

### D — Denial of Service in Big Data
- **NameNode memory exhaustion** via millions of small files.
- **YARN resource starvation** (one tenant hogging the cluster).
- **Kafka broker flooding** with junk topics.
- **MapReduce job with infinite-loop mappers** consuming all containers.

### E — Elevation of Privilege in Big Data
- **YARN job submission to privileged queue** via misconfiguration.
- **Root SSH access on every DataNode** (anti-pattern).
- **HiveServer2 becoming system admin** via unsafe UDFs.
- **Kafka ACL bypass** due to misconfigured `*` permissions.

## 2.2 DREAD Scoring Methodology — Stepwise Logic

The DREAD model uses an **ordinal scale (typically 1 to 10)** for each dimension. The aggregate risk score is computed as:

$$
R_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D'}{5}
$$

where the second **D** is *Discoverability*, distinguished as $D'$ to avoid confusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on DREAD formula variants:** Some industry references use a **multiplicative** form: $R = D \times R \times E \times A \times D'$. The **arithmetic mean** form is the original Microsoft formulation and is what KTU examiners expect.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Variables | Range | Units |
|---|----------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Arithmetic mean DREAD: $R = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D'}{5}$ | All ordinal 1–10 | $[1, 10]$ | Unitless score |
| 2 | Multiplicative DREAD (alt.): $R = D \cdot R \cdot E \cdot A \cdot D'$ | All ordinal 1–10 | $[1, 100000]$ | Unitless score |
| 3 | STRIDE cardinality: $\vert S_{\text{set}} \vert = 6$ | Threat classes | Fixed | Categorical |
| 4 | Risk priority band: High if $R \ge 7$ | DREAD score | 0–10 | Band label |
| 5 | Risk priority band: Medium if $4 \le R < 7$ | DREAD score | 0–10 | Band label |
| 6 | Risk priority band: Low if $R < 4$ | DREAD score | 0–10 | Band label |
| 7 | STRIDE × DREAD coverage: Threats $= \text{STRIDE} \times \text{Asset}$ | Categorical × integer | Per-asset | Threats |
| 8 | Big Data STRIDE per layer: Hadoop has $\approx 4$ layers | Layers | 1–4 | Layers |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloudera / Hortonworks** design reviews use STRIDE for architectural risk analysis.
- **Apache Ranger** and **Apache Sentry** map controls back to STRIDE categories.
- **NIST SP 800-154 (Draft)** for Big Data security mirrors STRIDE's structure.
- **DREAD scores** feed into **CVSS v3.1** style prioritization in DevSecOps pipelines.
- **PCI-DSS / HIPAA compliance audits** often demand STRIDE-based threat models for data lakes handling PII/PHI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** For KTU project work, when designing a mini-Hadoop cluster, document your STRIDE table as a 6-row matrix against assets (NameNode, DataNode, HDFS, YARN) and score each cell with DREAD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — DREAD Scoring for a Big Data Threat

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU-style problem):**
> Consider the threat: *A non-authenticated user submits a malicious MapReduce JAR to YARN, which then executes as the `yarn` user and exfiltrates HDFS data.*
> 
> Apply DREAD scoring on a 1–10 scale and determine the risk band.

### Step 1 — Assign Damage Potential (D)

If the JAR exfiltrates HDFS contents (potentially terabytes of customer data), the **business and legal damage is severe**.
$$
D = 9
$$
*Reasoning: Total data exfiltration = max damage tier.*

### Step 2 — Assign Reproducibility (R)

Once the JAR is crafted, the attack can be launched **repeatedly** by any user with YARN submission rights.
$$
R = 8
$$
*Reasoning: Requires only valid YARN client access, no special timing.*

### Step 3 — Assign Exploitability (E)

YARN REST APIs and `yarn jar` CLI are **publicly documented**. Exploiting requires only basic Hadoop knowledge.
$$
E = 7
$$
*Reasoning: Standard off-the-shelf exploit skill; no zero-day required.*

### Step 4 — Assign Affected Users (A)

All users of the HDFS dataset are affected; in a multi-tenant cluster, this is the **entire organization**.
$$
A = 10
$$
*Reasoning: Cluster-wide impact, cross-tenant.*

### Step 5 — Assign Discoverability (D')

YARN's job-submission API is **externally visible** (port 8088 on default configs); the vulnerability is easily found via Nmap.
$$
D' = 9
$$
*Reasoning: Trivial port scan reveals endpoint.*

### Step 6 — Compute Aggregate DREAD Score (Arithmetic Mean)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{DREAD}} &= \frac{D + R + E + A + D'}{5} \\
&= \frac{9 + 8 + 7 + 10 + 9}{5} \\
&= \frac{43}{5} \\
&= 8.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Risk Band Classification

$$
R_{\text{DREAD}} = 8.6 \;\ge\; 7 \;\Longrightarrow\; \textbf{HIGH RISK}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This threat must be remediated in the *current sprint* with controls such as: Kerberos authentication on YARN, Ranger-based YARN ACLs, container-executor (non-root) sandboxing, and TLS-enabled RPC.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — STRIDE × Big Data Components

The following matrix scores *typical severity* on a 1–5 qualitative scale. (Score is illustrative; the official DREAD computation must be done per-deployment.)

| Big Data Component \ STRIDE | S (Spoofing) | T (Tampering) | R (Repudiation) | I (Info Disclosure) | D (DoS) | E (Elevation) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NameNode** | 3 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 5 |
| **DataNode** | 4 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| **HDFS (data blocks)** | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 2 |
| **YARN ResourceManager** | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 5 |
| **MapReduce / Spark** | 3 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| **Hive Metastore** | 3 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| **HBase RegionServer** | 3 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 3 |
| **Kafka Broker** | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 3 |
| **ZooKeeper Ensemble** | 5 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| **Cloud IAM (S3/ADLS)** | 5 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** A cell with value 5 means "STRIDE class X is highly relevant to component Y in default configurations." A cell with value 1 means "this class is largely mitigated by default."

---

## 3.3 Derived DREAD Worked Example — Low-Risk Threat

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A user tries to read HDFS file metadata (ls) without authentication on a Kerberos-enabled cluster.
> 
> The attack fails — the threat is theoretical.

### Step 1 — Damage
If attempted, only file *names* leak, not contents.
$$
D = 2
$$

### Step 2 — Reproducibility
Cannot reproduce because Kerberos blocks unauthenticated RPC.
$$
R = 1
$$

### Step 3 — Exploitability
Exploit fails at handshake.
$$
E = 1
$$

### Step 4 — Affected Users
None (no data exposed).
$$
A = 1
$$

### Step 5 — Discoverability
The endpoint is *visible* but harmless.
$$
D' = 4
$$

### Step 6 — Compute DREAD

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{DREAD}} &= \frac{2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4}{5} \\
&= \frac{9}{5} \\
&= 1.8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Risk Band

$$
R_{\text{DREAD}} = 1.8 \;<\; 4 \;\Longrightarrow\; \textbf{LOW RISK}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Remediation effort: *nice-to-have* (e.g., add `hadoop.security.authentication` banner), not urgent.

---

## 3.4 Comparative DREAD — Arithmetic vs. Multiplicative

For the HIGH-RISK threat above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{arith}} &= 8.6 \quad (\text{band: HIGH}) \\
R_{\text{mult}} &= 9 \times 8 \times 7 \times 10 \times 9 = 45360 \quad (\text{band: extreme})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **For KTU exams, ALWAYS use the arithmetic-mean formula** unless the question explicitly states "multiplicative." Examiners mark the additive form as the default.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 STRIDE Threat Modeling Workflow for a Hadoop Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Big Data System Asset: Hadoop Cluster] --> A1[Identify Components: NameNode, DataNode, YARN, HDFS]
    A1 --> A2[Decompose Data Flows: Ingest, Process, Store, Serve]
    A2 --> A3[Apply STRIDE per Component: S T R I D E]
    A3 --> A4[List Threats in Threat Table]
    A4 --> A5[Score Each Threat with DREAD]
    A5 --> A6{Compute Aggregate R}
    A6 --> A7[R greater or equal 7: HIGH Risk]
    A6 --> A8[R between 4 and 7: MEDIUM Risk]
    A6 --> A9[R less than 4: LOW Risk]
    A7 --> A10[Remediate This Sprint]
    A8 --> A11[Schedule for Next Release]
    A9 --> A12[Accept and Document]
    A10 --> A13[Re-validate via STRIDE]
    A11 --> A13
    A12 --> A13
```

## 4.2 STRIDE Threat Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SYS[Big Data System] --> S[S: Spoofing Identity]
    SYS --> T[T: Tampering Data]
    SYS --> R[R: Repudiation of Action]
    SYS --> I[I: Information Disclosure]
    SYS --> D[D: Denial of Service]
    SYS --> E[E: Elevation of Privilege]
    S --> S1[Fake Kerberos Principal]
    S --> S2[Spoofed DataNode]
    T --> T1[HDFS Block Modify]
    T --> T2[MapReduce JAR Inject]
    R --> R1[Missing YARN Audit]
    R --> R2[No S3 CloudTrail]
    I --> I1[Unencrypted HDFS]
    I --> I2[RPC Sniffing]
    D --> D1[NameNode Memory Flood]
    D --> D2[Kafka Broker Flood]
    E --> E1[Root SSH on DataNode]
    E --> E2[HiveServer2 UDF Exploit]
```

## 4.3 DREAD Scoring Engine — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TH[Identified Threat from STRIDE] --> Q1[Score Damage D 1 to 10]
    Q1 --> Q2[Score Reproducibility R 1 to 10]
    Q2 --> Q3[Score Exploitability E 1 to 10]
    Q3 --> Q4[Score Affected Users A 1 to 10]
    Q4 --> Q5[Score Discoverability Dprime 1 to 10]
    Q5 --> FORM[Aggregate: R equals D plus R plus E plus A plus Dprime over 5]
    FORM --> BAND{R greater or equal 7}
    BAND -->|Yes| HI[HIGH: Remediate Now]
    BAND -->|No| BAND2{R greater or equal 4}
    BAND2 -->|Yes| MED[MEDIUM: Schedule]
    BAND2 -->|No| LOW[LOW: Accept or Document]
```

## 4.4 STRIDE to Big Data Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Identity and Auth: Kerberos, LDAP]:::layerStyle
    L2[Layer 2: Data at Rest: HDFS, HBase, S3]:::layerStyle
    L3[Layer 3: Data in Motion: Spark, MapReduce, Kafka]:::layerStyle
    L4[Layer 4: Metadata: Hive Metastore, ZooKeeper]:::layerStyle

    L1 -->|S, E| T1[STRIDE Threats]
    L2 -->|T, I| T1
    L3 -->|T, D, I| T1
    L4 -->|R, I| T1
    T1 --> DREAD[DREAD Prioritization Engine]
    DREAD --> OUT[Risk-Ranked Remediation Backlog]

    classDef layerStyle fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List and briefly explain the six categories of the STRIDE threat model as applied to a Hadoop cluster.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correct category, ½ mark for Big Data relevance):**

The STRIDE model, developed by Microsoft, classifies threats into six categories:

1. **Spoofing (S):** Illegally accessing a system by pretending to be another identity, e.g., a forged Kerberos principal in Hadoop or a spoofed Kafka broker.
2. **Tampering (T):** Malicious modification of data, e.g., altering HDFS block contents during replication or injecting malicious MapReduce JARs.
3. **Repudiation (R):** A user denying an action because no audit trail exists, e.g., missing YARN job-submission logs in unconfigured clusters.
4. **Information Disclosure (I):** Exposure of confidential data, e.g., unencrypted HDFS data at rest or RPC traffic sniffed on a flat network.
5. **Denial of Service (D):** Making the system unavailable, e.g., NameNode memory exhaustion from millions of small files.
6. **Elevation of Privilege (E):** A user gaining higher access than entitled, e.g., submitting a YARN job to the `root` queue via misconfigured ACLs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write generic definitions without tying them to Big Data components. You *must* give a Hadoop/HDFS/YARN/Spark example for each to score the full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the DREAD model. Why is it used in conjunction with STRIDE for Big Data security assessment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DREAD is a risk-scoring model that quantitatively **prioritizes** threats identified by STRIDE. Each threat is scored 1–10 on five dimensions:

- **D — Damage Potential:** severity of impact if exploited.
- **R — Reproducibility:** ease of repeating the attack.
- **E — Exploitability:** ease of launching.
- **A — Affected Users:** number of impacted users/systems.
- **D — Discoverability:** ease of finding the vulnerability.

The aggregate risk score is:

$$
R_{\text{DREAD}} = \frac{D + R + E + A + D'}{5}
$$

A score $\ge 7$ is **HIGH**, $4 \le R < 7$ is **MEDIUM**, and $R < 4$ is **LOW**.

**Why paired with STRIDE in Big Data?**
STRIDE *classifies* (asks "what kind?"), while DREAD *quantifies* (asks "how bad?"). In Big Data systems with thousands of attack surfaces (every DataNode, every HDFS block, every Kafka topic), DREAD gives engineers a **numeric priority** so they know which threat to patch first — essential for resource-constrained security teams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not state that DREAD stands for six categories — it is **five**. Do not write the multiplicative formula unless the question asks. Always end with the risk-band thresholds (7, 4) for full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **STRIDE threat modeling methodology** as applied to Big Data systems. List at least **two Big Data examples** for each STRIDE category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Hadoop cluster, consider the following threat:
> *"An attacker who has compromised a single DataNode tampers with HDFS block replicas, altering customer transaction records."*
>
> Apply the **DREAD model** (use a 1–10 scale with appropriate justification for each dimension), compute the aggregate risk score, and classify the threat into HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW band. Suggest **two specific controls** from Apache Ranger or Kerberos. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** STRIDE is a Microsoft-developed threat classification model with six categories: S, T, R, I, D, E.
2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Should show: Big Data System → Decompose → Apply STRIDE → Threat Table → Pass to DREAD → Output. (See SECTION 4.1.)
3. **Two Big Data examples per category (4 marks = 6 categories × 2 examples, choose best 8 in concise form):**
   - **S:** Fake Kerberos principal, spoofed DataNode heartbeat.
   - **T:** HDFS block modification, malicious YARN JAR.
   - **R:** Missing audit log for Hive query, no CloudTrail for S3.
   - **I:** Unencrypted HDFS data, RPC packet sniffing.
   - **D:** NameNode memory exhaustion, Kafka broker flooding.
   - **E:** Root SSH on all DataNodes, YARN privileged-queue abuse.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Damage (D):** Tampered transaction records could cause financial loss, audit failures, regulatory penalties.
$$
D = 9
$$

**Step 2 — Reproducibility (R):** Once one DataNode is compromised, the attacker can repeatedly write to any block.
$$
R = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Exploitability (E):** Requires only a foothold on a single node; standard post-exploitation skill.
$$
E = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Affected Users (A):** All customers with transaction records stored in HDFS.
$$
A = 10
$$

**Step 5 — Discoverability (D'):** Detected only via integrity-checking tools; otherwise silent corruption.
$$
D' = 6
$$

**Step 6 — Aggregate score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{DREAD}} &= \frac{9 + 8 + 7 + 10 + 6}{5} \\
&= \frac{40}{5} \\
&= 8.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Band:** $R = 8.0 \ge 7 \;\Rightarrow\;$ **HIGH RISK**

**Step 8 — Two specific controls (1 mark each):**
1. **Apache Ranger HDFS plugin** with audit + tag-based policies detecting unauthorized writes.
2. **HDFS Transparent Encryption (HDFS-6134 / Hadoop Crypto Codec)** combined with **Kerberos authentication** on DataNode RPC, so only authenticated nodes can write blocks.
3. (Bonus) **HDFS checksum verification + Cloudera Navigator** for integrity auditing.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]

**(a)** Differentiate between **STRIDE and DREAD** models. Why is STRIDE alone insufficient for Big Data security? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Spark Streaming application processes payment events from Kafka. The Kafka cluster lacks ACLs. Apply the **STRIDE model** to identify potential threats (at least one per category), and then apply **DREAD** to score the most critical threat. Suggest three mitigations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | STRIDE | DREAD |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Classification | Prioritization |
| Type | Categorical | Quantitative |
| Output | Threat *type* | Numeric risk score |
| Origin | Microsoft (1999) | Microsoft (1999) |
| Big Data use | Enumerate threats on Hadoop, Spark, Kafka | Rank them for sprint planning |
| Question answered | "What kind of threat?" | "How bad is it?" |

**Why STRIDE alone is insufficient (3 marks):**
1. STRIDE only *labels* threats; in a Big Data cluster with thousands of components, engineers cannot fix everything at once.
2. STRIDE provides no **priority** for remediation.
3. STRIDE is **not quantitative**, making it incompatible with DevSecOps pipelines that require risk-based SLAs.
4. STRIDE does not account for **business context** (regulatory fines, brand damage), which DREAD's *Damage* dimension captures.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**STRIDE Identification (3 marks):**
- **S:** Fake Kafka producer publishing forged payment events.
- **T:** Tampered Spark job JAR injected into YARN.
- **R:** No Kafka audit → producer cannot be traced.
- **I:** Unencrypted Kafka topic leaks payment data to network sniffers.
- **D:** Kafka broker flood by junk topics → payment stream stalls.
- **E:** Producer with `*` ACL becomes cluster admin.

**DREAD on the most critical threat (e.g., Information Disclosure from unencrypted Kafka):**

- **D** (damage if PII leaks) $= 10$
- **R** (reproducibility — always-on sniff) $= 9$
- **E** (exploitability — passive sniffing) $= 8$
- **A** (all payment customers) $= 10$
- **D'** (discoverability — Wireshark on broker port 9092) $= 7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{DREAD}} &= \frac{10 + 9 + 8 + 10 + 7}{5} \\
&= \frac{44}{5} \\
&= 8.8 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{HIGH RISK}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Three Mitigations (3 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Enable Kafka SSL/TLS** for in-transit encryption (`security.inter.broker.protocol=SSL`).
2. **Enable Kerberos SASL** for producer/consumer authentication (`sasl.mechanism=PLAIN` / `GSSAPI`).
3. **Implement Apache Ranger Kafka plugin** for centralized ACLs and audit logging.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Question A & B):** Students often skip the **formula calculation** and write only scores. Always show the explicit $\frac{D+R+E+A+D'}{5}$ substitution. Also, ALWAYS attach Big Data components to STRIDE — generic security answers lose 1–2 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for STRIDE / DREAD in Big Data (Module 4)**

- [ ] **STRIDE = 6 categories:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- [ ] **DREAD = 5 dimensions:** Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability.
- [ ] **STRIDE classifies; DREAD prioritizes** — they are *complementary*, not alternatives.
- [ ] **DREAD formula (default KTU):** $R = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D'}{5}$.
- [ ] **Risk bands:** $\ge 7$ HIGH; $4 \le R < 7$ MEDIUM; $R < 4$ LOW.
- [ ] **Score range:** 1 to 10 (ordinal, integer-typical).
- [ ] **Big Data components to remember:** NameNode, DataNode, HDFS, YARN, MapReduce, Spark, Kafka, HBase, Hive, ZooKeeper, S3/ADLS.
- [ ] **Big Data–specific STRIDE examples:**
  - S → Kerberos ticket impersonation, fake DataNode.
  - T → HDFS block tampering, malicious JAR injection.
  - R → Missing YARN audit, no CloudTrail.
  - I → Unencrypted HDFS / Kafka / RPC.
  - D → NameNode memory flood, Kafka broker flood.
  - E → Root SSH, YARN privileged queue abuse.
- [ ] **Tools to mention for controls:** Apache Ranger, Apache Sentry, Kerberos, HDFS Transparent Encryption, Kafka SSL/SASL, TLS, Cloudera Navigator, Knox Gateway.
- [ ] **Common exam traps:**
  - Confusing STRIDE (6) with DREAD (5).
  - Writing multiplicative DREAD when question asks default.
  - Forgetting Big Data examples (generic answers lose marks).
  - Skipping the final risk band classification.
  - Confusing "Repudiation" with "Reputation" — it is *denial of action*.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** *"Silly Turtles Run In Dark Elevators"* — STRIDE.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** *"DREAD = Don't Reproduce Exploits Against Discoverers"* (5 D's and an R, but the last D = Discoverability).
- [ ] **For 14-mark answers:** Always include the **block diagram**, the **formula substitution**, the **band conclusion**, and **two specific Big Data controls**.
- [ ] **Origin fact (good for 1 mark):** Both STRIDE and DREAD were developed by **Microsoft** in **1999** by Praerit Garg and Loren Kohnfelder.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Topic — STRIDE / DREAD for Big Data Systems**
> *You are now equipped to answer any Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question KTU can pose on this topic.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Best Practices: Data masking, tokenization, and maintaining audit trails
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Best Practices: Data masking, tokenization, and maintaining audit trails

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Data Protection Best Practices in Big Data

In the context of **Big Data Security**, sensitive information (Personally Identifiable Information, Payment Card Information, Protected Health Information) flows through multiple layers — ingestion, storage, processing, and analytics. Three security best practices form the operational backbone of protecting this data at rest and in use:

1. **Data Masking** — Hiding real data with realistic substitutes.
2. **Tokenization** — Swapping sensitive values with non-reversible surrogates.
3. **Audit Trails** — Recording every access, modification, and administrative action for accountability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), this topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO4** — *"Apply security best practices and compliance controls to big data ecosystems."* It is a frequent 14-mark question in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

### Data Masking
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Data masking* is a security technique that replaces original sensitive data with structurally similar, fictitious but realistic values, so that the masked data can be used in non-production environments (development, testing, training, analytics) without exposing real information.

**Key Property:** Masked data preserves **format** and **referential integrity** but loses **confidentiality**.

### Tokenization
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Tokenization* is a security process that substitutes a sensitive data element (e.g., a 16-digit credit card number) with a non-sensitive equivalent, called a **token**, which has no exploitable mathematical relationship to the original value. The original data is securely stored in a centralized **token vault**.

**Key Property:** Tokens are **non-reversible** outside the token vault (no mathematical decryption).

### Audit Trail
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *audit trail* (also called audit log) is a chronological, tamper-evident record of all system, user, and administrative activities that provide documentary evidence of the sequence of operations affecting a specific operation, record, or data element.

**Key Property:** Audit trails must be **immutable, time-stamped, and attributable** to a specific principal (user, service, or system).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Data Masking** | A restaurant publishing a "sample menu" with real dish *names* but *fictional prices* and *fake ingredients*. The menu looks authentic but reveals nothing proprietary. |
| **Tokenization** | A coat check at a theatre. You hand over your expensive coat (sensitive PAN) and receive a brass token **#4827**. Only the checkroom clerk (token vault) can return your actual coat. If a thief steals the token, it's useless — no coat is inside it. |
| **Audit Trail** | The black box flight recorder on an aircraft. It continuously records who pressed which button, when, and from where. It cannot be edited in flight and is used **after the fact** to reconstruct the sequence of events. |

---

## 1.4 The "Why" — Threat Model Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Big data platforms (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL stores, Data Lakes) replicate data across clusters, increasing the **attack surface**. Without these controls, a single compromised node, a rogue analyst, or a stolen backup tape could leak millions of customer records. Data masking, tokenization, and audit trails are **defense-in-depth** countermeasures mandated by regulations such as **PCI DSS v4.0 (Req. 3, 10)**, **HIPAA Security Rule (45 CFR §164.312(b))**, and **GDPR Article 32**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Masking — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 Classification by Execution Time

| Type | When Applied | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Data Masking (SDM)** | At rest, on a copy/snapshot | Creating sanitized dev/test databases |
| **Dynamic Data Masking (DDM)** | In real-time, on query result | Hiding PII from unauthorized DB users |
| **On-the-Fly Masking** | During ETL/data movement | Streaming data into analytics pipelines |

### 2.1.2 Masking Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

- **Substitution:** Replace with a value from a lookup table (e.g., real "Rahul" → fake "Arjun").
- **Shuffling (Permutation):** Reorder values within a column (no row sees its own value).
- **Number/Date Variance:** Apply a random delta within ±N%.
- **Masking Out:** Replace characters with a fixed symbol (e.g., `ravi@gmail.com` → `r***@*****.com`).
- **Encryption (Format-Preserving):** Replace with a cipher that retains length/charset (FPE — Format Preserving Encryption).
- **Nulling/Deletion:** Replace the field with `NULL` (last resort, breaks referential integrity).
- **Synthetic Data Generation:** Create statistically similar but entirely fabricated datasets.

### 2.1.3 Irreversibility Principle

A masking function $M$ is considered **irreversible** if, given only the masked value $M(x)$, it is computationally infeasible to derive the original $x$.

For substitution and shuffling, **the lookup table itself becomes the secret** — so it must be protected with the same rigor as the original data (or more).

---

## 2.2 Tokenization — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Architecture Components

The **Tokenization System Architecture** consists of four logical entities:

1. **Token Requester** — Application initiating a tokenization call.
2. **Tokenization Engine** — Service that generates and validates tokens.
3. **Token Vault** — Encrypted database storing `(PAN, Token)` mappings.
4. **Token Domain/Scope** — The contextual boundary (e.g., per-merchant, per-transaction).

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Properties of a Token

For a sensitive value $S$ and its token $T$:

$$T = f(S, k_{\text{vault}})$$

where $f$ is a **non-deterministic, one-way** function and $k_{\text{vault}}$ is the secret vault key. Critically:

$$S \neq g(T) \quad \text{for any polynomial-time } g$$

In other words, **the token is not a ciphertext** — it cannot be decrypted because it is not the result of an invertible cryptographic operation.

### 2.2.3 Vault-Token Reversibility Property

Only inside the secure vault boundary:

$$S = h(T, k_{\text{vault}})$$

where $h$ is the lookup function. This makes the **vault the single point of compromise** — hence it requires HSM (Hardware Security Module) backing in PCI-DSS compliant systems.

### 2.2.4 Format-Preserving Tokenization (FPT)

A token must typically match the format of the original (e.g., a 16-digit PAN tokenizes to a 16-digit string). This is achieved through:

$$T = \text{FPE-FF1}(S, \text{tweak}, K)$$

where **FF1** is the NIST SP 800-38G *Format-Preserving Encryption* algorithm, used as a token-generation primitive.

---

## 2.3 Audit Trails — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 The "5 W's + 1 H" Audit Event Model

Every audit record **must** capture:

| Element | Question | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Who** | Which principal? | `user_id = "analyst_42"` |
| **What** | What action? | `READ`, `WRITE`, `DELETE`, `EXPORT` |
| **When** | At what time? | `2024-11-15T10:32:18.441Z` (RFC 3339 UTC) |
| **Where** | From where? | Source IP `10.0.5.21`, hostname `etl-node-3` |
| **Why** | With what justification? | `ticket_id = "INC-9981"` |
| **How** | With what mechanism? | SQL `SELECT * FROM customers WHERE ...` |

### 2.3.2 Tamper-Evidence via Cryptographic Chaining

A common technique (and KTU favorite) is the **hash-chained log**:

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Event}_i \parallel H_{i-1})$$

where $H_0$ is a known genesis hash. Any retroactive modification of $\text{Event}_j$ (for $j < i$) breaks the chain at $H_i$, making tampering **detectable**.

### 2.3.3 Compliance Mapping (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Regulation | Audit Trail Requirement |
|---|---|
| **PCI DSS v4.0 Req. 10** | Log all access to cardholder data; retain ≥ 12 months, ≥ 3 months immediately available |
| **HIPAA §164.312(b)** | Hardware, software, procedural audit mechanisms for PHI access |
| **SOX Section 404** | 7-year retention of financial system audit records |
| **GDPR Article 30** | Records of processing activities (ROPA) |
| **ISO 27001 A.12.4** | Logging and monitoring of user activities and exceptions |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Property | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Masking Reversibility | $M(x) \not\Rightarrow x$ (computationally hard) | Lookup tables must be protected |
| 2 | Token Generation | $T = f(S, k_{\text{vault}})$ | Non-deterministic, non-invertible |
| 3 | Vault Resolution | $S = h(T, k_{\text{vault}})$ | Only inside the HSM-backed vault |
| 4 | FPE Tokenization | $T = \text{FPE-FF1}(S, \text{tweak}, K)$ | NIST SP 800-38G standard |
| 5 | Hash-Chained Audit | $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(E_i \mid\mid H_{i-1})$ | $H_0$ = genesis hash |
| 6 | Log Retention (PCI) | $\geq 12$ months total, $\geq 3$ months online | PCI DSS v4.0 Req. 10.5 |
| 7 | Log Retention (SOX) | $\geq 7$ years | 18 CFR § 210 |
| 8 | Irreversibility Bound | $P[\text{recover } S \mid T] \leq 2^{-128}$ | Equivalent to AES-128 strength |
| 9 | Token Uniqueness | $P[T_i = T_j] \leq 2^{-n}$ for $n$-bit token | Collision probability |
| 10 | Anonymization Set Size | $\vert k \text{-anonymity class}\vert \geq k$ | K-anonymity property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The bolded items in the cheat sheet are the most frequently tested in the 14-mark module questions. Memorize the **hash-chained audit equation** and the **PCI DSS retention period** — these appeared in past KTU university exams.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fintech:** Banks tokenize 16-digit PANs before storing in Hadoop for fraud analytics, shrinking PCI scope.
- **Healthcare:** Hospitals mask patient records before sharing with machine-learning researchers training diagnostic models.
- **Cloud (AWS S3, Azure Data Lake):** Dynamic masking and tokenization are first-class services (e.g., AWS Macie, Azure Purview).
- **Regulatory Forensics:** Audit trails are the primary evidence in breach investigations — a missing log often implies a cover-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hash-Chained Audit Trail Integrity

**Problem Setup:** Design a tamper-evident log of $n$ events $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$. Show that any retroactive tampering with event $E_k$ (where $1 \leq k \leq n$) is detectable at the verifier check.

### Step 1 — Define the Genesis Hash
A trusted authority publishes an initial hash $H_0$ (e.g., a constant, or the SHA-256 of a public nonce).

$$H_0 = \text{SHA-256}(\text{"KTU-AUDIT-GENESIS-2024"})$$

This value is known publicly and acts as the **anchor** of trust.

### Step 2 — Compute the First Event's Hash
For the first recorded event $E_1$ (e.g., *"user analyst_42 SELECT * FROM customers at 10:00 UTC"*):

$$H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(E_1 \parallel H_0)$$

### Step 3 — Recursive Hash Computation
For each subsequent event $E_i$:

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(E_i \parallel H_{i-1})$$

This produces a sequence $H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n$.

### Step 4 — Tampering Detection Logic
Suppose an attacker modifies $E_k$ to $E_k'$. Because SHA-256 is a cryptographic hash:

$$\text{SHA-256}(E_k' \parallel H_{k-1}) \neq H_k$$

with overwhelming probability (≈ $1 - 2^{-256}$). The verifier detects this by recomputing the chain from scratch and comparing $H_n^{\text{(stored)}}$ with $H_n^{\text{(recomputed)}}$.

### Step 5 — Probability of Successful Tampering
The attacker must find a collision $E_k' \neq E_k$ such that:

$$\text{SHA-256}(E_k' \parallel H_{k-1}) = H_k$$

By the **collision resistance property** of SHA-256:

$$P[\text{success}] \leq \frac{q(q-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{256}}$$

for $q$ hashing attempts (birthday-bound approximation). For $q \leq 2^{80}$ (computationally infeasible today), $P[\text{success}] \leq 2^{-97}$, which is cryptographically negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Hash-chained audit trails provide **provable tamper-evidence** under standard cryptographic assumptions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Masking vs. Tokenization vs. Encryption

This is the **single most important comparison** for KTU 14-mark questions.

| Dimension | Data Masking | Tokenization | Encryption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Generally irreversible (lookup table may allow) | Reversible only via vault | Fully reversible with key |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Substitution, permutation | Random mapping | AES, RSA, ECC |
| **Format Preservation** | Yes (usually) | Yes (FPT) | No (ciphertext is binary) |
| **Performance Overhead** | Low | Medium (vault lookup) | Low–Medium |
| **Searchable** | Yes (on masked values) | No (tokens are random) | No (without deterministic IV) |
| **PCI Scope Reduction** | Partial | **Maximum** (card data never leaves vault) | Partial |
| **Vault/Key Required** | Lookup table (protected) | Token Vault (HSM) | Key Management (HSM/KMS) |
| **Use in Big Data** | Analytics, ML training | Payment processing, PII | Data-in-transit, data-at-rest |
| **Auditability** | Moderate | High (vault is auditable) | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** If asked *"Which technique best reduces PCI scope?"* — the answer is **Tokenization**, not encryption. This is a classic trick question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Tokenizing a Credit Card Number

**Given:** A 16-digit PAN `4532 1234 5678 9010` is submitted by a payment gateway.

**Step 1 — Generate Token**
The tokenization engine receives the PAN, queries the vault for an unused token slot, and assigns:

$$T = \text{"}7891\text{-}4567\text{-}2345\text{-}6789\text{"}$$

**Step 2 — Store Mapping in Vault**

| PAN (encrypted at rest) | Token | Created At | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4532-1234-5678-9010 | 7891-4567-2345-6789 | 2024-11-15T10:00:00Z | ACTIVE |

**Step 3 — Return Token to Caller**
The application now stores only `7891-4567-2345-6789` in its Hadoop cluster. The real PAN is **never persisted** outside the vault.

**Step 4 — Detokenization (Refund Scenario)**
For a refund, the system calls `detokenize(T)` which authenticates against the vault, retrieves the encrypted PAN, decrypts it inside the HSM, and returns the original — **without the calling application ever handling the raw PAN long-term**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) Policy

A hospital has a table `patients(ssn, name, diagnosis, dob)`. Three roles exist:

| Role | SSN | Name | Diagnosis | DOB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Doctor | Full | Full | Full | Full |
| Nurse | Masked: `XXX-XX-1234` | Full | Full | Full |
| Researcher | Masked: `XXX-XX-####` | Masked: `J*** D**` | Full | Generalized: Year only |

**SQL Implementation (conceptual):**

```sql
CREATE MASKING POLICY mask_ssn
  AS (val CHAR(11)) RETURNS CHAR(11) ->
  CASE
    WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'DOCTOR'  THEN val
    WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'NURSE'   THEN CONCAT('XXX-XX-', RIGHT(val, 4))
    WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'RESEARCHER' THEN 'XXX-XX-####'
  END;
```

**Audit Trail Generated:**

| Timestamp | Role | Action | Resource | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2024-11-15 10:05:00 | RESEARCHER | SELECT | patients.ssn | MASKED |
| 2024-11-15 10:05:01 | DOCTOR | SELECT | patients.ssn | FULL |
| 2024-11-15 10:05:02 | NURSE | SELECT | patients.ssn | PARTIAL |

Each query is logged with the role, the masking policy applied, and a hash of the query — enabling post-hoc investigation of who accessed what and under what policy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Masking Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Database] --> cls[Classification Engine]
    cls --> pii{PII Detected?}
    pii -- Yes --> pol[Masking Policy Lookup]
    pii -- No --> fwd[Forward Untouched]
    pol --> sub[Substitution]
    pol --> shu[Shuffling]
    pol --> var[Variance Delta]
    sub --> tgt[Masked Target]
    shu --> tgt
    var --> tgt
    fwd --> tgt
    tgt --> aud[Audit Logger]
    aud --> store[Masked Dataset Store]
```

## 4.2 Tokenization Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    app[Application] --> api[Tokenization API]
    api --> auth{Authenticated?}
    auth -- No --> rej[Reject Request]
    auth -- Yes --> gen[Token Generator]
    gen --> vault[(Token Vault HSM)]
    vault --> mp{Mapping Found?}
    mp -- Token Request --> assign[Assign New Token]
    mp -- Detokenize Request --> retrieve[Return Original PAN]
    assign --> log1[Audit Log: New Token]
    retrieve --> log2[Audit Log: Detokenize]
    log1 --> siem[(Centralized SIEM)]
    log2 --> siem
```

## 4.3 Hash-Chained Audit Trail Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e0[H0 Genesis Hash] --> e1[H1 SHA256 of E1 + H0]
    e1 --> e2[H2 SHA256 of E2 + H1]
    e2 --> e3[H3 SHA256 of E3 + H2]
    e3 --> e4[Hn SHA256 of En + Hn-1]
    e4 --> ver[Verifier Re-Computes Chain]
    ver --> cmp{Hn Stored equals Hn Computed?}
    cmp -- Yes --> ok[Tamper-Evident: INTEGRITY OK]
    cmp -- No --> al[ALERT: Tampering Detected]
```

## 4.4 End-to-End Security Best Practices Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ingestion[Data Ingestion Layer]
        raw[Raw PII Data]
    end
    subgraph protection[Protection Layer]
        cls[Classify Sensitivity]
        tkn[Tokenize High-Risk Fields PAN SSN]
        msk[Mask Medium-Risk Fields Names Emails]
    end
    subgraph storage[Storage Layer]
        vault[(Token Vault HSM)]
        lake[(Masked Data Lake)]
    end
    subgraph monitoring[Monitoring Layer]
        alog[Application Audit Logs]
        ssys[System Audit Logs]
        clu[Cluster Audit Logs]
        siem[(SIEM Correlation Engine)]
    end
    raw --> cls
    cls --> tkn
    cls --> msk
    tkn --> vault
    msk --> lake
    alog --> siem
    ssys --> siem
    clu --> siem
    siem --> dsh[Security Analyst Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs follow the alphanumeric rule (e.g., `e0`, `e1`, `vault`, `siem`). No reserved keywords are used. Labels are kept in clean uppercase alphanumeric text to ensure Mermaid compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between data masking and tokenization. Give one real-world scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Data Masking | Tokenization |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Replaces data with realistic substitutes | Replaces data with non-sensitive surrogates (tokens) |
| Reversibility | Generally irreversible (or via protected lookup) | Reversible only via token vault |
| Format | Preserved | Preserved (via FPT) |
| **Preferred Scenario** | **Hospitals** sharing patient data with ML researchers for diagnostic model training — the researchers need **statistically realistic** values to train models, but do not need to retrieve the originals. | **E-commerce payment gateways** processing credit card transactions — they need to **reverse the token** during refunds, so the original PAN must be retrievable from a secure vault. |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for valid scenarios)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: List any three mandatory fields that must be captured in every audit trail record. Justify why each is essential.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Timestamp (When)** — Essential for chronological reconstruction; required by PCI DSS Req. 10.2 to prove the order of events during forensic investigation.
2. **User/Principal Identity (Who)** — Essential for accountability; required by SOX 404 and ISO 27001 A.12.4.1 to attribute actions to a specific individual or service.
3. **Action Type (What)** — Essential for detecting anomalous behaviour (e.g., mass `SELECT` on `customers` table); enables anomaly-based intrusion detection.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per field with justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of a tokenization system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Token Vault** and the security controls required to protect it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **hash-chained audit trails** in detail. Derive the recurrence relation and explain how tampering at any intermediate event is detected. State the cryptographic assumption that makes the chain secure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Architecture of a Tokenization System:**

A tokenization system consists of four logical components:
1. **Token Requester** (Application Server): Initiates a `tokenize()` or `detokenize()` call.
2. **Tokenization Engine**: Validates request, queries vault, applies format-preserving logic.
3. **Token Vault**: Encrypted database (typically HSM-backed) storing `(PAN, Token)` mappings.
4. **Audit Subsystem**: Logs every request/response with `who, what, when, where, why`.

**Role of the Token Vault:**

The vault is the **single point of compromise**. It stores the only copy of the mapping between original sensitive values and their tokens. If the vault is breached, the entire tokenization scheme collapses.

**Security Controls for the Vault:**

| Control | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **HSM (FIPS 140-2 Level 3+)** | Encryption keys never leave hardware boundary |
| **Multi-Party Authentication (M-of-N)** | No single admin can decrypt; requires quorum |
| **Network Segmentation** | Vault on isolated VPC/subnet, no internet ingress |
| **Mandatory Access Logging** | Every vault access recorded immutably |
| **Key Rotation** | Master keys rotated per PCI DSS Req. 3.6 |
| **Geofencing / Replicas** | Geographic restrictions on vault replicas |

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for vault role, 3 for security controls)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Hash-Chained Audit Trails — Derivation:**

Consider a sequence of $n$ audit events $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$. Define a publicly known **genesis hash** $H_0$.

**Recurrence relation:**

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(E_i \parallel H_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Tampering Detection:**

Suppose an attacker retroactively modifies event $E_k$ to $E_k'$ (for $1 \leq k \leq n$). The stored value of $H_k$ no longer matches $\text{SHA-256}(E_k' \parallel H_{k-1})$. The verifier, recomputing the chain from $H_0$ through $H_n$, observes:

$$H_n^{\text{(computed)}} \neq H_n^{\text{(stored)}}$$

and raises an integrity alert.

**Cryptographic Assumption:**

The chain's security rests on the **collision resistance** of SHA-256. Formally, for any probabilistic polynomial-time adversary $\mathcal{A}$:

$$P\left[\mathcal{A}(E_k') = H_k \;\wedge\; E_k' \neq E_k\right] \leq 2^{-128}$$

This is the standard assumption of a **cryptographic hash function's pre-image and collision resistance**, and SHA-256 is widely believed to satisfy it.

*(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 3 for tampering detection, 2 for cryptographic assumption)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper]**
**Q: (a)** Discuss **static and dynamic data masking** techniques with suitable examples. Compare them in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is an audit trail? List and explain the **compliance requirements** for audit trails under **PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOX** with specific retention periods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Static Data Masking (SDM):**

In SDM, a sanitized copy of the database is created **at rest**, typically on a snapshot. The original production database remains untouched, and a separate masked database is provisioned to development, testing, and training environments.

**Example:**

| Original (Production) | Masked (Test DB) |
|---|---|
| `ravi@ktu.ac.in` | `arjun@ktu.ac.in` |
| `+91-9847012345` | `+91-9847000000` |
| `Aadhaar: 1234-5678-9012` | `Aadhaar: 9999-8888-7777` |

**Dynamic Data Masking (DDM):**

In DDM, masking is applied **at query time** based on the role of the executing user. The underlying data is unchanged, but the result set presented to the user is masked.

**Example:**

```sql
-- A 'NURSE' role querying SSN
SELECT mask_ssn(ssn) FROM patients;
-- Returns: 'XXX-XX-1234'

-- A 'DOCTOR' role querying the same SSN
SELECT ssn FROM patients;
-- Returns: '123-45-6789'
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Static Data Masking | Dynamic Data Masking |
|---|---|---|
| When Applied | At rest, on copy | At query time |
| Data at Rest | Masked | Original |
| Latency | None (pre-computed) | Slight overhead per query |
| Environment | Dev/Test/Training | Production |
| Policy Storage | Separate snapshot | Centralized policy engine |
| Use Case | Sharing data with third-party vendors | Role-based access in production |

*(7 marks: 2 each for SDM & DDM explanations, 2 for examples, 1 for comparison table)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Definition of Audit Trail:**

> An audit trail is a chronological, tamper-evident record of system, user, and administrative activities that provides documentary evidence of the sequence of operations within an information system.

**Compliance Requirements:**

| Regulation | Specific Clause | Requirement | Retention |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCI DSS v4.0** | Requirement 10.5 | Retain audit logs for at least **12 months**, with the most recent **3 months** immediately available for analysis. | **12 months** (3 months online) |
| **HIPAA** | 45 CFR § 164.316(b)(2)(i) | Retain documentation of policies, procedures, and audit logs for **6 years** from creation or last effective date. | **6 years** |
| **SOX** | SEC Rule 17a-4(f) | Retain audit records of financial systems and electronic communications for **7 years** in a non-rewriteable, non-erasable format (WORM). | **7 years** (WORM storage) |

**Common Controls Across All Three:**

- Log every access to sensitive data.
- Capture user ID, timestamp, action, and resource accessed.
- Protect logs from unauthorized modification or deletion.
- Review logs at least daily for security events.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition, 6 for the three compliance requirements with retention periods)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse masking with encryption.** Encryption is reversible with a key; masking is generally irreversible. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this interchange.
> 2. **PCI DSS retention is 12 months, NOT 7 years.** Students commonly write 7 years (which is SOX). Read the regulation name carefully!
> 3. **Hash-chained audit is a chain, not a tree.** The recurrence $H_i = H(E_i, H_{i-1})$ must be in **linear sequential form** — not parallel — for full marks.
> 4. **Tokenization ≠ Encryption.** A token is not a ciphertext. This is the single most common conceptual error.
> 5. Always state the **cryptographic assumption** (collision resistance) when explaining hash chains — without it, the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Data Masking** = Replaces sensitive data with realistic substitutes; preserves format; generally irreversible.
- ✅ **Static vs Dynamic** = SDM is at rest on a copy; DDM is at query time based on user role.
- ✅ **Tokenization** = Substitutes sensitive data with non-sensitive tokens stored in a **Token Vault**; tokens are **not ciphertexts**.
- ✅ **Token Vault** = The single point of compromise; must be HSM-backed with multi-party authentication.
- ✅ **FPT (Format-Preserving Tokenization)** = Uses NIST SP 800-38G FPE-FF1 algorithm under the hood.
- ✅ **Audit Trail** = Chronological, tamper-evident record of system activities.
- ✅ **The 5 W's + 1 H** = Who, What, When, Where, Why, How — must be captured for every event.
- ✅ **Hash-Chained Audit** = $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(E_i \parallel H_{i-1})$; tampering breaks the chain.
- ✅ **PCI DSS Req. 10** = Audit logs retained **12 months** (3 months online).
- ✅ **HIPAA §164.316(b)(2)(i)** = Retain audit documentation for **6 years**.
- ✅ **SOX 17a-4(f)** = Retain audit records for **7 years** in **WORM** (Write Once Read Many) format.
- ✅ **GDPR Article 32** = Mandates appropriate technical measures including pseudonymization (related to masking/tokenization).
- ✅ **Tokenization reduces PCI scope the most** — not encryption.
- ✅ **SHA-256 collision resistance** is the cryptographic assumption backing hash-chained audit.
- ✅ **Audit logs must be immutable** — append-only or WORM storage is mandatory for SOX compliance.
- ✅ **Big Data platforms (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL)** amplify the need for these controls due to data replication across clusters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure coding practices for Big Data applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure coding practices for Big Data applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Secure coding practices for Big Data applications** refer to a disciplined, methodology-driven set of software engineering principles, coding standards, and verification techniques specifically tailored to address the unique attack surface introduced by distributed, high-volume, high-velocity, and high-variety data processing frameworks such as Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, NoSQL databases (HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB), and cloud-native data lakes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Secure coding in Big Data is the proactive integration of confidentiality, integrity, availability, and accountability safeguards directly into the application source code, build pipeline, and runtime configuration — rather than treating security as a post-deployment patch. It encompasses input validation, output encoding, identity & access management, cryptography, secure deserialization, audit logging, dependency hardening, and secure-by-default configuration of Big Data frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fortified Smart City"

Imagine a **Smart City** that collects data from millions of sensors (traffic, weather, surveillance, health, banking). The city cannot rely on a single wall around it — it must:

- **Validate every vehicle** entering (Input Validation)
- **Sign every dispatch order** so no one can forge a traffic signal command (Authentication & Integrity)
- **Encrypt every radio transmission** so eavesdroppers hear only noise (Encryption in Transit)
- **Lock every data vault** at rest (Encryption at Rest)
- **Keep tamper-evident logs** of who touched what (Audit Logging)
- **Rotate keys regularly** instead of using one master key forever (Key Management)
- **Inspect every package** delivered to citizens (Output Encoding)

Big Data applications are exactly this Smart City — they ingest petabytes from millions of endpoints. **One weak link (one unvalidated input, one hardcoded secret, one unpatched library) can compromise the entire data pipeline.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Big Data needs a SPECIALIST secure coding model:**
> Traditional secure coding (e.g., OWASP Top 10 for Web) addresses monolithic applications. Big Data introduces **distributed trust boundaries**, **multi-tenant data lakes**, **non-deterministic execution (YARN/Kubernetes)**, **polyglot persistence**, and **data lineage challenges** that demand framework-specific practices such as securing MapReduce jobs, hardening Spark UDFs, securing Kafka producers, and preventing NoSQL injection.

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Secure Big Data Coding (High-Yield Mapping)

| Pillar | Engineering Objective | KTU CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Validation & Sanitization** | Reject malformed/malicious payloads before they reach data pipelines | CO4 |
| **Authentication & Authorization (AAA)** | Verify identity and enforce least-privilege on every cluster interaction | CO4 |
| **Cryptographic Protection** | Protect data in transit, at rest, and (emerging) in use | CO4 |
| **Secure Configuration & Hardening** | Eliminate default credentials, open ports, verbose errors | CO4 |
| **Audit Logging & Monitoring** | Tamper-evident record of who did what, when, and to which data | CO5 |
| **Dependency & Supply Chain Hygiene** | Eliminate vulnerable third-party libraries and container images | CO4 |
| **Secure APIs & Data Sharing** | OAuth 2.0, mTLS, tokenization for data exchange | CO5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** Industry data (Verizon DBIR 2024) indicates that **~74% of breaches involve a human element** — primarily misuse of credentials, misconfiguration, and social engineering. Secure coding directly mitigates this by enforcing machine-enforced policy that does not rely on human vigilance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Layer Secure Big Data Coding Model

A structured, academically rigorous breakdown of secure coding practice domains, each tied to a specific Big Data framework or runtime context.

### Layer 1 — Input Validation & Injection Prevention

- **Why it matters:** Big Data ingest endpoints (Kafka topics, REST APIs to Spark, HDFS file uploads, Sqoop imports) are the *frontline* of attack. An attacker uploading a malicious Parquet/Avro file with a deserialization gadget can achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) on every executor.
- **How it works:**
  1. **Whitelist validation** — accept only expected schema, types, and value ranges.
  2. **Schema enforcement** — use Avro/Protobuf with strict schema validation; reject mismatches.
  3. **Length and size caps** — prevent Resource Exhaustion (e.g., a single 10 GB Kafka message).
  4. **NoSQL injection prevention** — never string-concatenate user input into MongoDB/Cassandra queries; use parameterized query builders.
  5. **SQL injection prevention** — use prepared statements, ORMs, and parameterized views in Hive/Spark SQL.
- **Big Data Specific Threats:**
  - **HQL Injection** in Hive (analogous to SQLi)
  - **NoSQL Injection** in MongoDB (`{"$gt": ""}` bypasses authentication)
  - **Spark SQL Injection** via concatenated DataFrame filters
  - **Deserialization RCE** in Java/Scala UDFs (Java `ObjectInputStream`, Kryo, Pickle)

### Layer 2 — Authentication & Identity

- **Mechanisms:**
  - **Kerberos** (de-facto standard for Hadoop ecosystem)
  - **LDAP / Active Directory** integration
  - **OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect** for REST APIs (NiFi, Kafka REST Proxy, Spark Thrift Server)
  - **mTLS (mutual TLS)** for service-to-service communication
  - **Service Accounts** for automated jobs (no shared human credentials)
- **Best Practice:** **Never embed credentials in source code** — use Vault, HDFS Credential Provider, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Secret Manager.

### Layer 3 — Authorization & Least Privilege

- **Models:**
  - **RBAC** (Role-Based Access Control) — Apache Ranger, Apache Sentry
  - **ABAC** (Attribute-Based) — policy engines like Open Policy Agent (OPA)
  - **MAC** (Mandatory Access Control) — column/row-level masking
- **Big Data Implementation:**
  - **Apache Ranger** — central policy administration for HDFS, HBase, Hive, Kafka, YARN, Solr, Knox.
  - **Apache Sentry** — fine-grained role-based authorization, popular in CDH.
  - **HDFS POSIX ACLs** + **extended ACLs**.
  - **S3 / ADLS / GCS** bucket policies + IAM.
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** A Spark job reading customer PII should run as a service account granted *only* `READ` on the specific path, not the entire data lake.

### Layer 4 — Cryptographic Protection

- **Data in Transit:** TLS 1.2+ (minimum), mTLS for service mesh, SASL/TLS for Kafka brokers, SSL for HDFS DataNode ↔ Client.
- **Data at Rest:**
  - HDFS Transparent Encryption (HDFS-6134) using Hadoop KMS
  - HBase column-family encryption
  - Hive column-level encryption
  - Disk-level encryption (LUKS, AWS EBS encryption)
  - Object-store server-side encryption (S3 SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C)
- **Data in Use (Emerging):** Confidential Computing via Intel SGX, AMD SEV, NVIDIA H100 CC. The **Formula**:

$$C_{enc} = E_k(P, IV)$$

where decryption requires $D_k(C_{enc}, IV) = P$. The runtime executes over $C_{enc}$ without ever exposing plaintext $P$ to the OS or hypervisor.

- **Key Management Formula (NIST SP 800-57):**

$$SK_L = \frac{1}{R_{compromise}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i \cdot V_i)$$

where $SK_L$ = security key lifetime, $R_{compromise}$ = rate of compromise, $D_i$ = data sensitivity, $V_i$ = volume. Higher sensitivity ⇒ shorter rotation.

### Layer 5 — Secure Configuration & Hardening

- **Disable** default accounts, verbose error pages, sample apps, JMX without auth, YARN web UI without Kerberos.
- **Enable** firewall rules, SELinux/AppArmor, seccomp profiles, Pod Security Standards in Kubernetes.
- **Secrets Management** — use HashiCorp Vault, CyberArk, or cloud-native KMS-backed secret stores.
- **Zero-Trust Networking** — default-deny network policies, micro-segmentation with Cilium/Calico.

### Layer 6 — Logging, Monitoring & Tamper-Evidence

- **Audit Log Content** (the "5 W's + How"): *Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How.*
- **Immutable Storage** — write-once-read-many (WORM) buckets, blockchain-anchored logs, append-only Kafka topics.
- **SIEM Integration** — ship logs to Splunk, ELK, Datadog, Chronicle.
- **Anomaly Detection** — ML-based UEBA (User & Entity Behavior Analytics) on top of audit logs.
- **GDPR / HIPAA Compliance** — log masking of PII, log retention policies.

### Layer 7 — Supply Chain & Dependency Hygiene

- **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** — CycloneDX, SPDX.
- **Vulnerability Scanning** — Trivy, Snyk, Grype, OWASP Dependency-Check.
- **Container Hardening** — distroless images, read-only filesystems, non-root users, signed images (Sigstore/Cosign), admission controllers (Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper).
- **Reproducible Builds** — bit-for-bit deterministic compilation.
- **Code Signing** — Sigstore for binaries, Notary v2 for containers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encryption (Symmetric) | $C = E_k(P)$ | AES-256, key $k$ in bits |
| 2 | Encryption (Asymmetric) | $C = E_{pub}(P),\ P = D_{priv}(C)$ | RSA-2048+, ECC P-256 |
| 3 | Hashing | $h = H(M)$, where $H$ is SHA-256/512 | One-way, collision-resistant |
| 4 | HMAC | $HMAC_k(M) = H((k \oplus opad) \Vert H((k \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | Message authentication |
| 5 | Digital Signature | $\sigma = Sign_{priv}(H(M))$ | Provides integrity + non-repudiation |
| 6 | TLS Handshake (cost) | $T_{TLS} \approx 2 \cdot RTT + E_{asym} + E_{sym}$ | $E$ = crypto time; critical for Kafka throughput |
| 7 | Kerberos Ticket Lifetime | $T_{valid} \leq 24h$ for TGT; $\leq 10h$ for service tickets | KTU standard value |
| 8 | PBKDF2 Iterations | $N_{iter} \geq 600{,}000$ (OWASP 2023) | For password hashing |
| 9 | bcrypt Cost | $cost = 2^{rounds}$, recommended $rounds \geq 12$ | Password storage |
| 10 | Argon2id | $m, t, p$ parameters (memory, iterations, parallelism) | Memory-hard hash |
| 11 | Key Rotation Period | $T_{rot} = \frac{365}{S}$ days, $S$ = sensitivity tier | NIST SP 800-57 |
| 12 | HDFS Replication Factor | $RF \geq 3$ for production | Balances durability vs storage |
| 13 | Erasure Coding Overhead | $Storage = \frac{k+m}{k}$ | e.g., RS(6,3) ⇒ 1.5× overhead |
| 14 | Kafka Replication | $acks = all$, $min.insync.replicas \geq 2$ | Strong durability |
| 15 | SQL Injection Defense | Use prepared statements: $Q = T(P, ...)$ | $T$ = parameterized template |
| 16 | NoSQL Injection Defense | Type-check + whitelist: $V = validate(V, schema)$ | Reject operator injection |
| 17 | CVSS Severity | $0.0 \le S \le 10.0$; $S \geq 7.0$ = High | CVSS v3.1 |
| 18 | Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | $MTTD = \frac{\sum t_{detect}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Lower is better |
| 19 | Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) | $MTTR = \frac{\sum t_{respond}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Lower is better |
| 20 | Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ | $L$ = length, $R$ = charset size, in bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a real Big Data platform, $T_{TLS}$ directly impacts Kafka throughput. Every broker handshake costs asymmetric crypto cycles. Tuning TLS session resumption (tickets) and using **mTLS with session resumption** can recover ~30-40% throughput lost to encryption.

## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

- **Financial Services:** PCI-DSS mandates encryption at rest and in transit; column-level encryption in Hive enables tokenization of PANs.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA requires audit logs of all PHI access — Apache Ranger audit logs feed SIEMs.
- **Telecommunications:** 5G analytics pipelines process millions of CDR records; NoSQL injection could expose customer locations.
- **Defense & Government:** FedRAMP and CMMC demand FIPS 140-3 validated crypto modules — directly informs the key management design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Entropy and Minimum Password Length

**Problem:** A Big Data admin console (e.g., Hue, Zeppelin, NiFi) requires a password. Compute the minimum length $L$ to achieve at least **80 bits of entropy** given a charset size $R = 94$ (printable ASCII).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — State the entropy formula.**

$$H = L \cdot \log_2(R)$$

where $L$ is the length of the password and $R$ is the cardinality of the character set.

**Step 2 — Set the entropy target.**

$$H_{target} = 80 \text{ bits}$$

This is the de-facto minimum for high-value admin accounts (NIST SP 800-63B).

**Step 3 — Solve for $L$.**

$$L = \frac{H_{target}}{\log_2(R)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R = 94$.**

$$\log_2(94) = \frac{\ln(94)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5433}{0.6931} \approx 6.5546 \text{ bits per character}$$

**Step 5 — Compute.**

$$L = \frac{80}{6.5546} \approx 12.205$$

**Step 6 — Round up (cannot have fractional characters).**

$$L_{min} = 13 \text{ characters}$$

**Conclusion:** A 13-character password from the 94-character ASCII set yields $\approx 85.21$ bits of entropy — meeting the 80-bit floor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TLS Handshake Cost on a Kafka Cluster

**Problem:** A Kafka cluster has $N = 10$ brokers and serves $C = 1000$ client connections per second. Each handshake requires $E_{asym} = 4$ ms of asymmetric crypto and $E_{sym} = 0.1$ ms of symmetric crypto. Network RTT = 50 ms. Estimate total crypto time per second and the impact of enabling session resumption (which eliminates $E_{asym}$ for 90% of resumed sessions).

**Step 1 — Time per full handshake.**

$$T_{full} = 2 \cdot RTT + E_{asym} + E_{sym}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$T_{full} = 2 \cdot 50 + 4 + 0.1 = 104.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Total crypto time without resumption.**

$$T_{total,noRes} = C \cdot T_{full} = 1000 \cdot 104.1 \text{ ms} = 104.1 \text{ seconds of CPU per second}$$

This is clearly unsustainable — the system would need 104 CPU cores dedicated to TLS.

**Step 4 — With 90% session resumption.**

$$T_{resumed} = 2 \cdot RTT + E_{sym} = 100 + 0.1 = 100.1 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{total,res} = 0.9 \cdot C \cdot T_{resumed} + 0.1 \cdot C \cdot T_{full}$$

$$T_{total,res} = 0.9 \cdot 1000 \cdot 100.1 + 0.1 \cdot 1000 \cdot 104.1$$

$$T_{total,res} = 90{,}090 + 10{,}410 = 100{,}500 \text{ ms} = 100.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Compute reduction.**

$$\Delta T = \frac{104.1 - 100.5}{104.1} \cdot 100\% \approx 3.46\%$$

**Conclusion:** Session resumption alone yields modest CPU savings (~3.5%) because $E_{asym}$ is a small fraction of $T_{full}$. The dominant cost is **network RTT**. To scale, engineers must use **connection multiplexing**, **HTTP/2**, or **long-lived persistent connections** (Kafka producers are designed this way).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Replication Factor Trade-off in HDFS

**Problem:** A 1 PB HDFS cluster uses $RF = 3$ and is upgraded to **Erasure Coding RS(6,3)**. Compute storage savings.

**Step 1 — HDFS Replication storage.**

$$S_{rep} = 1 \text{ PB} \cdot RF = 3 \text{ PB}$$

**Step 2 — Erasure Coding overhead.**

$$S_{EC} = S_{raw} \cdot \frac{k+m}{k} = 1 \text{ PB} \cdot \frac{6+3}{6} = 1.5 \text{ PB}$$

**Step 3 — Storage saved.**

$$\Delta S = 3 - 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ PB}$$

**Step 4 — Percent reduction.**

$$\%_{saved} = \frac{1.5}{3} \cdot 100\% = 50\%$$

**Step 5 — Durability comparison.**

HDFS RF=3 tolerates 2 simultaneous DataNode failures.

RS(6,3) tolerates 3 simultaneous failures out of 9 chunks (higher resilience per GB).

**Conclusion:** EC RS(6,3) cuts storage cost by **50%** while **increasing fault tolerance** — a net win for cold-data tiers in the data lake.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CVSS Risk Prioritization

**Problem:** A vulnerability in Apache Spark's `Spark SQL` has a CVSS v3.1 base score of $S = 8.1$ (High). Given that the cluster processes 50 TB of PII daily, compute the **risk priority**.

**Step 1 — CVSS Severity Tier.**

| Score Range | Severity |
|---|---|
| 0.0 – 3.9 | Low |
| 4.0 – 6.9 | Medium |
| 7.0 – 8.9 | High |
| 9.0 – 10.0 | Critical |

$S = 8.1$ ⇒ **High**.

**Step 2 — Risk formula (FAIR model, simplified).**

$$R = L \cdot I \cdot V$$

where:
- $L$ = Loss magnitude (financial + reputational)
- $I$ = Impact factor (PII exposure ⇒ $I = 1.0$)
- $V$ = Vulnerability factor (CVSS normalized) = $S / 10 = 0.81$

**Step 3 — Estimate Loss magnitude.**

Assume breach cost: $C_{breach} = \$164$ per record (IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2023).

$$N_{records} = 50 \text{ TB} \times 10^6 \text{ records/TB} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ records}$$

$$L = N_{records} \cdot C_{breach} = 5 \times 10^7 \cdot 164 = \$8.2 \text{ billion}$$

**Step 4 — Compute risk.**

$$R = 8.2 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.81 = \$6.642 \text{ billion}$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation.**

This is a **must-patch-within-SLA** vulnerability. Patch Spark within 24 hours; if no patch exists, apply compensating controls (WAF, network segmentation, Ranger row-level filters).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Threat × Practice Mapping

| Threat (STRIDE) | Big Data Attack Vector | Secure Coding Practice | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Fake Kerberos ticket, stolen Kafka SASL creds | mTLS, Kerberos with pre-auth, short ticket TTL | High |
