| **T**ampering | Malicious Avro/Parquet payload, HDFS block tampering | Strict schema validation, digital signatures, HDFS-append-only | High |
| **R**epudiation | Admin denies deleting data | WORM storage, immutable audit logs, blockchain anchoring | Medium |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Unencrypted PII in HDFS, verbose error pages | Encryption at rest + in transit, suppress error stack traces | High |
| **D**enial of Service | Oversized Kafka message, MapReduce job flooding YARN | Input size limits, YARN queues, rate limiting | Medium |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Hive UDF running as `hive` superuser, container escape | Sandboxed UDFs, Ranger policies, non-root containers, seccomp | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Secure Big Data Application Lifecycle (SDL-BD)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirements and Threat Modeling] --> B[Secure Design with Zero Trust]
    B --> C[Secure Coding Standards]
    C --> D[Static Analysis SAST]
    D --> E[Dependency and SBOM Scan]
    E --> F[Container Build and Sign]
    F --> G[Dynamic Analysis DAST]
    G --> H[Deploy to Cluster with mTLS]
    H --> I[Runtime Audit Logging]
    I --> J[Anomaly Detection and SIEM]
    J --> K[Incident Response and Forensics]
    K --> L[Feedback to Threat Model]
    L --> A

    classDef secure fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    classDef ops fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    classDef audit fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,color:#ffffff

    class A,B,C secure
    class D,E,F,G,H ops
    class I,J,K,L audit
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Layered Defense in a Big Data Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7["Layer 7 Supply Chain"]
        SC1[SBOM Generation]
        SC2[Vulnerability Scan]
        SC3[Signed Container Images]
    end

    subgraph L6["Layer 6 Logging and Monitoring"]
        L61[Audit Logs]
        L62[SIEM Integration]
        L63[Anomaly Detection ML]
    end

    subgraph L5["Layer 5 Hardening"]
        L51[Disable Defaults]
        L52[Network Policies]
        L53[Seccomp and AppArmor]
    end

    subgraph L4["Layer 4 Cryptography"]
        L41[TLS in Transit]
        L42[AES 256 at Rest]
        L43[KMS Key Rotation]
    end

    subgraph L3["Layer 3 Authorization"]
        L31[Apache Ranger]
        L32[RBAC and ABAC]
        L33[Row Column Masking]
    end

    subgraph L2["Layer 2 Authentication"]
        L21[Kerberos KDC]
        L22[OAuth 2 OIDC]
        L23[mTLS Service Identity]
    end

    subgraph L1["Layer 1 Input Validation"]
        L11[Schema Validation Avro Protobuf]
        L12[Whitelist Filters]
        L13[NoSQL Injection Defenses]
    end

    DATA[Untrusted Data Sources] --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
    L7 --> TRUST[Trusted Data Lake Output]

    classDef layer fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff
    classDef trust fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff

    class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6,L7 layer
    class TRUST trust
    class DATA data
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Apache Ranger Authorization Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as Data Engineer
    participant NiFi as NiFi or Zeppelin UI
    participant Ranger as Apache Ranger Admin
    plugin as HDFS or Hive Plugin
    data as Data Lake HDFS S3

    User->>NiFi: Submit Hive Query SELECT PII FROM customers
    NiFi->>plugin: Forward request with user identity
    plugin->>Ranger: Policy decision request with user resource action
    Ranger-->>plugin: ALLOW with column mask SSN last 4 digits
    plugin->>data: Execute query with transformed SELECT
    data-->>plugin: Raw result rows
    plugin-->>NiFi: Masked result rows
    NiFi-->>User: Masked output
    Ranger->>Ranger: Write audit log entry
```

    APPROVE --> PROD[Deploy to Production]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **secure coding** in the context of Big Data applications. List any **four** best practices.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Secure coding in Big Data refers to the practice of designing and writing application code for distributed data platforms (Hadoop, Spark, Kafka, NoSQL) in a way that proactively defends against vulnerabilities such as injection attacks, insecure deserialization, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration.

**Four best practices:**
1. **Input validation and schema enforcement** using Avro/Protobuf to reject malformed data.
2. **Principle of Least Privilege** — running Spark jobs as service accounts with minimal IAM/Ranger permissions.
3. **Encryption everywhere** — TLS 1.2+ in transit, AES-256 at rest via HDFS Transparent Encryption or cloud KMS.
4. **Audit logging** — capturing all data access events to Apache Ranger audit logs and forwarding to a SIEM.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
What is **NoSQL injection**? Give an example of how it can compromise a MongoDB-backed Big Data application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

NoSQL injection is a code-injection attack targeting NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra, Couchbase) where an attacker manipulates query operators to bypass authentication or extract unauthorized data.

**Example:** A login API accepts JSON like `{"user": "admin", "pass": "secret"}`. An attacker instead sends:

```json
{"user": "admin", "pass": {"$gt": ""}}
```

The `$gt` (greater-than) operator matches any non-empty string, effectively bypassing password validation and granting access as the admin user without knowing the password.

**Defense:** Use parameterized query builders (e.g., MongoDB's `MongoClient` with strict type-checking), never string-concatenate user input, and apply input whitelisting at the API layer.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Secure Coding Lifecycle & Apache Ranger

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven layers of secure coding practices** for Big Data applications with examples for each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture of **Apache Ranger** and explain how it enforces **fine-grained authorization** and **audit logging** in a Hadoop-based Big Data cluster. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Seven Layers of Secure Coding for Big Data Applications (7 marks):**

1. **Input Validation & Injection Prevention** — Strict Avro/Protobuf schema validation on data ingested via Kafka or Sqoop. Example: A Spark Streaming job rejects any Parquet row where `age < 0` or `age > 150`. Whitelist-based query construction prevents NoSQL injection in MongoDB.

2. **Authentication & Identity** — Kerberos for Hadoop daemons, OAuth 2.0 for REST APIs (NiFi, Spark Thrift Server), mTLS for service-to-service traffic in Kubernetes. Service accounts (no shared human creds) for automated jobs.

3. **Authorization & Least Privilege** — Apache Ranger policies that grant a marketing analyst `SELECT` on `customer_demographics` but **deny** access to `customer_ssn`. Use of resource-based policies on HDFS paths and Hive tables.

4. **Cryptographic Protection** — TLS 1.3 between Spark executors and Kafka brokers; HDFS Transparent Encryption (HDFS-6134) using Hadoop KMS; column-level encryption in Hive for PII fields like `credit_card`.

5. **Secure Configuration & Hardening** — Disable YARN Web UI without Kerberos, disable JMX without auth, enable SELinux, run containers as non-root, apply seccomp profiles.

6. **Logging, Monitoring & Tamper-Evidence** — Apache Ranger audit logs to Solr/Elasticsearch; SIEM integration; WORM storage for compliance; anomaly detection via ML on access patterns.

7. **Supply Chain & Dependency Hygiene** — SBOM generation (CycloneDX), Trivy scans on container images, signed images via Sigstore, reproducible builds, dependency pinning.

**(b) Apache Ranger Architecture & Enforcement (7 marks):**

Apache Ranger is a **centralized security framework** for the Hadoop ecosystem that provides:

**Core Components:**
- **Ranger Admin** — Web UI + REST API for policy authoring. Stores policies in MySQL/PostgreSQL.
- **Ranger Plugins** — Embedded in HDFS NameNode, HiveServer2, HBase Master, Kafka Broker, YARN RM, Solr, Knox. Plugins poll the Admin for policies every 30 seconds (configurable).
- **Ranger Usersync** — Synchronizes users/groups from LDAP/AD/Unix.
- **Ranger KMS** — Key Management Server for HDFS Transparent Encryption.
- **Ranger Audit** — Stores audit logs in Solr, HDFS, or Kafka.

**Authorization Flow:**
1. A user submits a Hive query via Zeppelin.
2. The HiveServer2 Ranger plugin intercepts the request, identifies the user, resource (database/table/column), and action (SELECT/INSERT).
3. The plugin checks its in-memory policy cache against the request.
4. If **deny**-rule matches, query is blocked. If **allow**-rule matches, the query proceeds (optionally with row/column masking or data masking policies applied).
5. The decision and request metadata are written to the audit store.

**Policy Types:**
- **Access Policies** — Allow/Deny by user/group/resource/action.
- **Masking Policies** — Replace sensitive values (e.g., mask all but last 4 of SSN).
- **Row-Level Filters** — Inject `WHERE` clauses (e.g., users see only their region's data).
- **Tag-Based Policies** — Use Apache Atlas tags to apply policies dynamically.

**Audit Logging:** Every access decision (allow, deny, error) is logged with timestamp, user, resource, action, client IP, and result. Logs are tamper-evident and exportable to SIEM for compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Threat Modeling & Secure Coding Defenses

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Apply the **STRIDE threat model** to a Big Data pipeline (Kafka → Spark Streaming → HBase) and identify **six threats** with corresponding secure coding defenses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **input validation**, **deserialization safety**, and **dependency management** as critical secure coding practices in Big Data, with code-level examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) STRIDE Threat Modeling on Kafka → Spark Streaming → HBase Pipeline (7 marks):**

| STRIDE | Threat | Secure Coding Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Attacker forges Kafka producer identity to inject malicious events | mTLS between producers and brokers; SASL/SCRAM authentication; ACLs on Kafka topics |
| **Tampering** | Malicious Avro payload causes Spark executor to deserialize a gadget chain (RCE) | Strict Avro schema validation; reject unknown fields; sandbox UDFs with SecurityManager |
| **Repudiation** | Admin deletes HBase rows and denies the action | WORM storage for audit logs; HBase versioning + timestamp-based forensic reconstruction |
| **Information Disclosure** | Sensitive data in HBase visible to all analysts | Apache Ranger column-family encryption + cell-level visibility labels; TLS for HBase RPC |
| **Denial of Service** | Oversized Kafka messages (1 GB each) starve Spark executors | Input size limits (`max.message.bytes`); YARN queue quotas; Spark `maxRecordSize` enforcement |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Spark UDF runs as `spark` superuser and accesses `/etc/shadow` | Run executors as non-root; seccomp profile blocking sensitive syscalls; Ranger deny-by-default |

**(b) Input Validation, Deserialization Safety, Dependency Management (7 marks):**

**1. Input Validation (2.5 marks):**
- Always use a strict schema (Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema) and reject data that does not conform.
- **Insecure code (Python Spark UDF):**
  ```python
  df.filter(df["city"] == user_input)  # user_input could be: "Mumbai' OR '1'='1"
  ```
- **Secure code (parameterized):**
  ```python
  safe_input = user_input.replace("'", "''")  # Whitelist-based escaping
  df.filter(df["city"] == safe_input)
  # Or better: use Column expressions that parameterize safely
  df.filter(col("city").isin(allowed_cities))
  ```

**2. Deserialization Safety (2.5 marks):**
- Java's `ObjectInputStream` is a well-known RCE vector. Avoid native Java serialization; use JSON, Avro, or Protobuf.
- **Insecure code (Java UDF):**
  ```java
  ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(new FileInputStream(userFile));
  MyObject obj = (MyObject) ois.readObject();  // RCE via gadget chain
  ```
- **Secure code:**
  ```java
  MyObject obj = mapper.readValue(new File(userFile), MyObject.class);  // Jackson with default typing disabled
  // Or use Avro:
  DatumReader<MyObject> reader = new SpecificDatumReader<>(MyObject.class);
  DataFileReader<MyObject> dataFileReader = new DataFileReader<>(new File(userFile), reader);
  ```

**3. Dependency Management (2 marks):**
- Pin all third-party libraries to exact versions; use private artifact repositories (Nexus, Artifactory).
- Scan every build with **OWASP Dependency-Check**, **Snyk**, or **Trivy**.
- Generate **SBOM** in CycloneDX format for regulatory compliance.
- Subscribe to security advisories via `dependabot` or GitHub Dependabot alerts.
- Example: A Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) in `log4j-core 2.14.1` ⇒ upgrade to `log4j-core 2.17.1+`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "encryption at rest" with "encryption in transit"** — Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if you don't explicitly name **HDFS Transparent Encryption** or **TLS 1.2+** as the actual implementation.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Why"** — Don't just list practices; explain *why* they matter (e.g., why NoSQL injection works in MongoDB — operator-based query construction).
> 3. **Mixing up Kerberos with OAuth** — Kerberos is for Hadoop daemon-to-daemon auth; OAuth 2.0 is for user-facing REST APIs.
> 4. **Skipping audit logging** — Many students omit logging, but KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists it as a separate CO5 outcome.
> 5. **No marks for "use strong passwords"** — Be specific: name the algorithm (Argon2id, bcrypt cost 12, PBKDF2 600k iterations) and entropy target (≥ 80 bits).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- ✅ Secure coding ≠ just writing functional code — it is **proactive defense against the OWASP Big Data Top 10**.
- ✅ **Input validation** is the #1 defense — use Avro/Protobuf schemas, whitelist filtering, parameterized queries.
- ✅ **NoSQL injection** in MongoDB works via operators (`$gt`, `$ne`, `$regex`); defend with type-checked query builders.
- ✅ **Deserialization RCE** is a top Hadoop/Spark risk — never use Java `ObjectInputStream` on untrusted data; prefer Avro/Protobuf/JSON.
- ✅ **Apache Ranger** = central authorization; **Apache Sentry** = alternative; **Knox** = perimeter gateway.
- ✅ **Encryption everywhere** — TLS 1.2+ in transit, AES-256 at rest, KMS-backed key rotation.
- ✅ **Kerberos** is the de-facto Hadoop authN; **OAuth 2.0/OIDC** for APIs; **mTLS** for service mesh.
- ✅ **Principle of Least Privilege** — service accounts, no shared creds, deny-by-default policies.
- ✅ **Audit logs must be tamper-evident** — WORM, blockchain-anchored, or append-only Kafka.
- ✅ **SBOM + SCA scanning** (Snyk, Trivy) catches Log4Shell-class vulnerabilities *before* deployment.
- ✅ **Container hardening** — non-root user, distroless base, read-only FS, seccomp, signed images.
- ✅ **CVSS ≥ 7.0 = High** ⇒ patch within 24-72 hours per most org SLAs.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-63B** password guideline: ≥ 8 chars (or ≥ 12 for high-value), 80+ bits entropy.
- ✅ **bcrypt cost ≥ 12**, **PBKDF2 ≥ 600,000 iterations**, **Argon2id** preferred.
- ✅ **HDFS RF=3** vs **Erasure Coding RS(6,3)** — EC saves 50% storage with higher fault tolerance.
- ✅ **Kafka `min.insync.replicas ≥ 2`** with `acks=all` for strong durability.
- ✅ **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- ✅ **Code-level mantra**: validate input, parameterize queries, escape output, encrypt sensitive data, log access events, scan dependencies, harden containers.
- ✅ KTU 2024 CO mapping: **CO4** = secure coding practices; **CO5** = audit, monitoring, compliance.
- ✅ **Always cite specific technologies** in exam answers (Ranger, Kerberos, HDFS-TE, TLS 1.3, Argon2id) — generic answers get 60% marks at best.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Monitoring and Incident Response: Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) for Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Monitoring and Incident Response: Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) for Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is an integrated cybersecurity solution that combines *Security Information Management (SIM)* — long-term log archival and compliance reporting — with *Security Event Management (SEM)* — real-time correlation, monitoring, and alerting of security events. In the context of Big Data ecosystems, SIEM platforms ingest, normalize, correlate, and analyze **petabyte-scale** machine-generated telemetry (logs, flows, packets, endpoint telemetry) originating from distributed infrastructure such as Hadoop clusters, Spark workers, NoSQL databases, cloud workloads, IoT gateways, and Software-Defined Networks (SDN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** SIEM is treated as the **central nervous system** of a Security Operations Center (SOC). For Big Data environments, the SIEM must support the **"5 V's"** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value — while preserving the **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of both data-at-rest and data-in-transit.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Control Tower"

Imagine a major international airport handling **thousands of flights per day** from hundreds of airlines:

| Airport Component | SIEM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Radar screens tracking every flight | Log collectors / forwarders ingesting event streams |
| Air Traffic Controllers | SOC analysts + correlation engine |
| Flight manifests from every airline (different formats) | Heterogeneous log sources (Apache, syslog, JSON, NetFlow) |
| Central control tower | SIEM correlation engine |
| Emergency response teams (fire, medical) | Incident Response (IR) team / SOAR platform |
| Black-box flight recorders | Long-term log archival & forensic storage |

Just as the control tower **normalizes** varied flight data into a unified radar display and **correlates** anomalies (e.g., two aircraft on collision course), a SIEM normalizes heterogeneous Big Data logs and correlates them to surface sophisticated multi-stage attacks that would otherwise be invisible inside individual log streams.

---

## 1.3 Why SIEM is Critical for Big Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume Reality Check:** A single mid-sized Hadoop cluster with **500 nodes** can generate over **1 TB of audit logs per day**. Manual analysis is impossible — automation via SIEM is a *necessity*, not an option.

**Key drivers for SIEM adoption in Big Data platforms:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance** — PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023, and KTU-mandated audit trails require immutable, time-synchronized log retention (typically **6 months hot + 2 years cold**).
2. **Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) Detection** — Modern attackers dwell inside networks for an average of **277 days** (per industry reports) before exfiltration.
3. **Insider Threat Mitigation** — Privileged users with HDFS admin or root access can exfiltrate sensitive data.
4. **Distributed Attack Surface** — Big Data spans HDFS, YARN, Spark, Kafka, Hive, HBase — each layer introduces attack vectors.

---

## 1.4 Core Functional Pillars of SIEM

The **six** foundational pillars of any production-grade SIEM are:

1. **Data Collection** — Agents, syslog forwarders, APIs, tap/spanning ports.
2. **Normalization & Parsing** — Converting vendor-specific formats into a common event schema (CEF, LEEF, JSON).
3. **Storage & Indexing** — Distributed storage using technologies like Elasticsearch, HDFS, or columnar databases.
4. **Correlation Engine** — Rule-based, statistical, and machine-learning-based detection.
5. **Alerting & Visualization** — Dashboards (Kibana, Grafana), ticketing integration.
6. **Incident Response Integration** — SOAR playbooks, automated containment actions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SIEM Architecture for Big Data — Layered Model

A SIEM tailored for Big Data environments follows a **5-layer reference architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Collection / Ingestion Layer
This is the **edge** of the SIEM pipeline. Data arrives from heterogeneous sources:
- **Hadoop ecosystem:** NameNode, DataNode, YARN ResourceManager, HBase RegionServers, HiveServer2, Spark drivers.
- **Network:** Firewalls (iptables, pfSense), IDS/IPS (Snort, Suricata), NetFlow/sFlow collectors.
- **Endpoints:** OS auditd, Sysmon, EDR agents.
- **Cloud:** AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs.
- **Authentication:** LDAP, Kerberos KDC, OAuth/OIDC providers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forwarders Used in Production:** Logstash Forwarders, Filebeat, Fluentd, Flume, Kafka Connect sinks. In Big Data, **Apache Kafka** is the *de facto* durable buffer between producers and the SIEM backend.

### Layer 2 — Normalization & Enrichment Layer
Raw events are transformed into a canonical schema. Each event typically contains:

$$
\text{Event} = \{t, src, dst, user, action, result, asset, severity, raw\}
$$

where **t** is the timestamp, **src/dst** are source/destination endpoints, **user** is the principal, **action** is the operation type, **result** indicates success/failure, **asset** is the affected resource, **severity** is the alert weight, and **raw** preserves the original payload.

**Enrichment** augments events with threat intelligence (IPs, file hashes, CVEs), asset criticality, and user role context.

### Layer 3 — Storage / Indexing Layer
Big Data SIEMs employ **distributed, horizontally-scalable** storage:
- **Hot tier** (last 30 days): SSD-backed Elasticsearch, OpenSearch.
- **Warm tier** (30–90 days): Compressed columnar stores.
- **Cold tier** (> 90 days): HDFS + Parquet/ORC files, S3-compatible object storage with WORM (Write Once Read Many) compliance.

### Layer 4 — Correlation & Analytics Layer
This is the **brain** of the SIEM. Three correlation paradigms are applied:

1. **Rule-based correlation** — Boolean logic over event attributes (e.g., "5 failed SSH logins within 60 seconds from same IP").
2. **Statistical correlation** — Z-score, moving averages, entropy analysis to detect deviations from baseline.
3. **Machine Learning correlation** — Supervised (Random Forest, XGBoost) and unsupervised (K-Means, Isolation Forest, LSTM autoencoders) models for anomaly and threat detection.

### Layer 5 — Presentation & Response Layer
Dashboards, alerts, and SOAR-driven automated playbooks close the loop with the SOC.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| Events Per Second (EPS) | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{N_{\text{events}}}{\Delta t}$ | Capacity planning & licensing |
| Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occurrence},i})$ | SOC efficiency KPI |
| Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{resolved},i} - t_{\text{detect},i})$ | IR team effectiveness KPI |
| Detection Coverage (DC) | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lvert \text{Detected Threats} \rvert}{\lvert \text{Total Known Threats} \rvert} \times 100\%$ | Visibility metric (use $\lvert$ escaped form) |
| False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Correlation rule tuning |
| False Negative Rate (FNR) | $\text{FNR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | Missed attack measurement |
| Z-Score for anomaly detection | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Statistical correlation baseline |
| Shannon Entropy (event diversity) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i)\log_2 P(x_i)$ | Detecting covert channels in logs |
| Storage Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | HDFS cold-tier efficiency |
| Risk Score (CVSS-inspired) | $\text{Risk} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood}$ | Alert prioritization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked about SIEM metrics, **always define the units** (EPS = events/second, MTTD = hours, FPR/FNR = dimensionless ratio). Marks are awarded for *units*, not just the formula.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | SIEM + Big Data Use Case |
|---|---|
| Banking & Finance | Real-time fraud detection across millions of transactions |
| Healthcare | HIPAA audit logging for EHR access patterns |
| E-Commerce | Bot detection, credential stuffing identification |
| Telecommunications | CDR (Call Detail Record) anomaly detection for SIM swap fraud |
| IoT / Smart Cities | Aggregating telemetry from millions of sensors for attack correlation |
| Government / Defense | Nation-state APT detection across classified Hadoop clusters |

---

## 2.4 SOAR — The Evolution Beyond SIEM

**Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR)** extends SIEM by:
- **Orchestration:** Integrating disparate security tools via APIs.
- **Automation:** Executing playbooks without human intervention (e.g., auto-quarantine an infected host).
- **Response:** Coordinating incident containment, eradication, and recovery.

> [!NOTE]
> **SIEM vs SOAR vs XDR:** SIEM = aggregation + correlation. SOAR = orchestration + automation. XDR (Extended Detection & Response) = endpoint-native telemetry + integrated response. They are *complementary*, not competitive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) from Raw SOC Logs

**Problem Context:** A SOC operates on a Hadoop cluster and processes security events from **8 NameNodes** over a 7-day observation window. The following incident detection log is recorded:

| Incident ID | Occurrence Time $t_o$ | Detection Time $t_d$ |
|---|---|---|
| INC-001 | Day 1, 02:14:00 | Day 1, 02:14:11 |
| INC-002 | Day 2, 18:42:30 | Day 2, 18:42:39 |
| INC-003 | Day 3, 05:09:12 | Day 3, 05:11:48 |
| INC-004 | Day 5, 11:30:00 | Day 5, 11:30:07 |
| INC-005 | Day 7, 22:05:55 | Day 7, 22:08:21 |

**Step 1 — Compute the per-incident detection latency $\Delta t_i$ for each row:**

$$
\Delta t_1 = t_{d,1} - t_{o,1} = 02\text{h}:14\text{m}:11\text{s} - 02\text{h}:14\text{m}:00\text{s} = 11 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t_2 = 18\text{h}:42\text{m}:39\text{s} - 18\text{h}:42\text{m}:30\text{s} = 9 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t_3 = 05\text{h}:11\text{m}:48\text{s} - 05\text{h}:09\text{m}:12\text{s} = 156 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t_4 = 11\text{h}:30\text{m}:07\text{s} - 11\text{h}:30\text{m}:00\text{s} = 7 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t_5 = 22\text{h}:08\text{m}:21\text{s} - 22\text{h}:05\text{m}:55\text{s} = 146 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the MTTD formula:**

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Delta t_i = \frac{11 + 9 + 156 + 7 + 146}{5}
$$

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{329}{5} = 65.8 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
An MTTD of **65.8 seconds** is considered **excellent** in industry benchmarks (target is typically < 5 minutes). The two outliers (INC-003 and INC-005) suggest deeper forensic investigation — possibly slow disk I/O during off-peak hours or correlation rule latency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Score Anomaly Detection in SIEM

**Problem Context:** A Spark Streaming job monitors login attempts per user. Over a **30-day baseline**, the average login count is $\mu = 42$ attempts/day with standard deviation $\sigma = 5$. On Day 31, a user **alice@bank.com** generates $x = 87$ logins. Determine whether this is anomalous.

**Step 1 — Recall the Z-score definition:**

$$
Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
Z = \frac{87 - 42}{5} = \frac{45}{5} = 9.0
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret against the empirical rule:**
- $\lvert Z \rvert < 1$ → Normal behavior (within $1\sigma$).
- $1 \le \lvert Z \rvert < 2$ → Mild deviation.
- $2 \le \lvert Z \rvert < 3$ → Likely anomaly (within 95% CI).
- $\lvert Z \rvert \ge 3$ → **Strong anomaly** (outside 99.7% CI).

Since $Z = 9.0 \gg 3$, this is a **critical anomaly** indicating either credential stuffing, brute-force attack, or compromised account. The SIEM correlation engine should immediately trigger an alert and escalate to the IR team.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to express the **threshold interpretation**. Writing only the Z-value without stating the rule loses 2 marks. Always pair numerical answers with threshold commentary.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detection Coverage (DC) Computation

**Problem Context:** A SIEM is configured with **120 correlation rules**. During a red-team exercise, **340 attack techniques** from the MITRE ATT&CK matrix are simulated. The SIEM correctly detected **272** of them.

**Step 1 — Recall DC formula:**

$$
\text{DC} = \frac{\lvert \text{Detected Threats} \rvert}{\lvert \text{Total Known Threats} \rvert} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{DC} = \frac{272}{340} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
A detection coverage of **80%** is above industry average (typically 50–70%). The remaining **20% gap** (68 undetected techniques) should drive rule-engineering efforts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SIEM Tools for Big Data

| SIEM Tool | Big Data Backend | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Splunk Enterprise | Splunk S2/S3 indexers | Mature correlation language (SPL) | High licensing cost |
| IBM QRadar | PostgreSQL + Ariel DB | Strong compliance reporting | Scaling beyond 1M EPS expensive |
| ArcSight ESM | CORRE-typed database | High-throughput ingestion | Steep learning curve |
| Elastic Security (ELK) | Elasticsearch cluster | Open-source, highly scalable | Manual rule engineering |
| Apache Metron | Kafka + Storm + HBase | Purpose-built for Big Data SIEM | Retired, now Apache Spot / Streamline |
| OSSIM (AlienVault) | MySQL + Elasticsearch | Unified SIEM + asset discovery | Limited ML capabilities |
| Wazuh | OpenSearch / Wazuh Indexer | Free, cloud-native | Smaller community |
| Azure Sentinel (Defender) | Log Analytics Workspace | Native cloud integration | Azure-centric |

---

## 3.5 SIEM Data Pipeline — End-to-End Flow (Derivation Logic)

The **eight sequential stages** a log entry traverses inside a Big Data SIEM are:

1. **Generation** — Source produces raw event (e.g., NameNode audit log line).
2. **Collection** — Filebeat / Logstash agent ships event to Kafka topic.
3. **Buffering** — Kafka provides durable, partitioned storage.
4. **Parsing & Normalization** — Logstash / Flink converts to ECS / CEF format.
5. **Enrichment** — GeoIP lookup, threat intel feed, asset database join.
6. **Correlation** — Rules engine evaluates against active correlation rules.
7. **Indexing** — Event stored in Elasticsearch with inverted indices.
8. **Visualization / Alerting** — Kibana dashboards, PagerDuty alerts.

Each stage introduces **latency** $L_i$, and the total end-to-end latency is:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} L_i
$$

For a well-tuned pipeline, $L_{\text{total}} \le 5$ seconds is the production target.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIEM Architecture for Big Data — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hadoop Ecosystem Logs] --> K[Apache Kafka Broker]
    B[Network Devices and Firewalls] --> K
    C[Cloud Audit Trails] --> K
    D[Endpoint EDR Agents] --> K
    K --> E[Ingestion and Parsing Layer]
    E --> F[Normalization and Enrichment Engine]
    F --> G[Correlation Engine]
    G --> H[Hot Tier SSD Storage]
    G --> I[Warm Tier Compressed Storage]
    G --> J[Cold Tier HDFS Object Storage]
    G --> L[Alerting and Dashboard Layer]
    L --> M[SOC Analyst Console]
    L --> N[SOAR Automation Playbook]
    N --> O[Incident Response Actions]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Sources A–D (left) feed a **Kafka** broker acting as a resilient message bus.
- The Kafka topic feeds Layer E (Ingestion), which is a Flink or Logstash job.
- Outputs flow to **three storage tiers** (H, I, J) for cost-optimized retention.
- The Correlation Engine G drives both analyst dashboards (M) and automated SOAR playbooks (N).

---

## 4.2 Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Preparation] --> P2[Detection and Analysis]
    P2 --> P3[Containment Eradication and Recovery]
    P3 --> P4[Post Incident Activity]
    P4 -. Feedback Loop .-> P1
    P2 --> SIEM2[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    SIEM2 --> P2
    P3 --> SOAR2[SOAR Playbook Executor]
    SOAR2 --> P3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The four NIST phases cycle continuously.
- The **SIEM** continuously augments the Detection phase with correlated events.
- The **SOAR** platform accelerates Containment via automated playbooks (e.g., "isolate host", "revoke token").

---

## 4.3 SIEM Correlation Workflow — Rule Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Event Arrives at Correlation Engine] --> R2{Schema Valid?}
    R2 -- No --> R3[Drop and Log Parser Error]
    R2 -- Yes --> R4[Apply Field Mappings]
    R4 --> R5[Evaluate Rule Set 1 to N]
    R5 --> R6{Any Rule Matched?}
    R6 -- No --> R7[Index for Search Only]
    R6 -- Yes --> R8[Calculate Risk Score]
    R8 --> R9{Score Above Threshold?}
    R9 -- No --> R10[Low Priority Alert]
    R9 -- Yes --> R11[Trigger Alert and SOAR]
    R11 --> R12[Notify SOC Analyst]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- R1 → R2: Schema validation prevents malformed events from poisoning the rule engine.
- R5: Multiple correlation rules fire in parallel.
- R8: Risk scoring aggregates weighted signals (severity, asset criticality, threat intel confidence).
- R11: Only **high-priority** alerts invoke SOAR playbooks to avoid alert fatigue.

---

## 4.4 Big Data SIEM Data Flow — Detailed Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Source Tier]
        S1[HDFS Audit Logs]
        S2[YARN and Spark Logs]
        S3[Kafka Application Logs]
        S4[Network NetFlow Data]
    end
    subgraph BUF[Buffering Tier]
        B1[Kafka Topic security events]
        B2[Schema Registry]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Tier]
        P1[Flink Normalization Job]
        P2[Spark Enrichment Job]
        P3[Rule Engine Drools]
    end
    subgraph STOR[Storage Tier]
        D1[Elasticsearch Hot Index]
        D2[Parquet on HDFS Warm]
        D3[S3 Cold Archive]
    end
    subgraph RESP[Response Tier]
        Q1[Kibana Dashboard]
        Q2[Grafana Visualization]
        Q3[PagerDuty Alert]
        Q4[SOAR Phantom Playbook]
    end
    SRC --> B1
    B1 --> P1
    P2 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    P3 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Four source tiers (S1–S4) feed a single Kafka topic (B1).
- Flink handles *stateless* normalization (P1); Spark handles *stateful* enrichment (P2).
- The Drools rule engine (P3) executes deterministic correlation.
- A **tiered storage** strategy (D1 → D2 → D3) balances query performance with cost.
- The Response Tier (Q1–Q4) provides both human-facing visualization and machine-driven remediation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SIEM and explain its two constituent components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** is a centralized cybersecurity solution that combines two complementary functions:

1. **Security Information Management (SIM)** — Handles long-term storage, archival, and compliance reporting of security logs. It focuses on *historical* analysis, audit trail preservation, and regulatory evidence generation.

2. **Security Event Management (SEM)** — Performs *real-time* monitoring, correlation, and alerting of security events as they arrive from the network and hosts.

Together, SIM + SEM provide a unified platform that ingests heterogeneous logs, normalizes them, correlates events to detect multi-stage attacks, and generates alerts for the Security Operations Center (SOC). In Big Data contexts, the SIEM scales horizontally across distributed storage to handle petabyte-scale telemetry.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition, 1.5 marks SIM + SEM breakdown, 0.5 mark Big Data context)*

---

### Question 2: What is MTTD and why is it important in a SOC? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** is the average elapsed time between the occurrence of a security incident and its detection by the SIEM/SOC. Mathematically,

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{\text{detect},i} - t_{\text{occurrence},i})
$$

**Importance:**
1. It is a **primary KPI** for SOC operational efficiency.
2. Lower MTTD directly reduces attacker dwell time, limiting data exfiltration and lateral movement.
3. Industry benchmarks target MTTD < **5 minutes** for critical incidents and < **24 hours** for low-severity events.
4. Regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, GDPR breach-notification clauses) implicitly penalize high MTTD.

*(Allocation: 1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation of importance)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): SIEM Architecture for Big Data `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **5-layer SIEM architecture** for a Big Data environment. Discuss the role of Apache Kafka in the ingestion layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable correlation rule example, describe how a SIEM detects a **brute-force SSH attack** on a Hadoop edge node. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The five layers are:**

**1. Collection / Ingestion Layer:** Gathers logs from Hadoop daemons (NameNode, DataNode, YARN), network devices, and endpoints. Forwarders like Filebeat, Flume, or syslog-ng are deployed on each source. *(1 mark)*

**2. Normalization & Enrichment Layer:** Parses heterogeneous formats (syslog, JSON, Avro) into a canonical schema (ECS/CEF). Augments with GeoIP, threat intelligence, and asset context. *(1 mark)*

**3. Storage / Indexing Layer:** Distributes data across hot, warm, and cold tiers using Elasticsearch (hot), Parquet on HDFS (warm), and S3-compatible object storage (cold). Enables cost-efficient long-term retention. *(1 mark)*

**4. Correlation & Analytics Layer:** Evaluates rule-based, statistical, and ML-based correlations. Rule engines (Drools), Z-score analytics, and LSTM autoencoders are typical implementations. *(1 mark)*

**5. Presentation & Response Layer:** Dashboards (Kibana, Grafana) for analysts; SOAR integration for automated containment. *(1 mark)*

**Role of Apache Kafka (2 marks):**
Apache Kafka acts as a **durable, partitioned, replayable message bus** between source producers and downstream SIEM consumers. It decouples ingestion from processing, enabling horizontal scalability, fault tolerance, and exactly-once semantics. Big Data SIEMs (e.g., Apache Metron, Confluent's security stack) leverage Kafka topics such as `security.events.network`, `security.events.endpoint`, and `security.events.iam` to organize streams by domain, allowing independent scaling of consumers (Flink for normalization, Spark for enrichment).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Correlation Rule Example — Brute-Force SSH Detection:**

**Scenario:** A Hadoop edge node `edge01.cluster.local` is being probed by an attacker IP `203.0.113.45`.

**Sample correlation rule (pseudocode for clarity):**

```
RULE "SSH Brute Force Detection"
{
  WHEN
    count(
      event.action = "ssh.login.failed",
      source.ip = "203.0.113.45",
      destination.host = "edge01.cluster.local",
      time.window = 300 seconds
    ) >= 5
  THEN
    raise_alert(
      severity = HIGH,
      name = "Possible SSH Brute Force",
      risk_score = 85,
      trigger_soar_playbook = "isolate_host_and_block_ip"
    )
}
```

**Detection Flow:** *(3 marks)*

1. The SSH daemon writes each failed attempt to `/var/log/secure`. A Filebeat agent ships the line to the Kafka topic `security.events.endpoint`.
2. The normalization layer parses the line into fields: `event.action=login.failed`, `source.ip`, `destination.host`, `user.name`, `timestamp`.
3. The correlation engine evaluates the rule. After the **5th failure within 300 seconds**, the rule fires.
4. The risk score of **85** (above threshold of 70) triggers both an analyst alert (PagerDuty) and a SOAR playbook.
5. The SOAR playbook automatically: (i) blocks IP `203.0.113.45` at the perimeter firewall, (ii) disables the targeted user account, (iii) creates a ServiceNow ticket for forensic review.

**Why this defeats the attack:** Manual review of 5 individual log lines would have likely been deprioritized as routine noise. Only the **temporal correlation** across the 5-minute window reveals the attack pattern.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Incident Response & Metrics `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle** and explain how SIEM integrates with each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **MTTD, FPR, and Detection Coverage** for a SOC given the following one-month dataset, and interpret each metric. **(7 marks)**

**Dataset:**
- Total simulated attacks: 400
- Attacks correctly detected by SIEM: 312
- True Negatives (correctly ignored benign events): 8500
- False Positives (benign events flagged as malicious): 188
- Detection latencies (in seconds) for 5 sample incidents: 18, 22, 245, 31, 14

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**NIST Incident Response Lifecycle — Four Phases:**

**1. Preparation (1.5 marks):** Establish IR policies, train SOC analysts, configure SIEM correlation rules, deploy endpoint agents, and define escalation procedures. The SIEM is pre-configured with dashboards, baselines, and threat intelligence feeds.

**2. Detection & Analysis (2 marks):** The SIEM continuously ingests logs, correlates events, and raises alerts. Analysts triage alerts using the SIEM's search interface (e.g., Splunk SPL, Kibana KQL). Pattern matching against MITRE ATT&CK tactics accelerates triage.

**3. Containment, Eradication & Recovery (2 marks):** The SIEM provides forensic timeline reconstruction. SOAR integration (driven by SIEM alerts) executes containment actions: host isolation, account disablement, IOC blocklisting. Eradication involves removing malware/persistence; recovery restores services from known-good backups.

**4. Post-Incident Activity (1.5 marks):** The SIEM's archived logs are used to author an after-action report. Lessons learned feed back into **rule tuning**, **playbook updates**, and **training** — closing the feedback loop.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — MTTD Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{18 + 22 + 245 + 31 + 14}{5} = \frac{330}{5} = 66 \text{ seconds}
$$

Interpretation: An MTTD of **66 seconds** is excellent, though incident #3 (245 s) indicates an opportunity for rule optimization (likely correlation latency or I/O bottleneck during that event).

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{188}{188 + 8500} = \frac{188}{8688} \approx 0.0216 \approx 2.16\%
$$

Interpretation: An FPR of **2.16%** is within industry-acceptable range (< 5%). Indicates well-tuned correlation rules with minimal alert fatigue.

**Step 3 — Detection Coverage (2 marks):**

$$
\text{DC} = \frac{312}{400} \times 100\% = 78\%
$$

Interpretation: Coverage of **78%** indicates the SIEM is missing roughly **22%** (88 techniques) of the simulated attacks. Rule engineering against MITRE ATT&CK gaps and ML-based anomaly detection improvements are recommended.

**Step 4 — Composite Interpretation (1 mark):**
The SOC demonstrates strong operational maturity: fast detection, low false positives, and acceptable coverage. Strategic investments in ML-driven analytics and broader MITRE ATT&CK rule coverage would push performance into the top quartile.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in metric answers.** Writing "MTTD = 66" without "seconds" loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing FPR with FNR.** False Positive Rate uses FP+TN in the denominator; False Negative Rate uses FN+TP. Mixing them up is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Omitting threat intel enrichment.** When asked about SIEM architecture, simply listing layers without explaining **enrichment** loses 2 marks. Enrichment is the differentiator between a log collector and a true SIEM.
> 4. **Skipping the feedback loop in IR lifecycle.** Post-incident activity must explicitly mention feeding lessons learned back into **rule tuning and analyst training**.
> 5. **Using `|` (pipe) inside markdown tables.** This breaks table rendering in many parsers. Always use `\lvert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation in exam answers if typed in markdown.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SIEM = SIM (long-term storage & compliance) + SEM (real-time correlation & alerting).** Both halves are mandatory for full marks.
- **Five layers:** Collection → Normalization → Storage → Correlation → Presentation/Response. Memorize the role of each.
- **Apache Kafka** is the de facto message bus in modern Big Data SIEMs for decoupling producers from consumers.
- **MTTD and MTTR** are the two most important SOC KPIs — always quote units (seconds/minutes).
- **Detection Coverage (DC)** = (Detected Threats / Total Known Threats) × 100%. Target ≥ 80%.
- **Z-score anomaly detection** — values with $\lvert Z \rvert \ge 3$ indicate strong anomalies.
- **NIST IR Lifecycle** has four phases: Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident Activity.
- **SIEM vs SOAR vs XDR:** SIEM detects, SOAR automates response, XDR unifies endpoint-native detection.
- **Tiered storage** (Hot → Warm → Cold) balances query performance with archival cost.
- **Threat intelligence enrichment** is what elevates a SIEM from a log aggregator to an attack-detection platform.
- **MITRE ATT&CK** is the industry-standard taxonomy used to measure detection coverage and engineer correlation rules.
- **Common tools:** Splunk, QRadar, Elastic Security, Wazuh, Azure Sentinel. Open-source options include the **ELK Stack** and **Wazuh**.
- **Always pair Z-scores, MTTD, FPR, FNR with threshold interpretation**, not just numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident response strategies for Big Data breaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT743 - BIG DATA SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Incident response strategies for Big Data breaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incident Response (IR) in Big Data environments** is the structured, policy-driven set of coordinated procedures and technical actions undertaken by an organization to **detect, contain, eradicate, and recover** from security incidents (unauthorized access, data exfiltration, ransomware, insider threats, or Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks) that compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of large-scale, distributed data systems such as Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL clusters, and cloud-based data lakes.

According to the **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** Computer Security Incident Handling Guide, an incident response strategy for Big Data breaches must address the **unique scale, velocity, and distributed nature** of modern data pipelines, integrating the four primary lifecycle phases: **Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Airport Security Control Tower" Model)

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **massive international airport** handling 500 flights per hour across hundreds of gates. A Big Data cluster behaves exactly like this airport:
> - **Air Traffic Controllers** = SIEM systems (Splunk, ELK, ArcSight) that monitor the data traffic.
> - **Runways** = Data pipelines (Kafka, Spark Streaming, Sqoop).
> - **Radar Systems** = Anomaly detection algorithms (Isolation Forest, Autoencoders).
> - **Emergency Crash Teams** = Incident Response Teams (CSIRT/CERT).
> - **Black Box Recorders** = Distributed audit logs (HDFS audit logs, AWS CloudTrail).
>
> When a "suspicious aircraft" (malicious query/exfiltration attempt) is detected, the response must be **immediate, coordinated, and protocol-driven** — exactly mirroring how Big Data IR strategies function.

### 1.3 Core Incident Classification Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard IR Metrics (NIST-Aligned):**
> - **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect):** Average time elapsed between compromise onset and detection.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover):** Average time from detection to full service restoration.
> - **MTTC (Mean Time To Contain):** Time taken to isolate the affected node/cluster segment.
> - **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Maximum acceptable downtime before business impact.
> - **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum acceptable data loss (measured in time).

### 1.4 Big Data Breach — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Big Data breach** is defined as a confirmed security event in which an adversary gains **unauthorized access** to petabyte-scale data repositories (e.g., HDFS, S3 buckets, Cassandra rings, MongoDB shards), exfiltrates sensitive records (PII, PHI, financial data, intellectual property), or compromises the integrity/availability of distributed computational frameworks (Spark, Flink, Hive, Presto).

### 1.5 Big Data IR — Why Traditional IR Fails

Traditional IR playbooks assume:
- Single-server architecture
- Bounded log volumes
- Static perimeter defenses

Big Data environments break these assumptions because of:
- **Volume (Terabytes/Petabytes of logs)** — overwhelms traditional SIEMs.
- **Velocity (Real-time streaming data)** — attack windows shrink from days to milliseconds.
- **Variety (Structured + Unstructured + Semi-structured)** — heterogeneous attack surfaces.
- **Distributed Trust Boundaries** — compromised nodes still participate in cluster consensus.
- **Multi-tenancy in Cloud Data Lakes** — noisy neighbor attacks and cross-tenant pivoting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NIST Incident Response Lifecycle (Adapted for Big Data)

The IR process is iterative and cyclic, not linear. Each phase feeds intelligence back into the next iteration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Big Data Incident Response (NIST SP 800-61):**

**Phase 1: Preparation**
- Establish CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Team) roles.
- Deploy forensic-ready logging across all nodes (`auditd`, `HDFS audit logs`, `Kafka JMX metrics`).
- Build hardened **gold images** of Hadoop/Spark nodes for rapid redeployment.
- Pre-authorize network isolation scripts (ACLs, Security Groups).
- Conduct tabletop exercises simulating ransomware on NameNode.

**Phase 2: Detection & Analysis**
- Aggregate logs using distributed SIEM (Splunk Enterprise, Elastic Security).
- Apply **anomaly detection** models trained on baseline cluster behavior.
- Correlate indicators of compromise (IoCs) across HDFS, YARN, Hive, and edge nodes.
- Triage: classify as **True Positive (TP)**, **False Positive (FP)**, or **Benign True Positive (BTP)**.

**Phase 3: Containment, Eradication & Recovery**
- **Short-term containment:** Quarantine compromised DataNode (revoke Kerberos ticket).
- **Long-term containment:** Isolate entire cluster segment via VPC peering restrictions.
- **Eradication:** Wipe infected nodes, rotate all service principal keys, invalidate Kerberos tickets via `kadmin`.
- **Recovery:** Restore HDFS snapshots from immutable backup (e.g., `distcp` from cold storage).

**Phase 4: Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned)**
- Forensic root-cause analysis (RCA).
- Update detection signatures and YARA rules.
- File regulatory disclosures (GDPR Article 33: 72-hour breach notification).
- Publish executive summary and technical postmortem.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical formulas for KTU 2024 exam calculations on Incident Response metrics:**

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit | Application in Big Data IR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MTTD** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum (T_{detect,i} - T_{compromise,i})}{N}$ | seconds / minutes | Detecting intrusions in streaming pipelines |
| **MTTR** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum (T_{recovered,i} - T_{detect,i})}{N}$ | seconds / minutes | Cluster restoration from backup |
| **MTTC** | $MTTC = \frac{\sum (T_{contained,i} - T_{detect,i})}{N}$ | seconds / minutes | Quarantine latency |
| **Incident Severity Score (ISS)** | $ISS = (C \times I) + (D \times R)$ | dimensionless (0–25) | Breach prioritization |
| **Data Exfiltration Volume Rate** | $R_{exfil} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{baseline}}{t_{window}}$ | GB / hour | Detecting large-scale data theft |
| **Anomaly Z-Score Threshold** | $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Flagging outlier queries |
| **Detection Precision** | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | ratio (0–1) | SIEM alert quality |
| **Detection Recall** | $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ratio (0–1) | Coverage of real threats |
| **F1-Score (Detection)** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | ratio (0–1) | Balanced detection metric |
| **Recovery Completeness** | $RC = \frac{\sum Data_{recovered}}{\sum Data_{affected}}$ | ratio (0–1) | HDFS snapshot fidelity |

**Where:**
- $T_{detect,i}$ = Detection timestamp of incident $i$
- $T_{compromise,i}$ = Initial compromise timestamp of incident $i$
- $T_{recovered,i}$ = Full recovery timestamp of incident $i$
- $C$ = Confidentiality impact (0–5)
- $I$ = Integrity impact (0–5)
- $D$ = Downtime impact (0–5)
- $R$ = Regulatory exposure (0–5)
- $V_{out}$ = Outbound data volume
- $V_{baseline}$ = Normal outbound volume
- $TP, FP, FN$ = True Positive, False Positive, False Negative counts

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Big Data IR strategies are deployed across:
> - **Finance:** Real-time fraud detection in transaction streaming (Spark Streaming + ML).
> - **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant breach containment in genomic data lakes.
> - **Telecommunications:** CDR (Call Detail Record) anomaly investigation.
> - **E-Commerce:** Customer PII protection under GDPR Article 32.
> - **Government:** APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) hunting in national-scale sensor networks.

### 2.4 Big Data-Specific Attack Vectors Requiring Tailored IR

1. **NameNode Compromise** — Metadata tampering attacks; requires immediate Kerberos ticket invalidation.
2. **DataNode Ransomware** — HDFS block encryption by malicious YARN containers; requires snapshot rollback.
3. **Kafka Topic Poisoning** — Malicious event injection in stream pipelines; requires topic quarantine.
4. **Hive Metastore Injection** — Malicious schema definitions enabling SQL injection at petabyte scale.
5. **Spark Driver Hijacking** — Adversarial ML model injection into distributed training jobs.
6. **NoSQL Injection (MongoDB/Cassandra)** — Schema-less injection exploiting weak query validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Severity Scoring for a Big Data Breach

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Hadoop cluster experiences a breach where an attacker exfiltrated **2 TB of PII** over **6 hours** by compromising a single DataNode. The breach went undetected for **18 hours**, full recovery took **9 hours**, and 480 million user records were affected. Calculate MTTD, MTTR, Incident Severity Score (ISS), and Detection Precision (given TP=480, FP=15, FN=20).

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Time To Detect (MTTD):**

Since we have a single incident in this calculation window:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTD &= T_{detect} - T_{compromise} \\
MTTD &= 18 \text{ hours} - 0 \text{ hours} \\
MTTD &= 18 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the elapsed time between compromise onset (hour 0) and alert generation (hour 18).

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Time To Respond (MTTR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= T_{recovered} - T_{detect} \\
MTTR &= (18 + 9) \text{ hours} - 18 \text{ hours} \\
MTTR &= 9 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the operational time the CSIRT consumed to fully restore the cluster.

**Step 3 — Compute Incident Severity Score (ISS):**

We assign the following ratings (scale 0–5):

| Dimension | Rating |
|---|---|
| Confidentiality (C) | 5 (PII exfiltration) |
| Integrity (I) | 2 (data not modified, only stolen) |
| Downtime (D) | 4 (9-hour recovery on petabyte data) |
| Regulatory (R) | 5 (GDPR + HIPAA exposure) |

Applying the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= (C \times I) + (D \times R) \\
ISS &= (5 \times 2) + (4 \times 5) \\
ISS &= 10 + 20 \\
ISS &= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since ISS > 25, this incident is classified as **CRITICAL (Severity Level 1)** and triggers immediate executive escalation and 72-hour GDPR breach notification.

**Step 4 — Compute Detection Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
Precision &= \frac{480}{480 + 15} \\
Precision &= \frac{480}{495} \\
Precision &= 0.9697 \approx 96.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This indicates that **96.97% of alerts** generated by the SIEM were genuine threats.

**Step 5 — Compute Detection Recall:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Recall &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
Recall &= \frac{480}{480 + 20} \\
Recall &= \frac{480}{500} \\
Recall &= 0.96 = 96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.9697 \cdot 0.96}{0.9697 + 0.96} \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.930912}{1.9297} \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot 0.48246 \\
F1 &= 0.9649 \approx 96.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A high F1-Score (96.49%) demonstrates that the SIEM detection pipeline is well-calibrated. KTU examiners expect students to **comment on what these metrics imply operationally** — e.g., "Precision could be improved by raising the anomaly Z-score threshold from $\sigma$ to $2\sigma$."

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Containment Strategy Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix for Big Data Breach Containment:**

| Attack Vector | Containment Action | Tools | MTTC Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| NameNode compromise | Revoke Kerberos ticket, isolate node | `kadmin`, iptables | < 5 min |
| DataNode ransomware | Snapshot rollback, block invalidation | `distcp`, HDFS snapshots | < 30 min |
| YARN malicious job | Kill application, block user | YARN REST API | < 2 min |
| Hive SQL injection | Disable Metastore, audit queries | Hive Ranger policies | < 10 min |
| Kafka topic poisoning | Pause consumers, quarantine topic | Kafka ACLs | < 5 min |
| Cloud S3 exfiltration | Revoke IAM keys, enable GuardDuty | AWS IAM, SCPs | < 15 min |
| MongoDB injection | Shutdown mongos, rotate creds | MongoDB RBAC | < 10 min |

### 3.3 Derivation: Detection Threshold via Z-Score

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Hive query logs show baseline outbound data transfer of $\mu = 50$ GB/hour with $\sigma = 8$ GB/hour. At what threshold $X$ should an alert fire if we use a 3-$\sigma$ rule?

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \\
3 &= \frac{X - 50}{8} \\
X &= 50 + 3 \cdot 8 \\
X &= 50 + 24 \\
X &= 74 \text{ GB/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any hourly outbound transfer exceeding **74 GB** should trigger an automatic SIEM alert. This follows the empirical rule that 99.7% of normal behavior falls within $\pm 3\sigma$ under Gaussian assumptions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**  
**Define "Incident Response" in the context of Big Data. List any THREE unique challenges that Big Data environments pose to traditional IR strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — distribution shown):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Incident Response (IR) in Big Data is the structured, policy-driven set of coordinated procedures used to detect, contain, eradicate, and recover from security incidents that compromise large-scale distributed data systems such as Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL clusters, and cloud data lakes.

**Three Unique Challenges (2 Marks — 0.67 each):**

1. **Volume of Logs** — Petabyte-scale log streams overwhelm traditional SIEM ingestion pipelines, causing alert fatigue and missed detections.
2. **Velocity of Data** — Real-time streaming systems (Kafka, Flink) have millisecond-level attack windows where reactive containment is impossible.
3. **Distributed Trust** — Compromised DataNodes continue participating in HDFS replication and YARN scheduling, spreading malware across the cluster.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**  
**Explain the significance of MTTD and MTTR metrics in Big Data incident response. Why is reducing MTTR more critical than reducing MTTD in modern data lake architectures?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** measures the average time between a compromise and its detection by the SIEM. It reflects the **visibility maturity** of the monitoring stack. **(1 Mark)**

> **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond/Recover)** measures the average time from detection to full restoration. It reflects the **operational preparedness** of the CSIRT and the effectiveness of pre-built automation playbooks. **(1 Mark)**

> **Why MTTR reduction is more critical (1 Mark):** In Big Data architectures, exfiltration happens rapidly (often within minutes). Even if MTTD is high, a low MTTR — achieved via **immutable snapshots, IaC (Infrastructure as Code) re-deployment, and pre-authorized network ACLs** — limits blast radius. Industry benchmarks (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report) show that **MTTR reduction yields higher ROI** than equivalent MTTD reduction because the post-detection phase involves irreversible damage control.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Model]**  
**(a)** Discuss the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle** in detail. For each of the four phases, explain **two Big Data-specific actions** that must be performed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Spark cluster running on AWS EMR processes 10 TB of customer transaction data daily. An attacker compromises an EMR worker node and exfiltrates data over 4 hours at a rate of 250 GB/hour. The security team takes 12 hours to detect and another 6 hours to recover. Compute the **MTTD, MTTR, Data Exfiltration Rate**, and determine if this exceeds the Z-score threshold if baseline transfer is $\mu = 100$ GB/hour and $\sigma = 30$ GB/hour. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — NIST Lifecycle with Big Data Actions (7 Marks)

**Phase 1: Preparation (2 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Preparation is the **single most important phase** because it determines the success of all subsequent phases.

- **Action 1:** Deploy **distributed audit logging** across all cluster nodes using `auditd` (Linux), HDFS audit logs, and YARN application logs aggregated into a centralized SIEM like Splunk or Elastic. Configure immutable log shipping to AWS S3 with Object Lock enabled to prevent attacker log tampering.
- **Action 2:** Build **hardened golden AMIs** for EMR worker nodes and HDFS DataNodes. Pre-install endpoint detection agents (CrowdStrike, Wazuh) and pre-authorize network isolation IAM policies so the CSIRT can quarantine nodes in under 60 seconds.

**Phase 2: Detection & Analysis (2 Marks)**

- **Action 1:** Implement **anomaly detection ML models** (Isolation Forest, LSTM Autoencoders) trained on baseline cluster behavior — normal query volumes, typical YARN job submission patterns, baseline outbound network traffic. Trigger alerts when Z-scores exceed $3\sigma$.
- **Action 2:** Establish **IoC (Indicator of Compromise) feeds** from MITRE ATT&CK and integrate them with the SIEM for automated correlation. Tag and prioritize alerts using the **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** and **ISS (Incident Severity Score)**.

**Phase 3: Containment, Eradication & Recovery (2 Marks)**

- **Action 1:** Execute **short-term containment** by immediately invalidating compromised Kerberos service tickets via `kadmin -q "purgekeys -all"` and blocking the node's IP at the security group level. Then perform **forensic snapshot** of the compromised EBS volumes before wiping.
- **Action 2:** Conduct **eradication and recovery** by rotating ALL service principal keys, regenerating TLS certificates, restoring HDFS data from immutable snapshots (validated via `hdfs dfs -checksum`), and re-launching Spark jobs from known-clean checkpoints stored in S3 Glacier.

**Phase 4: Post-Incident Activity (1 Mark)**

- **Action 1:** Conduct a **blameless postmortem** documenting root cause, timeline, detection gaps, and recovery friction points. Update YARA rules and SIEM correlation queries to prevent recurrence.
- **Action 2:** File **regulatory notifications** (GDPR Article 33 within 72 hours, HIPAA breach notification within 60 days) and conduct customer/stakeholder communications per the crisis communication plan.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Daily data processed: 10 TB
- Exfiltration duration: $t = 4$ hours
- Exfiltration rate observed: $250$ GB/hour
- MTTD given: $12$ hours
- MTTR given: $6$ hours
- Baseline: $\mu = 100$ GB/hour, $\sigma = 30$ GB/hour

**Step 1 — MTTD (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTD &= T_{detect} - T_{compromise} \\
MTTD &= 12 \text{ hours} - 0 \\
MTTD &= 12 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= T_{recovered} - T_{detect} \\
MTTR &= (12 + 6) - 12 \\
MTTR &= 6 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Data Exfiltrated (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{exfil} &= R_{exfil} \times t_{window} \\
V_{exfil} &= 250 \text{ GB/hour} \times 4 \text{ hours} \\
V_{exfil} &= 1000 \text{ GB} = 1 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Data Exfiltration Rate vs Baseline (2 Marks):**

Excess above baseline:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{excess} &= V_{exfil} - V_{baseline} \\
V_{excess} &= 1000 \text{ GB} - (100 \text{ GB/hour} \times 4 \text{ hours}) \\
V_{excess} &= 1000 - 400 \\
V_{excess} &= 600 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Z-Score Test (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \\
Z &= \frac{250 - 100}{30} \\
Z &= \frac{150}{30} \\
Z &= 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $Z = 5.0 > 3.0$, the exfiltration rate is **5 standard deviations** above baseline — a **statistically extreme anomaly** that should have triggered immediate SIEM alerts. The fact that MTTD was 12 hours indicates a **critical detection gap** in the deployed monitoring.

**Engineering Interpretation:** The MTTD of 12 hours for a Z=5.0 anomaly is unacceptable. The CSIRT must recalibrate SIEM thresholds and investigate why ML anomaly models failed to fire.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Alternative]**  
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Incident Severity Scoring (ISS)**. Compute the ISS for a breach with Confidentiality Impact = 4, Integrity Impact = 3, Downtime Impact = 5, and Regulatory Exposure = 5. Classify the severity level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **forensic readiness** in Big Data environments. Compare and contrast the challenges of forensic investigation in **Hadoop HDFS** versus **AWS S3 Data Lake** environments. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — ISS Computation (7 Marks)

**Definition (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Incident Severity Score (ISS)** is a composite metric used by CSIRTs to prioritize breach response. It is computed as:
>
> $ISS = (C \times I) + (D \times R)$
>
> where $C, I, D, R \in [0, 5]$. The score ranges from 0 (no impact) to 50 (catastrophic), with classification tiers:
> - 0–9: **Low (Severity 4)** — Monitor and patch
> - 10–19: **Medium (Severity 3)** — Standard response SLA
> - 20–29: **High (Severity 2)** — CSIRT activation required
> - 30–50: **Critical (Severity 1)** — Executive escalation, regulatory notification

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

Given $C = 4$, $I = 3$, $D = 5$, $R = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= (C \times I) + (D \times R) \\
ISS &= (4 \times 3) + (5 \times 5) \\
ISS &= 12 + 25 \\
ISS &= 37
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **ISS = 37 > 30**, the breach is classified as **CRITICAL (Severity Level 1)**. This triggers:
> - Immediate CSIRT activation
> - Executive board notification
> - 72-hour GDPR/HIPAA regulatory disclosure
> - External forensic engagement
> - Customer crisis communication

#### Part (b) — Forensic Readiness Comparison (7 Marks)

**Definition of Forensic Readiness (2 Marks):**

Forensic readiness is the **proactive capability** of an organization to preserve, collect, and analyze digital evidence in a legally admissible manner **before, during, and after** a security incident. In Big Data environments, this requires distributed log aggregation, tamper-evident storage, and chain-of-custody protocols.

**Hadoop HDFS Forensics (2.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | HDFS Characteristics |
|---|---|
| Log Sources | NameNode audit logs, DataNode block reports, YARN application logs |
| Evidence Volatility | High — blocks are replicated and may be deleted by HDFS rebalancing |
| Forensic Tools | Apache Accumulo, Cloudera Navigator, custom Hadoop log parsers |
| Key Challenge | **Block-level forensic reconstruction** requires reassembling files from distributed replicas; **HDFS trash** is not enabled by default in production, so deleted evidence is often unrecoverable |
| Chain of Custody | Difficult across multi-tenant HDFS clusters with shared Kerberos realms |

**AWS S3 Data Lake Forensics (2.5 Marks):**

| Aspect | AWS S3 Characteristics |
|---|---|
| Log Sources | S3 access logs, CloudTrail events, S3 Inventory reports |
| Evidence Volatility | Moderate — Object Lock and Versioning can preserve evidence |
| Forensic Tools | AWS Macie (data classification), Athena (SQL queries on logs), Detective |
| Key Challenge | **Cross-region replication** may scatter evidence across accounts; **IAM-based access** means an attacker with stolen keys can delete logs |
| Chain of Custody | Stronger via CloudTrail integrity validation and AWS Artifact audit reports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** S3 data lakes offer **stronger forensic primitives** (Object Lock, Versioning, CloudTrail integrity) out-of-the-box, but **HDFS forensics** require custom tooling and proactive log archival to S3 Glacier or equivalent cold storage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (and how to avoid losing marks):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MTTR with MTTC** — MTTR includes full recovery; MTTC ends at containment. KTU examiners specifically check whether you distinguish these. **(Lose up to 1 mark if confused)**
> 
> 2. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — Always write "12 hours" not just "12". KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in IR metric calculations.
> 
> 3. **Writing generic IR without Big Data context** — When asked about "incident response," mentioning "install antivirus" or "patch the server" without Big Data specifics (HDFS, Kerberos, YARN) earns **zero marks**. Always anchor answers in the distributed computing context.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the regulatory dimension** — Big Data breaches almost always have GDPR/HIPAA implications. Failing to mention the **72-hour notification window** in post-incident activity typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Incorrect Z-score interpretation** — Students often forget to state the **null hypothesis rejection** ("Since Z > 3, we reject the null hypothesis of normal behavior and classify as anomaly"). This single phrase distinguishes a top-scoring answer from an average one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Big Data Incident Response Strategies:**

- ✅ **IR is cyclical, not linear** — NIST lifecycle loops back from post-incident to preparation.
- ✅ **Four NIST phases:** Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident.
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, MTTC, RTO, RPO, ISS, F1-Score, Precision, Recall.
- ✅ **ISS Formula:** $ISS = (C \times I) + (D \times R)$, range 0–50, severity tier at 30.
- ✅ **Z-Score Threshold:** $3\sigma$ rule is the standard anomaly detection trigger.
- ✅ **Big Data IR Challenges:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Distributed Trust, Multi-tenancy.
- ✅ **Big Data-Specific Tools:** Splunk, ELK, Kerberos, Ranger, Atlas, Cloudera Navigator, AWS Macie.
- ✅ **Containment Speed:** NameNode compromise → 5 min MTTC; DataNode ransomware → 30 min MTTC.
- ✅ **Regulatory:** GDPR 72-hour notification, HIPAA 60-day notification, SOC 2 audit trail.
- ✅ **Forensic Readiness:** Immutable logs in S3 Glacier with Object Lock; chain-of-custody documentation.
- ✅ **NIST Reference:** SP 800-61 Rev. 2 is the authoritative source.
- ✅ **NIST Functions Complement:** IR aligns with **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** functions: Detect, Respond, Recover (with Identify and Protect feeding preparation).
- ✅ **Automation Trend:** **SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response)** platforms (e.g., Splunk SOAR, Cortex XSOAR) are now standard for reducing MTTR in Big Data environments.
- ✅ **Zero Trust Principle:** Never trust an internal DataNode — assume breach and validate continuously via Kerberos ticket rotation.
